### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q:**
**(a)** Explain the three Golden Rules of Accounting with suitable examples. State the limitations of the Double Entry System. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** From the following transactions of Ms. Lakshmi, prepare the Journal Entries and post them to the Ledger: **(7 marks)**

| Date | Transaction | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 May | Lakshmi started business with cash | 4,00,000 |
| 3 May | Deposited cash into bank | 2,00,000 |
| 5 May | Purchased goods from Sita on credit | 1,00,000 |
| 8 May | Sold goods to Geetha for cash | 1,50,000 |
| 10 May | Paid carriage on purchases | 5,000 |
| 12 May | Returned goods to Sita | 10,000 |
| 15 May | Cheque received from Geetha | 50,000 |
| 18 May | Issued a cheque to Sita | 89,000 (1,000 discount) |
| 20 May | Paid general expenses | 8,000 |
| 25 May | Withdrew from bank for office use | 50,000 |

---

#### Part (a) — Solution: Three Golden Rules and Limitations (7 marks)

**The Three Golden Rules of Accounting:**

**Rule 1 — Personal Accounts (Accounts of Persons and Firms):**
- **Debit the Receiver** of value
- **Credit the Giver** of value

*Example:* If salary of ₹ 25,000 is paid to Mr. Sharma, the entry is:
- Salary A/c Dr. ₹ 25,000 (Nominal — debit expenses)
- To Mr. Sharma ₹ 25,000 (Personal — credit the receiver? No — credit the giver)
- Wait, corrected: Salary paid means cash goes out (credit the giver of salary service is Sharma, the receiver of cash is Sharma, but he is the **giver of services**, so we credit him.)

**Correction:** Salary A/c Dr. 25,000 / To Cash A/c 25,000.

A cleaner example: When a loan is **received** from Mr. X — Cash Dr. / To Mr. X (credit the giver).

**Rule 2 — Real Accounts (Accounts of Assets and Things):**
- **Debit what comes in**
- **Credit what goes out**

*Example:* Purchase of furniture for ₹ 50,000 in cash:
- Furniture A/c Dr. ₹ 50,000 (comes in)
- To Cash A/c ₹ 50,000 (goes out)

**Rule 3 — Nominal Accounts (Accounts of Expenses, Losses, Incomes, Gains):**
- **Debit all expenses and losses**
- **Credit all incomes and gains**

*Example:* Rent received ₹ 30,000:
- Cash A/c Dr. ₹ 30,000
- To Rent Received A/c ₹ 30,000 (credit income)

**Limitations of the Double Entry System:**

1. **Requires expertise** — Bookkeeping staff must be trained in accounting principles.
2. **Time-consuming** — Every transaction requires dual recording.
3. **Costly** — Maintaining two aspects increases administrative cost.
4. **Window dressing possible** — Manipulative practices can hide the true financial position.
5. **Personal transactions of the owner** can be misused (e.g., disguised drawings).
6. **Does not record price-level changes** — It records only monetary values, not inflation effects.
7. **Not based on original cost** — Assets are recorded at historical cost, which may be irrelevant for replacement decisions.

---

#### Part (b) — Solution: Journal Entries and Ledger (7 marks)

**Journal Entries in Ms. Lakshmi's Books:**

| Date | Particulars | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 May | Cash A/c .............. Dr. | 4,00,000 | — |
|  |    To Capital A/c | — | 4,00,000 |
| 3 May | Bank A/c .............. Dr. | 2,00,000 | — |
|  |    To Cash A/c | — | 2,00,000 |
| 5 May | Purchases A/c .............. Dr. | 1,00,000 | — |
|  |    To Sita A/c | — | 1,00,000 |
| 8 May | Cash A/c .............. Dr. | 1,50,000 | — |
|  |    To Sales A/c | — | 1,50,000 |
| 10 May | Carriage Inward A/c .............. Dr. | 5,000 | — |
|  |    To Cash A/c | — | 5,000 |
| 12 May | Sita A/c .............. Dr. | 10,000 | — |
|  |    To Purchase Returns A/c | — | 10,000 |
| 15 May | Bank A/c .............. Dr. | 50,000 | — |
|  |    To Sales A/c | — | 50,000 |
| 18 May | Sita A/c .............. Dr. | 90,000 | — |
|  |    To Bank A/c | | 89,000 |
|  |    To Discount Received A/c | — | 1,000 |
| 20 May | General Expenses A/c .............. Dr. | 8,000 | — |
|  |    To Cash A/c | — | 8,000 |
| 25 May | Cash A/c .............. Dr. | 50,000 | — |
|  |    To Bank A/c | — | 50,000 |

**Selected Ledger Account — Cash A/c**

| Date | Particulars | Amount (₹) | Date | Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 May | To Capital | 4,00,000 | 3 May | By Bank | 2,00,000 |
| 8 May | To Sales | 1,50,000 | 10 May | By Carriage | 5,000 |
| 25 May | To Bank | 50,000 | 20 May | By Gen. Exp. | 8,000 |
|  |  |  | 30 May | **By Balance c/d** | **3,87,000** |
|  | **Total** | **6,00,000** |  | **Total** | **6,00,000** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. **Bank and Cash are separate accounts** — many students treat them as one, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Purchase Returns (Returns Outward)** is credited, **Sales Returns (Returns Inward)** is debited — easy confusion.
> 3. **Carriage Inward** is added to purchases (Trading Account), NOT to carriage outward (P&L).
> 4. **Cheque received from Geetha** = Bank A/c Dr. (not Cash A/c). Forgetting this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Accounting Process" (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- ☐ The accounting process is a **cyclic, sequential pipeline** — never parallel or independent steps.
- ☐ **Eight steps** are mandatory: Identify → Journal → Ledger → Trial Balance → Adjusting → Adjusted TB → Financial Statements → Closing.
- ☐ The **Double Entry System** is governed by the **equation** $\Delta A = \Delta L + \Delta E$.
- ☐ The **three golden rules** must be memorized verbatim:
  - **Personal:** Dr. the Receiver / Cr. the Giver
  - **Real:** Dr. what comes in / Cr. what goes out
  - **Nominal:** Dr. expenses and losses / Cr. incomes and gains
- ☐ **Source documents** (invoices, vouchers) are the foundation of journalizing — never record a transaction without one.
- ☐ The **Trial Balance** proves the **arithmetical accuracy** of the books, NOT the **factual accuracy**.
- ☐ A **balanced Trial Balance** does **not** mean there are no errors — errors of **omission, commission, principle, and compensating** can still exist.
- ☐ **Adjusting entries** must be made **before** preparing financial statements.
- ☐ **Closing entries** transfer all nominal account balances to the **Profit & Loss Account**.
- ☐ **Drawings** reduce capital but are **not** an expense — never include in P&L.
- ☐ **Discount Allowed** is an expense (P&L debit); **Discount Received** is an income (P&L credit).
- ☐ **Purchases Returns** → credit; **Sales Returns** → debit.
- ☐ The **Balance Sheet** is **not an account** — it is a **statement of financial position** as on a particular date.
- ☐ The **Trading Account** and **P&L Account** are **accounts** (they have debit and credit sides).
- ☐ For KTU 2024, expect **one full 14-mark problem** (journal + ledger + TB) in the ESE, and **one or two 3-mark conceptual questions** in Part A.
- ☐ Memorize the **format of the journal entry** (date, particulars, L.F., debit, credit, narration) — presentation carries **1–2 marks** in ESE valuation.
- ☐ Always end journal entries with a brief **narration** in brackets — missing narration is a common 0.5-mark deduction.
- ☐ **Net Profit formula:** $\text{NP} = \text{Gross Profit} - \text{Operating Expenses} + \text{Operating Incomes} - \text{Indirect Expenses} + \text{Indirect Incomes}$.
- ☐ **Closing Capital formula:** $\text{Closing Capital} = \text{Opening Capital} + \text{Additional Capital} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Drawings}$.
- ☐ In **full settlement** cases, **always pass a two-credit entry** — one to the receiver, one to discount received (when paying a creditor) or **two-debit entry** — discount allowed and cash (when receiving from a debtor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** Practice at least **3 full numerical problems** of 14 marks each before the exam. Focus on **neat ledger posting** and **proper journal format** — these are the two areas where KTU examiners deduct the maximum marks even when the underlying logic is correct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Purpose of Accounting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Purpose of Accounting ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Accounting** is the systematic process of **identifying, recording, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, interpreting, and communicating** financial transactions and events of a business entity to its stakeholders in a meaningful and useful manner. The **Purpose of Accounting** refers to the specific objectives, ends, and functional reasons for which the discipline of accounting is practiced. It answers the fundamental question: *"Why do businesses and organizations maintain accounts at all?"* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Reference):** > *"The purpose of accounting is to provide quantitative financial information about economic entities, primarily to enable informed economic decision-making by both internal and external users, and to discharge accountability and stewardship obligations."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🏥 The "Hospital Health Report" Analogy** > > Imagine a business as a **living human body**. Just as a doctor orders blood tests, X-rays, and ECGs to: > - Check whether the body is **healthy** (profit or loss), > - Measure **strengths and weaknesses** (assets and liabilities), > - Track **progress over time** (year-on-year growth), > - Warn about **impending risks** (liquidity issues), > > ...**accounting acts as the financial doctor of a business.** The "Purpose of Accounting" is simply the **list of medical questions** this financial doctor is trying to answer for the patient (the business). A small shopkeeper in Kerala writing daily sales in a ledger, a startup founder checking monthly profit, a bank officer evaluating a loan application — **all are using accounting to fulfill one or more of its core purposes**. ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering (OEC Context) As engineering students, you may design bridges, write code, or build machines — but every project you will ever lead, every startup you may co-found, and every procurement decision you make will involve **money**. The Purpose of Accounting is the **why** behind every rupee recorded in any organization's books. Without understanding *why* accounting exists, you cannot interpret financial statements, evaluate vendors, or even read your own salary slip properly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Highlight (Frequently Asked in KTU ESE):** > Accounting is **NOT** the same as Book-keeping. > - **Book-keeping** = Recording phase (mechanical, routine). > - **Accounting** = Recording + Classifying + Summarizing + Analyzing + Interpreting (analytical, decision-oriented). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Core Purposes of Accounting — Structured Logic Breakdown The purpose of accounting can be classified into **7 master purposes**, each serving a specific stakeholder or business need: #### **Purpose 1: Systematic Recording of Transactions (Memory Function)** - **Why:** Human memory is finite and fallible. A business may execute thousands of transactions daily. - **How:** Every transaction (purchase, sale, payment, receipt) is entered chronologically in a **Journal (Primary Book)** and then posted to **Ledger Accounts**. - **Outcome:** Permanent, verifiable, chronological record exists for every financial event. #### **Purpose 2: Ascertainment of Profit or Loss (Performance Evaluation)** - **Why:** The owner needs to know whether the business is making money or losing money over a given period. - **How:** Through the preparation of the **Trading Account** and **Profit & Loss Account** (under traditional approach) or the **Income Statement** (under modern approach). - **Outcome:** Net Profit or Net Loss is quantified for the accounting period. #### **Purpose 3: Ascertainment of Financial Position (Solvency Snapshot)** - **Why:** The owner, lenders, and creditors need to know "what the business owns" and "what it owes" on a given date. - **How:** Through the preparation of the **Balance Sheet** (Statement of Financial Position). - **Outcome:** A clear picture of **Assets**, **Liabilities**, and **Equity** is established. #### **Purpose 4: Providing Information to Stakeholders (Decision-Usefulness)** - **Why:** Different users (owners, creditors, banks, government, employees, investors) require financial data tailored to their decisions. - **How:** Through published **Financial Statements** — Balance Sheet, P&L, Cash Flow Statement, and Notes to Accounts. - **Outcome:** Each stakeholder gets relevant data to make informed economic choices. #### **Purpose 5: Legal Compliance & Statutory Obligation** - **Why:** The Companies Act 2013, Income Tax Act 1961, GST Act, and other statutes mandate maintenance of books of accounts. - **How:** Maintaining statutory registers, filing returns, and producing audited statements. - **Outcome:** The entity remains compliant and avoids penalties/imprisonment. #### **Purpose 6: Taxation & Government Reporting** - **Why:** Governments require businesses to declare income, pay taxes, and contribute to national revenue. - **How:** Accounting records form the **basis for tax computation** — Income Tax, GST, Customs Duty, etc. - **Outcome:** Accurate tax liability is determined; honest contribution to public exchequer. #### **Purpose 7: Planning, Budgeting, and Control (Managerial Use)** - **Why:** Management needs forward-looking data to plan future operations and control current spending. - **How:** Through **Budgetary Control**, **Variance Analysis**, **Cost Accounting**, and **Management Accounting** tools. - **Outcome:** Future strategies are evidence-based; deviations are detected early. ### 2.2 Engineering Field Utility — Real-World Applications | Engineering Domain | Practical Use of Accounting Purpose | |---|---| | **Civil Engineering** | Evaluating tender profitability, project cost control, depreciation of heavy machinery | | **Mechanical Engineering** | Inventory valuation of spare parts, ROI on new equipment, breakeven analysis | | **Computer Science/IT** | SaaS revenue recognition, server asset capitalization, R&D expenditure treatment | | **Electrical Engineering** | Capital budgeting for power projects, working capital management for grid operations | | **Startup / Entrepreneurship** | Investor pitch financials, burn rate tracking, valuation modelling | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > Although "Purpose of Accounting" is primarily a conceptual topic, KTU examiners often combine it with the **Accounting Equation** in 14-mark questions. Memorize the following table. | Concept | Formula / Expression | Application | |---|---|---| | **Basic Accounting Equation** | $A = L + C$ | Foundation of double-entry system | | **Expanded Equation** | $A = L + C + R - E$ | Introduces Revenue and Expenses | | **Profit Equation** | $Profit = Revenue - Expenses$ | Used in P&L Account | | **Closing Capital** | $C_{closing} = C_{opening} + Profit - Drawings$ | Used in Statement of Affairs | | **Debt-Equity Indicator** | $Solvency \ Ratio = \dfrac{L}{C}$ | Derived from Balance Sheet | | **Owner's Equity Growth** | $\Delta C = (Capital\;Introduced + Profit) - (Capital\;Withdrawn + Loss)$ | Year-on-year equity movement | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever you write $A = L + C$ in an exam, immediately expand it to its **full form** $A = L + (C_{opening} + Revenue - Expenses - Drawings)$ to fetch the **extra logic mark** examiners look for. ### 2.4 The "Why-How-Outcome" Logical Triad > [!NOTE] > For every purpose in a 14-mark answer, KTU examiners award marks using the **3-layer logical triad**. Use this structure religiously: 1. **WHY** (1 mark) — State the underlying need or problem. 2. **HOW** (1–2 marks) — Explain the accounting tool/mechanism used. 3. **OUTCOME** (1 mark) — State the resultant benefit or deliverable. This triad alone can fetch you **3 marks per purpose** × 4 purposes = **12 marks** in a typical 14-mark question. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation & Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Accounting Equation from First Principles Let us derive the foundation equation of accounting step by step. This derivation often appears as a sub-part (a) in 14-mark KTU questions. **Step 1: Definition of the three fundamental elements** Let: - $A$ = Total Assets (what the business **owns**) - $L$ = Total Liabilities (what the business **owes** to outsiders) - $C$ = Capital (what the business owes to the **owner**) **Step 2: Recognition of the dual-aspect concept** Every business transaction has **two equal and opposite effects**. The assets of a business are always financed by either outsiders (liabilities) or the owner (capital). Therefore, the **total of all assets must equal the total of all claims** against those assets. **Step 3: Formation of the equation** $$A = L + C$$ This is the **Basic Accounting Equation** (also called the **Balance Sheet Equation**). **Step 4: Introducing Revenue and Expenses** Revenue increases capital (it is an inflow of economic benefits). Expenses decrease capital (they are outflows of economic benefits). Let: - $R$ = Total Revenue during the period - $E$ = Total Expenses during the period Then the expanded equation becomes: $$A = L + C + (R - E)$$ **Step 5: Introducing Drawings (Owner's Withdrawals)** The owner may withdraw cash or goods for personal use. These are called **Drawings ($D$)** and they **reduce** capital. $$A = L + C_{opening} + R - E - D$$ **Step 6: Restating for the closing position of capital** $$C_{closing} = C_{opening} + R - E - D$$ Therefore, the **full expanded accounting equation** is: $$A = L + (C_{opening} + R - E - D)$$ **Step 7: Interpretation** This equation reveals a deep truth — the **balance sheet always balances** because every asset is matched by an equal claim, and every transaction maintains this equilibrium. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Stakeholders vs. Their Information Needs > [!NOTE] > This **stakeholder-information matrix** is the **most important analytical tool** for answering 7-mark sub-parts in KTU 14-mark questions. Memorize it. | Stakeholder | Primary Information Required | Purpose of Accounting Served | |---|---|---| | **Owner / Shareholders** | Profit, dividends, growth, equity value | Purpose 2, 4, 7 | | **Creditors / Banks** | Solvency, liquidity, debt-servicing capacity | Purpose 3, 4 | | **Government / Tax Authorities** | Taxable income, GST payable, compliance status | Purpose 5, 6 | | **Employees / Trade Unions** | Profitability (wage negotiations), job security | Purpose 2, 4 | | **Management** | Cost data, budget variances, performance KPIs | Purpose 7 | | **Investors / Analysts** | Future earnings potential, risk profile | Purpose 4, 7 | | **Suppliers / Vendors** | Creditworthiness of buyer | Purpose 3, 4 | | **Customers (in some cases)** | Long-term viability of supplier | Purpose 3, 4 | | **Society / Public** | CSR, employment, environmental impact | Purpose 4, 6 | | **Regulatory Bodies (RBI, SEBI, IRDA)** | Prudential compliance, systemic stability | Purpose 5 | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Application) **Question:** A sole proprietorship firm commenced business on 1st April 2024 with Capital of ₹5,00,000. During the year, additional capital introduced was ₹1,00,000. Total revenue earned was ₹3,50,000 and total expenses incurred were ₹2,00,000. The owner withdrew ₹50,000 for personal use. Compute the closing capital and the total assets assuming liabilities of ₹3,00,000. **Solution (Step-by-Step):** $$C_{closing} = C_{opening} + Additional\;Capital + Revenue - Expenses - Drawings$$ Substituting the values: $$C_{closing} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000$$ $$C_{closing} = 10{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000$$ $$C_{closing} = 7{,}50{,}000$$ Now applying the accounting equation: $$A = L + C_{closing}$$ $$A = 3{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}50{,}000$$ $$A = 10{,}50{,}000$$ **Interpretation:** The business owns total assets worth **₹10,50,000**, financed by outsiders (₹3,00,000) and the owner (₹7,50,000). This single numerical demonstrates **three purposes simultaneously** — recording, profit ascertainment, and financial position disclosure. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Accounting Process Flow — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD input1[Financial Transactions
Source Documents]:::inp step1[Step 1 Recording
Journal Entry]:::proc step2[Step 2 Classifying
Ledger Posting]:::proc step3[Step 3 Summarizing
Trial Balance]:::proc step4[Step 4 Analyzing
Final Accounts]:::proc step5[Step 5 Interpreting
Ratio Analysis]:::proc out1[Final Output
Financial Statements]:::out user1[Internal Users
Management]:::usr user2[External Users
Investors Creditors Government]:::usr input1 --> step1 step1 --> step2 step2 --> step3 step3 --> step4 step4 --> step5 step5 --> out1 out1 --> user1 out1 --> user2 classDef inp fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000000 classDef proc fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000000 classDef out fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000 classDef usr fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.2 The Purpose of Accounting Ecosystem ```mermaid flowchart LR center((PURPOSE OF
ACCOUNTING)):::core p1[Purp 1 Systematic
Recording]:::purp p2[Purp 2 Profit and
Loss Ascertainment]:::purp p3[Purp 3 Financial
Position]:::purp p4[Purp 4 Information
to Users]:::purp p5[Purp 5 Legal
Compliance]:::purp p6[Purp 6 Tax
Reporting]:::purp p7[Purp 7 Planning
and Control]:::purp p1 --> center p2 --> center p3 --> center p4 --> center p5 --> center p6 --> center p7 --> center center --> eng[Application in
Engineering Projects]:::eng classDef core fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef purp fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000 classDef eng fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Stakeholder Mapping Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Accounting Records
Books of Accounts]:::src branch1[Internal Stakeholders]:::br branch2[External Stakeholders]:::br i1[Owners and Partners]:::int i2[Management Team]:::int i3[Employees]:::int e1[Banks and Creditors]:::ext e2[Government and Tax]:::ext e3[Investors and Public]:::ext e4[Suppliers and Customers]:::ext purpose[Purpose 4 of Accounting
Providing Useful Information]:::goal start --> branch1 start --> branch2 branch1 --> i1 branch1 --> i2 branch1 --> i3 branch2 --> e1 branch2 --> e2 branch2 --> e3 branch2 --> e4 i1 --> purpose i2 --> purpose i3 --> purpose e1 --> purpose e2 --> purpose e3 --> purpose e4 --> purpose classDef src fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000000 classDef br fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000000 classDef int fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000000 classDef ext fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000000 classDef goal fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Hierarchy of Decision-Making Supported by Accounting ```mermaid flowchart TD level0[Daily Operations
Cash Book and Petty Cash]:::lvl level1[Tactical Decisions
Departmental Budgets]:::lvl level2[Strategic Decisions
Capital Budgeting M and A]:::lvl level3[Compliance Decisions
Statutory Filings Audit]:::lvl level4[Long Term Wealth
Shareholder Value]:::lvl level0 --> level1 level1 --> level2 level2 --> level3 level3 --> level4 classDef lvl fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Define accounting. State any four primary purposes of accounting.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Accounting is the systematic process of recording, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, interpreting, and communicating financial transactions of a business entity to its users for decision-making. **Four Primary Purposes (½ Mark Each = 2 Marks):** 1. **Systematic Recording of Transactions** — To maintain a permanent, chronological record of all financial events so that memory does not fail. 2. **Ascertainment of Profit or Loss** — To determine the net result (profit/loss) of business operations over a given accounting period. 3. **Ascertainment of Financial Position** — To know what the business owns (assets) and owes (liabilities) on a particular date. 4. **Providing Information to Users** — To supply relevant financial data to owners, creditors, government, employees, and other stakeholders for informed decision-making. *(Any other valid purpose such as legal compliance, taxation, planning, and control may also be accepted.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]** **Distinguish between Book-keeping and Accounting. Why is the purpose of accounting broader than book-keeping?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Basis of Difference | Book-keeping | Accounting | |---|---|---| | **Scope** (1 Mark) | Limited to recording only | Includes recording, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, interpreting | | **Stage** (½ Mark) | Primary / Routine stage | Secondary / Analytical stage | | **Skill Required** (½ Mark) | Mechanical, clerical | Analytical, judgmental, decision-oriented | | **Purpose** (1 Mark) | To maintain records | To fulfill broader purposes — profit ascertainment, financial position, decision support, tax compliance, planning & control | **Conclusion:** Accounting begins where book-keeping ends. While book-keeping only *records* data, accounting *uses* that data to fulfill strategic purposes such as decision-making, taxation, legal compliance, and forward planning — making its purpose significantly broader. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice Format > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** In Part B, you are given a choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both carry 14 marks and are divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must answer EITHER A OR B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]* **a) Explain the various purposes/objectives of accounting in detail. (7 Marks)** **b) Discuss the different users of accounting information and their information needs. (7 Marks)** #### **Model Answer for (a) — Purposes of Accounting (7 Marks)** Accounting serves the following **seven major purposes**. Each is explained using the **Why-How-Outcome** triad as expected by KTU examiners. **1. Systematic Recording of Transactions (1 Mark):** - **Why:** Business transactions occur continuously and in large volumes; human memory cannot retain them accurately. - **How:** Each transaction is recorded in the **Journal** (chronological book of original entry) and then posted to the **Ledger** (book of final entry) using the **double-entry system**. - **Outcome:** A complete, verifiable, permanent record of all financial events is created, forming the foundation for all other purposes. **2. Ascertainment of Profit or Loss (1 Mark):** - **Why:** The owner must know whether the business has earned a profit or incurred a loss during the period. - **How:** The **Trading Account** and **Profit & Loss Account** are prepared to match revenue earned against expenses incurred. - **Outcome:** Net profit or net loss for the accounting period is determined, which is essential for dividend, tax, and reinvestment decisions. **3. Ascertainment of Financial Position (1 Mark):** - **Why:** Stakeholders need to know the economic status of the business on a given date — its assets, liabilities, and owner's equity. - **How:** The **Balance Sheet** is prepared at the end of the accounting period, listing all assets and liabilities. - **Outcome:** A snapshot of the business's solvency and net worth is obtained, helping in credit, loan, and investment decisions. **4. Providing Information to Users (1 Mark):** - **Why:** Various internal and external parties need financial data tailored to their specific decisions. - **How:** Through published **Financial Statements** — Income Statement, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow Statement, and Notes to Accounts. - **Outcome:** Owners, creditors, government, employees, and investors receive relevant information for their respective decision-making. **5. Legal Compliance (1 Mark):** - **Why:** Statutes such as the **Companies Act 2013**, **Income Tax Act 1961**, and **GST Act** legally require maintenance of books of accounts. - **How:** By maintaining statutory registers, filing periodic returns, and getting accounts audited where required. - **Outcome:** The entity avoids penalties, prosecution, and loss of license; maintains lawful status. **6. Taxation and Government Reporting (1 Mark):** - **Why:** The government requires businesses to declare income and pay taxes honestly. - **How:** Accounting records form the **base data** for computing Income Tax, GST, Customs Duty, Professional Tax, etc. - **Outcome:** Correct tax liability is determined and discharged, contributing to the nation's revenue. **7. Planning, Budgeting, and Control (1 Mark):** - **Why:** Management needs forward-looking data to set targets, allocate resources, and control deviations. - **How:** Through **Budgetary Control**, **Standard Costing**, **Variance Analysis**, and **Management Accounting** tools. - **Outcome:** Future operations are planned scientifically; inefficiencies are identified and corrected promptly. #### **Model Answer for (b) — Users of Accounting Information (7 Marks)** Accounting information is used by a wide variety of stakeholders. These are classified into **Internal Users** and **External Users**. **A. Internal Users (3 Marks):** 1. **Owners / Proprietors / Partners (½ Mark):** They need profit data, dividend calculations, and growth trends to assess return on investment and plan future capital infusion. 2. **Management (½ Mark):** Requires cost data, budget variances, segment-wise performance, and KPIs for operational control and strategic planning. 3. **Employees and Trade Unions (½ Mark):** Use profitability data to negotiate wages, bonuses, and to assess job security and long-term employment prospects. 4. **Internal Auditors (½ Mark):** Use accounting data to verify compliance and detect operational inefficiencies. 5. **Other Departments (½ Mark):** Purchase, Production, Marketing, and HR departments use cost and revenue data for their respective decisions. 6. **Example (½ Mark):** In Infosys, internal MIS reports use accounting data to track project profitability and resource utilization. **B. External Users (3 Marks):** 1. **Creditors and Banks (½ Mark):** Assess solvency, liquidity, and debt-servicing capacity before granting credit or loans. They study the **Debt-Equity Ratio** and **Current Ratio**. 2. **Government and Tax Authorities (½ Mark):** Use accounting records to determine taxable income, verify GST compliance, and assess statutory dues. 3. **Investors and Potential Investors (½ Mark):** Analyze earnings per share, return on equity, and future growth potential before investing. 4. **Suppliers and Vendors (½ Mark):** Evaluate the creditworthiness of the buyer before extending trade credit. 5. **Customers (½ Mark):** In some industries (e.g., airlines, telecom), customers assess the long-term viability of the supplier. 6. **Regulatory Bodies (½ Mark):** RBI, SEBI, IRDA use accounting data for prudential supervision and systemic stability. 7. **Society and Public (½ Mark):** Interested in CSR activities, employment generation, and environmental impact reported in financial statements. **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Accounting serves a remarkably diverse set of users with varied information needs. The ability of accounting to satisfy such varied requirements — from a sole proprietor's daily cash needs to a multinational investor's strategic analysis — is what makes it one of the most versatile information systems in modern commerce. --- ### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]* **a) Explain the qualitative characteristics that accounting information must possess in order to fulfill its purpose. (7 Marks)** **b) "Accounting has certain limitations." Discuss this statement with suitable examples. (7 Marks)** #### **Model Answer for (a) — Qualitative Characteristics of Accounting Information (7 Marks)** For accounting to fulfill its purpose of being useful for decision-making, the information it provides must possess certain qualitative characteristics prescribed by the **IASB Conceptual Framework** and **Ind AS Framework**. **1. Understandability (1 Mark):** Information must be presented in a manner that is **comprehensible** to users who have reasonable business and accounting knowledge. Complex jargon should be avoided in financial statements. **2. Relevance (1 Mark):** Information must be **relevant** to the decision-making needs of users. It should have **predictive value**, **confirmatory value**, or both. For example, segment-wise revenue data is relevant for an investor analyzing growth areas. **3. Reliability (Faithful Representation) (1 Mark):** Information must be free from material error and bias. It should represent faithfully the transactions it purports to depict. Three sub-qualities: - **Faithful representation** - **Substance over form** (e.g., a finance lease is capitalized even though legal ownership is not transferred) - **Neutrality** (free from bias) **4. Comparability (1 Mark):** Users must be able to **compare** financial statements across periods (intra-firm comparison) and across different firms (inter-firm comparison). Consistent application of accounting policies is essential. **5. Consistency (½ Mark):** Similar transactions should be treated similarly from one period to another, and changes in policy should be disclosed with justification. **6. Verifiability (½ Mark):** Information should be capable of being verified by independent observers using the same measurement methods. This is the foundation of **audit assurance**. **7. Timeliness (½ Mark):** Information must reach users **in time** to influence their decisions. Outdated information loses its relevance. **8. Conservatism (Prudence) (½ Mark):** Revenues and profits should **not** be recognized until realized, but provisions should be made for all known liabilities and losses. This protects users from overly optimistic reporting. **Conclusion (½ Mark):** These qualitative characteristics ensure that accounting information is not just numerically correct but **decision-useful**, thereby fulfilling the broader purpose of accounting. #### **Model Answer for (b) — Limitations of Accounting (7 Marks)** Despite its critical role, accounting is not without limitations. The following are the major limitations: **1. Records Only Monetary Transactions (1 Mark):** Accounting ignores **non-monetary events** such as quality of management, employee morale, customer satisfaction, brand reputation, and environmental impact. Example: A company with strong brand equity may appear "weaker" on paper than a brand-less competitor. **2. Based on Historical Cost (1 Mark):** Assets are recorded at the price paid at acquisition. **Inflation, deflation, and market appreciation are ignored.** Example: Land bought in 1990 for ₹10 lakhs may be worth ₹5 crores today, but the balance sheet still shows ₹10 lakhs. **3. Personal Bias of the Accountant (1 Mark):** Accounting involves numerous estimates and judgments — depreciation method, bad debt provision, inventory valuation, etc. These are influenced by the accountant's personal bias, affecting the **objectivity** of financial statements. **4. Window Dressing Possible (1 Mark):** Managers may manipulate accounting policies (e.g., changing depreciation method, recognizing revenue early) to make financial statements look more favorable. This is **legal but unethical** and defeats the very purpose of accounting. **5. Ignores Price Level Changes (1 Mark):** Traditional accounting does not adjust for **inflation**. Comparing figures from different years without inflation adjustment leads to misleading conclusions. **6. Inability to Measure Human Capital (½ Mark):** Employees' skills, experience, and knowledge are not reflected in the balance sheet, even though they may be the most valuable asset of a knowledge-based firm like TCS or Infosys. **7. Fixed Assets Depreciation is Arbitrary (½ Mark):** Methods like Straight Line Method (SLM) and Written Down Value (WDV) are estimates. The "true" depreciation in market terms may differ significantly. **8. Difficulty in Verification of All Data (½ Mark):** Some items like goodwill, brand value, and contingent liabilities are based on estimates and cannot be verified with precision. **Conclusion (½ Mark):** While accounting is indispensable, its users must be aware of its limitations. Combined with **non-financial indicators** and modern tools like **fair value accounting** and **sustainability reporting**, the usefulness of accounting can be enhanced. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **🚨 Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Examiner's Perspective):** > > 1. **Treating Book-keeping and Accounting as synonyms** — This is the **#1 mistake**. Examiners deduct 1 full mark instantly. Always clarify that book-keeping is the *recording subset* of accounting. > > 2. **Listing purposes without explanation** — Writing only "To know profit" without the *How* and *Outcome* fetches only **½ mark** per purpose. You need the **Why-How-Outcome triad** to fetch the full **1 mark per purpose**. > > 3. **Forgetting the Accounting Equation** — In any "purpose" question, examiners expect a reference to the equation $A = L + C$ at least once. Omitting it loses **1 mark**. > > 4. **Confusing "Users" with "Purposes"** — Many students list users *as* purposes. Remember: **Users** are the *consumers* of accounting; **Purposes** are the *objectives* accounting aims to achieve. > > 5. **Ignoring limitations** — In a 7-mark sub-part on "Discuss the purposes", many students skip the limitations. Add a short paragraph on limitations to fetch **2 extra marks**. > > 6. **No diagram/illustration** — KTU 2024 scheme awards **bonus marks** (sometimes 1–2 marks) for hand-drawn or schematic flow diagrams. Always include a simple flowchart of the accounting process. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Purpose of Accounting** - **Definition:** Accounting is the systematic process of **identifying, recording, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, interpreting, and communicating** financial information. - **Origin of Term:** The word "Account" derives from the Latin *computare* (to count/calculate). - **Book-keeping ≠ Accounting** — Book-keeping is the recording subset; accounting is the broader analytical discipline. - **7 Master Purposes:** Systematic Recording → Profit/Loss Ascertainment → Financial Position → Information to Users → Legal Compliance → Taxation → Planning & Control. - **Accounting Equation:** $A = L + C$ (basic); $A = L + C_{opening} + R - E - D$ (expanded form). - **Internal Users:** Owners, Management, Employees, Internal Auditors, Other Departments. - **External Users:** Creditors, Banks, Government, Tax Authorities, Investors, Suppliers, Customers, Regulatory Bodies, Society. - **Qualitative Characteristics:** Understandability, Relevance, Reliability, Comparability, Consistency, Verifiability, Timeliness, Conservatism. - **Limitations:** Monetary-only recording, historical cost bias, personal bias of accountant, window dressing, ignores price level changes, ignores human capital, arbitrary depreciation. - **Stakeholder-Information Matrix:** Always map each user to the **specific purpose** they are served by — examiners reward this linkage with bonus marks. - **Engineering Application:** Every project you will ever handle has a **cost**, a **budget**, a **depreciation schedule**, and a **profitability metric** — all rooted in these seven purposes. - **Board-Examiner Favourite Line:** *"Accounting is the language of business. The purpose of accounting is to make this language intelligible, reliable, and decision-useful."* — quote this in your conclusion for the final **impression mark**. - **Mandatory Closing Sentence in 14-Mark Answers:** Always end with a concluding paragraph linking the **purposes to the broader goal of economic decision-making** — this fetches the elusive final mark that separates **A-grade from B-grade** answers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Examiner's Mantra:** *"If you cannot explain why accounting exists, you cannot use it. If you cannot use it, you cannot lead. KTU 2024 expects you to be a leader, not just a recorder."*

topics.title: Classification of Accounting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Accounting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Accounting** refers to the systematic categorization of accounting into distinct branches or types based on the **purpose, end-user, regulatory environment, and reporting framework** that govern the recording, summarizing, and communication of financial and non-financial information.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECET725 - Finance for Engineering)**, classification is taught as the first conceptual step that helps engineering students identify *which* accounting data is relevant for *which* business decision. Each branch has its own conventions, legal backing, and stakeholder group.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of accounting like a hospital.** A hospital has different departments — Cardiology, Neurology, Pediatrics, Radiology. A patient is the *same person*, but the doctor uses different tools, knowledge, and language depending on which organ they are treating. Similarly, a business transaction is the same event, but accountants use different "departments" (branches) to view it:
> - For tax filing → **Tax Accounting**
> - For internal cost control → **Cost Accounting**
> - For investor reporting → **Financial Accounting**
> - For management decisions → **Management Accounting**

So when we say "Classification of Accounting", we mean **"the process of dividing the master discipline of Accounting into specialized branches so that each branch can serve a specific information need."**

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters for Engineers

Engineering students often ask, *"Why am I, a future engineer, studying accounting?"* The answer is:

- Every engineering project is also a **financial project**.
- Engineers must understand **cost accounting** for project budgeting.
- Engineers involved in **tendering, contracts, or project finance** must understand financial accounting.
- Engineers running **startups or product companies** must know tax accounting and management accounting.
- Engineers working in **audits, compliance, or public sector units (PSUs)** need governmental accounting knowledge.

The **three primary classifications** that KTU emphasizes are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Three of Accounting (Most Frequently Asked in KTU ESE):**
> 1. **Financial Accounting** — External users, statutory, historical.
> 2. **Cost Accounting** — Internal users, product/service costing, operational.
> 3. **Management Accounting** — Internal users, future-oriented, decision-making.

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants Reference

While this is a non-quantitative theoretical topic, the following **standard conventions** are important:

- **Reporting Period** = **12 months** (1 financial year) for Financial Accounting
- **Going Concern Assumption** = Indefinite business life (used in Financial Accounting)
- **Money Measurement Concept** = All values recorded in a common **monetary unit** (₹, $, €)
- **Historical Cost Convention** = Assets recorded at **acquisition cost**, not market value (default in Financial Accounting)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Classification Tree

Accounting is classified along **four primary dimensions**:

1. **Based on the User Group** (External vs Internal)
2. **Based on the Purpose** (Recording, Costing, Decision-making, Compliance)
3. **Based on the Regulatory Framework** (Statutory, Non-statutory, International)
4. **Based on the Nature of Entity** (Corporate, Government, Non-profit, Personal)

## 2.2 Detailed Branch-Wise Breakdown

### A. Financial Accounting (FA)

- **Definition**: The branch of accounting that records, summarizes, and reports the financial transactions of a business entity to **external stakeholders** (shareholders, creditors, banks, regulators, tax authorities) in accordance with **statutory frameworks** (Ind AS, IFRS, GAAP).
- **Key Features**:
  - **Historical in nature** (records past transactions).
  - **Mandatory** for companies under the Companies Act, 2013.
  - **Standardized format** (Balance Sheet, P&L Statement, Cash Flow Statement).
  - **Audited** by independent Chartered Accountants.
  - Time horizon = **Annual / Quarterly** reporting.
- **Primary Users**: Investors, lenders, government, regulatory bodies, suppliers, customers, public at large.
- **Engineering Relevance**: Engineers working in listed companies, IPO-bound startups, or public sector units must understand these statements to read annual reports and assess corporate health.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule:** Financial Accounting is **backward-looking** and **externally focused**.

### B. Cost Accounting (CA)

- **Definition**: The branch of accounting concerned with the **recording, classification, allocation, and analysis of costs** incurred in producing a product or delivering a service.
- **Key Features**:
  - Focuses on **ascertaining the cost per unit** of output.
  - Helps in **inventory valuation**, **pricing decisions**, and **cost control**.
  - Governed by **Cost Accounting Standards (CAS)** issued by the **ICAI (Cost)**: specifically **CAS 1 to CAS 24** applicable to companies in regulated sectors.
  - Highly relevant for manufacturing, construction, and engineering projects.
- **Cost Components (The 3 Ms)**: **Materials + Labor + Expenses (Overheads)**.
- **Primary Users**: Internal management, production managers, project managers, pricing teams.
- **Engineering Relevance**: An engineer in a manufacturing plant must compute the cost of each component to decide whether to **make or buy**, and to file **cost audit reports** (mandatory for certain industries under Section 148 of the Companies Act, 2013).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Highlight — Total Cost:**
> $$\text{Total Cost} = \text{Direct Material} + \text{Direct Labor} + \text{Direct Expenses} + \text{Overheads}$$

### C. Management Accounting (MA)

- **Definition**: The branch of accounting that provides **financial and non-financial information to internal management** for **planning, controlling, decision-making, and performance evaluation**.
- **Key Features**:
  - **Future-oriented** (uses budgets, forecasts, projections).
  - **No statutory requirement** (purely discretionary).
  - Uses techniques like **CVP Analysis, Budgetary Control, Standard Costing, Variance Analysis, Ratio Analysis, Responsibility Accounting**.
  - Information is **tailor-made** for each decision.
- **Primary Users**: Top management, functional managers, strategic planners.
- **Engineering Relevance**: Project managers use management accounting for **NPV, IRR, payback period** analysis of engineering projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule:** Management Accounting is **forward-looking** and **internally focused**.

### D. Tax Accounting

- **Definition**: The branch of accounting that deals with the **computation, recording, and reporting of tax liabilities** of an entity in compliance with the **Income Tax Act, 1961, GST Act, 2017, and other tax statutes**.
- **Key Features**:
  - Governed by **tax laws**, not accounting standards.
  - Often diverges from Financial Accounting (e.g., depreciation methods differ).
  - Filed annually (ITR, GST returns, TDS returns).
- **Primary Users**: Tax authorities, internal tax team, auditors.

### E. Forensic Accounting

- **Definition**: A specialized branch that combines **accounting, auditing, and investigative skills** to detect fraud, embezzlement, and financial irregularities.
- **Engineering Relevance**: Engineers in **internal audit, vigilance, or anti-corruption** roles in PSUs and large corporates.

### F. Governmental / Public Sector Accounting

- **Definition**: Accounting practiced by government departments, municipalities, and public sector undertakings, governed by formats prescribed by the **CAG (Comptroller and Auditor General)**, **Budget Manual**, and **GFR (General Financial Rules), 2017**.
- **Key Feature**: Uses **fund accounting** rather than profit accounting.
- **Engineering Relevance**: Engineers in **PWD, CPWD, Irrigation, Railways, Defense, and PSU projects** must understand government accounting for **bill passing, work order accounting, and audit objections**.

### G. Inflation Accounting

- **Definition**: Adjusting financial statements to reflect the **impact of changing price levels** using methods like **Current Purchasing Power (CPP)** and **Current Cost Accounting (CCA)**.
- **Why needed**: Historical cost accounting fails during high inflation (e.g., 1970s oil crisis, 2022–2023 inflation in India).

### H. Social Responsibility Accounting (Social Accounting)

- **Definition**: Reports the **social and environmental impact** of a business (CSR spending, carbon footprint, community welfare).
- **Mandated by**: **Section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013** (CSR spending ≥ 2% of average net profits).

### I. Human Resource Accounting (HRA)

- **Definition**: Accounting for the **value of human assets** (employees) as intangible assets of the firm.
- **Status**: Still experimental; not part of mainstream financial statements.

### J. Auditing (Independent Branch)

- **Definition**: The **systematic examination of financial records** to verify their accuracy and compliance with standards.
- **Types**: Statutory Audit, Internal Audit, Tax Audit (Section 44AB), Cost Audit (Section 148), Management Audit, Performance Audit.

## 2.3 The 4-Question Classification Decision Rule

To classify any accounting information, ask:

1. **Who needs it?** → External (FA, Tax) or Internal (CA, MA)?
2. **When is it needed?** → Past (FA, CA) or Future (MA)?
3. **Is it legally required?** → Yes (FA, Tax, Govt) or No (MA, HRA)?
4. **What is the purpose?** → Reporting (FA), Costing (CA), Decision (MA), Compliance (Tax/Audit)?

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Classification Table for KTU 2024 ESE**

| **Parameter** | **Financial Accounting** | **Cost Accounting** | **Management Accounting** | **Tax Accounting** | **Govt. Accounting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| User | External | Internal | Internal | Tax Authority + Internal | Govt + Public |
| Time Focus | Past (Historical) | Past (Cost focus) | Future (Forecast) | Past (Statutory) | Past (Budget vs Actual) |
| Mandatory | **Yes** (Statute) | Conditional (CAS) | **No** (Voluntary) | **Yes** (Tax Laws) | **Yes** (GFR/Constitution) |
| Standard | Ind AS / IFRS | CAS (ICAI-CMA) | None specific | Income Tax Act, GST Act | CAG Guidelines |
| Reports | Balance Sheet, P&L, CF | Cost Sheet, COGM | Budgets, Variance, CVP | ITR, GST Returns | Appropriation Account |
| Audit | Statutory Audit | Cost Audit (where applicable) | Internal Audit | Tax Audit (Sec 44AB) | CAG Audit |
| Frequency | Annual / Quarterly | Continuous | Continuous / Periodic | Annual | Annual + Monthly |
| Unit of Measure | Money | Money + Physical | Money + Non-monetary | Money (taxable value) | Money |
| Engineering Use | Reading annual reports | Make-or-buy, pricing | Capex decisions, NPV | TDS, GST, IT filing | PWD/Railway bill passing |
| Profit Focus | **Yes** (Net Profit) | No (Cost focus) | No (Decision focus) | **Yes** (Taxable Profit) | No (Service focus) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle but high-yield point:** Cost Accounting deals with **COST** (not profit), Management Accounting deals with **DECISIONS** (not just cost), Financial Accounting deals with **PROFIT & POSITION** for outsiders.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Link to Engineering:**
> - **Tata Steel (Manufacturing Engineer)** → Cost Accounting (per-ton steel cost).
> - **L&T (Civil Engineer)** → Cost + Management Accounting (project costing, NPV).
> - **ISRO (R&D Engineer)** → Government Accounting (budget utilization, audit).
> - **Flipkart Tech (Software Engineer)** → Management Accounting (cloud spend, capex decisions).
> - **Reliance Jio (Telecom)** → Tax Accounting (GST, TDS compliance) + Financial Accounting (IPO disclosures).
> - **PWD Engineer (Kerala)** → Government Accounting (bill preparation, work accounts, MB).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Comparative Derivations

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logic for Classification of Cost in Cost Accounting

While "Classification of Accounting" is primarily qualitative, KTU expects students to **derive the cost of a product** as a numerical illustration. Let us exhaustively derive the **Cost Sheet of a manufacturing company**, which is the most common KTU numerical.

### Problem Set-Up (Illustrative)

A Kerala-based engineering firm (e.g., a steel furniture maker) provides the following data for the year ending 31-Mar-2025:

| **Item** | **Amount (₹)** |
|---|---|
| Raw Materials Consumed | 5,00,000 |
| Direct Wages (Direct Labour) | 3,00,000 |
| Factory Rent | 1,20,000 |
| Factory Insurance | 30,000 |
| Depreciation on Plant & Machinery | 80,000 |
| Indirect Materials | 50,000 |
| Indirect Labour | 70,000 |
| Office Salaries | 90,000 |
| Selling & Distribution Expenses | 1,10,000 |
| Opening Stock of Raw Material | 60,000 |
| Closing Stock of Raw Material | 40,000 |
| Opening Stock of Finished Goods | 80,000 |
| Closing Stock of Finished Goods | 1,20,000 |
| Opening Work-in-Progress (WIP) | 30,000 |
| Closing Work-in-Progress (WIP) | 20,000 |

### Step 1: Compute Raw Materials Consumed

The **Raw Materials Consumed** is given directly, but the standard derivation is:

$$\text{Raw Material Consumed} = \text{Opening Stock of RM} + \text{Purchases} - \text{Closing Stock of RM}$$

In our problem, this is **already provided as ₹ 5,00,000**. We accept it as the input.

### Step 2: Compute Prime Cost

**Prime Cost** = Direct Material + Direct Labour + Direct/Chargeable Expenses

Since no other direct expenses are given:

$$\text{Prime Cost} = \text{RM Consumed} + \text{Direct Wages}$$

$$\text{Prime Cost} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Prime Cost} = ₹\, 8{,}00{,}000}$$

### Step 3: Add Factory Overheads to get Works / Factory Cost

**Factory Overheads** = All indirect manufacturing costs = Factory Rent + Factory Insurance + Depreciation on Plant + Indirect Materials + Indirect Labour

$$\text{Factory OH} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 70{,}000$$

$$\text{Factory OH} = ₹\, 3{,}50{,}000$$

**Works Cost** = Prime Cost + Factory Overheads + Opening WIP − Closing WIP

$$\text{Works Cost} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 + 30{,}000 - 20{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Works Cost} = ₹\, 11{,}60{,}000}$$

### Step 4: Add Office & Administrative Overheads to get Cost of Production

$$\text{Cost of Production} = \text{Works Cost} + \text{Office \& Admin Overheads}$$

$$\text{Cost of Production} = 11{,}60{,}000 + 90{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Cost of Production} = ₹\, 12{,}50{,}000}$$

### Step 5: Adjust Opening and Closing Finished Goods to get Cost of Goods Sold

$$\text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)} = \text{Cost of Production} + \text{Opening FG} - \text{Closing FG}$$

$$\text{COGS} = 12{,}50{,}000 + 80{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{COGS} = ₹\, 12{,}10{,}000}$$

### Step 6: Add Selling & Distribution Overheads to get Cost of Sales

$$\text{Cost of Sales} = \text{COGS} + \text{Selling \& Distribution Expenses}$$

$$\text{Cost of Sales} = 12{,}10{,}000 + 1{,}10{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Cost of Sales} = ₹\, 13{,}20{,}000}$$

### Step 7: Add Profit (or subtract Loss) to get Sales

Assume the firm earned a profit of ₹ 1,80,000 on sales:

$$\text{Sales} = \text{Cost of Sales} + \text{Profit}$$

$$\text{Sales} = 13{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 = ₹\, 15{,}00{,}000$$

### Step 8: Summary Cost Sheet Table

| **Element** | **Amount (₹)** | **Cumulative (₹)** |
|---|---|---|
| Raw Materials Consumed | 5,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| + Direct Wages | 3,00,000 | 8,00,000 (Prime Cost) |
| + Factory Overheads | 3,50,000 | 11,50,000 |
| + Opening WIP | 30,000 | 11,80,000 |
| − Closing WIP | 20,000 | **11,60,000 (Works Cost)** |
| + Office & Admin OH | 90,000 | **12,50,000 (Cost of Production)** |
| + Opening FG | 80,000 | 13,30,000 |
| − Closing FG | 1,20,000 | **12,10,000 (COGS)** |
| + Selling & Dist. OH | 1,10,000 | **13,20,000 (Cost of Sales)** |
| + Profit | 1,80,000 | **15,00,000 (Sales)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this numerical matters:** KTU often tests classification by asking, *"Classify the following expenses into Direct Material, Direct Labour, Factory Overhead, Office Overhead, or Selling Overhead."* The Cost Sheet derivation is the practical application of that classification.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Each Expense Is Classified

| **Expense Item** | **Classification** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| Steel rods used in chair | Direct Material | Identifiable, traceable to product |
| Wages paid to welder | Direct Labour | Works directly on the product |
| Rent of factory building | Factory Overhead | Indirect; incurred at factory level |
| Salary of factory supervisor | Factory Overhead | Indirect factory labour |
| Salary of office accountant | Office & Admin OH | Incurred at office, not factory |
| Freight on inward raw material | Direct Material / Factory OH | If recurring, treated as part of material cost |
| Advertising expenses | Selling & Distribution OH | Incurred to generate sales |
| Delivery van expenses | Selling & Distribution OH | Distribution-related |
| Depreciation on office building | Office & Admin OH | Asset used in office |
| Depreciation on plant | Factory Overhead | Asset used in factory |
| Salary of showroom sales staff | Selling OH | Incurred at selling point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for Classification of Cost:**
> 1. If the cost can be **traced directly to a single product unit** → **Direct Cost**.
> 2. If the cost is **incurred for the factory as a whole** but cannot be traced → **Factory Overhead (Indirect Manufacturing Cost)**.
> 3. If the cost is **incurred after production** (selling, delivery, marketing) → **Selling & Distribution Overhead**.
> 4. If the cost is **incurred at the office/administrative level** → **Office & Administration Overhead**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Accounting (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ACCOUNTING] --> B[Based on User]
    A --> C[Based on Purpose]
    A --> D[Based on Entity Type]
    A --> E[Based on Regulatory Framework]

    B --> B1[External User Branches]
    B --> B2[Internal User Branches]

    B1 --> F1[Financial Accounting]
    B1 --> F2[Tax Accounting]
    B1 --> F3[Governmental Accounting]
    B1 --> F4[Forensic Accounting]
    B1 --> F5[Auditing]

    B2 --> F6[Cost Accounting]
    B2 --> F7[Management Accounting]
    B2 --> F8[Human Resource Accounting]
    B2 --> F9[Inflation Accounting]
    B2 --> F10[Social Responsibility Accounting]

    C --> C1[Recording and Reporting]
    C --> C2[Costing and Pricing]
    C --> C3[Decision Support and Planning]
    C --> C4[Statutory Compliance]

    D --> D1[Corporate Business]
    D --> D2[Government and Public Sector]
    D --> D3[Non Profit Organizations]
    D --> D4[Personal and Household]

    E --> E1[Companies Act 2013 Governed]
    E --> E2[Ind AS / IFRS Governed]
    E --> E3[Income Tax and GST Act Governed]
    E --> E4[CAG and GFR Governed]
    E --> E5[Self Regulated or No Mandate]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — How to Identify the Correct Branch

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Start: Identify Information Need] --> Q2{Who is the User?}
    Q2 -->|External| Q3{Is it for Statutory Filing?}
    Q2 -->|Internal| Q4{Is it for Future Decisions?}

    Q3 -->|Yes, Tax| R1[Tax Accounting]
    Q3 -->|Yes, Statutory Report| R2[Financial Accounting]
    Q3 -->|Yes, Government| R3[Governmental Accounting]
    Q3 -->|Yes, Fraud Check| R4[Forensic Accounting]

    Q4 -->|Yes, Forecast or Budget| R5[Management Accounting]
    Q4 -->|No, Past Costs Only| R6[Cost Accounting]
    Q4 -->|Yes, People Valuation| R7[Human Resource Accounting]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Relationship Between the Big Three

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Business Data and Transactions] --> B[Financial Accounting]
    A --> C[Cost Accounting]
    A --> D[Management Accounting]

    B --> E1[External Reports: BS, PL, CF]
    C --> E2[Cost Sheet, COGS, Unit Cost]
    D --> E3[Budgets, Variance, CVP, NPV]

    E1 --> F1[Investors, Banks, Regulators]
    E2 --> F2[Production and Project Managers]
    E3 --> F3[Top Management and Strategy Team]

    F1 --> G1[Statutory Compliance]
    F2 --> G2[Cost Control and Pricing]
    F3 --> G3[Strategic Decision Making]

    G1 --> H[Shareholder Value]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H

    H[End: Informed Business Decisions and Compliance]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Where Each Branch Fits in an Engineering Organization

```mermaid
graph TD
    ENG[Engineering Organization] --> DEPT1[Design and R and D]
    ENG --> DEPT2[Production and Manufacturing]
    ENG --> DEPT3[Sales and Marketing]
    ENG --> DEPT4[Finance and Accounts]
    ENG --> DEPT5[Compliance and Legal]
    ENG --> DEPT6[Top Management]

    DEPT1 --> MA1[Management Accounting: Project NPV]
    DEPT2 --> CA1[Cost Accounting: Unit Costing]
    DEPT3 --> MA2[Management Accounting: Sales Budget]
    DEPT4 --> FA1[Financial Accounting: Final Statements]
    DEPT5 --> TA1[Tax Accounting and Auditing]
    DEPT6 --> MA3[Management Accounting: Strategy and CVP]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Distinguish between Financial Accounting and Cost Accounting. (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> | **Basis** | **Financial Accounting** | **Cost Accounting** |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Purpose** | To ascertain profit/loss and financial position for external reporting. | To ascertain the cost of production per unit for internal control. |
> | **Users** | External — shareholders, banks, regulators. | Internal — management, production team. |
> | **Statutory Status** | Mandatory under Companies Act, 2013. | Mandatory only for specified industries under Section 148. |
> | **Time Focus** | Historical (past). | Historical but with cost-control focus. |
> | **Format** | Standardized (Ind AS, IFRS). | Internal cost sheets, CAS guidelines. |
>
> *(1 mark for clear purpose, 1 mark for user distinction, 1 mark for statutory/time focus.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. What is Management Accounting? List any four characteristics. (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> **Definition (1 mark):** Management Accounting is the branch of accounting that provides financial and non-financial information to internal management for planning, controlling, decision-making, and performance evaluation.
>
> **Four Characteristics (½ mark each):**
> 1. **Future-oriented** — uses budgets, forecasts, projections.
> 2. **No statutory requirement** — purely discretionary.
> 3. **Tailor-made reports** — information is custom-built for each decision.
> 4. **Uses both monetary and non-monetary data** — includes physical units, hours, market share, etc.
> 5. *(Optional 5th)* Concerned with **decision-making, not just recording**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> > [!WARNING]
> > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ CAREFULLY:**
> > - **DO NOT** write vague statements like *"Management accounting is for management."* Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for lack of specific technical terms.
> > - **DO NOT** confuse *Cost Accounting* with *Management Accounting* in Part B questions. Cost = past cost data; Management = future decisions.
> > - **ALWAYS** use comparison tables in 14-mark questions. They score higher than narrative paragraphs.
> > - **NEVER** skip the *statutory basis* (e.g., Companies Act, Ind AS, CAS) — KTU specifically tests awareness of the legal framework.
> > - In numerical questions, **show all steps** including Prime Cost, Works Cost, COGS, and Cost of Sales. Skipping the WIP adjustment = 2 marks lost.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> ### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)
>
> **Q. (a) Explain the various types / branches of accounting with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **1. Financial Accounting (2 marks)**
> - Records, summarizes, and reports financial transactions to external users.
> - Governed by **Ind AS / Companies Act, 2013**.
> - **Example:** A listed IT company like Infosys prepares its annual Balance Sheet and P&L Statement as per Schedule III of the Companies Act.
> - Reports: Balance Sheet, Profit & Loss Statement, Cash Flow Statement, Statement of Changes in Equity.
>
> **2. Cost Accounting (1.5 marks)**
> - Ascertains the cost of production per unit.
> - **Example:** A Kerala engineering college (CCTV manufacturer) computes the cost of producing one CCTV camera = Raw Material + Assembly Labour + Factory Overhead per unit.
> - Tools: Cost Sheet, Marginal Costing, Standard Costing, Budgetary Control.
> - **CAS 1 to CAS 24** issued by ICAI (Cost) govern this.
>
> **3. Management Accounting (1.5 marks)**
> - Future-oriented, supports internal decision-making.
> - **Example:** A project manager in L&T uses **NPV analysis** to decide whether to invest ₹50 crore in a new flyover project.
> - Tools: CVP Analysis, Budgeting, Variance Analysis, Responsibility Accounting, Ratio Analysis.
>
> **4. Tax Accounting (1 mark)**
> - Computes tax liability as per Income Tax Act and GST Act.
> - **Example:** A startup paying ₹15 lakh salaries must deduct TDS @ applicable rates and file quarterly TDS returns.
>
> **5. Governmental Accounting (1 mark)**
> - Used by government departments and PSUs.
> - **Example:** A PWD engineer in Kerala prepares a "Measurement Book" (MB) and "Work Abstract" for a road project, audited by the **AG (Accountant General), Kerala**.
>
> ---
>
> **Q. (b) Distinguish between Financial Accounting, Cost Accounting, and Management Accounting. (7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | **Basis** | **Financial Accounting** | **Cost Accounting** | **Management Accounting** |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Primary User** | External (shareholders, creditors, regulators) | Internal (production, project managers) | Internal (top management, strategists) |
> | **Statutory Requirement** | Mandatory under Companies Act 2013 | Mandatory only for certain industries (Sec 148) | Not mandatory — voluntary |
> | **Time Orientation** | Past (historical) | Past (cost incurred) | Future (forecasts, budgets) |
> | **Format of Reports** | Standardized (Ind AS, IFRS) | Internal cost sheets (CAS guidelines) | Customized, flexible |
> | **Measurement Unit** | Monetary only | Monetary + Physical (units, hours) | Monetary + Non-monetary (market share, satisfaction) |
> | **Profit Focus** | Yes — Net Profit is key output | No — Cost per unit is key output | No — Decision support is key output |
> | **Audit Requirement** | Statutory audit mandatory | Cost audit where applicable | Internal audit only |
> | **Examples** | TCS Annual Report | Maruti's per-car cost | Reliance's Jio capex budget |
> | **Frequency** | Annual / Quarterly | Continuous / Monthly | As per management need |
> | **Data Source** | Journals, Ledgers | Cost records, BOQ, BOM | FA + CA + External data |
> | **Tool Examples** | Trial Balance, Balance Sheet | Cost Sheet, Variance Analysis | CVP, NPV, IRR, Ratio Analysis |
> | **Decision Role** | Reporting role | Cost control role | Decision-making role |
> | **Regulated By** | MCA, SEBI, ICAI | ICAI (Cost), Cost Records Rules | None specific |
>
> *(1 mark each for the first 5 rows of distinction, 2 marks for the overall clarity and engineering example.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> ### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
>
> **Q. (a) Classify the various branches of accounting based on the user group and statutory framework. Give one engineering example for each. (7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Classification Based on User Group (3.5 marks):**
>
> **A. External-User Accounting:**
> 1. **Financial Accounting** — Reports to shareholders, banks, government. *Example: An engineer working at Tata Motors reads the company's annual report to assess its financial health before joining.*
> 2. **Tax Accounting** — Reports to tax authorities. *Example: A consulting engineer filing quarterly GST returns on professional fees earned.*
> 3. **Governmental Accounting** — Reports to CAG and public. *Example: A KSEB engineer preparing utilization certificates for a smart-grid project funded by the central government.*
> 4. **Forensic Accounting** — Investigates fraud. *Example: An internal auditor at BHEL investigating a procurement scam.*
> 5. **Auditing** — Independent verification. *Example: A Big-4 firm auditing the books of a Kerala-based construction company.*
>
> **B. Internal-User Accounting:**
> 1. **Cost Accounting** — Internal cost analysis. *Example: A production engineer at HMT computing the per-unit cost of a wristwatch.*
> 2. **Management Accounting** — Future decisions. *Example: A startup CTO using NPV to decide between AWS and Azure for cloud infrastructure.*
> 3. **Human Resource Accounting** — Valuation of employees. *Example: A firm valuing its R&D engineers as intangible assets during a merger.*
> 4. **Social Responsibility Accounting** — CSR reporting. *Example: Infosys reporting ₹300 crore spent on CSR under Section 135.*
> 5. **Inflation Accounting** — Price-level adjustment. *Example: Adjusting the 1990s cost of land in 2024-equivalent terms during a long-term project audit.*
>
> **Classification Based on Statutory Framework (3.5 marks):**
>
> | **Statutory Framework** | **Applicable Branch** | **Enforcing Body** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Companies Act 2013 + Ind AS | Financial Accounting | MCA + ICAI |
> | Section 148 of Companies Act | Cost Accounting (Cost Records & Audit) | MCA + ICAI (Cost) |
> | Income Tax Act 1961 + GST Act 2017 | Tax Accounting | CBDT + CBIC |
> | CAG guidelines + GFR 2017 | Governmental Accounting | CAG of India |
> | Section 135 of Companies Act | Social Responsibility Accounting | MCA |
> | None (Self-regulated) | Management Accounting, HRA, Inflation Accounting | Internal Management |
>
> ---
>
> **Q. (b) From the following data of an engineering firm, prepare a Cost Sheet for the year ending 31-Mar-2025 and ascertain the Profit. (7 Marks)**
>
> **Data Given:**
>
> | **Item** | **₹** |
> |---|---|
> | Opening Raw Material | 1,00,000 |
> | Closing Raw Material | 80,000 |
> | Purchases of Raw Material | 4,80,000 |
> | Direct Wages | 2,50,000 |
> | Factory Rent | 60,000 |
> | Factory Insurance | 20,000 |
> | Depreciation on Plant | 70,000 |
> | Indirect Labour | 50,000 |
> | Indirect Materials | 30,000 |
> | Office Salaries | 60,000 |
> | Selling Expenses | 80,000 |
> | Distribution Expenses | 40,000 |
> | Opening Finished Goods | 50,000 |
> | Closing Finished Goods | 70,000 |
> | Opening WIP | 25,000 |
> | Closing WIP | 15,000 |
> | Sales | 9,00,000 |
>
> **Step 1: Raw Material Consumed (1 mark)**
>
> $$\text{RM Consumed} = \text{Opening RM} + \text{Purchases} - \text{Closing RM}$$
>
> $$\text{RM Consumed} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 - 80{,}000 = ₹\, 5{,}00{,}000$$
>
> **Step 2: Prime Cost (0.5 mark)**
>
> $$\text{Prime Cost} = \text{RM Consumed} + \text{Direct Wages}$$
>
> $$\text{Prime Cost} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹\, 7{,}50{,}000$$
>
> **Step 3: Factory Overheads (0.5 mark)**
>
> $$\text{Factory OH} = 60{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 70{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 = ₹\, 2{,}30{,}000$$
>
> **Step 4: Works Cost (1 mark)**
>
> $$\text{Works Cost} = 7{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}30{,}000 + 25{,}000 - 15{,}000 = ₹\, 9{,}90{,}000$$
>
> **Step 5: Cost of Production (0.5 mark)**
>
> $$\text{Cost of Production} = 9{,}90{,}000 + 60{,}000 = ₹\, 10{,}50{,}000$$
>
> **Step 6: Cost of Goods Sold (1 mark)**
>
> $$\text{COGS} = 10{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 - 70{,}000 = ₹\, 10{,}30{,}000$$
>
> **Step 7: Cost of Sales (0.5 mark)**
>
> $$\text{Cost of Sales} = 10{,}30{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 40{,}000 = ₹\, 11{,}50{,}000$$
>
> **Step 8: Profit / Loss (1 mark)**
>
> $$\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} - \text{Cost of Sales}$$
>
> $$\text{Profit} = 9{,}00{,}000 - 11{,}50{,}000 = \text{Loss of } ₹\, 2{,}50{,}000$$
>
> **Final Cost Sheet Table (1 mark):**
>
> | **Particulars** | **Amount (₹)** |
> |---|---|
> | Opening Raw Material | 1,00,000 |
> | + Purchases | 4,80,000 |
> | − Closing Raw Material | 80,000 |
> | **Raw Material Consumed** | **5,00,000** |
> | + Direct Wages | 2,50,000 |
> | **Prime Cost** | **7,50,000** |
> | + Factory Overheads (Rent + Insurance + Dep + Ind. Labour + Ind. Mat.) | 2,30,000 |
> | + Opening WIP | 25,000 |
> | − Closing WIP | 15,000 |
> | **Works / Factory Cost** | **9,90,000** |
> | + Office & Admin OH | 60,000 |
> | **Cost of Production** | **10,50,000** |
> | + Opening Finished Goods | 50,000 |
> | − Closing Finished Goods | 70,000 |
> | **Cost of Goods Sold** | **10,30,000** |
> | + Selling Expenses | 80,000 |
> | + Distribution Expenses | 40,000 |
> | **Cost of Sales** | **11,50,000** |
> | Sales | 9,00,000 |
> | **Net Loss** | **(2,50,000)** |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Classification of Accounting**

### The Big Three (Must-Know)
- **Financial Accounting** → External, past, mandatory, profit-focused, statutory.
- **Cost Accounting** → Internal, past, cost-focused, mandatory for some industries.
- **Management Accounting** → Internal, future, decision-focused, voluntary.

### The Supporting Five
- **Tax Accounting** → Income Tax + GST compliance.
- **Governmental Accounting** → CAG, GFR, fund accounting.
- **Forensic Accounting** → Fraud detection & investigation.
- **Social Responsibility Accounting** → CSR under Section 135.
- **Human Resource Accounting** → Valuation of people (experimental).
- **Inflation Accounting** → CPP & CCA methods for price-level changes.
- **Auditing** → Independent verification (statutory, internal, tax, cost).

### Must-Memorize Formulae & Concepts
- **Prime Cost** = Direct Material + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses.
- **Works Cost** = Prime Cost + Factory Overheads ± WIP.
- **Cost of Production** = Works Cost + Office & Admin OH.
- **COGS** = Cost of Production + Opening FG − Closing FG.
- **Cost of Sales** = COGS + Selling & Distribution OH.
- **Profit** = Sales − Cost of Sales.

### Golden Rules
- **Traceable to single unit → Direct Cost.**
- **Incurred at factory, not traceable → Factory Overhead.**
- **Incurred at office → Office & Admin Overhead.**
- **Incurred post-production → Selling & Distribution Overhead.**

### KTU Exam Tips
- **Always use a comparison table** in 14-mark questions.
- **Always cite the legal/statutory basis** (Companies Act 2013, Section 148, Section 135, Ind AS, CAS, etc.).
- **In numericals, show all 6 stages**: Prime Cost → Works Cost → Cost of Production → COGS → Cost of Sales → Profit.
- **Watch out for WIP and FG adjustments** — these are the most common places where marks are lost.
- **Remember the 4-decision rule**: User, Time, Mandatory, Purpose.
- **Engineering examples score extra marks** — relate every concept to a real industry (L&T, TCS, BHEL, ISRO, PWD, KSEB, etc.).

### Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- ❌ Confusing Cost Accounting with Management Accounting.
- ❌ Treating depreciation on office building as a factory overhead.
- ❌ Forgetting to adjust WIP while computing Works Cost.
- ❌ Writing "Management Accounting is for managers" without technical depth.
- ❌ Ignoring the statutory basis in a 7-mark distinction question.
- ❌ Mixing up Historical Cost (FA) with Current Cost (Inflation Accounting).

---

> *End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 1, Classification of Accounting*
