## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in the Valuation Hall (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting the "To" prefix on the credit account** in journal entries (–1 mark per occurrence).
> 2. **Wrongly identifying** Outstanding Rent as a Real Account — it is a *Representative Personal Account* (–1 mark).
> 3. **Recording Drawings as an expense** (Nominal Account). Drawings reduce capital, but are *not* an expense of the business; they are debited to a Capital-related account.
> 4. **Omitting the narration** (e.g., "Being cash introduced as capital") in journal entries. Examiners expect a one-line narration; missing it costs ½ mark per entry.
> 5. **Putting an opening balance of a liability on the debit side** of the Trial Balance. The *normal* balance of a liability is credit, so even the opening balance appears on the credit side.
> 6. **Showing the Trial Balance total mismatch** silently — if a Trial Balance does not tally, write a **Suspense A/c** of the balancing figure to maintain the equality, and never leave a blank cell.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Double Entry System** is a complete, scientific system where every transaction is recorded in **at least two accounts** with **equal** debit and credit.
- **Luca Pacioli (1494)** is credited as the Father of Accounting for codifying the system.
- **Three Golden Rules of Accounting:** Personal (Dr. receiver / Cr. giver), Real (Dr. what comes in / Cr. what goes out), Nominal (Dr. expenses & losses / Cr. incomes & gains).
- **Accounting Equation:** $A = L + C$; expanded form $A = L + C + R - E$.
- **Accounting Cycle:** Source Document → Journal → Ledger → Trial Balance → Final Accounts.
- **Trial Balance** is *not* an absolute proof of correctness; it only checks the **arithmetical equality** of debits and credits.
- **Drawings** reduce capital but are *not* a business expense.
- **Real Accounts** deal with assets only; **Nominal Accounts** deal with income, expenses, gains, and losses.
- **Personal Accounts** are of three sub-types: Natural, Artificial/Legal, and Representative.
- **Every transaction preserves the accounting equation** — this is the fundamental invariant of the system.
- Always attach a **narration** to journal entries and ensure **$\sum Dr = \sum Cr$** in the Trial Balance.

topics.title: Preparation of Journal, Ledger and Trial Balance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Journal, Ledger and Trial Balance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the Accounting Cycle

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum for *Finance for Engineering*, understanding the foundational accounting cycle is non-negotiable. The preparation of the **Journal**, **Ledger**, and **Trial Balance** forms the bedrock of the entire double-entry bookkeeping system used universally in engineering project finance, corporate accounting, and cost management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - OECET725):** This topic carries high weightage in **Module 1** and is repeatedly tested in both **Continuous Assessment (CA)** and **End Semester Examination (ESE)**. Mastery of Journal-Ledger-Trial Balance flow guarantees scoring in Part A (3-mark) questions.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board Standard Terminology)

### Journal
A **Journal** is the book of **original or primary entry**. It is a chronological (day-by-day) record of all business transactions in a systematic manner, showing the two-fold aspect (debit and credit) of every transaction according to the rules of **Double Entry System**. The process of recording transactions in the journal is called **Journalising**.

### Ledger
A **Ledger** is the book of **final or secondary entry** containing a classified summary of all transactions in the form of separate accounts. The act of transferring entries from the Journal to the Ledger is called **Posting**.

### Trial Balance
A **Trial Balance** is a statement prepared on a particular date to verify the arithmetical accuracy of the books of accounts. It is a list of all **Ledger balances** on a particular date, showing total **Debit balances** equal to total **Credit balances**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a library system:**
> - **Journal** = The *Daily Logbook* at the library entrance. Every visitor (transaction) is recorded the moment they arrive, with timestamp, name, and purpose — but books aren't organized yet.
> - **Ledger** = The *Subject-wise Filing Cabinets*. The librarian (accountant) transfers each logbook entry into specific subject folders (Cash folder, Sales folder, Salary folder) so all related data sits together.
> - **Trial Balance** = The *Monthly Audit Report*. The head librarian (auditor) opens all folders, sums up money/value in each, and checks if inflows match outflows (Debits = Credits).

For an engineering student, think of a **construction project**:
- The **Journal** records every petty cash expense, material purchase, and labor payment as it happens.
- The **Ledger** consolidates all "Cement purchases" into one Cement A/c, all "Steel purchases" into one Steel A/c.
- The **Trial Balance** at month-end confirms: Total Resources Used = Total Payments Made (the books balance!).

## 1.4 The Three Golden Rules of Accounting (Foundational Standard)

These are the **standard accounting axioms** universally applied:

| # | Type of Account | Rule | Memory Aid |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Personal Account** (Persons, Firms, Companies, Banks) | Debit the Receiver, Credit the Giver | "Give → Credit" |
| 2 | **Real Account** (Assets: Cash, Building, Machinery, Land) | Debit what comes in, Credit what goes out | "In → Debit" |
| 3 | **Nominal Account** (Expenses, Incomes, Losses, Gains) | Debit all expenses and losses, Credit all incomes and gains | "Expense → Debit" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In KTU valuation, if you misclassify an account (e.g., treating "Salary" as a Personal account), **full marks are forfeited** even if the numerical answer is correct. Always identify the account type FIRST.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Mechanics of the Accounting Cycle

The accounting cycle for this topic follows a strict sequential pipeline:

### Step 1: **Transaction Identification**
- Analyze the source document (invoice, receipt, voucher).
- Identify the **two accounts** affected.
- Classify each account as **Personal, Real, or Nominal**.

### Step 2: **Journalising (Primary Recording)**
The standard journal entry format (for KTU board exams) is:

```
Date        | Particulars                           | L.F. | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹)
------------|---------------------------------------|------|------------|------------
[Date]      | [Debit Account Name] A/c ........ Dr   |      | [Amount]   |
            |   To [Credit Account Name] A/c        |      |            | [Amount]
            |   [Narration: Brief explanation]      |      |            |
            | --------------------------------------|------|------------|------------
```

**Key Formatting Rules (KTU Strict):**
- The **Debit account** is written first, suffixed with `A/c ... Dr`.
- The **Credit account** is written on the next line, prefixed with `To`, and indented.
- The abbreviation `Dr` stands for **Debit**.
- The **Narration** must begin with `To` and explain the transaction briefly (e.g., "Being cash received from Ram").

### Step 3: **Posting to Ledger (Secondary Recording)**
Each ledger account has a **T-shape (T-account)** structure:

```
        [Account Name] A/c
   -------------------------------------------------
   |   Debit Side           |   Credit Side         |
   -------------------------------------------------
   | Date | Particulars| Amt| Date | Particulars| Amt|
   -------------------------------------------------
   |      | To [Acc] A/c|    |      | By [Acc] A/c|    |
   -------------------------------------------------
```

- **To** is used on the **Debit side** (indicating credit account from journal).
- **By** is used on the **Credit side** (indicating debit account from journal).
- Posting is done **date-wise** into the respective ledger account.

### Step 4: **Balancing the Ledger**
- Difference between the two sides of a ledger account is called the **Balance**.
- If Debit > Credit → **Debit Balance** (Asset/Expense account).
- If Credit > Debit → **Credit Balance** (Liability/Income/Capital account).
- The smaller side is made equal to the larger side by writing `To balance c/d` (Debit side) or `By balance c/d` (Credit side).

### Step 5: **Preparation of Trial Balance**
Trial Balance is prepared by listing:
- All **Debit balances** in one column.
- All **Credit balances** in another column.
- The total of both columns **must be equal**.

## 2.2 Methods of Preparing Trial Balance

| Method | Description | KTU Preference |
|---|---|---|
| **Balance Method** | Takes the closing balances of all ledger accounts | **Most commonly tested in KTU** |
| **Total Method** | Takes the total of both debit and credit sides of each ledger | Rarely asked |
| **Total and Balance Method** | Combination of both | Occasionally in Part B |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Accounting Equation | **Assets = Liabilities + Capital** | Always in ₹ (Rupees) |
| Journal Entry Balancing | Total Debits = Total Credits | ₹ (must be equal) |
| Ledger Balance (Debit) | $\text{Debit Total} - \text{Credit Total}$ | ₹ (positive value) |
| Ledger Balance (Credit) | $\text{Credit Total} - \text{Debit Total}$ | ₹ (positive value) |
| Trial Balance Total | $\sum \text{Debit Balances} = \sum \text{Credit Balances}$ | ₹ (equality check) |
| Suspense A/c (if imbalance) | $\text{Debit Total} - \text{Credit Total} = \text{Suspense}$ | ₹ (plug figure) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In engineering project finance, these same journal-ledger principles are used in **ERP systems (SAP, Oracle)** for tracking project costs, material inventories, and contractor payments. The trial balance concept is embedded in every modern accounting software's monthly close process.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Construction Project Accounting:** Each subcontractor payment, material receipt, and equipment hire is journaled and posted to project-specific ledger accounts.
- **Manufacturing Cost Tracking:** Raw material purchases → Materials Ledger; Labor wages → Wages Ledger; Overheads → Factory Overheads Ledger.
- **Engineering Consultancy:** Client billings, professional fees, and reimbursements flow through this exact journal-ledger-trial balance pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Double-Entry Mathematical Foundation

The entire Journal-Ledger-Trial Balance system is governed by the **Double-Entry Principle**, which can be mathematically expressed:

$$
\text{For every transaction: } \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Debit}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \text{Credit}_j
$$

Where every transaction has at least **one debit** and **one credit** of equal monetary value.

### Derivation of Trial Balance Equality

Starting from the fundamental accounting equation:

$$
A = L + C
$$

Where $A$ = Assets, $L$ = Liabilities, $C$ = Capital (Owner's Equity).

After every transaction, the equation must hold. Let us track the cumulative effect across $k$ transactions:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta A_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta L_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta C_i
$$

In the **Ledger**, all Asset accounts (Real accounts) accumulate debits when increased, and all Liability/Capital/Income accounts accumulate credits when increased. Expenses (Nominal) reduce capital and are debited; Incomes increase capital and are credited.

When all ledger accounts are balanced, the sum of all debit balances equals the sum of all credit balances, proving:

$$
\sum \text{Debit Balances} = \sum \text{Credit Balances}
$$

This is the **mathematical guarantee** of the Trial Balance.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Engineering-Finance Transactions

The following matrix is the **KTU high-yield reference** for solving journal problems:

| Transaction | Account Type | Debit | Credit | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cash sales of goods | Real + Nominal | Cash A/c | Sales A/c | Asset comes in, Income earned |
| Purchase of machinery for cash | Real | Machinery A/c | Cash A/c | Asset comes in, Asset goes out |
| Paid salaries to staff | Personal + Nominal | Salaries A/c | Cash A/c | Expense incurred, Asset goes out |
| Received cash from debtor | Personal + Real | Cash A/c | Debtor's Personal A/c | Asset comes in, Person gives |
| Paid creditor in full | Personal + Real | Creditor's Personal A/c | Cash A/c | Person receives, Asset goes out |
| Purchased goods on credit | Real + Personal | Purchases A/c | Creditor's A/c | Expense (or Stock) increases, Liability created |
| Owner invested capital in cash | Real + Personal | Cash A/c | Capital A/c | Asset comes in, Owner gives |
| Paid rent in advance | Real | Prepaid Rent A/c | Cash A/c | Asset (right to use) created, Cash goes out |
| Depreciation on machinery | Nominal | Depreciation A/c | Machinery A/c | Loss/Expense booked, Asset value reduced |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** From the following transactions of M/s. TechBuild Engineers, prepare the **Journal**, **Ledger Accounts**, and **Trial Balance** for the month of April 2024.

| Date | Transaction | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Apr 1 | Mr. Arun started business with cash | 5,00,000 |
| Apr 3 | Purchased machinery for cash | 2,00,000 |
| Apr 5 | Purchased materials from BuildMart on credit | 1,50,000 |
| Apr 8 | Cash sales of old machinery | 80,000 |
| Apr 10 | Paid salaries to staff | 25,000 |
| Apr 15 | Received cash from BuildMart (part payment) | 1,00,000 |
| Apr 20 | Paid rent for office | 15,000 |
| Apr 25 | Purchased a delivery van on credit from AutoCorp | 3,00,000 |
| Apr 28 | Paid electricity bill | 5,000 |
| Apr 30 | Withdrew cash for personal use | 10,000 |

### Step 1: Journal Entries (in ₹)

**April 1:** Mr. Arun brings capital
- Cash A/c ... Dr → 5,00,000
- To Capital A/c → 5,00,000
- *(Being cash introduced as capital by proprietor)*

**April 3:** Purchased machinery for cash
- Machinery A/c ... Dr → 2,00,000
- To Cash A/c → 2,00,000
- *(Being machinery purchased for cash)*

**April 5:** Credit purchase of materials
- Purchases A/c ... Dr → 1,50,000
- To BuildMart A/c → 1,50,000
- *(Being materials purchased on credit from BuildMart)*

**April 8:** Cash sale of old machinery
- Cash A/c ... Dr → 80,000
- To Machinery A/c → 80,000
- *(Being old machinery sold for cash)*

**April 10:** Salary payment
- Salaries A/c ... Dr → 25,000
- To Cash A/c → 25,000
- *(Being salaries paid to staff)*

**April 15:** Part payment received from creditor
- Cash A/c ... Dr → 1,00,000
- To BuildMart A/c → 1,00,000
- *(Being cash received from BuildMart in part settlement)*

**April 20:** Rent paid
- Rent A/c ... Dr → 15,000
- To Cash A/c → 15,000
- *(Being office rent paid)*

**April 25:** Van purchased on credit
- Delivery Van A/c ... Dr → 3,00,000
- To AutoCorp A/c → 3,00,000
- *(Being delivery van purchased on credit)*

**April 28:** Electricity bill paid
- Electricity Charges A/c ... Dr → 5,000
- To Cash A/c → 5,000
- *(Being electricity bill paid)*

**April 30:** Drawings (Personal withdrawal)
- Drawings A/c ... Dr → 10,000
- To Cash A/c → 10,000
- *(Being cash withdrawn for personal use)*

### Step 2: Ledger Posting (T-Accounts)

**Cash A/c (Real Account)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Dr Side (Debits):} \\
&\text{Apr 1: To Capital A/c} &= 5,00,000 \\
&\text{Apr 8: To Machinery A/c} &= 80,000 \\
&\text{Apr 15: To BuildMart A/c} &= 1,00,000 \\
&\text{Total Dr} &= 6,80,000 \\[4pt]
&\text{Cr Side (Credits):} \\
&\text{Apr 3: By Machinery A/c} &= 2,00,000 \\
&\text{Apr 10: By Salaries A/c} &= 25,000 \\
&\text{Apr 20: By Rent A/c} &= 15,000 \\
&\text{Apr 28: By Electricity A/c} &= 5,000 \\
&\text{Apr 30: By Drawings A/c} &= 10,000 \\
&\text{Apr 30: By Balance c/d} &= 4,25,000 \\
&\text{Total Cr} &= 6,80,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Closing Balance = ₹ 4,25,000 (Debit Balance)**

**Capital A/c**

- Apr 30: By Balance b/d = 5,00,000 (Cr opening equivalent in trial balance)
- Apr 1: By Cash A/c = 5,00,000
- **Balance = ₹ 5,00,000 (Credit)**

**Machinery A/c**

- Apr 3: To Cash A/c = 2,00,000 (Dr)
- Apr 8: By Cash A/c = 80,000 (Cr)
- **Balance = ₹ 1,20,000 (Debit)**

**BuildMart A/c (Creditor - Personal)**

- Apr 5: To Purchases A/c = 1,50,000 (Cr)
- Apr 15: By Cash A/c = 1,00,000 (Dr)
- **Balance = ₹ 50,000 (Credit)**

**AutoCorp A/c (Creditor - Personal)**

- Apr 25: To Delivery Van A/c = 3,00,000 (Cr)
- **Balance = ₹ 3,00,000 (Credit)**

**Purchases A/c (Nominal - Expense)**

- Apr 5: To BuildMart A/c = 1,50,000 (Dr)
- **Balance = ₹ 1,50,000 (Debit)**

**Salaries A/c (Nominal - Expense)**

- Apr 10: To Cash A/c = 25,000 (Dr)
- **Balance = ₹ 25,000 (Debit)**

**Rent A/c (Nominal - Expense)**

- Apr 20: To Cash A/c = 15,000 (Dr)
- **Balance = ₹ 15,000 (Debit)**

**Electricity Charges A/c (Nominal - Expense)**

- Apr 28: To Cash A/c = 5,000 (Dr)
- **Balance = ₹ 5,000 (Debit)**

**Delivery Van A/c (Real - Asset)**

- Apr 25: To AutoCorp A/c = 3,00,000 (Dr)
- **Balance = ₹ 3,00,000 (Debit)**

**Drawings A/c (Personal - Proprietor)**

- Apr 30: To Cash A/c = 10,000 (Dr)
- **Balance = ₹ 10,000 (Debit)**

### Step 3: Trial Balance

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Trial Balance of M/s. TechBuild Engineers} \\
&\textbf{as on 30th April 2024} \\[6pt]
&\begin{array}{|l|r|r|}
\hline
\textbf{Name of Account} & \textbf{Debit (₹)} & \textbf{Credit (₹)} \\
\hline
\text{Cash A/c} & 4,25,000 & - \\
\text{Machinery A/c} & 1,20,000 & - \\
\text{Delivery Van A/c} & 3,00,000 & - \\
\text{Purchases A/c} & 1,50,000 & - \\
\text{Salaries A/c} & 25,000 & - \\
\text{Rent A/c} & 15,000 & - \\
\text{Electricity Charges A/c} & 5,000 & - \\
\text{Drawings A/c} & 10,000 & - \\
\text{Capital A/c} & - & 5,00,000 \\
\text{BuildMart A/c} & - & 50,000 \\
\text{AutoCorp A/c} & - & 3,00,000 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total} & \mathbf{10,50,000} & \mathbf{10,50,000} \\
\hline
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Total Debits (₹ 10,50,000) = Total Credits (₹ 10,50,000) ✓

## 3.4 Common Pitfalls in Numerical Solutions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Missing `Dr` suffix:** Forgetting to write `A/c ... Dr` on debit accounts = **-0.5 to -1 mark per entry**.
> 2. **Indentation Error:** Not indenting the "To" credit account = **-0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Narration Omission:** Writing entries without proper narration = **-1 mark per entry** (strict KTU rule).
> 4. **Wrong Account Type:** Debiting a nominal account (like Sales) instead of crediting = **-1 mark**.
> 5. **Trial Balance Mismatch:** If TB does not tally, **2 marks are typically deducted** even with correct identification of error.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Accounting Cycle Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Documents: Invoice, Receipt, Voucher] --> B[Identify Accounts Affected]
    B --> C[Classify Account Type: Personal, Real, Nominal]
    C --> D[Apply Golden Rules of Accounting]
    D --> E[Record in Journal: Primary Entry with Narration]
    E --> F[Post to Ledger: Transfer to Respective T-Accounts]
    F --> G[Balance Each Ledger Account]
    G --> H[Prepare Trial Balance: List All Balances]
    H --> I{Total Debit equals Total Credit?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Books are Arithmetically Accurate]
    I -->|No| K[Identify Error and Rectify via Suspense A/c]
    K --> E
```

## 4.2 Ledger Account Structure (T-Account Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Debit Side (Left)"
        D1[Date]
        D2[Particulars - To ___ A/c]
        D3[Amount in Rs]
    end
    subgraph "Account Title at Top"
        T[LEDGER ACCOUNT NAME]
    end
    subgraph "Credit Side (Right)"
        C1[Date]
        C2[Particulars - By ___ A/c]
        C3[Amount in Rs]
    end
    T --> D1
    T --> C1
    D2 --> D3
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Golden Rules Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify the Account] --> B{What is the Account Type?}
    B -->|Person, Firm, Company, Bank| C[Personal Account]
    B -->|Asset: Cash, Building, Machinery, Stock| D[Real Account]
    B -->|Expense, Income, Loss, Gain| E[Nominal Account]
    C --> F[Debit the Receiver, Credit the Giver]
    D --> G[Debit what comes in, Credit what goes out]
    E --> H[Debit Expenses/Losses, Credit Incomes/Gains]
    F --> I[Apply to Journal Entry]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Trial Balance Preparation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[All Ledger Accounts] --> B[Close and Balance Each Account]
    B --> C{Debit Total Greater?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Debit Balance: Asset, Expense, Drawings]
    C -->|No| E[Credit Balance: Liability, Capital, Income]
    D --> F[Place in Debit Column of Trial Balance]
    E --> G[Place in Credit Column of Trial Balance]
    F --> H[Sum Both Columns]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Equal Totals?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Trial Balance Agrees - Arithmetically Correct]
    I -->|No| K[Investigate Errors: Posting, Casting, Principle]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define a Journal. What is the difference between a Journal and a Ledger?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Journal** is the book of original entry wherein all business transactions are recorded in chronological order, applying the **double-entry principle** with debit and credit aspects (1 mark).

**Differences (2 marks):**

| Basis | Journal | Ledger |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Book of primary/original entry | Book of secondary/final entry |
| Recording | Chronological (date-wise) | Account-wise (classified) |
| Purpose | Initial recording | Posting and summarization |
| Format | Journal entry format with narration | T-account (Dr and Cr sides) |
| Balancing | Not balanced | Balanced periodically |

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *State the three Golden Rules of Double Entry System of Accounting with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Personal Account:** Debit the Receiver, Credit the Giver. *Example: Paid cash to Ram → Ram's A/c Dr, To Cash A/c.* (1 mark)
2. **Real Account:** Debit what comes in, Credit what goes out. *Example: Purchased machinery for cash → Machinery A/c Dr, To Cash A/c.* (1 mark)
3. **Nominal Account:** Debit all expenses and losses; Credit all incomes and gains. *Example: Paid salaries → Salaries A/c Dr, To Cash A/c.* (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Modified]** Mr. Rahul, an engineering contractor, started his business on 1st January 2024 with the following transactions:

- Jan 1: Commenced business with cash ₹ 6,00,000
- Jan 3: Purchased building for cash ₹ 3,00,000
- Jan 5: Bought materials from SteelCo on credit ₹ 2,00,000
- Jan 8: Purchased a truck for cash ₹ 1,50,000
- Jan 12: Cash sales of materials ₹ 50,000
- Jan 15: Paid wages ₹ 20,000
- Jan 18: Received cash from SteelCo as part settlement ₹ 1,00,000
- Jan 22: Paid rent ₹ 12,000
- Jan 26: Paid electricity bill ₹ 4,000
- Jan 28: Rahul withdrew cash for personal use ₹ 15,000
- Jan 30: Paid salary to office staff ₹ 25,000

**Required:**

**(a)** Prepare the **Journal Entries** for the above transactions. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Prepare the necessary **Ledger Accounts** and the **Trial Balance** as on 31st January 2024. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Modified]** M/s. BuildPro Engineers had the following transactions during February 2024:

- Feb 1: Mr. Suresh started business with cash ₹ 4,00,000 and bank balance ₹ 1,00,000
- Feb 4: Purchased machinery for ₹ 1,50,000 (paid by cheque)
- Feb 7: Bought office furniture for cash ₹ 60,000
- Feb 10: Cash sales of services rendered ₹ 80,000
- Feb 14: Purchased raw materials on credit from MaterialMart ₹ 1,20,000
- Feb 18: Paid wages ₹ 35,000
- Feb 22: Received cash from a client Mr. Kiran ₹ 50,000
- Feb 25: Paid office rent ₹ 18,000
- Feb 27: Returned defective materials worth ₹ 20,000 to MaterialMart
- Feb 28: Paid salary to manager ₹ 30,000

**Required:**

**(a)** Record the above transactions in the **Journal** with proper narrations. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Post the entries to the **Ledger** and prepare the **Trial Balance**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution Outline for Question A (Complete Step-by-Step)

#### Part (a): Journal Entries (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify accounts and apply golden rules** for each of the 11 transactions (each entry carries approx 0.5-0.7 mark based on length).

**Jan 1:** Cash introduced as capital
- Cash A/c ... Dr ₹ 6,00,000
- To Capital A/c ₹ 6,00,000
- *(Being cash introduced as capital by proprietor)*

**Jan 3:** Building purchased
- Building A/c ... Dr ₹ 3,00,000
- To Cash A/c ₹ 3,00,000
- *(Being building purchased for cash)*

**Jan 5:** Credit purchase
- Purchases A/c ... Dr ₹ 2,00,000
- To SteelCo A/c ₹ 2,00,000
- *(Being materials purchased on credit from SteelCo)*

**Jan 8:** Truck purchased
- Truck A/c ... Dr ₹ 1,50,000
- To Cash A/c ₹ 1,50,000
- *(Being truck purchased for cash)*

**Jan 12:** Cash sales
- Cash A/c ... Dr ₹ 50,000
- To Sales A/c ₹ 50,000
- *(Being cash sales of materials)*

**Jan 15:** Wages paid
- Wages A/c ... Dr ₹ 20,000
- To Cash A/c ₹ 20,000
- *(Being wages paid)*

**Jan 18:** Part payment received
- Cash A/c ... Dr ₹ 1,00,000
- To SteelCo A/c ₹ 1,00,000
- *(Being cash received from SteelCo in part settlement)*

**Jan 22:** Rent paid
- Rent A/c ... Dr ₹ 12,000
- To Cash A/c ₹ 12,000
- *(Being office rent paid)*

**Jan 26:** Electricity bill paid
- Electricity Charges A/c ... Dr ₹ 4,000
- To Cash A/c ₹ 4,000
- *(Being electricity bill paid)*

**Jan 28:** Drawings
- Drawings A/c ... Dr ₹ 15,000
- To Cash A/c ₹ 15,000
- *(Being cash withdrawn for personal use)*

**Jan 30:** Salary paid
- Salary A/c ... Dr ₹ 25,000
- To Cash A/c ₹ 25,000
- *(Being salary paid to office staff)*

**Total of Journal = ₹ 17,86,000 on each side (verification of equality)**

#### Part (b): Ledger Accounts and Trial Balance (7 marks)

**Cash A/c** (showing balances posted from Journal above)
- Debit: Jan 1 Capital ₹ 6,00,000 + Jan 12 Sales ₹ 50,000 + Jan 18 SteelCo ₹ 1,00,000 = **Total Dr ₹ 7,50,000**
- Credit: Jan 3 Building ₹ 3,00,000 + Jan 8 Truck ₹ 1,50,000 + Jan 15 Wages ₹ 20,000 + Jan 22 Rent ₹ 12,000 + Jan 26 Electricity ₹ 4,000 + Jan 28 Drawings ₹ 15,000 + Jan 30 Salary ₹ 25,000 + Balance c/d ₹ 2,24,000 = **Total Cr ₹ 7,50,000**
- **Closing Balance: ₹ 2,24,000 (Debit Balance)**

**Other Account Balances (computed similarly):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Building A/c Balance} &= 3,00,000 \text{ (Dr)} \\
\text{Purchases A/c Balance} &= 2,00,000 \text{ (Dr)} \\
\text{Truck A/c Balance} &= 1,50,000 \text{ (Dr)} \\
\text{Sales A/c Balance} &= 50,000 \text{ (Cr)} \\
\text{Wages A/c Balance} &= 20,000 \text{ (Dr)} \\
\text{SteelCo A/c Balance} &= 2,00,000 - 1,00,000 = 1,00,000 \text{ (Cr)} \\
\text{Rent A/c Balance} &= 12,000 \text{ (Dr)} \\
\text{Electricity Charges A/c} &= 4,000 \text{ (Dr)} \\
\text{Drawings A/c Balance} &= 15,000 \text{ (Dr)} \\
\text{Salary A/c Balance} &= 25,000 \text{ (Dr)} \\
\text{Capital A/c Balance} &= 6,00,000 \text{ (Cr)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trial Balance as on 31st January 2024:**

$$
\begin{array}{|l|r|r|}
\hline
\textbf{Name of Account} & \textbf{Debit (₹)} & \textbf{Credit (₹)} \\
\hline
\text{Cash A/c} & 2,24,000 & - \\
\text{Building A/c} & 3,00,000 & - \\
\text{Truck A/c} & 1,50,000 & - \\
\text{Purchases A/c} & 2,00,000 & - \\
\text{Wages A/c} & 20,000 & - \\
\text{Rent A/c} & 12,000 & - \\
\text{Electricity Charges A/c} & 4,000 & - \\
\text{Salary A/c} & 25,000 & - \\
\text{Drawings A/c} & 15,000 & - \\
\text{Capital A/c} & - & 6,00,000 \\
\text{SteelCo A/c} & - & 1,00,000 \\
\text{Sales A/c} & - & 50,000 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total} & \mathbf{9,50,000} & \mathbf{9,50,000} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Trial Balance Tallies: ₹ 9,50,000 = ₹ 9,50,000 ✓**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Frequently Lost Marks):**
> 1. **Treating Drawings as Capital:** Drawings is NOT a Capital account. It is debited to Drawings A/c. Confusing this loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Sales with Cash Sales:** Both "Cash Sales" and "Credit Sales" are credited to Sales A/c, not Cash A/c. The cash or debtor is debited separately.
> 3. **Purchases of materials on credit:** If you credit Cash A/c, you have made a serious error - the credit goes to the supplier (SteelCo/MaterialMart).
> 4. **Narration format:** Always begin with "Being..." for proper KTU standard.
> 5. **TB Imbalance:** If your TB doesn't tally, the examiner will check for **casting errors, posting to wrong side, or omitting a transaction** - these are the three most common causes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- [ ] **Journal = Primary Entry** (chronological); **Ledger = Secondary Entry** (classified).
- [ ] Journal entry format: **Debit account ... Dr** on top, **To Credit account** indented below, then **Narration**.
- [ ] **Three Golden Rules:** Personal (Receiver/Giver), Real (In/Out), Nominal (Expense/Income).
- [ ] **Posting:** Use "To" on Debit side, "By" on Credit side of ledger.
- [ ] **Balancing:** `c/d` = carried down (to next period); `b/d` = brought down (from previous period).
- [ ] **Trial Balance** lists all ledger balances; Debit balances on left, Credit balances on right.
- [ ] **Total Debits MUST EQUAL Total Credits** in a balanced Trial Balance.
- [ ] If TB doesn't tally, the difference is placed in a **Suspense A/c** temporarily.
- [ ] **Capital** has a credit balance; **Drawings** has a debit balance; both relate to the proprietor.
- [ ] **Assets & Expenses** → Debit balances; **Liabilities, Capital & Incomes** → Credit balances.
- [ ] **Drawings** are NOT expenses (despite reducing capital) — they are a separate Personal account of the proprietor.
- [ ] Always write `A/c ... Dr` for the debit account; never use `Dr` for credit accounts.
- [ ] KTU exam tip: Ledger accounts with multiple entries should be properly totaled and balanced; half-mark deductions apply for missing totals.
- [ ] Trial Balance is NOT an account — it is a **statement** prepared to check arithmetical accuracy.
- [ ] Errors of **omission, commission, principle, original entry, compensating** can cause TB imbalance (advanced topic in Module 2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Financial Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Financial Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Financial Statements** are structured, formal reports that quantify the financial performance, position, and cash movements of a business entity, prepared in accordance with recognized accounting frameworks such as **Ind AS (Indian Accounting Standards)**, **IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards)**, or **GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles)**.

In the KTU 2024 OECET725 syllabus, financial statements are treated as the **primary output of the accounting cycle** and the **fundamental communication tool** between a business enterprise and its various stakeholders.

The four core financial statements mandated under the Companies Act, 2013 and studied in this module are:

1. **Statement of Profit and Loss** (Income Statement / P&L Account)
2. **Balance Sheet** (Statement of Financial Position)
3. **Cash Flow Statement**
4. **Statement of Changes in Equity**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for a First-Time Reader)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a business as a person running a lemonade stand for an entire year.**

| Personal Real-World Element | Financial Statement Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Money you earned from selling lemonade | **Revenue (Income Statement)** |
| Money spent on sugar, lemons, cups | **Expenses (Income Statement)** |
| Money left in your pocket at year-end | **Net Profit (Income Statement)** |
| What you OWN (juicer, cash, fridge) | **Assets (Balance Sheet)** |
| What you OWE (loan from Dad) | **Liabilities (Balance Sheet)** |
| What belongs to YOU (your share) | **Owner's Equity (Balance Sheet)** |
| Actual cash you handled during the year | **Cash Flow Statement** |

Just as a person keeps a **diary of money matters**, a company prepares **financial statements** so that everyone — owners, banks, the government, investors — can understand *what happened, what is owned, and what is owed* in a standardized language.

---

## 1.3 The Underlying Principle — The Accounting Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The entire edifice of financial statements rests on one master identity called the **Accounting Equation**:

$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity}$$

This equation is the **DNA of every Balance Sheet** ever produced. Every transaction recorded in the books ultimately preserves this identity — the system is therefore called **double-entry bookkeeping**.

---

## 1.4 Why Financial Statements Matter to Engineers

> [!NOTE]
> Even as engineering students, you will interact with finance when:
> - Pitching a **startup** to investors (need an Income Statement)
> - Applying for an **MSME loan** (need a Balance Sheet)
> - Managing **project budgets** in a company (need Cash Flow tracking)
> - Evaluating **supplier credit terms** (need working capital analysis)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Financial Statements — Structural Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Statement of Profit and Loss (Income Statement)

- **Purpose:** Reports financial **performance over a period of time** (e.g., FY 2023–24).
- **Logic Flow:** Revenue flows down, expenses are subtracted in stages, finally arriving at Net Profit.
- **Why it exists:** A business must answer the question — *"Did we make money this year?"*

### Pillar 2: Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position)

- **Purpose:** Reports financial **position at a SINGLE point in time** (e.g., as on 31-Mar-2024).
- **Logic Flow:** What the company owns (Assets) = What it owes (Liabilities) + What belongs to owners (Equity).
- **Why it exists:** A business must answer the question — *"What is our financial health TODAY?"*

### Pillar 3: Cash Flow Statement

- **Purpose:** Reports actual **cash inflows and outflows** during a period, classified by activity.
- **Logic Flow:** Operating + Investing + Financing Activities = Net Change in Cash.
- **Why it exists:** A business can be profitable (high net profit) yet run out of cash. This statement warns about that danger.

### Pillar 4: Statement of Changes in Equity

- **Purpose:** Reconciles the **opening and closing balances of shareholders' equity**.
- **Logic Flow:** Opening Equity + New Capital Issued + Profits Retained − Dividends Paid = Closing Equity.
- **Why it exists:** Shows owners how their stake evolved over the year.

---

## 2.2 Users of Financial Statements (Stakeholder Mapping)

| Stakeholder Group | Primary Information Need |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Owners / Shareholders** | Profitability and return on investment |
| **Creditors / Banks** | Ability to repay debt (liquidity & solvency) |
| **Management** | Internal decision-making and performance review |
| **Government / Tax Authorities** | Taxable income and statutory compliance |
| **Employees / Trade Unions** | Profit-sharing, job security, wage capacity |
| **Suppliers** | Short-term solvency to decide credit terms |
| **Customers** | Long-term viability of the firm (continuity of supply) |
| **Investors / Analysts** | Valuation, future earnings, and risk profile |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations to Memorize for KTU Board Exams (OECET725):**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $A = L + E$ | **Accounting Equation** — Assets (₹) = Liabilities (₹) + Equity (₹) |
| 2 | $\text{Gross Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{COGS}$ | COGS = Cost of Goods Sold |
| 3 | $\text{Operating Profit} = \text{Gross Profit} - \text{Operating Expenses}$ | EBIT proxy |
| 4 | $\text{EBIT} = \text{Net Profit} + \text{Tax} + \text{Interest}$ | Earnings Before Interest & Tax |
| 5 | $\text{EBITDA} = \text{EBIT} + \text{Depreciation} + \text{Amortization}$ | Earnings Before Interest, Tax, D&A |
| 6 | $\text{Net Profit} = \text{EBIT} - \text{Interest} - \text{Tax}$ | Bottom line |
| 7 | $\Delta \text{Cash} = \text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF}$ | Cash Flow identity |
| 8 | $\text{Working Capital} = \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}$ | Short-term liquidity |
| 9 | $\text{Retained Earnings}_{t} = \text{Retained Earnings}_{t-1} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Dividends}$ | Equity build-up |
| 10 | $\text{Ending Equity} = \text{Beginning Equity} + \text{Issued Capital} + \text{Net Income} - \text{Dividends}$ | Equity reconciliation |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Project Selection (Capital Budgeting):** Engineers evaluating machinery must project future cash flows — directly tied to Cash Flow Statement logic.
- **Cost Control:** Income Statement classification (Variable vs. Fixed) helps in **break-even analysis**.
- **Vendor Evaluation:** A supplier's Balance Sheet reveals its **creditworthiness** before signing long-term contracts.
- **Startup Valuation:** Income Statement trends determine whether the firm can attract venture capital funding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Net Profit from the Income Statement Structure

Let us derive **Net Profit** step-by-step, since this is a recurring KTU 14-mark derivation.

**Step 1 — Start with total revenue earned from core operations.**

$$
R = \text{Total Sales Revenue}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the direct cost of producing the goods/services sold (COGS).**

$$
\text{Gross Profit} = R - \text{COGS}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract all operating overheads (salaries, rent, R&D, marketing, admin).**

$$
\text{Operating Profit (EBIT)} = (R - \text{COGS}) - \text{Operating Expenses}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract interest paid on borrowings.**

$$
\text{EBT (Earnings Before Tax)} = \text{EBIT} - \text{Interest Expense}
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract income tax as per applicable tax slab.**

$$
\text{Net Profit (PAT)} = \text{EBT} - \text{Tax}
$$

**Final consolidated derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= R - \text{COGS} - \text{Operating Expenses} - \text{Interest} - \text{Tax} \\
&= (R - \text{COGS}) - (\text{Operating Expenses} + \text{Interest} + \text{Tax})
\end{aligned}
$$

This can be written in a more compact form often used in KTU answer scripts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= R - (C + O + I + T) \\
\text{where } C &= \text{COGS} \\
O &= \text{Operating Expenses} \\
I &= \text{Interest Expense} \\
T &= \text{Tax}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cash Flow Statement Identity

**Step 1 — Recognize the opening and closing cash balances.**

$$
C_{\text{end}} = C_{\text{opening}} + \Delta C
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the change in cash into three activity types.**

$$
\Delta C = \text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{end}} &= C_{\text{opening}} + \text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF} \\
\text{where CFO} &= \text{Cash from Operating Activities} \\
\text{CFI} &= \text{Cash from Investing Activities} \\
\text{CFF} &= \text{Cash from Financing Activities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification Example (Numerical):** Suppose a firm has Opening Cash = ₹50,000, CFO = ₹30,000, CFI = −₹20,000, CFF = ₹10,000. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{end}} &= 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 + (-20{,}000) + 10{,}000 \\
&= 50{,}000 + 20{,}000 \\
&= ₹70{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Statement of Changes in Equity (Equity Roll-Forward)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{closing}} &= E_{\text{opening}} + \text{Capital Issued} + \text{Net Income (Retained)} - \text{Dividends Paid} \\
E_{\text{closing}} - E_{\text{opening}} &= \text{Capital Issued} + \text{Net Income} - \text{Dividends}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** Opening Equity = ₹5,00,000; New shares issued = ₹1,00,000; Net Income = ₹2,00,000; Dividends = ₹50,000.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{closing}} &= 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 \\
&= 7{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A startup reports the following for FY 2023–24. Prepare the **Income Statement**.

| Item | Amount (₹) |
| :--- | ---: |
| Sales Revenue | 10,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 4,00,000 |
| Salaries & Wages | 1,50,000 |
| Rent Expense | 1,00,000 |
| Interest on Loan | 50,000 |
| Depreciation | 30,000 |
| Income Tax Rate | 30 % |

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Gross Profit} &= 10{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹6{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Step 2: Operating Profit (EBIT)} &= 6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹3{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Step 3: EBT} &= 3{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000 = ₹3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Step 4: Tax} &= 30\% \times 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹90{,}000 \\
\text{Step 5: Net Profit (PAT)} &= 3{,}00{,}000 - 90{,}000 = ₹2{,}10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** Depreciation of ₹30,000 is a **non-cash expense** and is typically included in operating expenses for accounting profit, but excluded from cash flow. Many KTU questions test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Financial Reporting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Business Transactions] --> A1[Recording in Journal]
    A1 --> A2[Posting to Ledger]
    A2 --> A3[Trial Balance]
    A3 --> A4[Adjusting Entries]
    A4 --> A5[Adjusted Trial Balance]
    A5 --> A6[Financial Statements]

    A6 --> B1[Statement of Profit and Loss]
    A6 --> B2[Balance Sheet]
    A6 --> B3[Cash Flow Statement]
    A6 --> B4[Statement of Changes in Equity]

    B1 --> C1[Performance Snapshot over a Period]
    B2 --> C2[Position Snapshot at a Point in Time]
    B3 --> C3[Cash Movement Snapshot over a Period]
    B4 --> C4[Equity Movement Snapshot over a Period]

    B1 --> D1[Shareholders and Tax Authorities]
    B2 --> D2[Creditors Banks and Investors]
    B3 --> D3[Management and Treasury Teams]
    B4 --> D4[Existing and Prospective Shareholders]
```

## 4.2 Income Statement Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Revenue from Operations] --> S1[Less: COGS]
    S1 --> S2[Gross Profit]
    S2 --> S3[Less: Operating Expenses]
    S3 --> S4[Operating Profit EBIT]
    S4 --> S5[Less: Interest]
    S5 --> S6[Earnings Before Tax EBT]
    S6 --> S7[Less: Income Tax]
    S7 --> S8[Net Profit PAT]
    S8 --> S9[Retained Earnings and Dividend Pool]
```

## 4.3 Cash Flow Statement Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP[Cash from Operating Activities] --> SUM[Net Change in Cash]
    IN[Cash from Investing Activities] --> SUM
    FN[Cash from Financing Activities] --> SUM
    SUM --> OC[Opening Cash Balance]
    OC --> CC[Closing Cash Balance]
```

## 4.4 Balance Sheet Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT_SIDE
    A1[Current Assets] --> A2[Total Assets]
    A3[Non Current Assets] --> A2
    end
    subgraph RIGHT_SIDE
    B1[Current Liabilities] --> B3[Total Liabilities and Equity]
    B2[Non Current Liabilities] --> B3
    B4[Shareholders Equity] --> B3
    end
    A2 --- B3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Define financial statements. List the four main types of financial statements prepared by a business entity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Financial statements are structured formal reports that present the financial performance, position, and cash flows of a business entity over a defined period or at a specific date, prepared as per applicable accounting standards (Ind AS / IFRS / GAAP).

The four main types are:

1. **Statement of Profit and Loss** (Income Statement) — measures performance
2. **Balance Sheet** (Statement of Financial Position) — measures position
3. **Cash Flow Statement** — measures cash movements
4. **Statement of Changes in Equity** — explains changes in owners' stake

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing + brief purpose.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**State and explain the Accounting Equation. Why is it considered the foundation of double-entry bookkeeping?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Accounting Equation states:
>
> $$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Owners' Equity}$$
>
> It represents the fundamental identity that every asset a firm owns is financed either by creditors (liabilities) or by owners (equity). It is the foundation of double-entry bookkeeping because every transaction has a dual effect — a debit on one account and an equal credit on another — thereby keeping the equation perpetually balanced.

*(Award 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for double-entry linkage.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** A 14-mark question has TWO sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B (full 14 marks).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a) Explain in detail the components of a Balance Sheet with suitable examples.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b) Prepare a sample Income Statement for a manufacturing company and explain each line item.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — Balance Sheet Components

The Balance Sheet is a snapshot of the financial position **as on a particular date**. It has two sides that always balance:

**Left Side — ASSETS** (Resources owned by the firm)

- **Current Assets:** Cash, Bank, Debtors, Stock (Inventory), Prepaid Expenses — convertible to cash within **12 months**.
- **Non-Current Assets:** Land, Building, Plant & Machinery, Furniture, Goodwill, Patents — long-term use, not meant for resale.

**Right Side — LIABILITIES + EQUITY** (Sources of funds)

- **Current Liabilities:** Creditors, Outstanding Expenses, Short-term Loans, Bank Overdraft — payable within **12 months**.
- **Non-Current Liabilities:** Debentures, Long-term Loans from Banks, Bonds.
- **Shareholders' Equity:** Share Capital (Equity & Preference), Reserves & Surplus (Retained Earnings).

**Example Format:**

| Liabilities (₹) | Amount | Assets (₹) | Amount |
| :--- | ---: | :--- | ---: |
| Share Capital | 5,00,000 | Land & Building | 6,00,000 |
| Long-Term Loan | 3,00,000 | Machinery | 4,00,000 |
| Creditors | 1,00,000 | Stock | 1,50,000 |
| Outstanding Salary | 50,000 | Debtors | 1,00,000 |
|  |  | Cash | 50,000 |
| **Total** | **9,50,000** | **Total** | **9,50,000** |

*(Award 2 marks for asset classification, 2 marks for liability classification, 2 marks for example, 1 mark for equation.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Sample Income Statement

**ABC Manufacturing Ltd. — Statement of Profit and Loss for the year ended 31-Mar-2024**

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
| :--- | ---: |
| Sales Revenue | 20,00,000 |
| **Less:** Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | 8,00,000 |
| **Gross Profit** | **12,00,000** |
| **Less:** Operating Expenses |  |
| Salaries & Wages | 3,00,000 |
| Rent | 1,00,000 |
| Depreciation | 1,00,000 |
| **Total Operating Expenses** | **5,00,000** |
| **Operating Profit (EBIT)** | **7,00,000** |
| **Less:** Interest on Loan | 1,00,000 |
| **Earnings Before Tax (EBT)** | **6,00,000** |
| **Less:** Income Tax (30 %) | 1,80,000 |
| **Net Profit After Tax (PAT)** | **4,20,000** |

**Explanations:**

- **Sales Revenue:** Total sales of manufactured goods.
- **COGS:** Direct material + direct labour + direct manufacturing overheads.
- **Gross Profit:** Earnings from core operations before indirect costs.
- **Operating Expenses:** Day-to-day running costs.
- **Depreciation:** Reduction in value of fixed assets over time (non-cash).
- **EBIT:** Earnings before interest and tax — measure of operating efficiency.
- **Tax:** Statutory income tax as per Income Tax Act, 1961.

*(Award 1 mark each for 7 line items = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a) Who are the various users of financial statements? Explain their information needs.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b) Describe the Cash Flow Statement and explain its three categories of activities with examples.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — Users of Financial Statements

| User | Information Need |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Shareholders / Owners** | Profitability, EPS, dividends, future growth |
| **Creditors / Banks** | Liquidity, solvency, ability to repay interest & principal |
| **Management** | Decision-making, performance review, planning |
| **Government / Tax Authorities** | Taxable income, GST compliance, statutory filings |
| **Employees / Unions** | Profitability for bonus, job security, wage capacity |
| **Suppliers** | Short-term creditworthiness, payment history |
| **Customers** | Long-term continuity of business operations |
| **Investors / Analysts** | Valuation, risk, return comparison |
| **Regulatory Bodies (SEBI, RBI)** | Compliance, systemic risk monitoring |

*(Award 1 mark per major user with need = 7 marks; max 7 users.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — Cash Flow Statement

The Cash Flow Statement shows actual **cash receipts and cash payments** during a period, classified into:

**1. Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO)** — Day-to-day business operations.

- *Inflows:* Cash received from customers, interest received.
- *Outflows:* Cash paid to suppliers, salaries, taxes, operating expenses.
- *Example:* A firm collects ₹5,00,000 from customers and pays ₹2,00,000 to suppliers → CFO = ₹3,00,000 (gross).

**2. Cash Flow from Investing Activities (CFI)** — Purchase/sale of long-term assets.

- *Inflows:* Sale of machinery, sale of investments, interest on investments.
- *Outflows:* Purchase of land, building, equipment, long-term investments.
- *Example:* Buying a new machine for ₹2,00,000 → CFI = −₹2,00,000.

**3. Cash Flow from Financing Activities (CFF)** — Transactions with owners and lenders.

- *Inflows:* Issue of shares, raising of long-term loans.
- *Outflows:* Repayment of loans, payment of dividends, buyback of shares.
- *Example:* Paying dividend of ₹50,000 → CFF = −₹50,000.

**Net Change in Cash:**

$$
\Delta C = \text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF}
$$

**Closing Cash:**

$$
C_{\text{closing}} = C_{\text{opening}} + \Delta C
$$

*(Award 2 marks each for the three activities + examples, 1 mark for the final identity = 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Cash Profit" with "Net Profit":** Net Profit includes non-cash items like Depreciation. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks for not clarifying this distinction.
> 2. **Ignoring the Period vs. Point-in-Time distinction:** Income Statement = period; Balance Sheet = single date. Mixing these in a 14-mark answer is a fatal error.
> 3. **Forgetting the Accounting Equation** at the end of a Balance Sheet problem — every KTU marking scheme awards 1 mark for the equation tally.
> 4. **Writing "Shareholders" loosely:** Use precise terms — *Equity Share Capital, Preference Share Capital, Reserves & Surplus*.
> 5. **Not classifying the three Cash Flow activities correctly** — many students wrongly classify loan repayment under Operating instead of Financing.
> 6. **Skipping the tax computation step** in Income Statement problems — 1 full mark is reserved for the tax slab application.
> 7. **Submitting the Balance Sheet with unbalanced totals** — examiner awards 0 if the equation does not balance, even if the rest is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Financial statements are formal, structured financial reports of a business.
- **Four Statements:** (1) P&L, (2) Balance Sheet, (3) Cash Flow, (4) Changes in Equity.
- **Master Equation:** $\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity}$ — must ALWAYS balance.
- **Income Statement Equation:** $\text{Net Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{All Expenses (incl. Tax)}$.
- **Cash Flow Identity:** $\Delta C = \text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF}$.
- **Period vs. Point-in-Time:** P&L and Cash Flow cover a **period**; Balance Sheet is at a **point in time**.
- **EBIT** = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (a measure of operating efficiency).
- **EBITDA** = EBIT + Depreciation + Amortization.
- **Net Profit (PAT)** is the **bottom line** — also called "Earnings Available to Equity Shareholders".
- **Depreciation** is a **non-cash** expense — included in P&L, excluded in Cash Flow (added back).
- **Working Capital** = Current Assets − Current Liabilities (liquidity measure).
- **Internal Users:** Management, Employees. **External Users:** Investors, Creditors, Government, Suppliers, Customers.
- **Standards Followed:** Companies prepare per **Ind AS / Schedule III of Companies Act, 2013**.
- **Golden Rule:** Every transaction is recorded using **double-entry** (debit = credit).
- **KTU Tip:** Always show the **final tally** of the Accounting Equation at the end of Balance Sheet answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Profit & Loss Account and Balance Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Profit & Loss Account and Balance Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Final Accounts?

**Final Accounts** are the concluding set of financial statements prepared by a business entity at the end of an accounting period (usually a financial year — April 1 to March 31 in India under the Companies Act, 2013) to determine the operational performance and the financial position of the business.

Final Accounts comprise **two primary statements**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Profit & Loss Account (P&L A/c)** — measures *performance* over a period.
> **2. Balance Sheet (B/S)** — measures *position* at a single point in time.

---

### 1.2 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Profit & Loss Account** is a nominal account that summarizes all indirect revenues (other than gross profit from trading) and all indirect expenses to arrive at the **Net Profit** or **Net Loss** for the accounting period.

$$\text{Net Profit} = \text{Gross Profit} + \text{Indirect Incomes} - \text{Indirect Expenses}$$

**Balance Sheet** is a real account (statement of financial position) that presents the balances of all **Assets, Liabilities, and Capital** as on the last day of the accounting period.

$$\text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Liabilities} + \text{Capital (Equity)}$$

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Report Card"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of your business as a small restaurant.**
>
> • At the end of the year, you ask: *"How much did I earn after all costs?"* — This is the **Profit & Loss Account** (a movie of the year).
>
> • On the last day of the year, you also ask: *"What do I own and what do I owe right now?"* — This is the **Balance Sheet** (a photograph of that one day).
>
> • The **Trading Account** is like asking: *"From selling food alone, did I make a margin?"* — this gives the **Gross Profit** (food revenue minus the raw materials — rice, oil, vegetables — and direct kitchen staff).

So the order of preparation is:
$$\text{Trial Balance} \rightarrow \text{Trading A/c} \rightarrow \text{P\&L A/c} \rightarrow \text{Balance Sheet}$$

---

### 1.4 The Three-Stage Final Accounts Chain

| Stage | Statement | Purpose | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Trading Account | Computes **Gross Profit** or **Gross Loss** | Revenue − Direct Cost |
| 2 | Profit & Loss Account | Computes **Net Profit** or **Net Loss** | GP + Other Income − Indirect Exp. |
| 3 | Balance Sheet | Reports **Financial Position** | Assets = Liabilities + Capital |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Convention:** Gross Profit appears on the **CREDIT** side of Trading A/c and on the **CREDIT** side of P&L A/c (as an indirect income). Gross Loss appears on the **DEBIT** side of both. Net Profit appears on the **CREDIT** side of P&L A/c and on the **LIABILITIES** side of Balance Sheet (as an addition to capital).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Trading Account — Operational Logic

The Trading Account captures **direct** items only. "Direct" means the cost is *traceable to the goods/services sold*.

**Debit Side of Trading A/c (Expenses/Deductions):**
- Opening Stock (Raw Materials + WIP + Finished Goods)
- Purchases (Net of Purchase Returns)
- Direct Expenses — Wages, Carriage Inward, Freight, Customs Duty, Manufacturing Expenses, Factory Rent, Coal/Gas/Water/Power, Royalty

**Credit Side of Trading A/c (Incomes/Additions):**
- Sales (Net of Sales Returns)
- Closing Stock (if given outside Trial Balance)
- Gross Profit (balancing figure)

**Core Formula (Debit Format):**
$$\text{Gross Profit} = \text{Sales} + \text{Closing Stock} - \text{Opening Stock} - \text{Purchases} - \text{Direct Expenses}$$

---

### 2.2 The Profit & Loss Account — Operational Logic

The P&L Account handles **indirect** items. "Indirect" means costs not tied to a specific product, but to running the business as a whole.

**Credit Side (Incomes added to Gross Profit):**
- Gross Profit (from Trading A/c)
- Discount Received
- Commission Received
- Interest Received (on investments, deposits)
- Rent Received
- Bad Debts Recovered
- Dividend Received
- Profit on Sale of Assets/Investments
- Apprenticeship Premium

**Debit Side (Expenses deducted from Gross Profit):**
- Office Salaries, Salaries to Partners
- Rent, Rates & Taxes (Office)
- Printing & Stationery
- Postage & Telegrams
- Insurance Premium
- Depreciation
- Audit Fees, Legal Expenses
- Discount Allowed
- Bad Debts + Provision for Doubtful Debts
- Interest on Loan, Bank Charges
- Loss on Sale of Assets
- General Expenses
- Advertisement
- Selling & Distribution Expenses

**Net Result Formula:**
$$\text{Net Profit} = \text{GP} + \text{Other Incomes} - \text{Office \& Admin Exp.} - \text{Selling Exp.} - \text{Financial Exp.} - \text{Losses}$$

---

### 2.3 The Balance Sheet — Operational Logic

It is **NOT** an account. It is a **statement** prepared on a particular date. The two sides must **always tally**.

**Liabilities Side (Sources of Funds):**
- Capital (Proprietor's / Partners' / Shareholders')
- Reserves & Surplus (including Net Profit added)
- Secured Loans (on Fixed Assets)
- Unsecured Loans
- Current Liabilities (Creditors, Bills Payable, Outstanding Expenses, Bank Overdraft, Short-term Loans)
- Provisions (for tax, dividend, etc.)
- Long-term Liabilities (Debentures)

**Assets Side (Applications of Funds):**
- Fixed Assets (Land, Building, Plant, Machinery, Furniture, Patents, Goodwill — less Depreciation)
- Current Assets (Closing Stock, Debtors, Bills Receivable, Cash in Hand, Cash at Bank, Prepaid Expenses, Accrued Income, Marketable Securities)
- Fictitious / Intangible Assets (Preliminary Expenses, Deferred Revenue Expenditure — written off)
- Investments (Long-term)

---

### 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Standard Usage | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $GP = S + CS - OS - P - DE$ | Trading A/c — credit side | Currency (₹) |
| 2 | $NP = GP + II - IE$ | P&L A/c — credit side | Currency (₹) |
| 3 | $A = L + C$ | Balance Sheet | Always balanced |
| 4 | $CS = OS + P - COGS$ | Stock reconciliation | Currency (₹) |
| 5 | $Dep = \dfrac{Cost - Scrap}{Useful Life}$ | Straight Line Method | Currency/year |
| 6 | $Dep_n = NBV \times Rate$ | Written Down Value Method | Currency/year |
| 7 | $Bad Debts Provision_{new} = (Debtors - Specific Bad) \times Rate$ | Adjustment entry | Currency (₹) |
| 8 | $Outstanding Exp._{add} = Expense + O/s$ | P&L debit side increase | Currency (₹) |
| 9 | $Prepaid Exp._{deduct} = Expense - Prepaid$ | P&L debit side decrease | Currency (₹) |
| 10 | $Accrued Income_{add} = Income + Accrued$ | P&L credit side increase | Currency (₹) |
| 11 | $Capital_{closing} = Capital_{opening} + NP - Drawings + Int.Cap. - Int.Draw. $ | For Proprietorship B/S | Currency (₹) |
| 12 | $Purchases_{net} = Purchases - Purchase Returns$ | Trading A/c debit | Currency (₹) |
| 13 | $Sales_{net} = Sales - Sales Returns$ | Trading A/c credit | Currency (₹) |

**Boundary / Convention Rules used by KTU Examiners:**

- **Depreciation** → Debit P&L A/c, Credit the respective Asset in B/S.
- **Outstanding Expenses** → Add to that expense on P&L debit side AND show under "Current Liabilities" in B/S.
- **Prepaid Expenses** → Deduct from that expense on P&L debit side AND show under "Current Assets" in B/S.
- **Accrued Income** → Add to that income on P&L credit side AND show under "Current Assets" in B/S.
- **Income Received in Advance** → Deduct from that income on P&L credit side AND show under "Current Liabilities" in B/S.
- **Closing Stock** → Credit Trading A/c AND show under "Current Assets" in B/S.
- **Manager's Commission** → If fixed, debit P&L; if % of profit, **first calculate profit *before* commission**, then commission = (Profit × Rate) and add to debit, reducing final NP.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility for Engineers

> [!NOTE]
> Engineering managers rarely prepare P&Ls themselves, but they MUST interpret them for:
> • **Project cost control** — comparing budgeted vs. actual indirect costs.
> • **Make-or-buy decisions** — gross profit margin analysis.
> • **Depreciation planning** — to forecast asset replacement cash flows.
> • **Vendor creditworthiness** — reading balance sheets of suppliers/contractors.
> • **Start-up valuation** — projecting 3-year P&L for investor pitch decks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

### 3.1 Comprehensive KTU-Style Solved Problem (14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Trial Balance of Mr. Rohan (Proprietor) as on 31-03-2024:**

| Debit Balances (₹) | Credit Balances (₹) |
|---|---|
| Opening Stock 30,000 | Capital 2,00,000 |
| Purchases 1,80,000 | Sales 4,00,000 |
| Sales Returns 10,000 | Discount Received 5,000 |
| Carriage Inward 6,000 | Commission Received 8,000 |
| Wages 24,000 | Bank Overdraft 40,000 |
| Rent & Taxes 18,000 | Creditors 60,000 |
| Salaries 36,000 | Bills Payable 25,000 |
| Bad Debts 4,000 | Provision for Doubtful Debts (old) 3,000 |
| Advertisement 12,000 | Outstanding Interest on Investment 2,000 |
| Insurance 6,000 | — |
| Depreciation on Machinery 10,000 | — |
| Drawings 20,000 | — |
| Machinery 1,50,000 | — |
| Furniture 40,000 | — |
| Debtors 80,000 | — |
| Cash in Hand 5,000 | — |
| Investments 50,000 | — |
| **Total ₹ 6,81,000** | **Total ₹ 7,43,000** |

**Additional Information (Adjustments):**

1. Closing Stock valued at **₹ 40,000**.
2. Create a Provision for Doubtful Debts at **5% on Debtors**.
3. Rent & Taxes include **₹ 3,000 prepaid**.
4. Salaries include **₹ 4,000 outstanding**.
5. Depreciation on Furniture **₹ 4,000** (additional).
6. Interest on Investment **₹ 2,000** accrued (already given as outstanding, so it is to be added).
7. Commission Received includes **₹ 2,000 received in advance**.

---

### 3.2 STEP 1 — Preparation of Trading Account

**Logic:** Trading A/c = Direct items only. Wages and Carriage Inward are *direct*. Salaries, Rent, Insurance, Depreciation on Furniture are *indirect* (do NOT enter Trading A/c).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Profit} &= \text{Net Sales} + \text{CS} - \text{OS} - \text{Net Purchases} - \text{Direct Exp.} \\
&= (4{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}000) + 40{,}000 - 30{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 - (6{,}000 + 24{,}000) \\
&= 3{,}90{,}000 + 40{,}000 - 30{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 - 30{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 1{,}90{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Trading Account (in ₹)**

| Debit | ₹ | Credit | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| To Opening Stock | 30,000 | By Sales | 4,00,000 |
| To Purchases | 1,80,000 | Less: Sales Returns | (10,000) → 3,90,000 |
| To Carriage Inward | 6,000 | By Closing Stock | 40,000 |
| To Wages | 24,000 | **By Gross Profit c/d** | **1,90,000** |
| **To Gross Profit c/d** | **1,90,000** | | |
| **Total** | **4,30,000** | **Total** | **4,30,000** |

---

### 3.3 STEP 2 — Preparation of Profit & Loss Account

**Adjustments Applied First:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Rent \& Taxes (net)} &= 18{,}000 - 3{,}000 \text{ (prepaid)} = \text{₹ } 15{,}000 \\
\text{Salaries (net)} &= 36{,}000 + 4{,}000 \text{ (outstanding)} = \text{₹ } 40{,}000 \\
\text{Total Depreciation} &= 10{,}000 \text{ (Machinery, given in TB)} + 4{,}000 \text{ (Furniture, adjustment)} = \text{₹ } 14{,}000 \\
\text{Old Provision} &= \text{₹ } 3{,}000 \text{ (reverse it on credit side)} \\
\text{New Provision} &= 5\% \text{ of } 80{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}000 \text{ (add to Bad Debts on debit)} \\
\text{Commission Received (net)} &= 8{,}000 - 2{,}000 \text{ (advance)} = \text{₹ } 6{,}000 \\
\text{Interest on Investment} &= \text{already accrued ₹ } 2{,}000 \rightarrow \text{fully taken as ₹ } 2{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Net Profit Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
NP &= GP + \text{Other Incomes (net)} - \text{Indirect Expenses (net)} \\
&= 1{,}90{,}000 + (5{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 3{,}000 \text{ old prov. reversed}) \\
&\quad - (15{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 4{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 14{,}000 + 4{,}000 \text{ new prov.}) \\
&= 1{,}90{,}000 + 16{,}000 - 95{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 1{,}11{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Profit & Loss Account (in ₹)**

| Debit | ₹ | Credit | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| To Rent & Taxes | 15,000 | By Gross Profit b/d | 1,90,000 |
| To Salaries | 40,000 | By Discount Received | 5,000 |
| To Bad Debts (old 4,000 + new prov 4,000) | 8,000 | By Commission Received | 6,000 |
| To Advertisement | 12,000 | By Interest on Investment | 2,000 |
| To Insurance | 6,000 | By Old Provision for DD (reversed) | 3,000 |
| To Depreciation (10,000 + 4,000) | 14,000 | | |
| **To Net Profit** | **1,11,000** | | |
| **Total** | **2,06,000** | **Total** | **2,06,000** |

---

### 3.4 STEP 3 — Preparation of Balance Sheet (as on 31-03-2024)

**Capital Reconciliation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Closing Capital} &= \text{Opening Capital} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Drawings} \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}11{,}000 - 20{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 2{,}91{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Balance Sheet (in ₹)**

| Liabilities | ₹ | Assets | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capital 2,00,000 | | Machinery 1,50,000 | |
| Add: Net Profit 1,11,000 | | Less: Dep. (10,000) → 1,40,000 | 1,40,000 |
| Less: Drawings (20,000) | 2,91,000 | Furniture 40,000 | |
| Bank Overdraft | 40,000 | Less: Dep. (4,000) → 36,000 | 36,000 |
| Creditors | 60,000 | Investments | 50,000 |
| Bills Payable | 25,000 | Closing Stock | 40,000 |
| Outstanding Salaries | 4,000 | Debtors 80,000 | |
| Advance Commission | 2,000 | Less: New Prov. (4,000) → 76,000 | 76,000 |
| | | Prepaid Rent | 3,000 |
| | | Accrued Interest | 2,000 |
| | | Cash in Hand | 5,000 |
| **Total** | **4,22,000** | **Total** | **4,22,000** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tally Check:** Both sides = **₹ 4,22,000** ✓ — The Balance Sheet balances, confirming the preparation is correct.

---

### 3.5 Verification Trail (Cross-Check Sum)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total of Asset side} &= 1{,}40{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 76{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 5{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}52{,}000 \text{ (non-cash assets + investments + stock)} \\
\text{Total of Liabilities side} &= 2{,}91{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 4{,}000 + 2{,}000 \\
&= 4{,}22{,}000 \\
\text{Match?} &= \text{Yes} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Final Accounts Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TB["Trial Balance"] --> ADJ["Apply Adjustments"]
    ADJ --> TRAD["Trading Account"]
    ADJ --> PL["Profit and Loss Account"]
    ADJ --> BS["Balance Sheet"]

    TRAD -->|"Gross Profit"| PL
    PL -->|"Net Profit"| BS
    TRAD -->|"Closing Stock"| BS

    TRAD --> TRAD_D["Debit: OS, Purchases, Direct Exp"]
    TRAD --> TRAD_C["Credit: Sales, Closing Stock, GP"]

    PL --> PL_D["Debit: Office Exp, Selling Exp, Financial Exp, Depreciation"]
    PL --> PL_C["Credit: GP, Discount Rcvd, Commission, Interest, Bad Debts Recovered"]

    BS --> BS_L["Liabilities: Capital, Loans, Creditors, Bills Payable, Outstanding, NP"]
    BS --> BS_A["Assets: Fixed Assets, Investments, Current Assets, Closing Stock, Debtors net"]
```

### 4.2 The Adjustment Routing Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AJ["Adjustment Item"] --> ROUTE{"Affects Where?"}

    ROUTE -->|"Closing Stock"| A1["Credit Trading A/c + Asset in B/S"]
    ROUTE -->|"Outstanding Expense"| A2["Add to P&L Debit + Liability in B/S"]
    ROUTE -->|"Prepaid Expense"| A3["Deduct from P&L Debit + Asset in B/S"]
    ROUTE -->|"Accrued Income"| A4["Add to P&L Credit + Asset in B/S"]
    ROUTE -->|"Income Received in Advance"| A5["Deduct from P&L Credit + Liability in B/S"]
    ROUTE -->|"Depreciation"| A6["Debit P&L + Reduce Asset in B/S"]
    ROUTE -->|"New Bad Debts Provision"| A7["Add to Bad Debts in P&L + Reduce Debtors in B/S"]
    ROUTE -->|"Old Bad Debts Provision"| A8["Reverse in P&L Credit (already on B/S)"]
```

### 4.3 The Profit Equation Derivation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Net Sales"] --> B["minus COGS"]
    COGS["Cost of Goods Sold = Opening Stock + Net Purchases + Direct Expenses - Closing Stock"]
    B --> GP["Gross Profit"]
    GP --> C["plus Other Incomes"]
    C --> D["minus Operating Expenses"]
    D --> E["minus Financial Expenses"]
    E --> F["minus Depreciation and Losses"]
    F --> NP["Net Profit / Net Loss"]
    NP --> G["Added to Capital in B/S"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

> **Distinguish between Trading Account and Profit & Loss Account. State any three items that appear exclusively in the Profit & Loss Account.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Trading Account | Profit & Loss Account |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Computes **Gross Profit** | Computes **Net Profit** |
| Items recorded | **Direct** items only | **Indirect** items only |
| Stage | First stage of Final A/c | Second stage of Final A/c |
| Closing Stock | Appears on **Credit** side | Does not appear |

**Three items exclusive to P&L A/c:**
1. Office Salaries
2. Rent, Rates & Taxes (office portion)
3. Depreciation on Fixed Assets
*(Any three — 1 mark each)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]**

> **What is a Balance Sheet? Why is it not considered an "Account"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Balance Sheet** is a statement of financial position of a business on a particular date. It shows assets on one side and liabilities + capital on the other. (1 mark)

It is **not an account** because:
1. It is a **statement**, not a ledger account — no debit/credit sides in the traditional sense. (1 mark)
2. It does **not record transactions**; it only lists balances extracted from the Trial Balance and adjustments. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]*

> **(a)** From the following Trial Balance of Sri. Anand, prepare the **Trading Account** and **Profit & Loss Account** for the year ended 31-03-2024 and find the **Net Profit**. **(7 marks)**

| Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|---|---|
| Opening Stock 25,000 | Sales 3,50,000 |
| Purchases 2,00,000 | Capital 1,80,000 |
| Wages 18,000 | Creditors 45,000 |
| Carriage Inward 4,000 | Discount Received 3,000 |
| Salaries 20,000 | Bank Overdraft 15,000 |
| Rent 12,000 | Outstanding Rent 2,000 |
| Bad Debts 3,000 | Provision for Doubtful Debts (old) 2,000 |
| Insurance 5,000 | — |
| Depreciation 6,000 | — |
| **Total 2,93,000** | **Total 5,95,000** |

**Additional Information:**
- Closing Stock = ₹ 35,000
- New Provision for Doubtful Debts = 4% on Debtors of ₹ 50,000
- Insurance prepaid = ₹ 1,000
- Depreciation on Machinery (additional) = ₹ 4,000

> **(b)** Prepare the **Balance Sheet** from the above and the following additional balances: Debtors ₹ 50,000; Cash in Hand ₹ 8,000; Machinery ₹ 1,20,000; Furniture ₹ 30,000; Land & Building ₹ 80,000; Drawings ₹ 15,000. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A:**

**(a) Trading Account (₹)**

| Debit | ₹ | Credit | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| To Opening Stock | 25,000 | By Sales | 3,50,000 |
| To Purchases | 2,00,000 | By Closing Stock | 35,000 |
| To Wages | 18,000 | **By Gross Profit c/d** | **1,38,000** |
| To Carriage Inward | 4,000 | | |
| **To Gross Profit c/d** | **1,38,000** | | |
| **Total** | **3,85,000** | **Total** | **3,85,000** |

**Working:**
$$\begin{aligned}
GP &= 3{,}50{,}000 + 35{,}000 - 25{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}000 - 4{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}85{,}000 - 2{,}47{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}38{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Profit & Loss Account (₹)**

| Debit | ₹ | Credit | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| To Salaries | 20,000 | By Gross Profit b/d | 1,38,000 |
| To Rent (12,000 + 2,000 O/s) | 14,000 | By Discount Received | 3,000 |
| To Bad Debts (3,000 + new prov 2,000) | 5,000 | By Old Prov. for DD reversed | 2,000 |
| To Insurance (5,000 − 1,000 prepaid) | 4,000 | | |
| To Depreciation (6,000 + 4,000) | 10,000 | | |
| **To Net Profit** | **90,000** | | |
| **Total** | **1,43,000** | **Total** | **1,43,000** |

**Working:**
$$\begin{aligned}
NP &= 1{,}38{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 2{,}000 - (20{,}000 + 14{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 4{,}000 + 10{,}000) \\
&= 1{,}43{,}000 - 53{,}000 = \text{₹ } 90{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Balance Sheet (as on 31-03-2024) (₹)**

| Liabilities | ₹ | Assets | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capital 1,80,000 | | Land & Building | 80,000 |
| Add: NP 90,000 | | Machinery 1,20,000 | |
| Less: Drawings (15,000) | 2,55,000 | Less: Dep. (10,000) → 1,10,000 | 1,10,000 |
| Bank Overdraft | 15,000 | Furniture | 30,000 |
| Creditors | 45,000 | Closing Stock | 35,000 |
| Outstanding Rent | 2,000 | Debtors 50,000 | |
| | | Less: New Prov. (2,000) → 48,000 | 48,000 |
| | | Prepaid Insurance | 1,000 |
| | | Cash in Hand | 8,000 |
| **Total** | **3,17,000** | **Total** | **3,17,000** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** Explain the **concept of Gross Profit and Net Profit** with a suitable example. Why is Gross Profit shown on the credit side of the Trading Account? **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** List **any seven adjustments** commonly given in KTU final accounts problems and state their **dual treatment** in Trading A/c, P&L A/c, and Balance Sheet. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Q.B:**

**(a)** Gross Profit is the excess of Net Sales over the cost of goods sold. Net Profit is the surplus remaining after deducting all operating, administrative, selling, and financial expenses from Gross Profit plus other incomes. (3 marks)

**Example:**
- Net Sales = ₹ 5,00,000
- COGS = ₹ 3,00,000
- GP = ₹ 2,00,000
- Indirect Expenses = ₹ 1,20,000
- Other Incomes = ₹ 10,000
- NP = 2,00,000 + 10,000 − 1,20,000 = **₹ 90,000** (4 marks)

Gross Profit is shown on the **credit side** of Trading A/c because it represents a *gain* (income) generated from the core trading activity and is therefore credited to the nominal account per the rules of double-entry bookkeeping. (extra credit)

**(b) Seven Adjustments with Dual Treatment:**

| # | Adjustment | Trading A/c | P&L A/c | Balance Sheet |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Closing Stock | Credit | — | Current Asset |
| 2 | Outstanding Expenses | — | Add to Expense (Debit) | Current Liability |
| 3 | Prepaid Expenses | — | Deduct from Expense (Debit) | Current Asset |
| 4 | Accrued Income | — | Add to Income (Credit) | Current Asset |
| 5 | Income Received in Advance | — | Deduct from Income (Credit) | Current Liability |
| 6 | Depreciation | — | Debit (Expense) | Reduce Asset |
| 7 | New Provision for Doubtful Debts | — | Add to Bad Debts (Debit) | Reduce Debtors (Asset) |
| 8 (bonus) | Old Provision Reversal | — | Credit (Income) | Already deducted in B/S |

*(1 mark per correct row × 7 = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 2–3 Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add the old Provision for Doubtful Debts back** on the credit side of P&L A/c (after debiting the new provision) — this causes a **₹ 2,000–₹ 5,000 swing** in Net Profit.
> 2. **Depreciation confusion:** If "Depreciation" is given in the Trial Balance AND in adjustments, **add both**. If only in adjustments, do NOT add a duplicate.
> 3. **Closing Stock:** Must appear in BOTH Trading A/c (credit side) and Balance Sheet (current asset). Showing it only once = −1 mark.
> 4. **Outstanding vs. Prepaid:** "Outstanding = +" (debit side expense), "Prepaid = −" (debit side expense). The reverse is for incomes. Mixing these up = full P&L wrongly computed.
> 5. **Manager's Commission (if asked):** Always calculate profit **BEFORE** commission, then deduct commission. A cyclic dependency trap.
> 6. **Balance Sheet Tally:** KTU examiners verify the tally first. If it doesn't balance by even ₹ 1, expect **strict marking** on the misclassified item.
> 7. **Drawing/Capital confusion:** Drawings reduce Capital; Net Profit increases Capital. Don't add Drawings to the debit side of P&L.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Final Accounts (KTU Module 1)**
>
> ✅ **Order of Preparation:** Trial Balance → Trading A/c → P&L A/c → Balance Sheet
>
> ✅ **Gross Profit Formula:** $GP = \text{Net Sales} + \text{CS} - \text{OS} - \text{Net Purchases} - \text{Direct Expenses}$
>
> ✅ **Net Profit Formula:** $NP = GP + \text{Other Incomes} - \text{Indirect Expenses}$
>
> ✅ **Balance Sheet Equation:** $\text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Liabilities} + \text{Capital}$
>
> ✅ **Stock Rule:** Closing Stock is shown ONLY in Trading A/c (credit) and B/S (asset). It NEVER appears in P&L A/c.
>
> ✅ **Depreciation Rule:** Debit P&L A/c, Credit (reduce) the specific asset in B/S.
>
