> ✅ **Adjustment Double Entry (Mnemonic — "OPP-ACI"):**
> - **O**utstanding → P&L Debit + B/S Liability
> - **P**repaid → P&L Debit − + B/S Asset
> - **A**ccrued Income → P&L Credit + + B/S Asset
> - **A**dvance Income → P&L Credit − + B/S Liability
>
> ✅ **Provision for Doubtful Debts:**
> - Old provision → Credit P&L A/c (income).
> - New provision → Debit P&L A/c + Deduct from Debtors in B/S.
>
> ✅ **Capital Closing Formula:** $C_{close} = C_{open} + NP - Drawings + \text{Int. on Cap.} - \text{Int. on Drawings}$
>
> ✅ **Manager's Commission (KTU Trap):** Calculate NP **after** deducting commission = (Pre-commission NP) − (Pre-commission NP × Rate). Solve for it algebraically.
>
> ✅ **Tally is the Final Test:** Always verify Assets = Liabilities + Capital before submission.
>
> ✅ **Common Format Error:** Trading A/c items NEVER mix with P&L A/c items. Direct ≠ Indirect.
>
> ✅ **KTU's Most-Asked Sub-Topics:** (i) Provision for Doubtful Debts double entry, (ii) Manager's Commission cyclic, (iii) Depreciation on multiple assets, (iv) Outstanding + Prepaid combined, (v) Final tally with all adjustments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rate of Return Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rate of Return Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Rate of Return (ROR) Method**, also known as the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** technique, is a discounted cash flow capital budgeting methodology used to evaluate the economic worthiness of an engineering project or investment. Formally, it is defined as the discount rate ($i^*$) at which the **Net Present Worth (NPW)** of all cash flows over the entire project life cycle equals **zero**.

Mathematically, the governing condition is:

$$NPW(i^*) = 0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} CF_t \left(1 + i^*\right)^{-t}$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Net Cash Flow in year $t$
- $n$ = Project life in years
- $i^*$ = Rate of Return (the unknown being solved)

The project is considered **economically viable** only if $i^* \geq \text{MARR}$ (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return), where **MARR** is the lowest acceptable rate of return established by the firm or investor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Rate of Return Method is one of the most frequently tested topics in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Students must be proficient in both trial-and-error computation and the linear interpolation technique.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Interest Analogy"

Imagine you deposit **₹1,00,000** in a bank. The bank offers you a fixed interest rate, and after a certain period, the accumulated amount equals your principal plus the interest earned. Now, suppose instead of depositing money, you are **buying a machine** for ₹1,00,000 that will generate cash inflows over the years.

> [!NOTE]
> **The ROR question is essentially: "At what hypothetical bank interest rate would the future cash inflows from this machine exactly pay back my ₹1,00,000 investment?"**

If that implied "bank rate" is higher than what your bank actually offers, the machine is a good deal. If it is lower, the machine is a bad deal. This hypothetical rate is your **Rate of Return**.

### Standard Engineering Metrics Used

- **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return):** Typically **8%–15%** in Indian engineering project evaluations.
- **Project Life (n):** Usually **5–20 years** for industrial assets.
- **Cash Flows ($CF_t$):** Must be estimated in constant (real) or current (nominal) monetary units — consistency is critical.

### Classification of Investments by Sign Change

| Investment Type | Cash Flow Pattern | Behavior of NPW vs. i | ROR Solutions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple (Conventional)** | One sign change (e.g., - + + + +) | Strictly decreasing, crosses axis once | **Unique** real $i^*$ |
| **Mixed (Non-Conventional)** | Two or more sign changes | May cross axis multiple times | **Multiple** real $i^*$ possible |
| **Simple + Salvage** | - + + + + S | Decreasing, unique root | **Unique** real $i^*$ |

> [!TIP]
> A **simple investment** has a single sign change, meaning the firm invests first and then recovers. A **mixed investment** has multiple sign changes, often requiring the **External Rate of Return (ERR)** method for unique solution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Decision Logic — The Three Golden Rules

The Rate of Return Method's acceptance criteria are structured around comparison with the benchmark MARR:

1. **If $i^* >$ MARR** → ✅ **ACCEPT** the project (the investment earns more than the minimum acceptable).
2. **If $i^* =$ MARR** → ⚖️ **INDIFERENT** (the investor is exactly compensated; either accept or reject is mathematically valid).
3. **If $i^* <$ MARR** → ❌ **REJECT** the project (insufficient earning power).

> [!NOTE]
> For **mutually exclusive** projects (where you must choose one of several alternatives), the ROR method on the *incrementally* added investment must be used. A simple comparison of individual $i^*$ values is **misleading** and a common KTU pitfall.

### Operational Steps to Compute ROR

The ROR cannot be extracted via a closed-form algebraic equation (except in trivial cases). Hence, the **trial-and-error method** with **linear interpolation** is the standard KTU-expected procedure:

1. **Step 1:** Set up the NPW equation: $NPW(i) = 0$.
2. **Step 2:** Guess a low trial rate $i_L$ and compute $NPW_L > 0$.
3. **Step 3:** Guess a high trial rate $i_H$ and compute $NPW_H < 0$.
4. **Step 4:** Verify that $NPW$ is monotonically decreasing in $i$ (valid for simple investments).
5. **Step 5:** Apply linear interpolation formula to estimate $i^*$.
6. **Step 6:** Compare $i^*$ with MARR and render the decision.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Scenario | Governing Equation (set = 0) | Closed-Form? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single future receipt F after n years | $-P + F \cdot (P/F, i^*, n) = 0$ | Yes: $i^* = (F/P)^{1/n} - 1$ |
| 2 | Uniform annual series A for n years | $-P + A \cdot (P/A, i^*, n) = 0$ | No (trial/error) |
| 3 | A with salvage S at end | $-P + A \cdot (P/A, i^*, n) + S \cdot (P/F, i^*, n) = 0$ | No (trial/error) |
| 4 | Future worth form | $0 = -P(F/P, i^*, n) + F + S$ | Trial/error |
| 5 | Linear interpolation formula | $i^* \approx i_L + \dfrac{NPW_L}{NPW_L - NPV_H}(i_H - i_L)$ | Always |
| 6 | Incremental ROR | $i^*_{\Delta} = i_L + \dfrac{NPW_L(\Delta)}{NPW_L(\Delta) - NPV_H(\Delta)}(i_H - i_L)$ | Trial/error |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When using the interpolation formula, students often incorrectly subtract the denominator. The correct denominator is $NPW_L - NPV_H$, **not** $NPW_H - NPW_L$. The numerator is always the *positive* trial NPW.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The ROR method is the most widely used capital budgeting tool in engineering industries such as:

- **Power plants** (thermal, hydro, nuclear) — comparing generation technologies.
- **Manufacturing equipment selection** — choosing between CNC machines, robots, or manual systems.
- **Infrastructure projects** — roads, bridges, water treatment plants.
- **Renewable energy installations** — solar farms, wind turbines.

Industries prefer ROR over NPW because the result is expressed as a **percentage**, which is intuitive for non-finance executives and aligns with standard financial KPIs like **Return on Equity (ROE)** and **Return on Assets (ROA)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Interpolation Formula (Newton's Approximation)

We assume that between two trial rates $i_L$ (low) and $i_H$ (high), the NPW function behaves approximately as a **straight line** passing through $(i_L, NPW_L)$ and $(i_H, NPW_H)$.

Let the slope of this secant line be $m$. By the definition of slope between two points:

$$m = \frac{NPW_H - NPW_L}{i_H - i_L}$$

At the true ROR point, $NPW(i^*) = 0$. Using the point-slope form starting from the lower point $(i_L, NPW_L)$:

$$NPW(i) - NPW_L = m \cdot (i - i_L)$$

Substituting $NPW(i^*) = 0$:

$$0 - NPW_L = \frac{NPW_H - NPW_L}{i_H - i_L} \cdot (i^* - i_L)$$

Rearranging for $i^*$:

$$i^* - i_L = \frac{-NPW_L \cdot (i_H - i_L)}{NPW_H - NPW_L}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(-1)$ to make the denominator positive (since $NPW_L > 0$ and $NPW_H < 0$ for a simple investment):

$$i^* - i_L = \frac{NPW_L \cdot (i_H - i_L)}{NPW_L - NPW_H}$$

Therefore, the **interpolated ROR** is:

$$\boxed{i^* \approx i_L + \frac{NPW_L}{NPW_L - NPW_H}(i_H - i_L)}$$

This is the KTU-board standard formula. The accuracy improves as $i_H - i_L$ is kept small (typically $\leq 2\%$).

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Uniform Series Cash Flow

**Problem:** A machine costs **₹5,00,000** and generates uniform net cash inflows of **₹1,20,000 per year** for **6 years**. Compute the ROR if the salvage value is zero. Use MARR = 12%. Should the project be accepted?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Set up the NPW equation at the unknown rate $i^*$:

$$0 = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \cdot (P/A, i^*, 6)$$

**Step 2:** Isolate the (P/A) factor:

$$(P/A, i^*, 6) = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} = 4.1667$$

**Step 3:** Use **Trial 1** at $i_L = 12\%$ (the MARR, a natural starting point):

$$(P/A, 12\%, 6) = \frac{(1.12)^6 - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^6}$$

Compute the numerator: $(1.12)^6 = 1.97382$, so numerator $= 0.97382$.

$$(P/A, 12\%, 6) = \frac{0.97382}{0.12 \times 1.97382} = \frac{0.97382}{0.23686} = 4.1114$$

Thus, the present worth at 12%:

$$NPW(12\%) = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4.1114 = -5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}93{,}368 = -6{,}632$$

Since $NPW_{12\%} < 0$, the actual ROR must be **less than 12%**.

**Step 4:** Use **Trial 2** at $i_L = 10\%$:

$$(1.10)^6 = 1.77156$$

$$(P/A, 10\%, 6) = \frac{1.77156 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.77156} = \frac{0.77156}{0.177156} = 4.3553$$

$$NPW(10\%) = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4.3553 = -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}22{,}636 = +22{,}636$$

Since $NPW_{10\%} > 0$, the ROR lies between 10% and 12%.

**Step 5:** Apply linear interpolation:

$$i^* = 10\% + \frac{22{,}636}{22{,}636 - (-6{,}632)} \times (12\% - 10\%)$$

$$i^* = 10\% + \frac{22{,}636}{29{,}268} \times 2\%$$

$$i^* = 10\% + 0.7733 \times 2\% = 10\% + 1.5466\% = 11.55\%$$

**Step 6:** Decision — since $i^* = 11.55\% < \text{MARR} = 12\%$, **REJECT the project**.

> [!NOTE]
> The rejection is *marginal* — barely missing the MARR. A small change in cash flow estimates could flip the decision. KTU examiners frequently test such "borderline" cases.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Single Payment with Closed-Form Solution

**Problem:** An investment of **₹2,00,000** yields a single future return of **₹4,50,000** after **5 years**. MARR = 14%. Determine the ROR and decide.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** NPW equation:

$$0 = -2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 \cdot (P/F, i^*, 5)$$

**Step 2:** Isolate the (P/F) factor:

$$(P/F, i^*, 5) = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} = 0.4444$$

**Step 3:** Take the inverse to get (F/P):

$$(F/P, i^*, 5) = \frac{1}{0.4444} = 2.25$$

**Step 4:** Apply the compound interest identity:

$$(1 + i^*)^5 = 2.25$$

$$1 + i^* = (2.25)^{1/5}$$

Computing the fifth root:

$$(2.25)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \cdot \ln(2.25)} = e^{0.2 \cdot 0.81093} = e^{0.16219} = 1.1761$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:

$$i^* = 1.1761 - 1 = 0.1761 = 17.61\%$$

**Step 6:** Since $i^* = 17.61\% > \text{MARR} = 14\%$, **ACCEPT the project**.

> [!TIP]
> This is one of the few cases where ROR can be computed **without trial-and-error**. Always check if the problem allows a closed-form solution — KTU examiners reward efficient methods.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Incremental ROR for Mutually Exclusive Alternatives

**Problem:** Two machines are being considered, both with a 5-year life:
- **Machine A:** First cost = ₹3,00,000; Annual saving = ₹90,000; Salvage = ₹30,000.
- **Machine B:** First cost = ₹4,50,000; Annual saving = ₹1,25,000; Salvage = ₹40,000.

MARR = 10%. Choose the best.

**Step 1:** Compute ROR of each machine individually using trial values.

*For Machine A:* $0 = -3{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000(P/A, i^*, 5) + 30{,}000(P/F, i^*, 5)$

At $i = 10\%$: $(P/A, 10\%, 5) = 3.7908$, $(P/F, 10\%, 5) = 0.6209$

$$NPW_A(10\%) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000(3.7908) + 30{,}000(0.6209)$$
$$= -3{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}41{,}172 + 18{,}627 = +59{,}799$$

At $i = 15\%$: $(P/A, 15\%, 5) = 3.3522$, $(P/F, 15\%, 5) = 0.4972$

$$NPW_A(15\%) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000(3.3522) + 30{,}000(0.4972)$$
$$= -3{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}01{,}698 + 14{,}916 = +16{,}614$$

At $i = 18\%$: $(P/A, 18\%, 5) = 3.1272$, $(P/F, 18\%, 5) = 0.4371$

$$NPW_A(18\%) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000(3.1272) + 30{,}000(0.4371) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}81{,}448 + 13{,}113 = -5{,}439$$

Interpolating: $i^*_A = 15\% + \frac{16{,}614}{16{,}614 + 5{,}439} \times 3\% = 15\% + 0.7532 \times 3\% = 17.26\%$

*For Machine B:* $0 = -4{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000(P/A, i^*, 5) + 40{,}000(P/F, i^*, 5)$

At $i = 10\%$: $NPW_B = -4{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000(3.7908) + 40{,}000(0.6209) = -4{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}73{,}850 + 24{,}836 = +48{,}686$

At $i = 12\%$: $(P/A, 12\%, 5) = 3.6048$, $(P/F, 12\%, 5) = 0.5674$

$$NPW_B(12\%) = -4{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000(3.6048) + 40{,}000(0.5674) = -4{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}600 + 22{,}696 = +23{,}296$$

At $i = 13\%$: $(P/A, 13\%, 5) = 3.5172$, $(P/F, 13\%, 5) = 0.5428$

$$NPW_B(13\%) = -4{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000(3.5172) + 40{,}000(0.5428) = -4{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}39{,}650 + 21{,}712 = +11{,}362$$

At $i = 14\%$: $(P/A, 14\%, 5) = 3.4331$, $(P/F, 14\%, 5) = 0.5194$

$$NPW_B(14\%) = -4{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000(3.4331) + 40{,}000(0.5194) = -4{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}29{,}138 + 20{,}776 = -86$$

Interpolating: $i^*_B = 13\% + \frac{11{,}362}{11{,}362 + 86} \times 1\% = 13\% + 0.9925 \times 1\% = 13.99\% \approx 14\%$

**Step 2:** Now, individual RORs are $i^*_A = 17.26\%$ and $i^*_B = 14\%$. Both exceed MARR = 10%, so both are individually acceptable. But we must use **incremental analysis** to choose the better one.

Order by initial cost: Machine A (₹3,00,000) is the *defender* (lower cost); Machine B (₹4,50,000) is the *challenger* (higher cost).

**Step 3:** Compute incremental cash flows: $\Delta CF_0 = -1{,}50{,}000$, $\Delta A = 35{,}000$, $\Delta S = 10{,}000$.

$$0 = -1{,}50{,}000 + 35{,}000(P/A, i^*_\Delta, 5) + 10{,}000(P/F, i^*_\Delta, 5)$$

At $i = 10\%$: $NPW_\Delta(10\%) = -1{,}50{,}000 + 35{,}000(3.7908) + 10{,}000(0.6209) = -1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}32{,}678 + 6{,}209 = -11{,}113$

At $i = 8\%$: $(P/A, 8\%, 5) = 3.9927$, $(P/F, 8\%, 5) = 0.6806$

$$NPW_\Delta(8\%) = -1{,}50{,}000 + 35{,}000(3.9927) + 10{,}000(0.6806) = -1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}39{,}744 + 6{,}806 = -3{,}450$$

At $i = 7\%$: $(P/A, 7\%, 5) = 4.1002$, $(P/F, 7\%, 5) = 0.7130$

$$NPW_\Delta(7\%) = -1{,}50{,}000 + 35{,}000(4.1002) + 10{,}000(0.7130) = -1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}43{,}507 + 7{,}130 = +637$$

Interpolating: $i^*_\Delta = 7\% + \frac{637}{637 + 3{,}450} \times 1\% = 7\% + 0.1559 \times 1\% = 7.16\%$

**Step 4:** Decision: since $i^*_\Delta = 7.16\% < \text{MARR} = 10\%$, the **extra investment in Machine B is NOT justified**. Choose **Machine A**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This example illustrates a classic KTU trap: Machine B has a lower individual ROR but higher absolute cash flows. Naive ROR comparison would wrongly favor B. **Incremental ROR analysis** is the rigorous method for mutually exclusive alternatives.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfall Summary

| Pitfall | Mistake | Correction |
|---|---|---|
| Sign of denominator | $NPW_H - NPW_L$ | $NPW_L - NPW_H$ (always positive) |
| Using $NPW$ vs $PW$ | Confusing absolute and incremental | Use $\Delta$ prefix for incremental |
| Trial range too wide | $i_H - i_L > 5\%$ | Keep $\leq 2\%$ for accuracy |
| Not checking sign pattern | Assumes unique root for all | Mixed investments need ERR |
| Comparing RORs directly | For mutually exclusive alternatives | Always use **incremental** ROR |
| Comparing to wrong MARR | Using bank FD rate | Use **opportunity cost**-based MARR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROR Computational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Identify all cash flows CF_t and project life n] --> nodeB[Set NPW i* = 0 with unknown rate i*]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Choose low trial rate iL e.g. MARR minus 2 percent]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute NPWL using discount factors at iL]
    nodeD --> nodeE{NPWL greater than 0?}
    nodeE -- No --> nodeF[Reduce iL and recompute]
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeG[Choose high trial rate iH greater than iL]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute NPWH using discount factors at iH]
    nodeH --> nodeI{NPWH less than 0?}
    nodeI -- No --> nodeJ[Increase iH and recompute]
    nodeJ --> nodeI
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeK[Apply linear interpolation formula]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Compute interpolated i asterisk]
    nodeL --> nodeM{i asterisk compared with MARR}
    nodeM -- Greater --> nodeN[ACCEPT project]
    nodeM -- Equal --> nodeO[INDIFFERENT]
    nodeM -- Less --> nodeP[REJECT project]
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic Architecture for Mutually Exclusive Projects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start with two or more alternatives] --> node2[Rank alternatives by initial cost ascending]
    node2 --> node3[Compute ROR of lowest cost alternative]
    node3 --> node4{ROR1 greater than MARR?}
    node4 -- No --> node5[Eliminate alternative 1 move to next]
    node4 -- Yes --> node6[Keep as defender baseline]
    node6 --> node7[Compute incremental ROR vs next higher cost]
    node7 --> node8{Incremental ROR greater than MARR?}
    node8 -- No --> node9[Reject challenger keep defender]
    node8 -- Yes --> node10[Adopt challenger as new defender]
    node10 --> node11{More alternatives remaining?}
    node9 --> node11
    node11 -- Yes --> node7
    node11 -- No --> node12[Final defender is optimal choice]
```

### 4.3 Cash Flow Sign Pattern Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Initial Investment CF0 negative] --> nodeB{Number of sign changes in CF series?}
    nodeB -- One sign change --> nodeC[Simple Investment]
    nodeB -- Two or more sign changes --> nodeD[Mixed Non Conventional Investment]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Use IRR method unique solution exists]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Use ERR method or Modified IRR]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Compare with MARR and decide]
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Position of ROR in Capital Budgeting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Capital Budgeting Techniques] --> nodeB[Non Discounted Methods]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Discounted Methods]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Payback Period]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Accounting Rate of Return]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Net Present Worth NPW]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Rate of Return ROR IRR]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Benefit Cost Ratio BCR]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[Decision: NPW greater than 0 Accept]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD2[Decision: ROR greater than MARR Accept]
    nodeC3 --> nodeD3[Decision: BCR greater than 1 Accept]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the Rate of Return Method. How is it different from the Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)?

**Model Answer:**

The **Rate of Return Method (ROR)** is a discounted cash flow technique that determines the discount rate at which the present worth of cash inflows equals the present worth of cash outflows over the project's life. It is a **time-value-of-money** based measure expressing profitability as a percentage.

| Feature | ROR Method | ARR Method |
|---|---|---|
| Time value of money | **Considered** (discounted) | Not considered (simple ratio) |
| Cash flow basis | Year-by-year net cash flows | Average accounting profit |
| Result interpretation | Internal rate (%) | Percentage of book investment |
| Use | Capital project appraisal | Managerial performance rating |

The ROR method is theoretically superior because it accounts for the timing of cash flows, while ARR uses book values and ignores the time value of money.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)? List any two factors that influence its value.

**Model Answer:**

**MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** is the lowest rate of return on an investment that a firm or investor considers acceptable before undertaking the project. It serves as the **benchmark** (also called the hurdle rate) against which the computed ROR is compared.

**Two key factors influencing MARR:**

1. **Cost of Capital** — The weighted average of interest on borrowed funds and the opportunity cost of equity. MARR must be at least equal to the cost of capital to ensure the firm does not erode shareholder value.

2. **Risk Premium** — Risky projects (e.g., R&D, new technology) require a higher MARR to compensate investors for the uncertainty of returns, while stable projects (e.g., government infrastructure) have lower MARR.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

A manufacturing company is evaluating the purchase of a new CNC machine for **₹8,00,000**. The machine is expected to generate net cash savings of **₹2,40,000 per year** for **5 years**, after which it will have a salvage value of **₹1,00,000**. The company's MARR is **15%**.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the Rate of Return (ROR) for this investment using the trial-and-error method with linear interpolation.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Based on your answer to (a), should the company purchase the machine? Justify your decision and also compute the exact ROR if the salvage value were zero.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The NPW equation is:

$$0 = -8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 \cdot (P/A, i^*, 5) + 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot (P/F, i^*, 5)$$

**Trial 1 at $i_L = 14\%$:**
$(P/A, 14\%, 5) = 3.4331$, $(P/F, 14\%, 5) = 0.5194$

$$NPW_L = -8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000(3.4331) + 1{,}00{,}000(0.5194)$$
$$= -8{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}23{,}944 + 51{,}940 = +75{,}884 > 0$$

**Trial 2 at $i_H = 18\%$:**
$(P/A, 18\%, 5) = 3.1272$, $(P/F, 18\%, 5) = 0.4371$

$$NPW_H = -8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000(3.1272) + 1{,}00{,}000(0.4371)$$
$$= -8{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}50{,}528 + 43{,}710 = -5{,}762 < 0$$

The ROR lies between 14% and 18%. Since the gap is 4%, let's narrow it.

**Trial 3 at $i_M = 17\%$:**
$(P/A, 17\%, 5) = 3.1993$, $(P/F, 17\%, 5) = 0.4561$

$$NPW_M = -8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000(3.1993) + 1{,}00{,}000(0.4561)$$
$$= -8{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}67{,}832 + 45{,}610 = +13{,}442 > 0$$

So ROR lies between 17% and 18%.

**Trial 4 at $i_H' = 17.5\%$:**
Using linear interpolation between 17% (NPW = +13,442) and 18% (NPW = -5,762):

$$i^* = 17\% + \frac{13{,}442}{13{,}442 + 5{,}762} \times (18\% - 17\%)$$
$$= 17\% + \frac{13{,}442}{19{,}204} \times 1\% = 17\% + 0.700\% = 17.70\%$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Since $i^* = 17.70\% > \text{MARR} = 15\%$, the company should **ACCEPT** the project. The investment earns 2.7 percentage points above the hurdle rate, which is a healthy margin.

**If salvage value = 0:** The equation becomes:

$$0 = -8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 \cdot (P/A, i^*, 5)$$

$$(P/A, i^*, 5) = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} = 3.3333$$

From the table, $(P/A, 15\%, 5) = 3.3522$ and $(P/A, 16\%, 5) = 3.2743$.

Interpolating: $i^* = 15\% + \frac{3.3522 - 3.3333}{3.3522 - 3.2743} \times 1\% = 15\% + \frac{0.0189}{0.0779} \times 1\% = 15\% + 0.243\% = 15.24\%$

Since $i^* = 15.24\%$ is *barely* above MARR = 15%, the project remains acceptable but is **far less attractive** without the salvage value.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

Two pumps are being considered for an irrigation project (mutually exclusive, life = 7 years):
- **Pump X:** Initial cost = ₹6,00,000; Annual operating cost = ₹1,80,000; Salvage = ₹50,000.
- **Pump Y:** Initial cost = ₹9,00,000; Annual operating cost = ₹1,40,000; Salvage = ₹80,000.

MARR = 12%. The benefit (water supply value) is assumed identical.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the ROR of each pump individually.
**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Using incremental ROR analysis, determine which pump should be selected.

**Model Solution for (a):**

For a **cost-minimization** problem (same benefit, different costs), the NPW equation becomes:

$$0 = -P + (A_{\text{operating}})(P/A, i^*, n) - S(P/F, i^*, n)$$

Wait — more correctly, we treat operating cost as a negative cash flow and salvage as positive. The "ROR" here is the rate at which the present worth of costs equals zero, which represents the break-even cost-effectiveness rate. A higher ROR is *more* attractive since it indicates cost recovery at a higher discount rate.

**Pump X:** $0 = -6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000(P/A, i^*, 7) + 50{,}000(P/F, i^*, 7)$

At $i = 12\%$: $(P/A, 12\%, 7) = 4.5638$, $(P/F, 12\%, 7) = 0.4523$

$$NPW_X(12\%) = -6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000(4.5638) + 50{,}000(0.4523)$$
$$= -6{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}21{,}484 + 22{,}615 = -13{,}98{,}869$$

At $i = 0\%$: $NPW = -6{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}60{,}000 + 50{,}000 = -18{,}10{,}000$

At $i = 50\%$ (rough): $NPW \approx -6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000(1.6) + 50{,}000(0.06) = -8{,}84{,}997$

The NPW is **always negative** for any feasible rate because the annual costs exceed the initial cost over 7 years, and the salvage is too small. So Pump X has **no positive ROR** in the conventional sense.

Let us reconsider — in cost minimization, we look at the Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) and pick the one with lower EAC. Alternatively, we can flip the cash flows to a benefit viewpoint.

**Revised formulation (Benefit equivalent view):** Treat the *avoided* cost as the benefit. Define equivalent annual benefit = ₹2,80,000 (a notional figure representing water supply revenue, assumed same for both).

Then for **Pump X:** $0 = -6{,}00{,}000 + (2{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000)(P/A, i^*, 7) + 50{,}000(P/F, i^*, 7)$
$$0 = -6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000(P/A, i^*, 7) + 50{,}000(P/F, i^*, 7)$$

At $i = 0\%$: $NPW = -6{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = +1{,}50{,}000 > 0$
At $i = 8\%$: $(P/A, 8\%, 7) = 5.2064$, $(P/F, 8\%, 7) = 0.5835$
$$NPW = -6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000(5.2064) + 50{,}000(0.5835) = -6{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}20{,}640 + 29{,}175 = -50{,}185 < 0$$

Interpolating: $i^*_X = 0\% + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}185} \times 8\% = 0\% + 0.7494 \times 8\% = 5.99\% \approx 6\%$

**Pump Y:** $0 = -9{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000(P/A, i^*, 7) + 80{,}000(P/F, i^*, 7)$

At $i = 0\%$: $NPW = -9{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}80{,}000 + 80{,}000 = +1{,}60{,}000 > 0$
At $i = 6\%$: $(P/A, 6\%, 7) = 5.5824$, $(P/F, 6\%, 7) = 0.6651$
$$NPW = -9{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000(5.5824) + 80{,}000(0.6651) = -9{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}81{,}536 + 53{,}208 = -65{,}256 < 0$$

At $i = 5\%$: $(P/A, 5\%, 7) = 5.7864$, $(P/F, 5\%, 7) = 0.7107$
$$NPW = -9{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000(5.7864) + 80{,}000(0.7107) = -9{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}10{,}096 + 56{,}856 = -33{,}048 < 0$$

At $i = 4\%$: $(P/A, 4\%, 7) = 6.0021$, $(P/F, 4\%, 7) = 0.7599$
$$NPW = -9{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000(6.0021) + 80{,}000(0.7599) = -9{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}40{,}294 + 60{,}792 = +1{,}086 > 0$$

Interpolating: $i^*_Y = 4\% + \frac{1{,}086}{1{,}086 + 33{,}048} \times 1\% = 4\% + 0.0318\% = 4.03\%$

So $i^*_X \approx 6\%$ and $i^*_Y \approx 4.03\%$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Pump X has higher individual ROR (6% vs. 4.03%). But since the investments differ, we must use **incremental ROR analysis**.

Order by initial cost: Pump X is the *defender* (lower cost, ₹6,00,000), Pump Y is the *challenger* (₹9,00,000).

**Incremental cash flows:** $\Delta P = -3{,}00{,}000$, $\Delta A_{\text{net}} = (1{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000) = -40{,}000$ (operating cost *savings* is negative — Pump Y actually costs more to operate), $\Delta S = +30{,}000$.

Note: Since Pump Y has *higher* annual operating cost, the incremental net annual cash flow from switching to Y is: Revenue is same, so incremental net cash flow = $\Delta A_{\text{net}} = +1{,}00{,}000$ (the *revenue* saved) minus $1{,}40{,}000$ (Pump Y's cost) = no, wait.

Let me redo this properly. Net cash flow = Revenue − Operating Cost.

For Pump X: Net annual CF = 2,80,000 − 1,80,000 = ₹1,00,000.
For Pump Y: Net annual CF = 2,80,000 − 1,40,000 = ₹1,40,000.

Incremental: $\Delta A = 1{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = +40{,}000$, $\Delta P = -3{,}00{,}000$, $\Delta S = +30{,}000$.

**Incremental NPW equation:** $0 = -3{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000(P/A, i^*_\Delta, 7) + 30{,}000(P/F, i^*_\Delta, 7)$

At $i = 10\%$: $(P/A, 10\%, 7) = 4.8684$, $(P/F, 10\%, 7) = 0.5132$
$$NPW_\Delta(10\%) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000(4.8684) + 30{,}000(0.5132) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}94{,}736 + 15{,}396 = -89{,}868$$

At $i = 8\%$: $(P/A, 8\%, 7) = 5.2064$, $(P/F, 8\%, 7) = 0.5835$
$$NPW_\Delta(8\%) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000(5.2064) + 30{,}000(0.5835) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}08{,}256 + 17{,}505 = -74{,}239$$

At $i = 4\%$: $(P/A, 4\%, 7) = 6.0021$, $(P/F, 4\%, 7) = 0.7599$
$$NPW_\Delta(4\%) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000(6.0021) + 30{,}000(0.7599) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}084 + 22{,}797 = -37{,}119$$

At $i = 0\%$: $NPW_\Delta = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}000 + 30{,}000 = +10{,}000$

So ROR is between 0% and 4%. Closer to 0%.

At $i = 0.5\%$: $(P/A, 0.5\%, 7) \approx 6.7955$, $(P/F, 0.5\%, 7) \approx 0.9656$
$$NPW_\Delta(0.5\%) \approx -3{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000(6.7955) + 30{,}000(0.9656) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}71{,}820 + 28{,}968 = +788$$

At $i = 1\%$: $(P/A, 1\%, 7) = 6.7282$, $(P/F, 1\%, 7) = 0.9327$
$$NPW_\Delta(1\%) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000(6.7282) + 30{,}000(0.9327) = -3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}69{,}128 + 27{,}981 = -2{,}891$$

Interpolating: $i^*_\Delta = 0.5\% + \frac{788}{788 + 2{,}891} \times 0.5\% = 0.5\% + 0.107\% = 0.61\%$

**Decision:** Since $i^*_\Delta = 0.61\% < \text{MARR} = 12\%$, the **extra ₹3,00,000 invested in Pump Y is NOT justified**. The company should **choose Pump X**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Loss of Marks Reason 1:** Directly comparing individual RORs (6% > 4.03% → "Pick X"). This earns *partial* credit but misses the rigorous incremental analysis.
> - **Loss of Marks Reason 2:** Forgetting to verify the sign pattern of incremental cash flows. A wrong $\Delta A$ sign flips the entire conclusion.
> - **Loss of Marks Reason 3:** Not stating the final decision in terms of the **defender** (Pump X), with explicit mention of the MARR comparison.
> - **Loss of Marks Reason 4:** In the cost-minimization context, students often forget that the *incremental net cash flow* accounts for the *revenue side*, not just operating cost differences. Always redefine net cash flow as (Revenue − Cost) before forming the incremental series.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ROR Definition:** The discount rate $i^*$ at which $NPW = 0$ for the entire project life.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** Accept if $i^* \geq \text{MARR}$; Reject otherwise.
- ✅ **Trial-and-Error:** Standard computational method. Always start with MARR as one trial rate.
- ✅ **Linear Interpolation:** $i^* = i_L + \dfrac{NPW_L}{NPW_L - NPV_H}(i_H - i_L)$. Keep trial gap $\leq 2\%$ for accuracy.
- ✅ **Closed-form Cases:** Possible for single-payment ($F$ or $P$) problems; otherwise trial-and-error is mandatory.
- ✅ **Simple vs. Mixed Investment:** Simple = one sign change (unique ROR). Mixed = multiple sign changes (need ERR/MIRR).
- ✅ **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** NEVER compare individual RORs directly. Always use **incremental ROR analysis** on the cash flow difference (challenger − defender).
- ✅ **Defender-Challenger Order:** Rank by initial cost; the lower-cost option is the defender baseline.
- ✅ **MARR Source:** Determined by cost of capital + risk premium; never use bank FD rate naively.
- ✅ **KTU Board Tip:** Show two complete trial calculations, then the interpolation, then the decision. Partial marks are awarded for the method even if the final answer has a small numerical error.
- ✅ **Incremental ROR Decision:** $i^*_\Delta \geq \text{MARR} \Rightarrow$ adopt challenger; $i^*_\Delta < \text{MARR} \Rightarrow$ retain defender.
- ✅ **Sign Pattern Check:** Always count sign changes in the cash flow series before deciding the computational method.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Scheme Students:** The Rate of Return Method is **part of Module 2** in OECET725. Combined with the **NPW (Net Present Worth) Method** and **BCR (Benefit-Cost Ratio) Method**, it forms the trifecta of discounted cash flow techniques. Master the ROR interpolation technique thoroughly — it is the single most-tested computational skill in this course.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** is the lowest rate of return that an organization or investor considers acceptable on an investment, before factoring in project-specific risks. It is the financial "hurdle" or "cut-off" rate that any proposed engineering project or capital investment must *at least* earn to be considered viable and economically justifiable.

In the framework of **Engineering Economics**, MARR functions as the **decision criterion benchmark** — the threshold against which the internal earning power of a project (typically measured by the Internal Rate of Return, IRR) is compared.

$$ \text{Decision Rule: } \quad \text{If } \text{IRR} \geq \text{MARR} \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPT the project} $$

$$ \text{If } \text{IRR} < \text{MARR} \Rightarrow \text{REJECT the project} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synonyms in KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:**
> - **Hurdle Rate**
> - **Required Rate of Return (RRR)**
> - **Cut-off Rate**
> - **Opportunity Cost of Capital (when used strictly)**
> - **Benchmark Discount Rate**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Landlord's Rent Floor"

Imagine you own a small shop on a busy street. Several businesses approach you wanting to rent your shop. You, as the landlord, have a *minimum acceptable monthly rent* — say, **₹50,000** — below which you would rather keep the shop vacant than lease it at a loss.

This minimum rent is your **personal MARR**:
- It compensates you for the **time value of money** (you could have invested that money elsewhere).
- It compensates for **risk** (what if the tenant damages the property?).
- It reflects your **opportunity cost** (the next-best alternative income forgone).

In the same way, a company has a **MARR** — the minimum return a project must generate to justify tying up the firm's scarce capital. Any project that returns less than MARR is like renting out your shop at ₹40,000 when your floor is ₹50,000: **economically irrational**, even if the project shows a "profit" in absolute terms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** A project can show a positive Net Present Value (NPV) and *still* be rejected if its return falls below MARR. The "profit" must be *adequate*, not just positive. MARR defines what "adequate" means.

## 1.3 Why MARR Exists — The Three Pillars

MARR is **not an arbitrary number** chosen by finance departments. It is mathematically grounded in three real economic forces:

| **Pillar** | **Description** | **Engineering Real-World Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Time Value of Money (TVM)** | A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow due to inflation and earning potential. | ₹1 Crore invested in a fixed deposit at 7% becomes ₹1.97 Cr in 10 years. |
| **2. Risk Premium** | Future cash flows are uncertain; investors demand compensation for bearing that uncertainty. | A new product launch has uncertain sales → higher MARR. |
| **3. Opportunity Cost** | Money invested in project A is unavailable for project B, stocks, or bonds. | A factory expansion that yields 9% may be rejected if alternative use of capital yields 12%. |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **MARR is dimensionless** (it is a *rate*, expressed as a **percentage per year**, % p.a.).
> - The most universally used **risk-free benchmark** is the **10-Year Government of India Securities (G-Sec)** yield.
> - For *bank-based project appraisal* in India, the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** typically forms the floor of MARR.
> - For *government/public sector projects*, MARR is often mandated at **10%–12%** by the Planning Commission / NITI Aayog guidelines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of MARR — The Build-Up Equation

The most widely accepted academic formulation decomposes MARR into **four additive components**:

$$ \boxed{\text{MARR} = i^* + IP + RP + OP} $$

Where:

- $i^*$ = **Pure (Risk-Free) Interest Rate** — the baseline time-value-of-money component, often proxied by the long-term government bond yield.
- $IP$ = **Inflation Premium** — the expected rate of general price-level increase over the project life.
- $RP$ = **Risk Premium** — additional return demanded to compensate for project-specific uncertainty.
- $OP$ = **Opportunity Cost Premium** (optional) — return foregone on the next-best alternative investment of equivalent risk.

> [!NOTE]
> In **rigorous Engineering Economy texts** (e.g., Newnan, Park), the *effective* MARR is often written as a single **i*** that already **internalizes** inflation, i.e., the **market interest rate**. In such a treatment, the MARR is *not* added to inflation separately; rather, **all future cash flows are estimated in *real* (constant) rupees and discounted at the *real* MARR**.

## 2.2 The Two Conceptual Treatments of MARR

### 2.2.1 Treatment A — Constant-Dollar (Real) Analysis

- All cash flows expressed in **today's rupees** (inflation already stripped out).
- The discount rate used is the **real MARR**, calculated using the **Fisher Equation**:

$$ 1 + i_{nominal} = (1 + i_{real})(1 + f) $$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad i_{real} = \frac{1 + i_{nominal}}{1 + f} - 1 \approx i_{nominal} - f $$

where $f$ = expected inflation rate.

### 2.2.2 Treatment B — Then-Current (Nominal) Analysis

- All cash flows expressed in **actual future rupees** (including inflation).
- The discount rate used is the **nominal MARR** (which already contains the inflation premium).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Mixing the two treatments — using **real cash flows** with a **nominal MARR**, or vice-versa — produces **incorrect, often wildly inflated, NPV values**. The KTU examiner frequently tests this subtlety.

## 2.3 Methods of Determining MARR in Practice

### 2.3.1 Method 1 — Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

The most theoretically rigorous method. MARR is set equal to the firm's blended cost of financing:

$$ \text{MARR} = \text{WACC} = w_d \cdot k_d \cdot (1 - T) + w_e \cdot k_e $$

Where:
- $w_d$ = proportion of debt in capital structure
- $w_e$ = proportion of equity in capital structure
- $k_d$ = before-tax cost of debt
- $T$ = corporate tax rate
- $k_e$ = cost of equity (often via CAPM: $k_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$)

### 2.3.2 Method 2 — Opportunity Cost Approach

$$ \text{MARR} = \text{Return available on the next-best investment of similar risk}} $$

### 2.3.3 Method 3 — Management Judgment / Hurdle Rate

Senior management sets a *subjective* cut-off, often **2%–4% above WACC**, to ensure only value-accretive projects pass.

## 2.4 MARR in the Project Selection Hierarchy

The role of MARR is illustrated in the following logical flow:

1. **Estimate project cash flows** over its economic life $N$.
2. **Compute the project's IRR** (the rate that makes NPV = 0).
3. **Compare IRR with MARR**:
   - IRR > MARR → Project creates **economic value above the hurdle** → **ACCEPT**.
   - IRR = MARR → Project breaks even against the required return → **MARGINAL** (often accepted if strategic).
   - IRR < MARR → Project **destroys shareholder value** → **REJECT**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Variables** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| MARR Build-up | $\text{MARR} = i^* + IP + RP + OP$ | $i^*, IP, RP, OP$ | % per annum |
| Real vs Nominal (Fisher) | $i_{real} = \frac{1+i_{nom}}{1+f} - 1$ | $f$ = inflation rate | Dimensionless rate |
| Approximate Real Rate | $i_{real} \approx i_{nom} - f$ | — | Valid for $f < 5\%$ |
| WACC | $w_d k_d (1-T) + w_e k_e$ | $w_d + w_e = 1$ | % per annum |
| CAPM (Cost of Equity) | $k_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$ risk-free, $\beta$ sensitivity | Dimensionless |
| IRR Decision Rule | $\text{IRR} \geq \text{MARR} \Rightarrow$ Accept | — | Strict inequality |
| NPV with MARR | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+\text{MARR})^t}$ | $\text{CF}_t$ at year $t$ | Currency (₹) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capital Budgeting in Manufacturing:** Choosing between two robotic assembly lines when both require ₹50 Cr but offer different ROI profiles.
- **Public Infrastructure:** The National Highways Authority of India (NHAI) appraises Build-Operate-Transfer (BOT) toll-road projects against a regulatory MARR set by the Ministry of Finance.
- **R&D Portfolio Selection:** Pharmaceutical companies use risk-adjusted MARR (often 15%–20%) to prioritize drug-development pipelines.
- **Energy Sector:** Power plants (thermal vs. solar vs. wind) are compared on levelized cost of electricity (LCOE) discounted at sector-specific MARR.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From the MARR to the Accept/Reject Decision Criterion

We will derive, from first principles, the formal justification for the IRR ≥ MARR decision rule.

### Step 1: Define Net Present Value (NPV) at a generic discount rate $i$

$$ \text{NPV}(i) = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i)^t} $$

where:
- $\text{CF}_t$ = net cash flow at the end of year $t$
- $N$ = project economic life in years
- $i$ = discount rate being tested

### Step 2: Define IRR as the discount rate that makes NPV identically zero

By definition, $\text{IRR}$ is the rate $i^*$ satisfying:

$$ 0 = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i^*)^t} $$

### Step 3: Examine the sign of NPV when $i = \text{MARR}$

We want to know: at $i = \text{MARR}$, is the NPV positive or negative?

$$ \text{NPV}(\text{MARR}) = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+\text{MARR})^t} $$

### Step 4: Use the monotonicity property of NPV with respect to $i$

For a *conventional* investment project (one initial outflow $\text{CF}_0 < 0$ followed by positive inflows $\text{CF}_t > 0$ for $t = 1, \ldots, N$):

$$ \frac{d(\text{NPV})}{di} = -\sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{t \cdot \text{CF}_t}{(1+i)^{t+1}} < 0 $$

This shows **NPV is a strictly decreasing function of $i$** for conventional projects.

### Step 5: Combine steps 2 and 4 to reach the decision rule

Since NPV is strictly decreasing in $i$:

$$ \text{If } \text{IRR} > \text{MARR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad i \text{ evaluated at MARR is less than IRR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NPV}(\text{MARR}) > 0 $$

$$ \text{If } \text{IRR} = \text{MARR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NPV}(\text{MARR}) = 0 $$

$$ \text{If } \text{IRR} < \text{MARR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NPV}(\text{MARR}) < 0 $$

### Step 6: Translate to the Accept/Reject rule

$$ \text{Project creates value} \iff \text{NPV}(\text{MARR}) > 0 \iff \text{IRR} \geq \text{MARR} $$

**∴ The decision rule is proven:** *Accept if and only if $\text{IRR} \geq \text{MARR}$.* ∎

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — MARR Determination Using WACC

> **Problem (KTU-Style):**
> *Zenith Engineering Ltd. is financed with 40% debt at a pre-tax cost of 9% and 60% equity at a cost of 14%. The applicable corporate tax rate is 30%. The firm's beta is 1.2, the risk-free rate is 7%, and the expected market return is 13%.*
> *(a) Compute the WACC and recommend this as the MARR. (b) If a project requires an initial investment of ₹25 lakh and generates ₹8 lakh per year for 5 years, determine whether the project should be accepted.*

### Part (a): WACC Computation

**Step 1: Compute the after-tax cost of debt**

$$ k_d(1-T) = 9\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 9\% \times 0.70 = 6.3\% $$

**Step 2: Compute the cost of equity using CAPM**

$$ k_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f) = 7\% + 1.2 \times (13\% - 7\%) $$

$$ k_e = 7\% + 1.2 \times 6\% = 7\% + 7.2\% = 14.2\% $$

**Step 3: Compute WACC (and hence MARR)**

$$ \text{MARR} = \text{WACC} = w_d \cdot k_d(1-T) + w_e \cdot k_e $$

$$ \text{MARR} = (0.40)(6.3\%) + (0.60)(14.2\%) $$

$$ \text{MARR} = 2.52\% + 8.52\% = 11.04\% $$

**∴ The MARR is approximately 11.04% p.a.**

### Part (b): Accept/Reject Decision

**Step 1: Compute NPV at MARR = 11.04%**

$$ \text{NPV} = -25 + 8 \times \left[\frac{1 - (1+0.1104)^{-5}}{0.1104}\right] $$

**Step 2: Compute the Present Worth Factor (P/A, 11.04%, 5)**

$$ (1 + 0.1104)^5 = 1.6858 $$

$$ (1.1104)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.6858} = 0.5932 $$

$$ \frac{1 - 0.5932}{0.1104} = \frac{0.4068}{0.1104} = 3.6848 $$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$ \text{NPV} = -25 + 8 \times 3.6848 = -25 + 29.478 = +4.478 \text{ lakh} $$

**Step 4: Decision**

$$ \text{NPV} = +₹4.478 \text{ Lakh} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Project earns more than MARR} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT} $$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — MARR Variations by Project Class

| **Project Class** | **Typical MARR Range** | **Reason for Variation** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Government infrastructure (low risk) | **8% – 10%** | Backed by sovereign guarantee; long asset life | Highway, metro rail |
| Established manufacturing (moderate risk) | **12% – 15%** | Predictable cash flows; modest competition | Auto-ancillary plant |
| High-tech / R&D (high risk) | **18% – 25%** | High obsolescence, demand uncertainty | New semiconductor fab |
| Speculative ventures (very high risk) | **25% – 40%+** | Equity-like return profile | Start-up, biotech |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — MARR Determination & Application Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Proposal Initiated] --> B[Identify Project Risk Profile]
    B --> C{Method of MARR Estimation}
    C --> D[Method 1: WACC Build-Up]
    C --> E[Method 2: Opportunity Cost]
    C --> F[Method 3: Management Hurdle]
    D --> G[Compute Pure Rate + Inflation Premium + Risk Premium + Opportunity Premium]
    E --> H[Identify Best Alternative Investment of Similar Risk]
    F --> I[Apply Strategic Margin over WACC]
    G --> J[Final MARR Established]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Estimate Project Cash Flows over Life N]
    K --> L[Compute Project IRR]
    L --> M{IRR versus MARR Comparison}
    M -->|IRR greater than MARR| N[ACCEPT - Project creates economic value]
    M -->|IRR equal to MARR| O[MARGINAL - Break-even, may accept for strategic reasons]
    M -->|IRR less than MARR| P[REJECT - Project destroys value]
    N --> Q[Proceed to Capital Allocation]
    O --> R[Strategic Review by Board]
    P --> S[Archive / Reject]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — MARR Build-Up Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MARR] --> B[Pure Time Value Component]
    A --> C[Inflation Premium]
    A --> D[Risk Premium]
    A --> E[Opportunity Cost Premium]
    B --> B1[10-Year G-Sec Yield as Proxy]
    C --> C1[Expected CPI / WPI Inflation]
    D --> D1[Project-Specific Uncertainty]
    D --> D2[Industry Beta Multiplier]
    E --> E1[Return on Next-Best Alternative]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Real vs Nominal MARR Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Cash Flow Convention] --> B{Cash Flows in Real or Nominal Rupees?}
    B -->|Real Constant Rupees| C[Use Real MARR via Fisher Equation]
    B -->|Nominal Then-Current Rupees| D[Use Nominal MARR already containing inflation]
    C --> E[Convert Nominal to Real: i_real equals 1+i_nom divided by 1+f, minus 1]
    D --> F[Apply Nominal MARR directly to nominal cash flows]
    E --> G[Compute NPV in Real Terms]
    F --> H[Compute NPV in Nominal Terms]
    G --> I[Decision: Accept if NPV greater than zero]
    H --> I
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR). Mention any two of its synonyms.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** is the lowest rate of return that a firm considers acceptable on an investment, representing the financial threshold below which a project will be rejected. It is also called the **Hurdle Rate** or the **Required Rate of Return (RRR)** or the **Cut-off Rate**. It serves as the benchmark discount rate used in Engineering Economics to evaluate and rank capital investment proposals. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any three components that constitute the MARR and briefly explain the role of the risk premium.**

**Model Answer:**

The three components of MARR are:
1. **Pure (risk-free) interest rate** — the time-value-of-money baseline.
2. **Inflation premium** — compensation for the expected decline in purchasing power.
3. **Risk premium** — additional return demanded for the project's inherent uncertainty.

The **risk premium** compensates investors for the possibility that actual cash flows will deviate from forecasts. Higher uncertainty (e.g., new-product launch) requires a higher risk premium and thus a higher MARR. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various methods of determining the MARR for an engineering project. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm is evaluating a project requiring an initial investment of ₹50 lakh. The project generates cash inflows of ₹15 lakh per annum for 6 years. The firm's cost of equity is 16%, the pre-tax cost of debt is 10%, the debt-equity ratio is 1:2, and the tax rate is 30%. Determine the MARR using the WACC approach and advise whether to accept the project. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Methods of Determining MARR (7 marks)

**1. Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) Approach (3 marks)**

The MARR is set equal to the firm's weighted average cost of capital. WACC considers the proportion of debt and equity in the firm's capital structure and their respective costs. Debt cost is taken on an after-tax basis, and equity cost is typically estimated using the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM):

$$ \text{WACC} = w_d \cdot k_d(1 - T) + w_e \cdot k_e $$

**2. Opportunity Cost Approach (2 marks)**

The MARR is set equal to the return available on the *next-best* investment opportunity of comparable risk. If the firm can earn 13% on a similar-risk alternative, then 13% becomes the MARR.

**3. Management-Set Hurdle Rate (2 marks)**

Senior management imposes a subjective cut-off rate, often set 2%–4% above the WACC, to ensure that only value-accretive projects clear the hurdle. This is common in private firms and start-ups.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify the capital structure weights**

Debt-equity ratio = 1:2 → For every ₹3 of total capital, ₹1 is debt and ₹2 is equity.

$$ w_d = \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333, \quad w_e = \frac{2}{3} = 0.6667 $$

**Step 2: Compute after-tax cost of debt**

$$ k_d(1 - T) = 10\% \times (1 - 0.30) = 7\% $$

**Step 3: Compute MARR (= WACC)**

$$ \text{MARR} = (0.3333)(7\%) + (0.6667)(16\%) $$

$$ \text{MARR} = 2.3333\% + 10.6667\% = 13.0\% $$

**∴ MARR = 13% per annum.**

**Step 4: Compute the Present Worth Factor (P/A, 13%, 6)**

$$ (1.13)^6 = 2.08195 $$

$$ (1.13)^{-6} = \frac{1}{2.08195} = 0.48032 $$

$$ \frac{1 - 0.48032}{0.13} = \frac{0.51968}{0.13} = 3.9976 $$

**Step 5: Compute NPV**

$$ \text{NPV} = -50 + 15 \times 3.9976 $$

$$ \text{NPV} = -50 + 59.96 = +₹9.96 \text{ lakh} $$

**Step 6: Decision**

Since NPV = +₹9.96 lakh > 0, the project earns a return in excess of the MARR of 13%. **∴ The project should be ACCEPTED.** **(7 marks complete)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the relationship between MARR and the cost of capital. How does the risk profile of a project influence the choice of MARR? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two mutually exclusive projects require the same initial investment of ₹30 lakh. Project A yields ₹9 lakh per year for 5 years, while Project B yields ₹7.5 lakh per year for 6 years. The firm's MARR is 10%. Using the IRR criterion, determine which project should be selected. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — MARR vs Cost of Capital and Risk (7 marks)

**MARR and Cost of Capital — Conceptual Link (4 marks):**

The cost of capital represents the *minimum* return that a firm must earn to satisfy its providers of capital (debt-holders and equity-holders). The MARR, on the other hand, is the *threshold return* required from a specific project. The MARR is set **equal to or greater than** the cost of capital. The WACC is the most theoretically sound *anchor* for MARR, but MARR is then adjusted upward for project-specific risk.

**Influence of Risk Profile (3 marks):**

Projects with **low, predictable cash flows** (e.g., expansion of an existing mature product line) may use MARR = WACC, e.g., 11%. Projects with **high, uncertain cash flows** (e.g., entry into a new export market) require a risk-augmented MARR, e.g., 18%–20%. The general principle: **higher project risk → higher MARR.** This is also why firms maintain **risk-adjusted hurdle rates** by division or business unit.

---

#### Part (b) — Mutually Exclusive Project Selection (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute IRR for Project A**

Set NPV = 0:

$$ 0 = -30 + 9 \times \left[\frac{1 - (1+i_A)^{-5}}{i_A}\right] $$

$$ \frac{1 - (1+i_A)^{-5}}{i_A} = \frac{30}{9} = 3.3333 $$

From the (P/A, i, 5) tables: $i_A$ lies between 15% (P/A = 3.3522) and 16% (P/A = 3.2743). Interpolating:

$$ i_A \approx 15\% + \frac{3.3522 - 3.3333}{3.3522 - 3.2743} \times 1\% = 15\% + \frac{0.0189}{0.0779} \times 1\% \approx 15.24\% $$

**Step 2: Compute IRR for Project B**

$$ 0 = -30 + 7.5 \times \left[\frac{1 - (1+i_B)^{-6}}{i_B}\right] $$

$$ \frac{1 - (1+i_B)^{-6}}{i_B} = \frac{30}{7.5} = 4.0 $$

From the (P/A, i, 6) tables: $i_B$ lies between 12% (P/A = 4.1114) and 13% (P/A = 3.9975). Interpolating:

$$ i_B \approx 12\% + \frac{4.1114 - 4.0}{4.1114 - 3.9975} \times 1\% = 12\% + \frac{0.1114}{0.1139} \times 1\% \approx 12.98\% $$

**Step 3: Compare with MARR = 10%**

$$ \text{IRR}_A \approx 15.24\% > \text{MARR} = 10\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{Accept A} $$
$$ \text{IRR}_B \approx 12.98\% > \text{MARR} = 10\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{Accept B} $$

**Step 4: Apply incremental IRR analysis for mutually exclusive projects**

Compare the *incremental cash flow* (A − B):
- Incremental initial outlay = 0 (same investment)
- Incremental annual cash flow for years 1–5 = ₹9 − ₹7.5 = ₹1.5 lakh
- Year 6: Project A gives ₹0, Project B gives ₹7.5 lakh → incremental cash flow in year 6 = −₹7.5 lakh

The incremental IRR of (A − B) is **negative** (because the longer-life B has a positive ₹7.5 lakh inflow in year 6, while A has nothing). The incremental NPV at MARR = 10% is positive in favour of B (since B earns the additional year-6 cash flow). Hence, by the *incremental analysis* rule, the project with the **longer economic life (Project B)** is preferred when the incremental IRR is below MARR.

**∴ Select PROJECT B** (longer life, same upfront capital, better overall return at MARR = 10%). **(7 marks complete)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating MARR with the discount rate used in NPV calculation** — they are *the same* numerically, but conceptually MARR is the *decision benchmark*, not a free parameter.
> 2. **Mixing real and nominal cash flows** with the wrong MARR (the most common error, costs 2–3 marks per problem).
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the after-tax adjustment** $k_d(1-T)$ in the WACC formula.
> 4. **For mutually exclusive projects**, *never* choose by simply picking the higher IRR — always perform **incremental IRR analysis**.
> 5. **Units slip-ups**: writing MARR as a decimal (0.11) when the problem states 11%, leading to $(1.11)^t$ errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MARR** = the minimum acceptable return on an investment; the financial "hurdle."
- **Synonyms:** Hurdle rate, RRR, cut-off rate, opportunity cost of capital.
- **Build-up equation:** $\text{MARR} = i^* + IP + RP + OP$.
- **Decision Rule:** Accept if $\text{IRR} \geq \text{MARR}$; equivalently, accept if $\text{NPV}(\text{MARR}) \geq 0$.
- **Determination Methods:** WACC, Opportunity Cost, Management Hurdle.
- **WACC formula:** $w_d k_d(1-T) + w_e k_e$, with $w_d + w_e = 1$.
- **CAPM formula for cost of equity:** $k_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$.
- **Fisher Equation:** $i_{real} = \frac{1+i_{nom}}{1+f} - 1 \approx i_{nom} - f$.
- **Risk principle:** Higher project risk → Higher MARR.
- **Mutually exclusive projects:** Use *incremental IRR analysis*, not simple IRR ranking.
- **Two treatments exist:** Real-terms (use real MARR) vs Nominal-terms (use nominal MARR) — **never mix them**.
- **Units:** MARR is always expressed as **% per annum**; the time horizon in cash flows must match.
- **Typical Indian benchmarks:** G-Sec yield ≈ 7%, bank project-lending rate ≈ 9%–11%, MARR for engineering projects ≈ 10%–15%.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — INTERNAL RATE OF RETURN (IRR)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is defined as the **discount rate** at which the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of all cash flows (both inflows and outflows) from a project or investment becomes exactly **zero**. In other words, IRR is the break-even rate of return where the present value of future cash inflows precisely equals the present value of cash outflows (initial investment).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *IRR is the discount rate (r) that equates the present value of a project's expected future cash inflows to the present value of its cash outflows, i.e., the rate at which NPV = 0.*

Mathematically, the IRR is the value of $r$ that satisfies the equation:

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Cash flow occurring at time period $t$
- $r$ = Discount rate (the IRR we want to find)
- $n$ = Total number of time periods
- $CF_0$ = Initial investment (typically negative)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏠 Real-World Analogy — The "Mortgage Break-Even" Concept**
>
> Imagine you invest **₹1,00,000** today in a project that will return money to you over the next 4 years. You ask yourself: *"At what annual interest rate would a bank have to compound my ₹1,00,000 deposit to make it grow to exactly the same amount that this project will give me?"* That magic interest rate is the **IRR** of your project.
>
> Think of IRR as the **project's own earning speedometer** — it tells you the speed (rate) at which the project is generating returns. If the project's IRR (8%, 12%, 18%, etc.) is **higher** than your minimum acceptable rate (called the **cost of capital** or **hurdle rate**), the project is worth pursuing.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of IRR

- **Time Value Sensitive:** IRR inherently accounts for the time value of money by discounting future cash flows.
- **Percentage Measure:** It is expressed as an **annual percentage rate (%)**, making it easy to compare against benchmark rates.
- **Project-Specific:** Each project has its own unique IRR, computed independently of external market conditions.
- **Break-Even Nature:** It is the rate at which the project neither creates nor destroys value (NPV = 0).

### 1.4 IRR vs. Cost of Capital — The Decision Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Decision Criterion:**
> - If **IRR > Cost of Capital (k)** → **ACCEPT** the project ✅
> - If **IRR < Cost of Capital (k)** → **REJECT** the project ❌
> - If **IRR = Cost of Capital (k)** → **Indifferent** (NPV = 0) ⚖️

### 1.5 Significance of IRR in Engineering Economics

In engineering project evaluation (machinery purchase, plant setup, R&D projects, infrastructure), IRR is a **widely used capital budgeting technique** because:
1. It gives a single percentage figure that is intuitively understandable.
2. It incorporates the time value of money.
3. It does not require prior knowledge of the cost of capital for computation (though it is needed for the accept/reject decision).
4. It is highly useful for **ranking mutually exclusive projects**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Underlying Logic

The IRR method is built on a deep understanding of the **Net Present Value (NPV)** function. To grasp IRR, we must first understand that:

- As the **discount rate (r) increases**, the NPV of a project **decreases**.
- As the discount rate **decreases**, the NPV **increases**.
- Therefore, for most conventional projects (one initial outflow followed by inflows), there is **exactly one rate** where NPV crosses zero — that is the IRR.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for IRR Computation

The computation of IRR follows a structured logical flow:

1. **Step 1 — Identify Cash Flows:** Tabulate the initial investment ($CF_0$) and all expected future cash inflows ($CF_1, CF_2, \ldots, CF_n$).
2. **Step 2 — Set Up the NPV Equation:** Substitute these cash flows into the NPV formula with $r$ as the unknown.
3. **Step 3 — Apply the NPV = 0 Condition:** Because IRR is the rate that makes NPV zero, set NPV = 0 and solve for $r$.
4. **Step 4 — Trial and Error Method:** Start with an arbitrary discount rate, compute NPV. If NPV > 0, try a higher rate. If NPV < 0, try a lower rate. Narrow down until NPV ≈ 0.
5. **Step 5 — Interpolation (Refinement):** Once two close rates ($r_L$ for lower bound with NPV > 0 and $r_H$ for upper bound with NPV < 0) are found, use linear interpolation to estimate the precise IRR.

### 2.3 Linear Interpolation Formula (KTU High-Yield!)

When two rates $r_L$ (lower rate) and $r_H$ (higher rate) bracket the IRR, the interpolation formula is:

$$\text{IRR} = r_L + \left[ \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \right] \times (r_H - r_L)$$

Where:
- $r_L$ = Lower trial discount rate (at which NPV is positive)
- $r_H$ = Higher trial discount rate (at which NPV is negative)
- $\text{NPV}_L$ = NPV at the lower rate
- $\text{NPV}_H$ = NPV at the higher rate

### 2.4 Modified Internal Rate of Return (MIRR) — Brief Overview

To overcome certain limitations of IRR (such as the multiple IRR problem), the **Modified IRR (MIRR)** is used:

$$\text{MIRR} = \left[ \frac{FV(\text{Positive CFs at cost of capital})}{PV(\text{Negative CFs at cost of capital})} \right]^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | NPV Equation | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $CF_t$ = Cash flow at $t$; $r$ = Discount rate; $n$ = Life | Conventional project: 1 outflow + inflows |
| 2 | IRR Definition (NPV = 0) | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} = 0$ | IRR is the unknown $r$ | NPV must be zero |
| 3 | Linear Interpolation | $\text{IRR} = r_L + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$ | $r_L < r_H$ | $\text{NPV}_L > 0$; $\text{NPV}_H < 0$ |
| 4 | MIRR | $\text{MIRR} = \left( \dfrac{FV_{+}}{PV_{-}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$ | $FV_{+}$ = Future value of positives; $PV_{-}$ = Present value of negatives | Single rate applied uniformly |
| 5 | Decision Rule | $\text{IRR} \gtreqless k$ | $k$ = Cost of capital / hurdle rate | Accept if IRR > $k$ |
| 6 | NPV Profile Slope | $\dfrac{d(\text{NPV})}{dr} = -\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{t \cdot CF_t}{(1+r)^{t+1}}$ | Negative slope for inflows | Convex NPV curve |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In engineering project appraisal, IRR is used for:
- **Equipment Replacement Decisions:** Whether to replace old machinery.
- **Plant Expansion:** Evaluating capacity addition projects.
- **R&D Investments:** Judging feasibility of new technology adoption.
- **Infrastructure Projects:** Roads, bridges, power plants, water treatment facilities.
- **Software/IT Capital Investments:** ERP systems, automation tools.

The IRR is directly comparable to the **weighted average cost of capital (WACC)** of the firm, making it a universal benchmark metric.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Example — IRR by Trial and Error + Interpolation

### 📋 Problem Statement

> A manufacturing company is evaluating a project requiring an **initial investment of ₹1,00,000**. The project is expected to generate cash inflows of **₹30,000, ₹35,000, ₹40,000, and ₹45,000** at the end of **Years 1, 2, 3, and 4**, respectively. The company's cost of capital is **10%**.
>
> **Required:**
> (a) Calculate the IRR of the project using the trial-and-error method.
> (b) Use linear interpolation to find the precise IRR.
> (c) Should the project be accepted?

### 🧮 Step 1 — Tabulate the Cash Flows

| Year (t) | Cash Flow (₹) | Type |
|----------|---------------|------|
| 0 | -1,00,000 | Outflow (Initial Investment) |
| 1 | +30,000 | Inflow |
| 2 | +35,000 | Inflow |
| 3 | +40,000 | Inflow |
| 4 | +45,000 | Inflow |

### 🧮 Step 2 — Set Up the NPV Equation with $r$ as Unknown

We need to find $r$ such that:

$$\text{NPV} = -1{,}00{,}000 + \frac{30{,}000}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{35{,}000}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{40{,}000}{(1+r)^3} + \frac{45{,}000}{(1+r)^4} = 0$$

### 🧮 Step 3 — First Trial at $r = 10\%$

We compute the present value of each cash flow at 10%:

| Year (t) | Cash Flow (₹) | Discount Factor $\dfrac{1}{(1.10)^t}$ | Present Value (₹) |
|----------|---------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 30,000 | 0.9091 | 27,272.73 |
| 2 | 35,000 | 0.8264 | 28,925.62 |
| 3 | 40,000 | 0.7513 | 30,052.59 |
| 4 | 45,000 | 0.6830 | 30,735.21 |
| | | **Total PV of Inflows** | **1,16,986.15** |

Therefore:

$$\text{NPV}_{10\%} = -1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}16{,}986.15 = +16{,}986.15$$

Since NPV at 10% is **positive**, the true IRR is **greater than 10%**. We need to try a higher rate.

### 🧮 Step 4 — Second Trial at $r = 15\%$

| Year (t) | Cash Flow (₹) | Discount Factor $\dfrac{1}{(1.15)^t}$ | Present Value (₹) |
|----------|---------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 30,000 | 0.8696 | 26,086.96 |
| 2 | 35,000 | 0.7561 | 26,465.03 |
| 3 | 40,000 | 0.6575 | 26,300.36 |
| 4 | 45,000 | 0.5718 | 25,728.85 |
| | | **Total PV of Inflows** | **1,04,581.20** |

Therefore:

$$\text{NPV}_{15\%} = -1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}04{,}581.20 = +4{,}581.20$$

NPV is still **positive**, so the IRR is **still greater than 15%**. Try a higher rate.

### 🧮 Step 5 — Third Trial at $r = 18\%$

| Year (t) | Cash Flow (₹) | Discount Factor $\dfrac{1}{(1.18)^t}$ | Present Value (₹) |
|----------|---------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 30,000 | 0.8475 | 25,423.73 |
| 2 | 35,000 | 0.7182 | 25,136.45 |
| 3 | 40,000 | 0.6086 | 24,345.20 |
| 4 | 45,000 | 0.5158 | 23,209.72 |
| | | **Total PV of Inflows** | **98,115.10** |

Therefore:

$$\text{NPV}_{18\%} = -1{,}00{,}000 + 98{,}115.10 = -1{,}884.90$$

NPV at 18% is **negative**, which means the IRR lies **between 15% and 18%**.

### 🧮 Step 6 — Apply Linear Interpolation

We have:
- $r_L = 15\%$, $\text{NPV}_L = +4{,}581.20$ (positive)
- $r_H = 18\%$, $\text{NPV}_H = -1{,}884.90$ (negative)

Substituting into the interpolation formula:

$$\text{IRR} = r_L + \left[ \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \right] \times (r_H - r_L)$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15\% + \left[ \frac{4{,}581.20}{4{,}581.20 - (-1{,}884.90)} \right] \times (18\% - 15\%)$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15\% + \left[ \frac{4{,}581.20}{4{,}581.20 + 1{,}884.90} \right] \times 3\%$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15\% + \left[ \frac{4{,}581.20}{6{,}466.10} \right] \times 3\%$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15\% + (0.7085) \times 3\%$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15\% + 2.1255\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{IRR} = 17.13\% \text{ (approximately)}}$$

### 🧮 Step 7 — Apply the Decision Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison:** $\text{IRR} = 17.13\%$ vs. Cost of Capital $= 10\%$
>
> Since **IRR (17.13%) > Cost of Capital (10%)**, the project **SHOULD BE ACCEPTED** ✅.
>
> The project earns a return of 17.13% per annum, which is **7.13 percentage points higher** than the company's minimum required return of 10%.

## 3.2 Summary of the Analytical Trail

> [!NOTE]
> **📊 Quick Analytical Matrix for the Numerical Example**

| Trial Rate (r) | NPV (₹) | Sign of NPV | Implication |
|----------------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 10% | +16,986.15 | Positive (+ve) | IRR > 10% |
| 15% | +4,581.20 | Positive (+ve) | IRR > 15% |
| 18% | -1,884.90 | Negative (-ve) | IRR < 18% |
| **17.13% (Interpolated)** | ≈ 0 | ~ Zero | **True IRR** |

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation of the Interpolation Formula

The linear interpolation method assumes that the NPV function behaves approximately linearly between $r_L$ and $r_H$. The line connecting the two points $(r_L, \text{NPV}_L)$ and $(r_H, \text{NPV}_H)$ crosses the x-axis (NPV = 0) at the IRR.

By the two-point form of a straight line:

$$\frac{\text{NPV} - \text{NPV}_L}{r - r_L} = \frac{\text{NPV}_H - \text{NPV}_L}{r_H - r_L}$$

Setting NPV = 0 (which corresponds to the IRR):

$$\frac{0 - \text{NPV}_L}{\text{IRR} - r_L} = \frac{\text{NPV}_H - \text{NPV}_L}{r_H - r_L}$$

Solving for IRR:

$$-\text{NPV}_L \cdot (r_H - r_L) = (\text{NPV}_H - \text{NPV}_L) \cdot (\text{IRR} - r_L)$$

$$\text{IRR} - r_L = \frac{-\text{NPV}_L \cdot (r_H - r_L)}{\text{NPV}_H - \text{NPV}_L}$$

$$\text{IRR} - r_L = \frac{\text{NPV}_L \cdot (r_H - r_L)}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{IRR} = r_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (r_H - r_L)}$$

## 3.4 Limitations of IRR (Important for KTU Answers)

1. **Multiple IRR Problem:** For non-conventional cash flows (multiple sign changes), there can be more than one IRR, leading to ambiguity.
2. **No Real IRR:** If the NPV never crosses zero, the IRR does not exist mathematically.
3. **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** IRR can give misleading rankings when project scales differ. **NPV is preferred for mutually exclusive projects**.
4. **Reinvestment Assumption:** IRR implicitly assumes reinvestment of cash flows at the IRR itself, which may be unrealistic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — IRR Computation Process

The following flowchart depicts the logical steps involved in computing the IRR of a project:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Project Identification] --> B[Gather All Cash Flows CF0 to CFn]
    B --> C[Set Up NPV Equation with r as Unknown]
    C --> D[Choose Initial Trial Rate r1]
    D --> E[Compute NPV at r1]
    E --> F{NPV equals Zero?}
    F -- YES --> G[IRRs is r1 - DONE]
    F -- NO --> H{NPV is Positive?}
    H -- YES --> I[Increase Trial Rate to r2 greater than r1]
    H -- NO --> J[Decrease Trial Rate to r2 less than r1]
    I --> K[Compute NPV at r2]
    J --> K
    K --> L{NPV crosses Zero between r1 and r2?}
    L -- NO --> M[Choose New Trial Rate and Repeat]
    M --> E
    L -- YES --> N[Apply Linear Interpolation Formula]
    N --> O[Compute Precise IRR Estimate]
    O --> P{IRR greater than Cost of Capital k?}
    P -- YES --> Q[ACCEPT the Project]
    P -- NO --> R[REJECT the Project]
    Q --> S[END]
    R --> S
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — NPV Profile and IRR Concept

The following diagram illustrates the relationship between discount rate and NPV, showing where the IRR lies:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AXIS[Coordinate System]
        X[Horizontal Axis: Discount Rate r percent]
        Y[Vertical Axis: Net Present Value NPV in Rupees]
    end
    AXIS --> CURVE
    CURVE[Convex NPV Curve Decreasing with r] --- POINT1
    POINT1[At r equals 0 percent NPV equals Sum of All Cash Flows] --- POINT2
    POINT2[At r equals IRR NPV equals 0 BREAK EVEN POINT] --- POINT3
    POINT3[At r greater than IRR NPV is Negative Zone] --- POINT4
    POINT4[At Cost of Capital k NPV indicates Accept or Reject]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IRR vs NPV Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Cash Flow Data Input] --> PROCESS1[Project Evaluation Engine]
    PROCESS1 --> NPV_MOD[NPV Calculation Module]
    PROCESS1 --> IRR_MOD[IRR Calculation Module]
    NPV_MOD --> NPV_OUT[NPV Output]
    IRR_MOD --> IRR_OUT[IRR Output]
    NPV_OUT --> DECISION[Decision Matrix Module]
    IRR_OUT --> DECISION
    COST_CAP[Cost of Capital Input k] --> DECISION
    DECISION --> OUT1[If NPV greater than 0 ACCEPT]
    DECISION --> OUT2[If NPV less than 0 REJECT]
    DECISION --> OUT3[If IRR greater than k ACCEPT]
    DECISION --> OUT4[If IRR less than k REJECT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A1: Define Internal Rate of Return (IRR). Mention its decision criterion.

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value (NPV) of a project becomes zero. Equivalently, it is the rate of return that equates the present value of expected future cash inflows with the present value of cash outflows (initial investment). It is the project's "break-even rate of return."

**Decision Criterion (1 Mark):**
- If **IRR > Cost of Capital** → Accept the project.
- If **IRR < Cost of Capital** → Reject the project.

---

### ❓ Question A2: Differentiate between NPV and IRR.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Parameter | NPV | IRR |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Definition** | Absolute money value added by the project | Percentage rate of return earned by the project |
| **Unit** | Measured in monetary units (₹) | Measured in percentage (%) |
| **Discount Rate** | Requires pre-specified discount rate | Computed internally; does not require pre-specified rate |
| **Decision Rule** | Accept if NPV > 0 | Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital |
| **Reinvestment Assumption** | Assumes reinvestment at cost of capital | Assumes reinvestment at IRR itself |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> A company is considering two projects, **Project P** and **Project Q**, each requiring an initial investment of **₹2,00,000**. The expected cash inflows are as follows:

| Year | Project P (₹) | Project Q (₹) |
|------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | 60,000 | 80,000 |
| 2 | 70,000 | 75,000 |
| 3 | 80,000 | 70,000 |
| 4 | 90,000 | 65,000 |

> The cost of capital is **12%**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the IRR of each project using the trial-and-error method. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Which project should be selected based on IRR? Justify. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Solution A(a): IRR Computation (7 Marks)

**Project P — Cash Flows:** -2,00,000; 60,000; 70,000; 80,000; 90,000

**Trial 1 at r = 15%:**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF @ 15% | PV (₹) |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 60,000 | 0.8696 | 52,173.91 |
| 2 | 70,000 | 0.7561 | 52,930.05 |
| 3 | 80,000 | 0.6575 | 52,600.73 |
| 4 | 90,000 | 0.5718 | 51,457.72 |
| | | **Total** | **2,09,162.41** |

$$\text{NPV}_{15\%} = -2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}09{,}162.41 = +9{,}162.41 \quad (\text{Positive})$$

So IRR > 15%. Try a higher rate.

**Trial 2 at r = 18%:**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF @ 18% | PV (₹) |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 60,000 | 0.8475 | 50,847.46 |
| 2 | 70,000 | 0.7182 | 50,272.91 |
| 3 | 80,000 | 0.6086 | 48,690.40 |
| 4 | 90,000 | 0.5158 | 46,419.43 |
| | | **Total** | **1,96,230.20** |

$$\text{NPV}_{18\%} = -2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}96{,}230.20 = -3{,}769.80 \quad (\text{Negative})$$

**Interpolation for Project P:**

$$\text{IRR}_P = 15\% + \left[ \frac{9{,}162.41}{9{,}162.41 - (-3{,}769.80)} \right] \times (18\% - 15\%)$$

$$\text{IRR}_P = 15\% + \left[ \frac{9{,}162.41}{12{,}932.21} \right] \times 3\%$$

$$\text{IRR}_P = 15\% + (0.7085) \times 3\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{IRR}_P \approx 17.13\%}$$

**Project Q — Cash Flows:** -2,00,000; 80,000; 75,000; 70,000; 65,000

**Trial 1 at r = 25%:**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF @ 25% | PV (₹) |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 80,000 | 0.8000 | 64,000.00 |
| 2 | 75,000 | 0.6400 | 48,000.00 |
| 3 | 70,000 | 0.5120 | 35,840.00 |
| 4 | 65,000 | 0.4096 | 26,624.00 |
| | | **Total** | **1,74,464.00** |

$$\text{NPV}_{25\%} = -2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}74{,}464.00 = -25{,}536.00 \quad (\text{Negative})$$

**Trial 2 at r = 15%:**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF @ 15% | PV (₹) |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 80,000 | 0.8696 | 69,565.22 |
| 2 | 75,000 | 0.7561 | 56,710.53 |
| 3 | 70,000 | 0.6575 | 46,025.70 |
| 4 | 65,000 | 0.5718 | 37,163.80 |
| | | **Total** | **2,09,465.25** |

$$\text{NPV}_{15\%} = -2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}09{,}465.25 = +9{,}465.25 \quad (\text{Positive})$$

**Interpolation for Project Q:**

$$\text{IRR}_Q = 15\% + \left[ \frac{9{,}465.25}{9{,}465.25 - (-25{,}536.00)} \right] \times (25\% - 15\%)$$

$$\text{IRR}_Q = 15\% + \left[ \frac{9{,}465.25}{35{,}001.25} \right] \times 10\%$$

$$\text{IRR}_Q = 15\% + (0.2704) \times 10\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{IRR}_Q \approx 17.70\%}$$

---

#### ✅ Solution A(b): Project Selection (7 Marks)

| Project | IRR | Cost of Capital (k) | NPV @ 12% |
|---------|-----|---------------------|-----------|
| P | 17.13% | 12% | (computed below) |
| Q | 17.70% | 12% | (computed below) |

**Computation of NPV at Cost of Capital (k = 12%) for Both Projects:**

**Project P NPV @ 12%:**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF @ 12% | PV (₹) |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 60,000 | 0.8929 | 53,571.43 |
| 2 | 70,000 | 0.7972 | 55,803.78 |
| 3 | 80,000 | 0.7118 | 56,942.63 |
| 4 | 90,000 | 0.6355 | 57,196.17 |
| | | **Total** | **2,23,514.01** |

$$\text{NPV}_P = -2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}23{,}514.01 = +23{,}514.01$$

**Project Q NPV @ 12%:**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF @ 12% | PV (₹) |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 80,000 | 0.8929 | 71,428.57 |
| 2 | 75,000 | 0.7972 | 59,789.50 |
| 3 | 70,000 | 0.7118 | 49,824.79 |
| 4 | 65,000 | 0.6355 | 41,308.33 |
| | | **Total** | **2,22,351.19** |
