$$\text{NPV}_Q = -2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}22{,}351.19 = +22{,}351.19$$

**Decision Summary:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Selection Analysis:**
>
> - **By IRR:** Project Q (17.70%) > Project P (17.13%) → **Select Project Q**
> - **By NPV:** Project P (₹23,514.01) > Project Q (₹22,351.19) → **Select Project P**
>
> **Conflict Resolution:** Since both projects have **equal initial investment**, both IRR and NPV give slightly conflicting signals. The difference is small. However, the **NPV method is the superior criterion** for project selection in capital budgeting because:
> 1. NPV measures absolute value addition to the firm in monetary terms.
> 2. NPV assumes reinvestment at the more realistic cost of capital.
> 3. IRR assumes reinvestment at the IRR, which can be overstated.
>
> **Final Recommendation:** Select **Project P** (or both, if capital permits, since both have IRR > 12% and NPV > 0).

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> A firm is evaluating a new machine costing **₹5,00,000**. The machine is expected to generate annual cash inflows of **₹1,50,000** for **6 years**. The salvage value at the end of year 6 is **₹50,000**. The cost of capital is **14%**.
>
> **(a)** Compute the IRR of the proposed investment. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Should the machine be purchased? Justify your answer using the IRR criterion. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Solution B(a): IRR Computation (7 Marks)

**Cash Flow Pattern:**
- $CF_0 = -5{,}00{,}000$ (Initial Investment)
- $CF_1 = CF_2 = \ldots = CF_6 = 1{,}50{,}000$ (Annual Inflow)
- $CF_6 = 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000$ (Year 6 includes salvage)

**Set Up the Equation:**

$$\text{NPV} = -5{,}00{,}000 + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{(1+r)} + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{(1+r)^2} + \ldots + \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1+r)^6} = 0$$

**Trial 1 at r = 20%:**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF @ 20% | PV (₹) |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 0.8333 | 1,25,000.00 |
| 2 | 1,50,000 | 0.6944 | 1,04,166.67 |
| 3 | 1,50,000 | 0.5787 | 86,805.56 |
| 4 | 1,50,000 | 0.4823 | 72,338.00 |
| 5 | 1,50,000 | 0.4019 | 60,281.66 |
| 6 | 2,00,000 | 0.3349 | 66,979.62 |
| | | **Total** | **5,15,571.51** |

$$\text{NPV}_{20\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}15{,}571.51 = +15{,}571.51 \quad (\text{Positive})$$

**Trial 2 at r = 22%:**

| Year | CF (₹) | DF @ 22% | PV (₹) |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 0.8197 | 1,22,950.82 |
| 2 | 1,50,000 | 0.6719 | 1,00,779.36 |
| 3 | 1,50,000 | 0.5507 | 82,606.85 |
| 4 | 1,50,000 | 0.4514 | 67,710.53 |
| 5 | 1,50,000 | 0.3700 | 55,500.44 |
| 6 | 2,00,000 | 0.3033 | 60,665.94 |
| | | **Total** | **4,90,213.94** |

$$\text{NPV}_{22\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}90{,}213.94 = -9{,}786.06 \quad (\text{Negative})$$

**Linear Interpolation:**

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + \left[ \frac{15{,}571.51}{15{,}571.51 - (-9{,}786.06)} \right] \times (22\% - 20\%)$$

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + \left[ \frac{15{,}571.51}{25{,}357.57} \right] \times 2\%$$

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + (0.6141) \times 2\%$$

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + 1.2282\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{IRR} \approx 21.23\%}$$

---

#### ✅ Solution B(b): Decision Justification (7 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Analysis:**
>
> - Computed IRR ≈ **21.23%**
> - Cost of Capital ($k$) = **14%**
> - Since **IRR (21.23%) > Cost of Capital (14%)** → **ACCEPT** the project.
>
> **Justification (3 key points):**
> 1. The machine generates an annual return of 21.23%, which exceeds the minimum required return of 14% by **7.23 percentage points**.
> 2. The project creates **positive value for the firm** because the actual earning rate exceeds the financing cost.
> 3. The NPV at 14% would be **positive** (since IRR > 14%), confirming wealth maximization.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Salvage Value Omission (–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to add the salvage value of ₹50,000 to the Year 6 cash flow. This leads to an incorrect IRR. **Always include salvage in the final year cash flow.**
> 2. **Interpolation Sign Error (–2 marks):** A very common mistake is to compute $(\text{NPV}_H - \text{NPV}_L)$ instead of $(\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H)$ in the denominator. This yields a negative IRR. The correct denominator is always $\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H$.
> 3. **Discount Factor Precision (–1 mark):** Using rounded discount factors (e.g., 0.83 instead of 0.8333) introduces errors. Carry at least 4 decimal places.
> 4. **Wrong Decision Rule (–1 mark):** Some students compare IRR to the wrong benchmark (e.g., bank deposit rate instead of cost of capital). Always use **cost of capital** as the benchmark.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Definition:** IRR is the discount rate at which **NPV = 0**.
- ✅ **Equation:** $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} = 0$
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** Accept if **IRR > Cost of Capital (k)**; Reject if **IRR < k**.
- ✅ **Computation Method:** **Trial and Error** followed by **Linear Interpolation**.
- ✅ **Interpolation Formula:** $\text{IRR} = r_L + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$
- ✅ **NPV must have opposite signs** at $r_L$ (positive) and $r_H$ (negative) for interpolation to work.
- ✅ **IRR is a percentage**, not an absolute rupee value — so do not add it to cash flows.
- ✅ **For mutually exclusive projects** with equal investment, NPV is preferred over IRR for ranking.
- ✅ **MIRR** (Modified IRR) overcomes the multiple IRR problem and reinvestment assumption issues.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Multiple IRRs (non-conventional cash flows), unrealistic reinvestment assumption, scale bias.
- ✅ **Common Examiner Trap:** Forgetting to include **salvage value** in the terminal year's cash flow.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Initial investment is **negative**; future cash inflows are **positive**.
- ✅ **NPV Profile:** The graph of NPV vs. discount rate is **downward-sloping and convex** for conventional projects.
- ✅ **Break-Even Point:** The point where the NPV profile crosses the x-axis is the **IRR**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Decision Making Using Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR) Analysis of Public Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Economic Decision Making Using Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR) Analysis of Public Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR)** is the discount rate at which the *net present value of the incremental cash flow* between two mutually exclusive alternatives becomes exactly **zero**. It mathematically represents the percentage yield earned on the **extra (additional) capital invested** when one moves from a lower-cost alternative to a higher-cost alternative.

For **Public Projects**, the IRoR analysis is adapted using:
- A **Social Discount Rate (SDR)** in place of the commercial MARR.
- Inclusion of **External Benefits (Positive Externalities)** and **External Costs (Negative Externalities)**.
- A baseline comparison with the **"Do-Nothing" alternative** (since public projects are mandated service-delivery obligations, not profit-seeking ventures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For revenue-generating public projects (e.g., toll roads, electricity distribution), the alternatives are compared against each other using IRoR. For non-revenue service projects (e.g., public parks, rural roads, street lighting), the IRoR is computed against the *Do-Nothing* baseline.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are choosing between two public transport options for a city:

- **Option A:** Standard diesel buses — cheaper to buy, but higher long-term fuel cost and more pollution.
- **Option B:** Electric buses — costlier upfront, but cheaper to operate, quieter, and emit no local pollution.

The **question** is: *Is the extra money we spend on Option B "earning" a good enough return compared to Option A?*

If the city invests an **extra ₹50 crore** in electric buses and in return gets fuel savings, maintenance savings, *and* social benefits (cleaner air, lower health costs), we calculate the **percentage return on that extra ₹50 crore**. That percentage is the **Incremental Rate of Return (B over A)**.

If the IRoR ≥ Social Discount Rate (say, 9%), the extra investment is justified. Otherwise, stick with the cheaper bus option.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Rule of Thumb:** IRoR answers the question — *"For every extra rupee I invest in the costlier option, am I getting back at least the social discount rate worth of value?"*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in KTU Valuation

- **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return):** Commercial benchmark, typically **10%–15%** in Indian engineering economy problems.
- **Social Discount Rate (SDR):** Lower than MARR, typically **8%–12%**, used to reflect society's time preference for public money.
- **Study Period (n):** Common values are **5, 10, 15, 20, 25, or 30 years**.
- **Salvage Value (SV):** Estimated terminal worth at the end of the study period.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why IRoR Analysis is Used in Public Projects

Public sector projects are characterized by:

1. **Mixed Cash Flows** — Often there is no direct commercial revenue (e.g., a flood-control dam), but enormous social benefits.
2. **Multiple, Often Intangible, Benefits** — Reduced travel time, improved health, environmental quality, national security.
3. **Mandated Service Delivery** — Government cannot simply "do nothing"; comparison is with a *minimum service standard*.
4. **Capital Rationing** — Public budgets are constrained, so every rupee must justify itself against social opportunity cost.
5. **Long Gestation Periods** — Many public projects have lifetimes of 20–50 years or more.

Given these realities, **NPV alone is insufficient** when comparing alternatives because all public projects might have positive NPV (good for society), but we still need to pick the *best* one when budgets are tight. **IRoR resolves this ranking problem.**

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of IRoR Analysis

**Step 1: Verify Mutual Exclusivity**
Alternatives must be mutually exclusive (only one can be chosen). If independent, simple ROR ranking is used.

**Step 2: Establish the "Defender" and "Challenger"**
- The **Defender** is the current best alternative (initially, the lowest initial cost option that meets minimum service requirements).
- The **Challenger** is the higher-cost alternative being tested.

**Step 3: Compute Incremental Cash Flows (B − A)**
The incremental cash flow is computed as:

$$\text{Incremental CF}_t = \text{CF}_t(\text{Challenger}) - \text{CF}_t(\text{Defender})$$

For public projects, this includes:

- Incremental Capital Cost
- Incremental O&M Cost
- Incremental Revenue (if any)
- Incremental Social Benefit (often estimated via *willingness-to-pay* surveys or *shadow pricing*)

**Step 4: Solve the IRoR Equation**

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \text{Incremental CF}_t \cdot (1 + \text{IRoR})^{-t} = 0$$

**Step 5: Decision Rule**

| Condition | Decision |
|---|---|
| $\text{IRoR} \geq i_{\text{SDR}}$ (or MARR) | **Adopt the Challenger** (extra investment is justified) |
| $\text{IRoR} < i_{\text{SDR}}$ (or MARR) | **Reject the Challenger**, retain the Defender |

**Step 6: Repeat for the Next Pair**
Once the Challenger is accepted, it becomes the new Defender. Test the next higher-cost alternative against it. Continue until all alternatives are ranked.

### 2.3 Special Considerations for Public Projects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shadow Pricing:** Since market prices for public goods may be distorted (subsidies, taxes, monopoly), economists often use *shadow prices* that reflect the true social opportunity cost. For KTU problems, this is usually given directly in the problem statement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Externalities:** Add quantifiable external benefits (e.g., reduced healthcare costs from cleaner air) and subtract external costs (e.g., noise, displacement). The IRoR is then computed on the *socially adjusted* incremental cash flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Do-Nothing Alternative:** If the only feasible comparison for a non-revenue project is "Do-Nothing," the IRoR is meaningless (no revenue stream). In such cases, KTU examiners expect the use of **BCR (Benefit-Cost Ratio)** with values: BCR > 1 ⇒ Accept.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Application / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Incremental Cash Flow | $\Delta \text{CF}_t = \text{CF}_t^{B} - \text{CF}_t^{A}$ | Always computed Challenger − Defender |
| IRoR Equation | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \Delta \text{CF}_t \cdot (1+i)^{-t} = 0$ | Roots found by trial or interpolation |
| Linear Interpolation for IRoR | $i^{*} = i_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H}(i_H - i_L)$ | $i_L, i_H$ are bracketing rates |
| Present Worth Factor (P/A) | $(P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Uniform series present worth |
| Present Worth Factor (P/F) | $(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}$ | Single future amount present worth |
| Capital Recovery (A/P) | $(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Converting P to A |
| Sinking Fund (A/F) | $(A/F, i, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Converting F to A |
| BCR (Public Project) | $\text{BCR} = \frac{\sum \text{PV of Benefits}}{\sum \text{PV of Costs}}$ | BCR ≥ 1 ⇒ Accept |
| Modified BCR | $\text{MBCR} = \frac{\text{PV of Benefits} - \text{PV of O\&M}}{\text{PV of Capital Cost}}$ | Alternative public-project metric |
| NPV Criterion | $\text{NPV} = \sum \text{CF}_t (1+i)^{-t}$ | Maximize NPV at $i_{\text{SDR}}$ |
| Decision Rule (IRoR) | $\text{IRoR} \geq i_{\text{SDR}} \Rightarrow$ Choose Challenger | Standard public-project rule |
| Social Discount Rate | $i_{\text{SDR}} \in [8\%, 12\%]$ | Typically lower than MARR |
| Incremental BCR | $\Delta \text{BCR} = \frac{\Delta \text{Benefits}}{\Delta \text{Costs}}$ | Used to rank BCRs of alternatives |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Public-Project Application |
|---|---|
| Civil / Transportation | Highway vs. railway vs. metro corridor for a city |
| Electrical / Energy | Hydro vs. thermal vs. solar for a state grid |
| Environmental | Sewage treatment plant sizing alternatives |
| Water Resources | Dam vs. barrage vs. canal for irrigation |
| Urban Planning | Flyover vs. underpass vs. roundabout |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The IRoR Derivation — From First Principles

The IRoR is defined as the discount rate $i^{*}$ that equates the present worth of incremental benefits to the present worth of incremental costs.

**Setting Up the Fundamental Equation:**

The present worth of the Challenger's cash flow stream is:

$$\text{PW}_B = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \text{CF}_t^{B} \cdot (1+i)^{-t}$$

Similarly, the present worth of the Defender's cash flow stream is:

$$\text{PW}_A = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \text{CF}_t^{A} \cdot (1+i)^{-t}$$

The **incremental present worth** of choosing B over A is:

$$\Delta \text{PW} = \text{PW}_B - \text{PW}_A = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \left(\text{CF}_t^{B} - \text{CF}_t^{A}\right) \cdot (1+i)^{-t}$$

By definition, the IRoR is the value of $i$ that makes this incremental present worth equal to zero:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \left(\text{CF}_t^{B} - \text{CF}_t^{A}\right) \cdot (1+i^{*})^{-t} = 0$$

This is a polynomial equation in $(1+i^{*})$ of degree $n$. It can yield multiple real roots (Descartes' rule of signs). For public projects with conventional incremental cash flows (negative initial investment, then positive operating savings/benefits), there is exactly one real positive root, which is the IRoR we seek.

### 3.2 Solved Analytical Example — Public Bridge Project

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical Style):**

A municipal corporation must choose between two bridge designs to span a river. Both have a study period of **20 years** with no salvage value. The Social Discount Rate is **10%**.

| Parameter | Design X (Defender) | Design Y (Challenger) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment | ₹ 2,00,00,000 | ₹ 3,50,00,000 |
| Annual O&M Cost | ₹ 8,00,000 | ₹ 5,00,000 |
| Annual Social Benefit (reduced congestion, accidents) | ₹ 30,00,000 | ₹ 42,00,000 |
| Annual Revenue (toll) | ₹ 15,00,000 | ₹ 18,00,000 |

Determine the IRoR (Y over X) and recommend the choice.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute Incremental Cash Flows (Y − X)**

| Year | Item | Design Y (₹) | Design X (₹) | Incremental (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial Investment | −3,50,00,000 | −2,00,00,000 | **−1,50,00,000** |
| 1–20 | O&M Cost | −5,00,000 | −8,00,000 | **+3,00,000** (savings) |
| 1–20 | Social Benefit | +42,00,000 | +30,00,000 | **+12,00,000** |
| 1–20 | Toll Revenue | +18,00,000 | +15,00,000 | **+3,00,000** |
| **Net Annual Incremental Benefit (Years 1–20)** | | | | **+18,00,000** |

**Solution — Step 2: Set Up the IRoR Equation**

We need $i^{*}$ such that:

$$-1,50,00,000 + 18,00,000 \cdot (P/A, i^{*}, 20) = 0$$

Solving for the annuity factor:

$$(P/A, i^{*}, 20) = \frac{1,50,00,000}{18,00,000} = 8.3333$$

**Solution — Step 3: Find $i^{*}$ from Standard Tables**

From the interest tables:

- At $i = 8\%$: $(P/A, 8\%, 20) = 9.8181$
- At $i = 10\%$: $(P/A, 10\%, 20) = 8.5136$
- At $i = 11\%$: $(P/A, 11\%, 20) = 7.9633$
- At $i = 12\%$: $(P/A, 12\%, 20) = 7.4694$

The target value $8.3333$ lies between $10\%$ and $11\%$.

**Solution — Step 4: Linear Interpolation**

$$i^{*} = 10\% + \frac{8.5136 - 8.3333}{8.5136 - 7.9633} \cdot (11\% - 10\%)$$

Let me compute this carefully:

$$i^{*} = 10\% + \frac{0.1803}{0.5503} \cdot 1\%$$

$$i^{*} = 10\% + 0.3276 \cdot 1\%$$

$$i^{*} = 10.33\%$$

**Solution — Step 5: Apply the Decision Rule**

Since $\text{IRoR} = 10.33\% \geq i_{\text{SDR}} = 10\%$, the extra investment in Design Y is justified.

**Recommendation:** The municipal corporation should adopt **Design Y**.

### 3.3 Re-Investment Rate Assumption (MIRR Correction)

A common **valuation pitfall** in IRoR analysis is the implicit assumption that intermediate cash flows are reinvested at the IRoR itself. For public projects, this is rarely realistic.

The **Modified IRoR (MIRR)** addresses this by assuming reinvestment at the Social Discount Rate:

$$\text{MIRR} = \left( \frac{\text{FV of positive incremental CFs at } i_{\text{SDR}}}{\vert \text{PV of negative incremental CFs at } i_{\text{SDR}} \vert} \right)^{1/n} - 1$$

For the bridge example:

$$\text{FV of positive flows} = 18,00,000 \cdot \frac{(1.10)^{20} - 1}{0.10} = 18,00,000 \cdot 57.2750 = ₹ 10,30,95,000$$

$$\text{MIRR} = \left( \frac{10,30,95,000}{1,50,00,000} \right)^{1/20} - 1 = (6.873)^{0.05} - 1$$

$$= e^{0.05 \cdot \ln(6.873)} - 1 = e^{0.05 \cdot 1.9275} - 1 = e^{0.0964} - 1 \approx 0.1012$$

$$\text{MIRR} \approx 10.12\%$$

This is more conservative than the conventional IRoR (10.33%) but still exceeds the SDR of 10%, so the recommendation is unchanged.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix for Incremental Comparison

| Rank | Alternative | Initial Cost | IRoR vs. Prev. | IRoR vs. MARR/SDR | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Do-Nothing | 0 | — | — | Baseline (non-revenue only) |
| 2 | A | Lowest positive | IRoR(A vs. Do-Nothing) = 12% | > 10% | Accept as current Defender |
| 3 | B | Mid | IRoR(B vs. A) = 10.33% | > 10% | Accept as new Defender |
| 4 | C | Highest | IRoR(C vs. B) = 8.7% | < 10% | Reject; retain B |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IRoR Decision Flow for Public Projects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: List of Mutually Exclusive Public Alternatives] --> B[Step 1: Rank by Initial Investment - Lowest to Highest]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify Defender = Lowest Cost Feasible Option]
    C --> D[Step 3: Identify Challenger = Next Higher-Cost Option]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute Incremental Cash Flows Challenger minus Defender]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute Incremental Social Benefits and Externalities]
    F --> G[Step 6: Solve IRoR Equation by Trial or Interpolation]
    G --> H{IRoR is greater than or equal to Social Discount Rate}
    H -- Yes --> I[Accept Challenger as New Defender]
    I --> J{Any Higher Cost Alternative Remaining}
    H -- No --> K[Reject Challenger Keep Current Defender]
    K --> J
    J -- Yes --> D
    J -- No --> L[Final Defender is the Selected Public Project]
    L --> M[End]
```

### 4.2 Public Project Evaluation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Public Project Evaluation Framework] --> P2[Step A: Define Service Objective]
    P2 --> P3[Step B: Identify All Feasible Alternatives including Do-Nothing]
    P3 --> P4[Step C: Estimate Social Discount Rate]
    P4 --> P5[Step D: Forecast Cash Flows over Study Period]
    P5 --> P6[Step E: Apply Shadow Pricing to Market Distortions]
    P6 --> P7[Step F: Quantify Externalities]
    P7 --> P8[Step G: Apply Decision Metric]
    P8 --> P9[Metric 1: NPV at Social Discount Rate]
    P8 --> P10[Metric 2: BCR or Incremental BCR]
    P8 --> P11[Metric 3: Incremental Rate of Return IRoR]
    P9 --> P12[Rank Alternatives]
    P10 --> P12
    P11 --> P12
    P12 --> P13[Select Highest Ranked Feasible Alternative]
```

### 4.3 Cash Flow Pattern for a Public Project (Incremental B over A)

```mermaid
graph LR
    CF0[Year 0: Negative Incremental Capital Cost] --> CF1[Year 1 to n: Net Positive Incremental Annual Benefit]
    CF1 --> CF2[Operating Savings plus Revenue plus Social Benefit]
    CF2 --> CF3[Subtract Incremental O and M Cost]
    CF3 --> CF4[Equals Net Incremental Cash Flow used in IRoR]
    CF4 --> IRR[Solve for IRoR where PV of Net Incremental CF equals Zero]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Public IRoR Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BLK1[Input Module: Alternatives, Cash Flows, Externalities, SDR] --> BLK2[Processing Module: Incremental CF Computation and IRoR Solver]
    BLK2 --> BLK3[Decision Module: Compare IRoR with SDR and Rank]
    BLK3 --> BLK4[Output Module: Recommended Alternative with Sensitivity Report]
    BLK4 --> BLK5[Policy Module: Integration with Multi-Attribute Criteria]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR). Why is it preferred over simple ROR when comparing mutually exclusive public projects?*

**Model Answer:**
The Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR) is the discount rate at which the net present value of the incremental cash flow between two mutually exclusive alternatives becomes zero. It measures the rate of return earned on the **extra (additional) capital** invested when moving from a lower-cost option to a higher-cost option.

It is preferred over simple ROR for mutually exclusive public projects because:

1. **ROR ignores scale of investment** — A small project with high ROR may involve less total social benefit than a large project with moderate ROR.
2. **Public budgets are capital-rationed** — Decision-makers must know whether the *extra* investment is socially productive, not just whether the whole project is productive.
3. **Mutually exclusive ranking requires incremental analysis** — Otherwise, the chosen alternative may not maximize total social welfare.
4. **Aligns with social opportunity cost** — Compares the extra return to the Social Discount Rate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between the Social Discount Rate (SDR) and the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR). State typical values used in Indian public-project evaluation.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MARR | Social Discount Rate (SDR) |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Minimum return acceptable to a private investor | Time-value preference of society for public funds |
| Used For | Private/commercial projects | Public/government projects |
| Reflects | Opportunity cost of private capital | Social opportunity cost (often lower) |
| Typical Indian Range | 12% – 18% | 8% – 12% |
| Source of Estimate | Market interest rates, risk premium | Treasury rate adjusted for social preferences |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern*

A state government must choose between three irrigation canal systems (X, Y, Z) to serve a drought-prone district. The study period is **25 years**, the **Social Discount Rate is 9%**, and the salvage values are negligible. The data are:

| Parameter | System X | System Y | System Z |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment (₹ Crore) | 10 | 16 | 22 |
| Annual O&M Cost (₹ Lakh) | 50 | 40 | 30 |
| Annual Irrigation Revenue (₹ Lakh) | 200 | 240 | 280 |
| Annual Social Benefit (₹ Lakh) | 100 | 200 | 350 |

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the IRoR for (Y over X) and (Z over Y). Recommend the best system.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Repeat the analysis using the Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) method and comment on whether the two methods agree.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — IRoR Analysis**

*Step 1: Incremental Cash Flow (Y − X)*

| Year | Item | Y (₹ Lakh) | X (₹ Lakh) | Incremental |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Capital | −1,600 | −1,000 | **−600** |
| 1–25 | O&M | −40 | −50 | **+10** (saving) |
| 1–25 | Revenue | +240 | +200 | **+40** |
| 1–25 | Social Benefit | +200 | +100 | **+100** |
| **Net Annual Incremental Benefit (1–25)** | | | | **+150** |

IRoR equation:

$$-600 + 150 \cdot (P/A, i^{*}, 25) = 0$$

$$(P/A, i^{*}, 25) = 4.00$$

From tables: at $i = 24\%$, $(P/A, 24\%, 25) = 4.1468$; at $i = 25\%$, $(P/A, 25\%, 25) = 3.9849$.

Interpolation:

$$i^{*}_{Y-X} = 24\% + \frac{4.1468 - 4.00}{4.1468 - 3.9849} \cdot 1\% = 24\% + 0.908\% = 24.91\%$$

Since $24.91\% \gg 9\%$ (SDR), **adopt Y as the new defender**.

*Step 2: Incremental Cash Flow (Z − Y)*

| Year | Item | Z (₹ Lakh) | Y (₹ Lakh) | Incremental |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Capital | −2,200 | −1,600 | **−600** |
| 1–25 | O&M | −30 | −40 | **+10** |
| 1–25 | Revenue | +280 | +240 | **+40** |
| 1–25 | Social Benefit | +350 | +200 | **+150** |
| **Net Annual Incremental Benefit (1–25)** | | | | **+200** |

IRoR equation:

$$-600 + 200 \cdot (P/A, i^{*}, 25) = 0 \Rightarrow (P/A, i^{*}, 25) = 3.00$$

At $i = 33\%$, $(P/A, 33\%, 25) = 3.0283$; at $i = 34\%$, $(P/A, 34\%, 25) = 2.9525$.

Interpolation:

$$i^{*}_{Z-Y} = 33\% + \frac{3.0283 - 3.00}{3.0283 - 2.9525} \cdot 1\% = 33\% + 0.375\% = 33.38\%$$

Since $33.38\% \gg 9\%$, **adopt Z as the final defender**.

**Final Recommendation: System Z is the most economically justified choice.**

**Part (b) — BCR Analysis**

BCR (using social discount rate 9%):

$(P/A, 9\%, 25) = 9.8226$

- **System X:** PV Benefits = $(200 + 100) \times 9.8226 = 2,946.78$; PV Costs = $1,000 + 50 \times 9.8226 = 1,491.13$; **BCR = 1.98**
- **System Y:** PV Benefits = $(240 + 200) \times 9.8226 = 4,323.0$; PV Costs = $1,600 + 40 \times 9.8226 = 1,992.90$; **BCR = 2.17**
- **System Z:** PV Benefits = $(280 + 350) \times 9.8226 = 6,189.24$; PV Costs = $2,200 + 30 \times 9.8226 = 2,494.68$; **BCR = 2.48**

By BCR alone, all three are acceptable (BCR > 1), and Z is also the best. **The two methods agree** in this case.

> [!NOTE]
> When IRoR and BCR methods agree, the decision is robust. When they disagree (rare in public projects with non-distorted cash flows), IRoR is the more theoretically sound ranking criterion for mutually exclusive alternatives.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* — *KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern*

A municipal corporation is evaluating two alternative designs for a public bus transit corridor. Both have a **study period of 15 years** and the **Social Discount Rate is 10%**. The data are:

| Parameter | Design P (BRT Lane) | Design Q (Electric Bus Fleet) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment (₹ Crore) | 25 | 45 |
| Annual Operating Cost (₹ Crore) | 3.0 | 1.5 |
| Annual Fare Revenue (₹ Crore) | 4.0 | 4.5 |
| Annual Carbon-Credit Revenue (₹ Crore) | 0 | 1.0 |
| Annual Social Benefit (₹ Crore) | 5.0 | 8.0 |
| Salvage Value (₹ Crore) | 5 | 8 |

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR) for Design Q over Design P using full cash-flow analysis (including salvage).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the Social Discount Rate is reduced to **8%**, what is your recommendation? Perform a sensitivity discussion.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — IRoR (Q over P)**

Incremental Cash Flows (Q − P):

| Year | Item | Q (₹ Cr) | P (₹ Cr) | Incremental (₹ Cr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Capital | −45 | −25 | **−20** |
| 1–15 | O&M | −1.5 | −3.0 | **+1.5** |
| 1–15 | Fare Revenue | +4.5 | +4.0 | **+0.5** |
| 1–15 | Carbon Credit | +1.0 | 0 | **+1.0** |
| 1–15 | Social Benefit | +8.0 | +5.0 | **+3.0** |
| **Net Annual Incremental Benefit (1–15)** | | | | **+6.0** |
| 15 | Salvage | +8 | +5 | **+3.0** |

Total incremental cash flow each year (1–14) = +6.0; Year 15 = +6.0 + 3.0 = **+9.0**

IRoR equation:

$$-20 + 6.0 \cdot (P/A, i^{*}, 15) + 3.0 \cdot (P/F, i^{*}, 15) = 0$$

Try $i = 25\%$:

- $(P/A, 25\%, 15) = 5.9244$
- $(P/F, 25\%, 15) = 0.1403$

NPV = $-20 + 6.0(5.9244) + 3.0(0.1403) = -20 + 35.546 + 0.421 = +15.967$

Try $i = 35\%$:

- $(P/A, 35\%, 15) = 4.6755$
- $(P/F, 35\%, 15) = 0.0600$

NPV = $-20 + 6.0(4.6755) + 3.0(0.0600) = -20 + 28.053 + 0.180 = +8.233$

Try $i = 45\%$:

- $(P/A, 45\%, 15) = 3.8052$
- $(P/F, 45\%, 15) = 0.0281$

NPV = $-20 + 6.0(3.8052) + 3.0(0.0281) = -20 + 22.831 + 0.084 = +2.915$

Try $i = 50\%$:

- $(P/A, 50\%, 15) = 3.3928$
- $(P/F, 50\%, 15) = 0.0190$

NPV = $-20 + 6.0(3.3928) + 3.0(0.0190) = -20 + 20.357 + 0.057 = +0.414$

Try $i = 51\%$:

- $(P/A, 51\%, 15) = 3.3221$
- $(P/F, 51\%, 15) = 0.0176$

NPV = $-20 + 6.0(3.3221) + 3.0(0.0176) = -20 + 19.933 + 0.053 = -0.014$

Linear interpolation between $i = 50\%$ and $i = 51\%$:

$$\text{IRoR} = 50\% + \frac{0.414}{0.414 + 0.014} \cdot 1\% = 50\% + 0.967\% \approx 50.97\%$$

Since $\text{IRoR} = 50.97\% \gg 10\%$, **Design Q is strongly preferred** at the 10% SDR.

**Part (b) — Sensitivity to SDR**

Reducing the SDR from 10% to 8% makes the discounting *less severe*, which **increases** the NPV of long-term benefits and **strengthens** the case for Design Q (which has higher long-term social and carbon benefits). The IRoR is independent of the SDR (it is a derived break-even rate), so the recommendation remains **Design Q**. The project is *not sensitive* to the discount rate in this range; the IRoR of ~51% provides a very large margin of safety.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to rank by initial cost first.** Always rank alternatives in increasing order of initial investment before incremental analysis. Reversing the order gives wrong conclusions.
> 2. **Using the wrong discount rate.** MARR is for commercial projects; public projects require the **Social Discount Rate** (typically lower).
> 3. **Ignoring social benefits and externalities.** Public-project analysis must include quantified social benefits, or the IRoR is artificially low.
> 4. **Forgetting salvage value.** Always include terminal-year salvage as a separate incremental cash flow at $t = n$.
> 5. **Linear interpolation errors.** Use NPVs at $i_L$ and $i_H$ (not the interest factors) in the interpolation formula.
> 6. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly.** Examiners award marks for the rule, not just the calculation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **IRoR = rate that makes incremental NPV = 0**; solve via trial and interpolation.
- ✅ **Always rank alternatives by initial investment** (lowest to highest) before incremental analysis.
- ✅ **Defender vs. Challenger:** Lower-cost is defender; higher-cost is challenger.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** If $\text{IRoR} \geq i_{\text{SDR}}$ (or MARR), adopt challenger; else retain defender.
- ✅ **Social Discount Rate (8%–12%)** is the benchmark for public projects, **lower** than commercial MARR.
- ✅ **Include social benefits, externalities, and shadow prices** in public-project cash flows.
- ✅ **Include salvage value** as a Year-$n$ incremental cash flow.
- ✅ **For non-revenue service projects**, IRoR may not be meaningful; use **BCR ≥ 1** rule.
- ✅ **Modified IRoR (MIRR)** reinvests positive flows at the SDR, giving a more realistic measure.
- ✅ **Linear interpolation formula:** $i^{*} = i_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H}(i_H - i_L)$
- ✅ **BCR method agrees with IRoR** for most conventional public projects; when they conflict, **IRoR is the theoretically correct ranking tool** for mutually exclusive alternatives.
- ✅ **Do-Nothing** is the baseline for non-revenue service projects; for revenue-generating public projects, "Do-Nothing" is excluded and the cheapest feasible option is the initial defender.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 expects** step-by-step working: cash-flow table, IRoR equation, table look-up, interpolation, and explicit decision statement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefit Cost Analysis and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Benefit Cost Analysis and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Benefit-Cost Analysis (BCA)** is a systematic, quantitative economic evaluation methodology used in engineering finance to determine the feasibility and desirability of a project, investment, or public policy by comparing the **present value of all expected benefits (B)** against the **present value of all expected costs (C)** over a defined time horizon, using a socially determined **discount rate (i)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, BCA is positioned as a **multi-criteria capital budgeting tool** that extends the traditional corporate Net Present Value (NPV) approach to include *intangible benefits*, *externalities*, and *social welfare considerations* — making it indispensable for public-sector engineering projects such as highways, water supply systems, power plants, and environmental remediation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"Benefit-Cost Analysis is the systematic tabulation, discounting, and comparison of all relevant benefits and costs of a project, expressed in monetary terms, to determine whether the project yields a net positive contribution to social welfare."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing at the entrance of a **two-door decision gateway**:

- **Door 1** leads to a *treasure room* (Benefits).
- **Door 2** leads to a *toll booth* (Costs).

Before you can walk through, BCA acts as a **scale** that weighs what you take home against what you paid at the booth — *not the gross weights, but the time-adjusted weights*. A rupee earned today weighs heavier than a rupee earned ten years from now (because of the **time value of money**).

**Real-World Analogy — The Metro Rail Project:**

When the Kochi Metro Rail project was evaluated, decision-makers did not simply look at ticket revenue. BCA forced them to quantify:
- ⏱ Time saved by commuters (converted to monetary value)
- ⛽ Fuel saved by diverted traffic
- 🌍 Reduction in pollution (health cost savings)
- 🏗 Land acquisition costs (often negative market value)
- 🌳 Environmental degradation costs

If the *present value of benefits* > *present value of costs*, the project is **socially desirable**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU 2024

| Term | Standard KTU Definition | Symbol |
|------|--------------------------|--------|
| **Discount Rate** | The opportunity cost of capital used to convert future cash flows to present value | $i$ |
| **Analysis Period** | The economic life over which benefits and costs are evaluated | $n$ |
| **Salvage Value** | The residual worth of an asset at the end of the analysis period | $SV$ |
| **Externalities** | Indirect benefits or costs imposed on third parties | $E$ |
| **Shadow Price** | Adjusted market price reflecting true social opportunity cost | $P_s$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Convention:** All currency values in BCA problems are expressed in **Lakhs (₹ L)** or **Crores (₹ Cr)**. Discount rates are quoted as **percentage per annum (e.g., 10% p.a.)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of BCA

BCA rests on three foundational pillars, each of which corresponds to a distinct decision criterion used by KTU examiners:

### Pillar 1 — Time Value of Money (TVM)
A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow. Hence, all future benefits $B_t$ and costs $C_t$ at year $t$ are discounted to **Present Value (PV)** using:

$$PV = \frac{Future\ Value}{(1+i)^t}$$

### Pillar 2 — Incremental Analysis
BCA in engineering projects is fundamentally **incremental** — we compare *Project Alternative B* against *Project Alternative A* (or the *do-nothing baseline*) to isolate the **net change** in benefits and costs.

### Pillar 3 — Social Welfare Maximization
Unlike private NPV (which uses *market prices* and *private discount rate*), BCA uses:
- **Shadow prices** for non-traded goods (labor in unemployment-ridden regions)
- **Social discount rate** (typically lower than private rate — KTU convention: **8% to 12%**)
- **Externalities** explicitly monetized

---

## 2.2 The Five BCA Decision Criteria

### Criterion 1: Net Present Value (NPV)

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

- **Decision Rule:** Accept the project if $NPV \geq 0$.
- **Intuition:** Measures the net gain in social welfare in *today's* rupees.

### Criterion 2: Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

$$BCR = \frac{\text{Present Value of Benefits (PVB)}}{\text{Present Value of Costs (PVC)}}$$

- **Decision Rule:** Accept the project if $BCR \geq 1.0$.
- **Two Variants:**
  - **Conventional BCR** = $\dfrac{PV\ of\ Benefits}{PV\ of\ Operating\ Costs}$ (operating costs only)
  - **Modified BCR** = $\dfrac{PV\ of\ Benefits - PV\ of\ Capital\ Costs}{PV\ of\ Operating\ Costs}$ (for mutually exclusive alternatives)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** BCR must be calculated with benefits and costs measured at the *same* point in time (usually present value). Mixing a *future-value benefit* with a *present-value cost* is a common error leading to **3-mark deduction** in ESE.

### Criterion 3: Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
The discount rate $i^*$ at which $NPV = 0$:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0$$

- **Decision Rule:** Accept the project if $IRR \geq$ Social discount rate.

### Criterion 4: Payback Period
The number of years required to recover the initial investment from net benefits:

$$\text{Payback} = n\ \text{where}\ \sum_{t=0}^{n} (B_t - C_t) = 0$$

### Criterion 5: Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost / Benefit (EUAC / EUAB)

$$EUAC = NPV \times \left[ \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} \right] = NPV \times (A/P, i, n)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula | Engineering Decision Use | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $PV = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^n}$ | Discount single future cash flow to today | ₹ |
| 2 | $PVB = \sum \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Total present value of all benefits | ₹ Lakh |
| 3 | $PVC = \sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Total present value of all costs | ₹ Lakh |
| 4 | $NPV = PVB - PVC$ | Net social welfare gain | ₹ Lakh |
| 5 | $BCR = \dfrac{PVB}{PVC}$ | Benefit per rupee invested | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $IRR$ from $NPV=0$ | Break-even social return | % |
| 7 | $(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}$ | Single-payment present worth factor | — |
| 8 | $(P/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Uniform series present worth factor | — |
| 9 | $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Capital recovery factor | — |
| 10 | $\Delta BCR = \dfrac{\Delta PVB}{\Delta PVC}$ | Incremental BCR for alternatives | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** The factor notation $(P/A, i, n)$ is the **uniform series present worth factor** — used extensively in BCA problems involving equal annual benefits/costs such as toll revenue, annual maintenance, etc.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications of BCA

| Application Domain | Example Project | Key BCA Quantification |
|--------------------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Transportation Engineering** | Six-laning of NH-66 | Time savings, accident reduction, fuel savings |
| **Water Resources** | Idukki Hydroelectric Dam | Power revenue, irrigation benefit, flood control |
| **Environmental Engineering** | Wastewater treatment plant | Health cost reduction, fishery recovery |
| **Energy Engineering** | Solar Park at Kasaragod | Carbon credits, displaced thermal generation |
| **Urban Infrastructure** | Smart City Kochi | Property value uplift, congestion relief |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting a Uniform Annual Benefit to Present Value

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):** A flyover generates an annual toll revenue of ₹ 50 Lakh for 20 years. What is the present value of this benefit stream at a social discount rate of 10%?

### Step 1 — Identify the cash flow structure
The ₹ 50 Lakh is received uniformly at the **end of each year** for $n = 20$ years. This is a **uniform annual series**.

### Step 2 — State the geometric series
The present value is the sum of 20 individual discounted values:

$$PVB = \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^n}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the geometric series formula
Multiplying the sum by the common ratio $(1+i)^{-1}$ and subtracting:

$$PVB = A \left[ \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} \right]$$

### Step 4 — Identify the standard factor
This expression is the **uniform series present worth factor**:

$$(P/A, i, n) = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute values
With $A = 50$ Lakh, $i = 0.10$, $n = 20$:

$$(P/A, 10\%, 20) = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-20}}{0.10}$$

$$(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275$$

$$(1.10)^{-20} = 0.14864$$

$$(P/A, 10\%, 20) = \frac{1 - 0.14864}{0.10} = \frac{0.85136}{0.10} = 8.5136$$

### Step 6 — Compute PVB
$$PVB = 50 \times 8.5136 = 425.68\ \text{Lakh} \approx \text{₹ } 4.257\ \text{Crore}$$

### Step 7 — Economic Interpretation
The 20-year toll revenue stream, when discounted at 10%, is equivalent to receiving **₹ 4.26 Crore today** in present-value terms.

---

## 3.2 Full BCA Numerical — KTU Model Problem

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
A municipal corporation is evaluating a **solid waste management plant** with the following data:
- Initial investment: ₹ 500 Lakh (at $t=0$)
- Annual operating cost: ₹ 40 Lakh (years 1–15)
- Annual benefit (avoided landfill cost + compost revenue): ₹ 120 Lakh (years 1–15)
- Salvage value: ₹ 50 Lakh (at end of year 15)
- Social discount rate: $i = 12\%$ p.a.

Determine: **(a)** NPV, **(b)** BCR, **(c)** Whether the project is acceptable.

### Step 1 — Identify cash flows at $t=0$
At $t=0$, the corporation incurs the **initial investment** (a cost only, no benefit):

$$B_0 = 0, \quad C_0 = 500\ \text{Lakh}$$

### Step 2 — Identify cash flows for years 1 to 14
Net annual cash flow = Benefit − Operating cost

$$B_t = 120\ \text{Lakh}, \quad C_t = 40\ \text{Lakh} \quad \text{for } t = 1\ to\ 14$$

### Step 3 — Identify cash flows at $t=15$
In the final year, we add the salvage value to the benefits:

$$B_{15} = 120 + 50 = 170\ \text{Lakh}, \quad C_{15} = 40\ \text{Lakh}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the uniform series factor
With $i = 12\% = 0.12$ and $n = 15$:

$$(P/A, 12\%, 15) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-15}}{0.12}$$

$$(1.12)^{15} = 5.4736$$

$$(1.12)^{-15} = 0.18270$$

$$(P/A, 12\%, 15) = \frac{1 - 0.18270}{0.12} = \frac{0.81730}{0.12} = 6.8109$$

### Step 5 — Compute PVB
Total benefits = annual benefit × factor + salvage × single-payment factor:

$$PVB = 120 \times 6.8109 + 50 \times (1.12)^{-15}$$

$$PVB = 817.31 + 50 \times 0.18270$$

$$PVB = 817.31 + 9.14 = 826.45\ \text{Lakh}$$

### Step 6 — Compute PVC
Total costs = initial investment + operating cost × factor:

$$PVC = 500 + 40 \times 6.8109$$

$$PVC = 500 + 272.44 = 772.44\ \text{Lakh}$$

### Step 7 — Compute NPV

$$NPV = PVB - PVC = 826.45 - 772.44 = 54.01\ \text{Lakh}$$

### Step 8 — Compute BCR

$$BCR = \frac{PVB}{PVC} = \frac{826.45}{772.44} = 1.07$$

### Step 9 — Decision
- $NPV = +₹ 54.01\ Lakh > 0$ ✅
- $BCR = 1.07 \geq 1.0$ ✅
- **Project is ACCEPTABLE** on both criteria.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Incremental BCA for Two Alternatives

**Scenario:** A road project has two design alternatives:

| Parameter | Alternative A (2-lane) | Alternative B (4-lane) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Initial Cost | ₹ 300 Lakh | ₹ 600 Lakh |
| Annual Maintenance | ₹ 10 Lakh | ₹ 20 Lakh |
| Annual Benefit (toll + time saving) | ₹ 80 Lakh | ₹ 140 Lakh |
| Salvage Value | ₹ 30 Lakh | ₹ 60 Lakh |
| Life | 20 years | 20 years |
| Discount Rate | 10% | 10% |

### Step 1 — Compute NPV of Alternative A
Using $(P/A, 10\%, 20) = 8.5136$ and $(P/F, 10\%, 20) = 0.1486$:

$$NPV_A = (80 - 10) \times 8.5136 + 30 \times 0.1486 - 300$$

$$NPV_A = 70 \times 8.5136 + 4.46 - 300 = 595.95 + 4.46 - 300 = 300.41\ \text{Lakh}$$

### Step 2 — Compute NPV of Alternative B

$$NPV_B = (140 - 20) \times 8.5136 + 60 \times 0.1486 - 600$$

$$NPV_B = 120 \times 8.5136 + 8.92 - 600 = 1021.63 + 8.92 - 600 = 430.55\ \text{Lakh}$$

### Step 3 — Incremental Analysis (B vs A)
$$\Delta NPV = 430.55 - 300.41 = 130.14\ \text{Lakh}$$

$$\Delta PVC = 600 - 300 = 300\ \text{Lakh (incremental capital)}$$

$$\Delta PVB = 130.14 + 300 = 430.14\ \text{Lakh}$$

$$\Delta BCR = \frac{430.14}{300} = 1.43 \geq 1.0$$

**Decision:** Choose **Alternative B** (4-lane) — incremental BCR exceeds unity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The BCA Evaluation Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Identification] --> B0[Define Analysis Period n]
    B0 --> C0[Identify All Benefits Bt]
    C0 --> D0[Identify All Costs Ct]
    D0 --> E0[Select Social Discount Rate i]
    E0 --> F0[Discount to Present Value]
    F0 --> F1[Compute PVB]
    F0 --> F2[Compute PVC]
    F1 --> G0[Compute NPV]
    F2 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[Compute BCR]
    H0 --> I0{Decision Criteria}
    I0 --> I1[NPV >= 0]
    I0 --> I2[BCR >= 1.0]
    I0 --> I3[IRR >= Social i]
    I1 --> J0{All Satisfied?}
    I2 --> J0
    I3 --> J0
    J0 -->|Yes| K0[ACCEPT Project]
    J0 -->|No| L0[REJECT or Redesign]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map: Components of a BCA Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Project Inputs] --> B[Engineering Estimates]
    A --> C[Market Prices]
    A --> D[Shadow Prices]
    A --> E[Externalities Monetization]
    B --> F[BCA Engine]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Discounted Cash Flow Table]
    G --> H[Decision Metrics]
    H --> H1[NPV]
    H --> H2[BCR]
    H --> H3[IRR]
    H --> H4[Payback]
    H1 --> I[Investment Decision]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
```

## 4.3 Incremental BCA Logic for Mutually Exclusive Alternatives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Rank by Increasing Initial Cost] --> B0[Compute NPV of Lowest Cost Alt]
    B0 --> C0{NPV >= 0?}
    C0 -->|No| D0[Reject All Higher Alts]
    C0 -->|Yes| E0[Compute Incremental NPV B vs A]
    E0 --> F0{Incremental NPV >= 0?}
    F0 -->|No| G0[Select Alternative A]
    F0 -->|Yes| H0[Compute Incremental BCR]
    H0 --> I0{Incremental BCR >= 1.0?}
    I0 -->|Yes| J0[Select Alternative B]
    I0 -->|No| K0[Select Alternative A]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. Define Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR). Mention the decision rule.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) is the ratio of the **present value of all expected benefits (PVB)** to the **present value of all expected costs (PVC)** of a project, evaluated over its entire life at a given social discount rate.

$$BCR = \frac{PVB}{PVC} = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}$$

**Decision Rule:** A project is **economically acceptable** if $BCR \geq 1.0$, which indicates that the present value of benefits equals or exceeds the present value of costs. A BCR of 1.5 means that for every ₹ 1 invested, ₹ 1.50 of benefits is generated.

> [!NOTE]
> *Allocation:* Definition (1.5) + Formula (1) + Decision rule (0.5) = **3 marks**.

---

### **Q2. What is a shadow price? Why is it used in BCA?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **shadow price** is the *imputed* or *accounting* price of a good, service, or factor of production that reflects its **true social opportunity cost**, rather than the distorted market price. It is used in BCA because market prices in developing economies often fail to capture the real scarcity value of resources due to **subsidies, taxes, price controls, unemployment, and externalities**.

For example, in a region with surplus labor, the market wage may be artificially low; the **shadow wage** reflects the marginal productivity of labor transferred from agriculture to the project. Similarly, foreign exchange may have a shadow price (premium) above the official rate when overvalued.

**Uses in BCA:**
- Convert market prices to economic prices (1)
- Adjust for market distortions (1)
- Reflect social opportunity cost (1)

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** A state government is evaluating a **bridge project** with the following data: Initial investment = ₹ 800 Lakh, Annual maintenance cost = ₹ 25 Lakh, Annual benefit (toll + time saved) = ₹ 200 Lakh, Salvage value = ₹ 100 Lakh, Life = 10 years, Social discount rate = 10% p.a. Calculate the **(i) NPV** and **(ii) BCR**. Should the project be accepted? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **incremental Benefit-Cost Analysis**. Why is it essential when comparing two mutually exclusive engineering alternatives? Illustrate with a hypothetical example showing how an incorrect decision may result if only absolute BCRs are compared. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $I_0 = 800$ Lakh, $A_{OM} = 25$ Lakh, $A_B = 200$ Lakh, $SV = 100$ Lakh
- $n = 10$ years, $i = 10\%$

**Step 1 — Standard factors at $i=10\%, n=10$:**

$$(P/A, 10\%, 10) = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.3855}{0.10} = 6.1446$$

$$(P/F, 10\%, 10) = (1.10)^{-10} = 0.3855$$

**Step 2 — Present Value of Benefits (PVB):**

$$PVB = A_B \times (P/A, 10\%, 10) + SV \times (P/F, 10\%, 10)$$

$$PVB = 200 \times 6.1446 + 100 \times 0.3855$$

$$PVB = 1228.92 + 38.55 = 1267.47\ \text{Lakh}$$

**Step 3 — Present Value of Costs (PVC):**

$$PVC = I_0 + A_{OM} \times (P/A, 10\%, 10)$$

$$PVC = 800 + 25 \times 6.1446 = 800 + 153.62 = 953.62\ \text{Lakh}$$

**Step 4 — NPV:**

$$NPV = 1267.47 - 953.62 = \mathbf{313.85\ \text{Lakh}}\ \text{(positive ✅)}$$

**Step 5 — BCR:**

$$BCR = \frac{1267.47}{953.62} = \mathbf{1.33} \geq 1.0$$

**Step 6 — Decision:** The bridge project is **ACCEPTABLE** since NPV is positive and BCR > 1.

> *Mark split:* Factors (1), PVB (1.5), PVC (1.5), NPV (1), BCR (1), Decision (1) = **7 marks**.

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Incremental BCA is the technique of evaluating the *additional* (incremental) benefits and costs of moving from a lower-cost alternative to a higher-cost alternative, rather than evaluating each alternative independently.

**Rationale (2 marks):** When two engineering projects (say, a 2-lane vs a 4-lane road) are *mutually exclusive* (only one can be built), the absolute BCR of each is **misleading**. A small project with a very high BCR may generate less total welfare than a larger project with a slightly lower BCR. The incremental test ensures the *extra investment* earns at least the social discount rate.

**Numerical Illustration (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Alt-A (2-lane) | Alt-B (4-lane) |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| PVC | ₹ 500 Lakh | ₹ 800 Lakh |
| BCR | 1.50 | 1.35 |
| NPV | ₹ 250 Lakh | ₹ 280 Lakh |

If judged by absolute BCR, **Alt-A wins (1.50 > 1.35)**. But Alt-B's NPV is higher, indicating greater total welfare. The incremental analysis:

$$\Delta BCR = \frac{800 \times 1.35 - 500 \times 1.50}{800 - 500} = \frac{1080 - 750}{300} = 1.10 > 1.0$$

Hence, the **extra ₹ 300 Lakh invested in Alt-B earns a 10% social surplus** — the correct choice is **Alt-B**. Choosing Alt-A purely on absolute BCR would **under-invest in public infrastructure**.

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** What are the **limitations of Benefit-Cost Analysis** when applied to public-sector engineering projects in the Indian context? Discuss any **five** limitations with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A small hydro-electric project requires an initial investment of ₹ 1,200 Lakh and generates annual net revenue (benefit minus operating cost) of ₹ 250 Lakh for 25 years. At the end of 25 years, the salvage value is ₹ 200 Lakh. If the social discount rate is 9% p.a., determine the **(i) NPV**, **(ii) BCR**, **(iii) IRR** (using trial-and-error), and **(iv) Equivalent Uniform Annual Net Benefit**. Advise on the project feasibility. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

The five major limitations of BCA in the Indian engineering context are:

1. **Difficulty in Monetizing Intangibles (1.5 marks):** Social benefits such as cultural heritage preservation, aesthetic value, biodiversity, or human life saved are hard to express in rupees. *Example:* The Sardar Sarovar Dam's displacement and rehabilitation costs involve cultural trauma that resists monetary quantification.

2. **Uncertainty in Long-Term Forecasting (1.5 marks):** Indian infrastructure projects face unpredictable cost overruns (typical 20–40% overrun per MoSPI data) and demand variations. A 25-year forecast of toll revenue or hydel output carries high variance.

3. **Choice of Social Discount Rate is Controversial (1.5 marks):** India lacks a single official social discount rate. The Planning Commission historically used 12%, while World Bank projects use 8–10%. A small change in $i$ dramatically alters the BCR.

4. **Equity and Distributional Issues Ignored (1.5 marks):** BCA measures *aggregate* social welfare, not its *distribution*. A project benefiting urban elites while displacing tribal communities (e.g., mining in Odisha) may show a positive NPV but be socially inequitable.

5. **Shadow Price Estimation is Data-Intensive (1 mark):** Constructing shadow prices for labor, foreign exchange, and capital requires detailed CGE (Computable General Equilibrium) modeling — beyond the capacity of most state-level implementing agencies.

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $I_0 = 1,200$ Lakh, Net annual benefit $A = 250$ Lakh, $SV = 200$ Lakh, $n = 25$, $i = 9\%$.

**Step 1 — Standard factors at $i = 9\%, n = 25$:**

$$(P/A, 9\%, 25) = \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-25}}{0.09} = \frac{1 - 0.1160}{0.09} = 9.8226$$

$$(P/F, 9\%, 25) = 0.1160$$

**Step 2 — NPV Calculation:**

$$PVB = 250 \times 9.8226 + 200 \times 0.1160 = 2455.65 + 23.20 = 2478.85$$

$$PVC = 1,200\ \text{Lakh}$$

$$NPV = 2478.85 - 1200 = \mathbf{1278.85\ \text{Lakh (positive ✅)}}$$

**Step 3 — BCR Calculation:**

$$BCR = \frac{2478.85}{1200} = \mathbf{2.07}\ (\text{highly attractive})$$

**Step 4 — IRR Calculation (Trial & Error):**

We seek $i^*$ such that $NPV = 0$, i.e., $250 \times (P/A, i^*, 25) + 200 \times (P/F, i^*, 25) = 1200$.

*Trial 1: $i^* = 18\%$*:
$(P/A, 18\%, 25) = 5.4671$, $(P/F, 18\%, 25) = 0.0130$
$PVB = 250 \times 5.4671 + 200 \times 0.0130 = 1366.78 + 2.60 = 1369.38$ → NPV = +169.38

*Trial 2: $i^* = 20\%$*:
$(P/A, 20\%, 25) = 4.9476$, $(P/F, 20\%, 25) = 0.0098$
$PVB = 250 \times 4.9476 + 200 \times 0.0098 = 1236.90 + 1.96 = 1238.86$ → NPV = +38.86

*Trial 3: $i^* = 21\%$*:
$(P/A, 21\%, 25) = 4.7210$, $(P/F, 21\%, 25) = 0.0085$
$PVB = 250 \times 4.7210 + 200 \times 0.0085 = 1180.25 + 1.70 = 1181.95$ → NPV = −18.05

**Interpolation:**
$$IRR \approx 20\% + 38.86 \times \frac{1\%}{38.86 + 18.05} = 20\% + 0.68\% \approx \mathbf{20.68\%}$$

Since $IRR = 20.68\% \gg 9\%$, the project is **highly feasible**.

**Step 5 — Equivalent Uniform Annual Net Benefit (EUANB):**

Capital recovery factor:
$$(A/P, 9\%, 25) = \frac{0.09 \times (1.09)^{25}}{(1.09)^{25} - 1} = \frac{0.09 \times 8.6231}{8.6231 - 1} = \frac{0.7761}{7.6231} = 0.1018$$

$$EUANB = NPV \times (A/P, 9\%, 25) = 1278.85 \times 0.1018 = \mathbf{130.19\ \text{Lakh/year}}$$

**Conclusion:** With NPV = ₹ 1,278.85 Lakh, BCR = 2.07, IRR = 20.68% > 9%, the small hydro-electric project is **strongly recommended** for execution.

> *Mark split:* Factor tables (1), NPV (1.5), BCR (1), IRR trials + interpolation (2), EUANB (1), Conclusion (0.5) = **7 marks**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in BCA Problems (ESE Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Salvage value misplacement (–2 marks):** The salvage value is a *benefit* at $t = n$, not added to the cost stream. Place it in the numerator of PVB with a $(P/F, i, n)$ factor.
>
> 2. **Mismatched factor tables (–2 marks):** If the uniform series is an *annuity due* (cash flow at the *beginning* of each year), multiply by $(1+i)$. Using the ordinary annuity table silently is a frequent error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting incremental BCR for mutually exclusive alternatives (–3 marks):** Two parts, each with its own BCR > 1, do NOT imply the higher-cost one is better. Always perform an *incremental* BCR test.
>
> 4. **IRR miscalculation (–2 marks):** IRR is found where the *net* present value is zero — not the gross PVB or PVC individually. Interpolation must use the *correct* NPV sign change.
>
> 5. **Discount rate unit mismatch (–1 mark):** KTU convention requires $i$ as a *decimal* (0.10), not a percentage (10). Students writing 10% directly into the formula lose one mark.
>
> 6. **No working shown for trial-and-error IRR (–1.5 marks):** Examiners expect at least *two* trial rates and an interpolation. A "magical" final IRR without working is heavily penalized.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Definition Layer
- ✅ BCA = systematic comparison of **PVB vs PVC** at a social discount rate.
- ✅ BCR is a **dimensionless ratio**; NPV is in **rupees**.

### 🔢 Formula Layer (Must Memorize)
- ✅ $NPV = \sum (B_t - C_t)(1+i)^{-t}$
- ✅ $BCR = PVB / PVC$
- ✅ $IRR$ via $NPV = 0$ condition
- ✅ $(P/A, i, n) = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ and its reciprocal $(A/P, i, n)$

### ⚖️ Decision Layer
- ✅ Accept if $NPV \geq 0$, $BCR \geq 1$, $IRR \geq i_{social}$.
- ✅ For **mutually exclusive** alternatives → use **Incremental BCR**, not absolute BCR.

### 🇮🇳 Context Layer (India-Specific)
- ✅ KTU convention: discount rate 8–12% for public projects; salvage must be discounted.
- ✅ Shadow prices essential for labor and foreign exchange.
- ✅ Externalities (pollution, health, time savings) are first-class BCA inputs.

### 🚨 Exam Strategy Layer
- ✅ Always state the **decision rule** explicitly in your answer.
- ✅ Show **factor lookup** explicitly; examiners award marks for it.
- ✅ Use a **tabular cash-flow layout** for clarity — saves time and avoids arithmetic errors.
- ✅ For IRR, perform **two trials + interpolation**; do not stop at one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breakeven Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Breakeven Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Breakeven Analysis (BEA)**, also referred to as **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis**, is a fundamental managerial accounting and financial management technique used to determine the point at which **Total Revenue (TR)** equals **Total Cost (TC)**, resulting in neither profit nor loss. The volume of output or sales level at which this equilibrium occurs is termed the **Break-Even Point (BEP)**.

Mathematically, the fundamental breakeven condition is:

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \text{Total Cost}$$

$$S \times Q = F + V \times Q$$

Where $S$ is the selling price per unit, $Q$ is the quantity sold, $F$ represents the total **Fixed Cost (FC)**, and $V$ represents the **Variable Cost (VC)** per unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Breakeven Analysis is a core decision-making tool under *Managerial Economics / Engineering Economics*. It directly assists engineering managers in **pricing decisions**, **make-or-buy analysis**, **plant capacity planning**, and **profit planning** under the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Maria's Bakery" Intuition

Imagine **Maria**, a B.Tech graduate in Kerala, starts a small home bakery making **chocolate cakes**. Before she begins, she wants to know: *"How many cakes must I sell each month just to recover what I spent?"*

- **Fixed Costs (FC):** Rent for the small kitchen space (₹10,000), oven EMI, electricity minimum charge, and her baking license fee. These costs exist **whether she bakes 1 cake or 100 cakes**.
- **Variable Cost (VC):** Flour, sugar, cocoa, eggs, milk, packaging box. These costs **rise with every additional cake** baked.
- **Selling Price (S):** ₹500 per cake.

For the very **first cake**, Maria's actual cost (say ₹350) is less than ₹500, so she makes a small profit. But the **rent (FC)** keeps adding up every month. She must sell enough cakes such that the **cumulative selling price** covers the **cumulative variable costs** AND the **monthly rent**. That magic number is her **Breakeven Point**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "No Profit, No Loss" Anchor:** Think of BEP as the **financial finish line** of a marathon. Before the line, you are "in the loss zone" (rent is killing you). At the line, you have exactly recovered all costs. Beyond the line, every additional cake sold drops pure profit into Maria's pocket.

## 1.3 Key Terminology — The Vocabulary of Breakeven

| Term | Definition | Behaviour |
|------|------------|-----------|
| **Fixed Cost (FC)** | Costs that remain constant irrespective of output (rent, salary, depreciation). | Constant in total; per unit decreases as output rises. |
| **Variable Cost (VC)** | Costs that vary directly and proportionally with output. | Total VC rises; per unit VC remains constant. |
| **Total Cost (TC)** | $TC = F + (V \times Q)$ | Sum of fixed and variable costs. |
| **Contribution (C)** | $C = S - V$ (per unit) | The amount each unit contributes toward recovering FC. |
| **P/V Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$ | Measures efficiency of production. |
| **Margin of Safety (MoS)** | $\text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$ | Buffer of sales above BEP. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic of Breakeven — Step-by-Step Reasoning

Breakeven analysis is built on **three foundational assumptions** which every KTU student must memorize:

1. **Linear Cost & Revenue Behaviour:** Both total cost and total revenue are straight-line functions of output.
2. **Constant Selling Price:** The unit selling price remains unchanged across the relevant production range.
3. **Constant Variable Cost per Unit:** Material and labour costs per unit do not fluctuate with volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these assumptions matter:** They allow the analyst to draw the famous **Breakeven Chart**, which is a graphical representation with **Output (Quantity) on the X-axis** and **Cost/Revenue (₹) on the Y-axis**. The intersection of the Total Revenue line and the Total Cost line is the **Breakeven Point**.

### The "Contribution Margin" Concept — The Heart of BEA

Every unit sold generates a **Selling Price (S)**. From this, we must first pay the **Variable Cost (V)**. Whatever remains is called the **Contribution** because it *contributes* toward recovering the Fixed Cost and then generating profit.

$$\text{Contribution per unit} = S - V$$

Once total contribution from all units sold **equals** the Fixed Cost, the company has reached the Breakeven Point. Beyond this point, every additional rupee of contribution becomes pure profit.

$$\text{Profit} = \text{Total Contribution} - \text{Fixed Cost}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

The following table contains **every formula** a KTU student requires to score full marks in Module 2 numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | BEP in Units | $\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{F}{S - V}$ | Units (Nos.) |
| 2 | BEP in Sales Value (₹) | $\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} = \dfrac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ | Indian Rupees |
| 3 | Contribution per Unit | $C = S - V$ | ₹ per unit |
| 4 | Total Contribution | $C_{\text{Total}} = (S - V) \times Q$ | ₹ |
| 5 | P/V Ratio (Profit Volume Ratio) | $\text{P/V} = \dfrac{C}{S} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 6 | Profit | $\text{Profit} = C_{\text{Total}} - F$ | ₹ |
| 7 | Margin of Safety (Amount) | $\text{MoS} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$ | ₹ |
| 8 | Margin of Safety (%) | $\text{MoS\%} = \dfrac{\text{MoS}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 9 | Profit using P/V Ratio | $\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} \times \text{P/V Ratio} - F$ | ₹ |
| 10 | Required Sales for Target Profit | $Q_{\text{target}} = \dfrac{F + \text{Target Profit}}{S - V}$ | Units (Nos.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR KTU EXAMS:** When writing the BEP formula in the answer sheet, always write $\dfrac{F}{S - V}$ in a single horizontal fraction. Examiners *prefer* clarity: clearly state **F = ₹___, S = ₹___, V = ₹___** before substituting.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Breakeven Analysis in Engineering

For a B.Tech graduate, BEA is not merely a commerce concept — it is an **engineering decision tool**:

- **Capacity Planning:** An engineer designing a new bottling plant uses BEA to determine the **minimum production volume** that justifies the capital investment.
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Should the firm manufacture a component in-house (incurring FC) or outsource (incurring only VC)? BEA provides the threshold.
- **Product Mix Selection:** When multiple products exist, BEA helps identify which product's contribution margin is most favourable.
- **Pricing Strategy:** Engineers can simulate "what if selling price drops by 5%?" and immediately see the new BEP.
- **Cost Control:** A high BEP signals excessive fixed costs — pushing the firm to convert FC into VC (e.g., outsourcing) to lower risk.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Breakeven Point (Algebraic)

We begin from the fundamental economic identity:

$$\text{Profit} = \text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total Cost}$$

At the Breakeven Point, the company makes **zero profit** (no profit, no loss). Therefore:

$$\text{Profit} = 0$$

$$\text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total Cost} = 0$$

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \text{Total Cost}$$

Now we substitute the structural forms of TR and TC. Let:
- $Q$ = Number of units produced and sold
- $S$ = Selling price per unit (in ₹)
- $F$ = Total fixed cost (in ₹)
- $V$ = Variable cost per unit (in ₹)

Then Total Revenue is simply price times quantity:

$$\text{TR} = S \times Q$$

And Total Cost is the sum of fixed and total variable cost:

$$\text{TC} = F + V \times Q$$

Setting them equal:

$$S \times Q = F + V \times Q$$

Isolating $Q$ on the left-hand side:

$$S \times Q - V \times Q = F$$

$$Q \times (S - V) = F$$

Therefore, the Breakeven Quantity is:

$$\boxed{\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{F}{S - V}}$$

The term $(S - V)$ is the **Contribution per unit**, denoted $C$. So equivalently:

$$\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{F}{C}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of BEP in Sales Value (₹)

We know that BEP in sales value equals BEP in units multiplied by the selling price per unit:

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} = \text{BEP}_Q \times S$$

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} = \dfrac{F}{S - V} \times S$$

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} = F \times \dfrac{S}{S - V}$$

Now recall the definition of the **P/V Ratio**:

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \dfrac{S - V}{S} = \dfrac{C}{S}$$

The reciprocal of the P/V Ratio is:

$$\dfrac{1}{\text{P/V}} = \dfrac{S}{S - V}$$

Substituting this reciprocal into our BEP sales expression:

$$\boxed{\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} = \dfrac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}}}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Example — KTU Board Exam Style

> **[Worked Example — KTU Pattern Problem]**
> A small-scale engineering unit in Kochi manufactures a specialized gearbox component. The following data is provided for the year 2024–25:
> - Selling Price per unit: **₹500**
> - Variable Cost per unit: **₹300**
> - Total Fixed Cost: **₹4,00,000**
> - Actual Sales in the year: **3,000 units**
>
> **Required:**
> (a) Calculate the Breakeven Point in units and in sales value.
> (b) Calculate the Profit for the year.
> (c) Determine the Margin of Safety in amount and percentage.
> (d) Find the number of units required to earn a target profit of ₹6,00,000.

### Part (a) — Breakeven Point in Units and Sales Value

**Step 1:** Identify the given parameters.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= ₹500 \text{ (Selling Price per unit)} \\
V &= ₹300 \text{ (Variable Cost per unit)} \\
F &= ₹4{,}00{,}000 \text{ (Total Fixed Cost)} \\
Q_{\text{actual}} &= 3{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the Contribution per unit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= S - V \\
C &= 500 - 300 \\
C &= ₹200 \text{ per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the BEP formula in units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP}_Q &= \dfrac{F}{C} \\
\text{BEP}_Q &= \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{200} \\
\text{BEP}_Q &= 2{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the P/V Ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P/V Ratio} &= \dfrac{C}{S} \times 100 \\
\text{P/V Ratio} &= \dfrac{200}{500} \times 100 \\
\text{P/V Ratio} &= 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate BEP in Sales Value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} &= \dfrac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}} \\
\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} &= \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{0.40} \\
\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} &= ₹10{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $\text{BEP}_Q \times S = 2{,}000 \times 500 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$ ✓ (Matches)

### Part (b) — Profit for the Year

**Step 6:** Calculate Total Sales Revenue.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Sales} &= S \times Q_{\text{actual}} \\
\text{Total Sales} &= 500 \times 3{,}000 \\
\text{Total Sales} &= ₹15{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Calculate Total Variable Cost.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total VC} &= V \times Q_{\text{actual}} \\
\text{Total VC} &= 300 \times 3{,}000 \\
\text{Total VC} &= ₹9{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8:** Calculate Total Contribution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Contribution} &= \text{Total Sales} - \text{Total VC} \\
\text{Total Contribution} &= 15{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Total Contribution} &= ₹6{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9:** Calculate Profit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= \text{Total Contribution} - F \\
\text{Profit} &= 6{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= ₹2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (c) — Margin of Safety

**Step 10:** Calculate Margin of Safety in amount.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MoS}_{\text{Amount}} &= \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales} \\
\text{MoS}_{\text{Amount}} &= 15{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{MoS}_{\text{Amount}} &= ₹5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11:** Calculate Margin of Safety as a percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MoS\%} &= \dfrac{\text{MoS}_{\text{Amount}}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100 \\
\text{MoS\%} &= \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
\text{MoS\%} &= 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company can afford a **33.33% drop in sales** before slipping into losses.

### Part (d) — Units for Target Profit of ₹6,00,000

**Step 12:** Apply the target profit formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{target}} &= \dfrac{F + \text{Target Profit}}{C} \\
Q_{\text{target}} &= \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000}{200} \\
Q_{\text{target}} &= \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{200} \\
Q_{\text{target}} &= 5{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

The company must sell **5,000 units** to earn a profit of ₹6,00,000.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix (Final Tabulated Answer)

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| BEP in Units | 2,000 units |
| BEP in Sales Value | ₹10,00,000 |
| P/V Ratio | 40% |
| Total Contribution | ₹6,00,000 |
| Annual Profit | ₹2,00,000 |
| Margin of Safety (Amount) | ₹5,00,000 |
| Margin of Safety (%) | 33.33% |
| Target Sales (for ₹6,00,000 profit) | 5,000 units |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Breakeven Analysis Flow Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **logical data flow** of a Breakeven computation, which is precisely how the KTU examiner expects a student to *mentally structure* the problem before solving.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT DATA COLLECTION] --> B[Fixed Cost F]
    A --> C[Variable Cost per unit V]
    A --> D[Selling Price per unit S]
    A --> E[Actual Sales Volume Q]

    B --> F[STEP 1: COMPUTE CONTRIBUTION]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> F1[C = S minus V]

    F1 --> G[STEP 2: COMPUTE P/V RATIO]
    D --> G
    G --> G1[P/V = C divided by S times 100]

    F1 --> H[STEP 3: COMPUTE BEP UNITS]
    B --> H
    H --> H1[BEP_Q = F divided by C]

    B --> I[STEP 4: COMPUTE BEP SALES]
    G --> I
    I --> I1[BEP_Sales = F divided by P_V]

    F1 --> J[STEP 5: COMPUTE PROFIT]
    B --> J
    E --> J
    J --> J1[Profit = C times Q minus F]

    J1 --> K[STEP 6: COMPUTE MARGIN OF SAFETY]
    E --> K
    I1 --> K
    K --> K1[MoS = Actual Sales minus BEP Sales]

    K1 --> L[FINAL OUTPUT: DECISION REPORT]
    H1 --> L
    I1 --> L
    J1 --> L

    L --> M{IS PROFIT POSITIVE?}
    M -- YES --> N[RECOMMEND CONTINUE PRODUCTION]
    M -- NO --> O[RECOMMEND COST RESTRUCTURING]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Chart Architecture

The classic **Breakeven Chart** can be schematically represented as the intersection of the Total Revenue line and the Total Cost line. The X-axis represents **Output Volume (Q)** and the Y-axis represents **Cost/Revenue (₹)**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AXIS["CARTESIAN PLANE - COST VOLUME PROFIT CHART"]
        direction LR
        XAXIS["X-AXIS: OUTPUT VOLUME Q in units"]
        YAXIS["Y-AXIS: COST OR REVENUE in RUPEES"]
    end

    subgraph CURVES["COST REVENUE BEHAVIOUR"]
        TRLINE["TOTAL REVENUE LINE: Starts at ORIGIN Rises with slope S"]
        TCLINE["TOTAL COST LINE: Starts at F on Y axis Rises with slope V"]
        FCLINE["FIXED COST LINE: Horizontal line at F"]
        VCLINE["VARIABLE COST LINE: Starts at ORIGIN Rises with slope V"]
    end

    subgraph ZONES["PROFIT LOSS ZONES"]
        BEPX["BREAKEVEN POINT BEP where TR equals TC"]
        LOSSZONE["LOSS ZONE: Below TCLINE and Above TRLINE for Q less than BEP"]
        PROFITZONE["PROFIT ZONE: Above TRLINE and Above TCLINE for Q greater than BEP"]
    end

    TRLINE --> BEPX
    TCLINE --> BEPX
    BEPX --> LOSSZONE
    BEPX --> PROFITZONE
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Profit-Ladder Diagram (Engineering Intuition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[START: Sales Revenue earned] --> P2[Subtract Variable Cost]
    P2 --> P3[EQUALS: Total Contribution]
    P3 --> P4[Subtract Fixed Cost]
    P4 --> P5[EQUALS: Operating Profit]

    P3 --> P6[Stage 1: Recover Fixed Cost]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 2: Generate Profit]

    P5 --> P8{Profit greater than zero}
    P8 -- YES --> P9[Profitable Operation]
    P8 -- NO --> P10[Loss Making Operation - Review BEP]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Set A]**
> **Q1. Define the term 'Break-Even Point'. Explain the concept of 'Margin of Safety'.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the level of sales at which **Total Revenue equals Total Cost**, resulting in **zero profit and zero loss**. At this point, the contribution from all units sold exactly covers the total fixed cost.

$$\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{F}{S - V}$$

The **Margin of Safety (MoS)** represents the amount by which **actual or expected sales exceed the breakeven sales**. It acts as a **safety cushion** indicating how much sales can drop before the firm begins to incur losses.

$$\text{MoS} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$$

A higher MoS indicates lower business risk.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam, Module 2]**
> **Q2. What is P/V Ratio? Why is it considered an important tool in Breakeven Analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **P/V Ratio (Profit/Volume Ratio)** is the ratio of **Contribution to Sales**, expressed as a percentage.

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 = \dfrac{S - V}{S} \times 100$$

**Importance in Breakeven Analysis:**
1. It enables direct computation of BEP in sales value (₹) without first finding units.
2. It indicates the **efficiency of production** — a higher P/V ratio means a greater portion of sales revenue contributes to fixed cost recovery.
3. It is used to compute **target sales** for a desired profit level.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, End Semester Examination, Module 2]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. (a)** A company manufacturing LED bulbs has the following data for the year 2024:
- Selling Price per bulb: ₹120
- Variable Cost per bulb: ₹80
- Total Fixed Cost: ₹6,00,000
- Actual Sales: 15,000 bulbs

**Calculate:**
(i) Breakeven Point in units and in sales value.
(ii) Profit earned during the year.
(iii) Margin of Safety in amount and percentage.

**(7 Marks)**

**Q.A. (b)** If the company desires a **target profit of ₹4,00,000**, determine the **number of bulbs to be sold**. Also comment on what would happen if the selling price is reduced by ₹20 per bulb due to market competition. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= ₹120 \text{ per bulb} \\
V &= ₹80 \text{ per bulb} \\
F &= ₹6{,}00{,}000 \\
Q_{\text{actual}} &= 15{,}000 \text{ bulbs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Contribution per unit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= S - V = 120 - 80 = ₹40 \text{ per bulb}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: BEP in Units.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP}_Q &= \dfrac{F}{C} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{40} = 15{,}000 \text{ bulbs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: P/V Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
