\text{P/V Ratio} &= \dfrac{40}{120} \times 100 = 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: BEP in Sales Value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} &= \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{0.3333} = ₹18{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total Sales & Total Variable Cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Sales} &= 15{,}000 \times 120 = ₹18{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Total VC} &= 15{,}000 \times 80 = ₹12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Total Contribution & Profit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Contribution} &= 18{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = ₹6{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= 6{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** The company is operating **exactly at the breakeven point** — neither profit nor loss.

**Step 7: Margin of Safety.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MoS}_{\text{Amount}} &= 18{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000 = ₹0 \\
\text{MoS\%} &= 0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Target Profit & Sensitivity Analysis**

**Step 8: Units for Target Profit of ₹4,00,000.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{target}} &= \dfrac{F + \text{Target Profit}}{C} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{40} = 25{,}000 \text{ bulbs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Sensitivity — Selling Price reduced by ₹20.**

New Selling Price: $S_{\text{new}} = 120 - 20 = ₹100$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{new}} &= 100 - 80 = ₹20 \text{ per bulb} \\
\text{BEP}_{\text{new}} &= \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{20} = 30{,}000 \text{ bulbs} \\
Q_{\text{target,new}} &= \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{20} = 50{,}000 \text{ bulbs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion of Sensitivity Analysis:** A ₹20 price reduction **doubles the breakeven volume from 15,000 to 30,000 bulbs**, and pushes the target sales for ₹4,00,000 profit from 25,000 to 50,000 bulbs. The company should **resist price cuts** and instead explore cost reduction.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**Q.B. (a)** Distinguish between **Fixed Cost and Variable Cost** with suitable examples. Explain why the classification of costs is critical for Breakeven Analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B. (b)** A firm has a total fixed cost of ₹3,00,000 and a P/V ratio of 40%. If the actual sales amounted to ₹12,00,000, find:
(i) The Breakeven Point in sales value.
(ii) The Margin of Safety in amount and percentage.
(iii) The Profit for the year. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Distinction between Fixed and Variable Cost**

| Aspect | Fixed Cost | Variable Cost |
|--------|------------|---------------|
| **Definition** | Costs that remain constant in total irrespective of output level. | Costs that vary directly and proportionately with output. |
| **Total Behaviour** | Constant in total | Increases with output |
| **Per Unit Behaviour** | Decreases as output rises | Remains constant per unit |
| **Examples** | Factory rent, plant depreciation, permanent staff salaries, insurance premium | Raw materials, direct labour wages, sales commission, packaging cost |
| **Time Horizon** | Usually contractual / long-term | Operational / short-term |
| **Control** | Harder to control in short run | Easier to control per unit |

**Why Classification is Critical for BEA:**
1. The **breakeven formula** $\text{BEP} = \dfrac{F}{S - V}$ requires separation of FC and VC.
2. The **P/V Ratio** depends on VC.
3. **Margin of Safety** and **operating leverage** calculations assume proper cost classification.
4. **Managerial decisions** (e.g., outsourcing) require knowing which costs are fixed vs. variable.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= ₹3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{P/V Ratio} &= 40\% = 0.40 \\
\text{Actual Sales} &= ₹12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: BEP in Sales Value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} &= \dfrac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = ₹7{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Margin of Safety (Amount).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MoS}_{\text{Amount}} &= 12{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}50{,}000 = ₹4{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Margin of Safety (%).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MoS\%} &= \dfrac{4{,}50{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 37.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Profit for the Year.**

Using the identity: $\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} \times \text{P/V Ratio} - F$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= (12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40) - 3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= 4{,}80{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Profit} &= ₹1{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Tabulated Answer for Q.B(b):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| BEP in Sales Value | ₹7,50,000 |
| Margin of Safety (Amount) | ₹4,50,000 |
| Margin of Safety (%) | 37.5% |
| Annual Profit | ₹1,80,000 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Students often write BEP in sales value as a number without the **₹ symbol**, costing 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Confusing P/V Ratio with Profit:** P/V Ratio is a *percentage* derived from contribution. Profit is an *absolute amount*.
> 3. **Mixing up MoS formulas:** Some students incorrectly calculate MoS as BEP - Actual Sales. Remember: **MoS = Actual - BEP (always positive when profitable)**.
> 4. **Not verifying BEP:** Always cross-check using $\text{BEP}_Q \times S = \text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}}$.
> 5. **Skipping sensitivity analysis:** When a question mentions a price change, the KTU examiner expects a *comment*, not just a new number.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **BEP Definition:** The sales volume where Total Revenue equals Total Cost (zero profit, zero loss).
- **Core BEP Formula:** $\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{F}{S - V}$ (units), $\text{BEP}_{\text{Sales}} = \dfrac{F}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ (₹).
- **Contribution:** $C = S - V$ — the engine of breakeven recovery.
- **P/V Ratio:** $\dfrac{C}{S} \times 100$ — efficiency metric; higher is better.
- **Profit Identity:** $\text{Profit} = (S - V) \times Q - F$.
- **Margin of Safety:** $\text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$ (in ₹) and $\dfrac{\text{MoS}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ (in %).
- **Target Sales Formula:** $Q_{\text{target}} = \dfrac{F + \text{Target Profit}}{C}$.
- **Assumptions:** Linear costs, constant SP, constant VC per unit, single product (or constant product mix).
- **Practical Insight:** A high BEP implies high risk — try to convert FC into VC.
- **Exam Rule:** Always substitute *before* simplifying. Always write units (₹ or units) in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed and Variable Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed and Variable Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In managerial cost accounting and engineering economics, costs incurred by a business enterprise are classified based on their **behavioral pattern** in response to changes in the level of production activity, output volume, or sales within a **relevant range**. The two foundational behavioral classifications are:

**Fixed Cost (FC):**
A cost that remains **constant in total** regardless of variations in the volume of output or activity level, within a defined relevant range and a specific time period. Fixed costs do not change with the number of units produced in the short run.

**Variable Cost (VC):**
A cost that **varies in direct proportion** to the volume of output or activity level. The total variable cost changes with the number of units produced, while the per-unit variable cost typically remains constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In KTU Finance for Engineering (OECET725), Module 2 focuses on cost concepts as a foundation for break-even analysis, cost-volume-profit (CVP) analysis, and budgeting. Understanding the distinction between fixed and variable cost is the **gateway concept** — every subsequent profitability model rests upon this classification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Auto Rickshaw Stand:**

Imagine you own an auto rickshaw stand in a small town:
- **Fixed Cost** = The daily rent you pay for the parking spot (₹200/day), the monthly EMI on the rickshaw (₹1,500/month), and the driver's fixed salary (₹500/day). Whether you ferry **1 passenger** or **50 passengers** a day, the rent and EMI stay the **same**. The cost is "fixed" — it does not move with your workload.
- **Variable Cost** = The fuel (petrol) you consume. If you run the rickshaw **more kilometers**, you burn **more fuel** and spend **more money** on petrol. If the rickshaw sits idle, you spend **nothing** on fuel. The cost is "variable" — it dances to the tune of your activity level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Fixed costs are like a *subscription* you pay whether you use the service or not (Netflix, gym, EMI). Variable costs are like a *pay-per-use* bill (electricity meter, Ola fare, petrol).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Boundary Conditions

| Metric | Standard Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|
| Output Quantity (Q) | Units produced per period |
| Relevant Range | The activity band within which cost behavior assumptions hold valid |
| Short Run | Period during which at least one input (usually capital) is **fixed** |
| Time Horizon | Typically **monthly, quarterly, or annually** for cost classification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary — The Relevant Range:**
> Fixed costs are fixed *only within the relevant range*. If output grows beyond capacity (say, you need a second rickshaw), the fixed cost "steps up" to a new, higher level. KTU examiners often test this nuance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Behavioral Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

**A. Fixed Cost Behavior — The "Constant Total, Falling Average" Logic**

1. **Total Fixed Cost (TFC)** remains unchanged at every output level within the relevant range.
2. As output (Q) increases, the **same fixed cost is spread over more units**.
3. Therefore, the **Average Fixed Cost (AFC)** continuously **declines** as output rises.
4. AFC approaches but **never reaches zero** — it asymptotically falls toward the Q-axis.
5. Beyond the relevant range (e.g., needing a new factory, new supervisor), TFC **jumps to a new step**.

**B. Variable Cost Behavior — The "Linear Total, Constant Average" Logic**

1. **Total Variable Cost (TVC)** rises in **direct proportion** to output (Q).
2. For each additional unit produced, an additional variable cost (v) is incurred.
3. The **Average Variable Cost (AVC)** remains **constant** at value 'v' per unit.
4. TVC originates from the **origin (0,0)** — when Q = 0, TVC = 0.
5. Variable cost is *truly variable*: zero activity implies zero variable cost.

### 2.2 The Master Cost Equation

The total cost of a firm is the sum of fixed and variable components:

$$TC = TFC + TVC$$

where each component is computed as:

$$TFC = \text{constant} \quad (\text{independent of } Q)$$

$$TVC = v \cdot Q$$

where **v** is the variable cost **per unit** of output.

Substituting TVC into TC:

$$TC = TFC + vQ$$

This is the **linear cost function** — a first-degree equation in Q. It forms the bedrock of break-even analysis in Module 2.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $TC$ | $TFC + TVC$ | Total Cost | ₹ (currency) |
| $TFC$ | $\text{constant}$ | Total Fixed Cost | ₹ |
| $TVC$ | $v \cdot Q$ | Total Variable Cost | ₹ |
| $AFC$ | $TFC \,/\, Q$ | Average Fixed Cost | ₹/unit |
| $AVC$ | $TVC \,/\, Q = v$ | Average Variable Cost | ₹/unit |
| $ATC$ | $TC \,/\, Q$ | Average Total Cost | ₹/unit |
| $MC$ | $\Delta TC \,/\, \Delta Q$ | Marginal Cost | ₹/unit |
| $v$ | $TVC \,/\, Q$ | Variable cost per unit | ₹/unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Engineers use the linear cost equation $TC = TFC + vQ$ in **make-or-buy decisions**, **break-even analysis**, **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** models, and **capital budgeting**. In industries like manufacturing, IT, and process engineering, separating fixed and variable costs determines pricing strategy and capacity planning.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Depreciation of machinery = Fixed Cost; Raw materials = Variable Cost.
- **Software Engineering:** Server lease = Fixed Cost; Cloud compute per API call = Variable Cost.
- **Civil Engineering Projects:** Site office rent = Fixed Cost; Cement and steel per cubic meter = Variable Cost.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Transformer installation cost = Fixed Cost; Electricity units consumed = Variable Cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Total Cost Equation

**Starting Premise:**
A firm's cost structure comprises a fixed component (independent of output) and a variable component (linearly dependent on output). We wish to express Total Cost (TC) as an explicit function of output Q.

**Step 1 — Define the Fixed Component:**
Let TFC denote the total fixed cost. By definition, TFC does not depend on Q.

$$TFC = k \quad \text{(where } k \text{ is a constant in ₹)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Per-Unit Variable Cost:**
Let $v$ denote the variable cost incurred per unit of output (in ₹/unit). This represents the slope of the TVC function.

$$v = \frac{\text{change in total variable cost}}{\text{change in output quantity}}$$

**Step 3 — Express Total Variable Cost:**
For Q units of output, the total variable cost is the per-unit variable cost multiplied by the quantity produced.

$$TVC = v \cdot Q$$

This is a linear function passing through the origin (0, 0) with slope $v$.

**Step 4 — Combine to Obtain Total Cost:**
By the additivity of cost components, the total cost is the sum of the fixed and variable portions.

$$TC = TFC + TVC$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Variable Cost Expression:**

$$TC = TFC + vQ$$

Or equivalently, using $k$ for the constant:

$$TC = k + vQ$$

**Interpretation:** This is a linear equation in Q. The intercept on the cost axis (when Q = 0) equals TFC, and the slope equals the per-unit variable cost $v$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Average Cost Curves

**Step 1 — Derive Average Fixed Cost (AFC):**
AFC is defined as TFC divided by output Q.

$$AFC = \frac{TFC}{Q}$$

**Logical Note:** Since TFC is constant and Q is in the denominator, AFC is a **hyperbolic function** that decreases as Q increases. Mathematically, AFC $\to 0$ as Q $\to \infty$, but never reaches zero.

**Step 2 — Derive Average Variable Cost (AVC):**
AVC is defined as TVC divided by Q.

$$AVC = \frac{TVC}{Q} = \frac{vQ}{Q} = v$$

**Logical Note:** AVC equals $v$ (the per-unit variable cost) at every output level. This is the hallmark of a **strictly linear variable cost** function.

**Step 3 — Derive Average Total Cost (ATC):**
ATC is TC divided by Q.

$$ATC = \frac{TC}{Q} = \frac{TFC + TVC}{Q} = \frac{TFC}{Q} + \frac{TVC}{Q}$$

$$\boxed{ATC = AFC + AVC}$$

**Logical Note:** ATC is the vertical sum of AFC and AVC at every output level. Since AFC falls and AVC is constant, ATC falls at a *decreasing rate* as Q rises.

**Step 4 — Derive Marginal Cost (MC):**
Marginal cost is the change in total cost resulting from a one-unit change in output.

$$MC = \frac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q} = \frac{\Delta(TFC + vQ)}{\Delta Q} = \frac{\Delta TFC}{\Delta Q} + \frac{\Delta(vQ)}{\Delta Q}$$

Since TFC is constant, $\Delta TFC = 0$. Therefore:

$$MC = 0 + v = v$$

**Conclusion:** Under the linear cost assumption, marginal cost equals average variable cost equals the per-unit variable cost:

$$MC = AVC = v$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A small-scale manufacturing unit has the following cost structure:
- Monthly factory rent = ₹50,000
- Permanent supervisor salary = ₹30,000
- Per-unit raw material cost = ₹200
- Per-unit direct labor cost = ₹100

**Step 1 — Identify Fixed and Variable Components:**

$$TFC = 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ ₹/month}$$

$$v = 200 + 100 = 300 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 2 — Write the Cost Function:**

$$TC = 80{,}000 + 300Q$$

**Step 3 — Compute Costs at Q = 100 units:**

$$TC = 80{,}000 + 300(100) = 80{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 110{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$AFC = \frac{80{,}000}{100} = 800 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

$$AVC = \frac{30{,}000}{100} = 300 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

$$ATC = \frac{110{,}000}{100} = 1{,}100 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Costs at Q = 200 units:**

$$TC = 80{,}000 + 300(200) = 80{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 140{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$AFC = \frac{80{,}000}{200} = 400 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

$$AVC = \frac{30{,}000}{200} = 300 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

$$ATC = \frac{140{,}000}{200} = 700 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 5 — Compare and Interpret:**

| Output (Q) | TFC | TVC | TC | AFC | AVC | ATC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 80,000 | 30,000 | 110,000 | 800 | 300 | 1,100 |
| 200 | 80,000 | 60,000 | 140,000 | 400 | 300 | 700 |

**Insight:** TFC remains ₹80,000 at both output levels (proves fixed nature). TVC doubles (₹30,000 → ₹60,000) when output doubles. AFC halves (₹800 → ₹400) — fixed cost spreads over more units. AVC is unchanged at ₹300. ATC falls from ₹1,100 to ₹700.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cost Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Cost TC] --> B[Fixed Cost TFC]
    A --> C[Variable Cost TVC]
    B --> D[Explicit Fixed Cost]
    B --> E[Implicit Fixed Cost]
    D --> D1[Rent]
    D --> D2[Salaries]
    D --> D3[Insurance]
    D --> D4[Depreciation]
    E --> E1[Opportunity Cost of Capital]
    E --> E2[Owners Time]
    C --> F[Direct Variable Cost]
    C --> G[Indirect Variable Cost]
    F --> F1[Raw Materials]
    F --> F2[Direct Labor]
    F --> F3[Packaging]
    G --> G1[Power and Utilities]
    G --> G2[Sales Commission]
    A --> H[Per Unit Averages]
    H --> H1[AFC equals TFC over Q]
    H --> H2[AVC equals v constant]
    H --> H3[ATC equals AFC plus AVC]
    A --> I[Marginal Analysis]
    I --> I1[MC equals Delta TC over Delta Q]
    I --> I2[Linear Case: MC equals AVC equals v]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Behavioral Graph — Cost Curves vs. Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Output Q axis] --> R1[Q 0: TC 80000 AFC undefined]
    R1 --> R2[Q 100: TC 110000 AFC 800 AVC 300]
    R2 --> R3[Q 200: TC 140000 AFC 400 AVC 300]
    R3 --> R4[Q 400: TC 200000 AFC 200 AVC 300]
    R4 --> R5[Q 800: TC 320000 AFC 100 AVC 300]
    subgraph Fixed Cost Behavior
        TFC1[TFC line: horizontal flat at 80000]
    end
    subgraph Variable Cost Behavior
        TVC1[TVC line: linear through origin with slope v]
    end
    subgraph Average Cost Behavior
        AFC1[AFC curve: hyperbolic declining toward zero]
        AVC1[AVC line: constant horizontal at 300]
        ATC1[ATC curve: falls asymptotically toward AVC]
    end
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Architecture — Cost Behavior Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input1[Production Output Q] --> Process1{Cost Classification Engine}
    Process1 --> Output1[Fixed Cost Bucket: TFC]
    Process1 --> Output2[Variable Cost Bucket: TVC equals vQ]
    Output1 --> Combine[Cost Aggregator: TC equals TFC plus vQ]
    Output2 --> Combine
    Combine --> Metrics[Per Unit Metrics]
    Metrics --> M1[AFC: TFC over Q]
    Metrics --> M2[AVC: TVC over Q equals v]
    Metrics --> M3[ATC: TC over Q]
    Metrics --> M4[MC: Delta TC over Delta Q equals v]
    M1 --> Insight[Engineering Decision Layer]
    M2 --> Insight
    M3 --> Insight
    M4 --> Insight
    Insight --> Decision1[Pricing Strategy]
    Insight --> Decision2[Break Even Volume]
    Insight --> Decision3[Make or Buy Choice]
    Insight --> Decision4[Profitability Forecast]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between fixed cost and variable cost with two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Cost | Variable Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Cost that remains constant in total regardless of output within relevant range | Cost that varies in direct proportion to output |
| Behavior | Total is constant; per-unit falls as output rises | Total varies linearly; per-unit remains constant |
| Example 1 | Factory rent ₹50,000/month | Raw material ₹200/unit |
| Example 2 | Depreciation on machinery | Direct labor wages per unit |

**Mark Allocation:** Definition (1 mark) + 2 examples each (1 mark) + Behavioral distinction (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the "relevant range" in cost behavior analysis? Why is it important?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **relevant range** is the band of activity (output levels) within which the assumed cost behavior patterns (fixed and variable) hold valid. It is important because: (i) fixed cost remains constant **only within** the relevant range — beyond it, TFC "steps up" to a new level, (ii) variable cost per unit remains constant only within this range, and (iii) cost estimates and CVP analysis are reliable only when the operation lies inside the relevant range.

**Mark Allocation:** Definition (1 mark) + 2 reasons for importance (2 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> In 14-mark questions, students often **confuse AFC with TFC** and write AFC = constant. Always remember: **TFC is constant; AFC falls** as Q rises. Also, students frequently forget to **state units** (₹/unit) for average and marginal costs — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units. Always write the cost equation in the form $TC = a + bQ$ explicitly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**
*Question A(a) [7 Marks]: A manufacturing company incurs a total fixed cost of ₹1,20,000 per month. The variable cost per unit is ₹150. The selling price is ₹250 per unit.*
*(i) Write the total cost and total revenue functions.*
*(ii) Find the break-even point in units and in rupees.*
*(iii) Calculate the profit when 2,000 units are sold.*

*Question A(b) [7 Marks]: Explain with a suitable diagram the behavior of Total Fixed Cost, Total Variable Cost, Total Cost, Average Fixed Cost, and Average Variable Cost as output increases.*

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$TFC = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}, \quad v = 150 \text{ ₹/unit}, \quad p = 250 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 2 — Total Cost function:**

$$TC = TFC + vQ = 1{,}20{,}000 + 150Q$$

**Step 3 — Total Revenue function:**

$$TR = p \cdot Q = 250Q$$

**Step 4 — Break-even point in units:**

At break-even, $TR = TC$:

$$250Q = 1{,}20{,}000 + 150Q$$

$$100Q = 1{,}20{,}000$$

$$Q_{BE} = 1{,}200 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Break-even point in rupees:**

$$TR_{BE} = 250 \times 1{,}200 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6 — Profit at Q = 2,000:**

$$\text{Profit} = TR - TC = 250(2{,}000) - [1{,}20{,}000 + 150(2{,}000)]$$

$$= 5{,}00{,}000 - [1{,}20{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000] = 5{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}20{,}000$$

$$= 80{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Model Solution to A(b):**
- **TFC:** Horizontal line parallel to Q-axis at ₹1,20,000. Constant at all output levels.
- **TVC:** Upward sloping straight line starting from origin, slope = ₹150/unit.
- **TC:** Upward sloping line starting at ₹1,20,000 on Y-axis, parallel to TVC. TC = TFC + TVC.
- **AFC:** Hyperbolic curve falling continuously toward Q-axis; starts very high, approaches zero.
- **AVC:** Horizontal line at ₹150/unit (constant average variable cost).
- **ATC:** Curve falling throughout, vertical sum of AFC and AVC, approaching AVC as Q increases.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Question B(a) [7 Marks]: A company has the following cost data:*
*Fixed costs = ₹80,000; Variable cost per unit = ₹60; Selling price per unit = ₹100.*
*Find: (i) Total cost when output is 3,000 units. (ii) Average cost per unit at 3,000 units. (iii) Break-even point in units. (iv) Margin of safety if actual sales = 4,000 units.*

*Question B(b) [7 Marks]: Classify the following into fixed, variable, or semi-variable costs with justification: (i) Telephone bill with fixed rental plus call charges, (ii) Depreciation on machinery, (iii) Direct materials, (iv) Sales commission, (v) Factory rent, (vi) Electricity bill with fixed charge plus consumption charge.*

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Step 1 — Total Cost at Q = 3,000:**

$$TC = TFC + vQ = 80{,}000 + 60(3{,}000) = 80{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 = 2{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2 — Average Cost at Q = 3,000:**

$$ATC = \frac{TC}{Q} = \frac{2{,}60{,}000}{3{,}000} = 86.67 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 3 — Break-even point in units:**

Contribution per unit = $p - v = 100 - 60 = 40$ ₹/unit

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{TFC}{p - v} = \frac{80{,}000}{40} = 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Margin of Safety at 4,000 units:**

$$\text{MoS} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{Break-even Sales} = 4{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{MoS in ₹} = 2{,}000 \times 100 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\text{MoS Ratio} = \frac{2{,}000}{4{,}000} \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Model Solution to B(b):**

| Item | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Telephone bill (rental + calls) | **Semi-variable** | Has both fixed (rental) and variable (calls) elements |
| Depreciation on machinery | **Fixed** | Constant per period regardless of output (straight-line method) |
| Direct materials | **Variable** | Varies directly with units produced |
| Sales commission | **Variable** | Proportional to sales volume |
| Factory rent | **Fixed** | Contracted amount irrespective of output |
| Electricity (fixed + usage charge) | **Semi-variable** | Combines fixed charge with consumption-based charge |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Fixed and Variable Cost**

- **Fixed Cost (FC):** Constant in **total**; **falls per unit** as output rises; examples: rent, salary, depreciation, insurance.
- **Variable Cost (VC):** Varies in **total** with output; **constant per unit**; examples: raw materials, direct labor, sales commission.
- **Master Equation:** $TC = TFC + vQ$ — a linear function in $Q$.
- **TFC is constant; AFC is not.** AFC = TFC / Q is a **hyperbola** approaching zero.
- **AVC is constant and equal to $v$** under linear cost assumption.
- **ATC = AFC + AVC** at every output level.
- **MC = v** for linear variable cost (MC equals AVC equals variable cost per unit).
- **Relevant Range:** The output band where cost behavior assumptions hold. Fixed cost is "fixed" *only* within this range.
- **Semi-variable (Mixed) Cost:** Has both fixed and variable components (e.g., telephone bill, electricity bill). Use high-low method or scattergraph to separate.
- **Contribution Margin:** $p - v$ (selling price minus variable cost per unit); used to compute break-even point: $Q_{BE} = TFC / (p - v)$.
- **Common Pitfall:** Never write AFC as constant — it falls. Always mention units in cost answers. Always state the relevant range assumption.
- **Graphical Signatures:** TFC = horizontal line; TVC = line through origin; TC = parallel to TVC, shifted up by TFC; AFC = declining hyperbola; AVC = horizontal; ATC = declines toward AVC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Total Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In engineering economics and managerial finance, **Total Cost (TC)** is defined as the aggregate monetary expenditure incurred by a firm or engineering project to produce a given level of output (Q) over a specific operating period. Mathematically, it is the summation of all **Fixed Costs (FC)** and **Variable Costs (VC)** that are economically relevant to production.

$$TC(Q) = FC + VC(Q)$$

Where:
- **TC(Q)** = Total Cost as a function of quantity produced (expressed in monetary units, e.g., ₹, $, €).
- **FC** = Fixed Cost (independent of output level).
- **VC(Q)** = Variable Cost (dependent on output level Q).
- **Q** = Quantity of goods produced or units of service rendered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Total Cost is a foundational cost concept in Module 2 (Cost Analysis & Estimation). It is a prerequisite for understanding Break-Even Analysis, Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) modeling, Make-or-Buy decisions, and Marginal Costing — all heavily tested in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚗

Imagine you own a **food truck (mobile restaurant)** parked near a college campus:

- **Fixed Costs (FC) — The "Must-Pay" Bills:** These are costs you pay *every month* regardless of how many vada pavs you sell. They include the **monthly EMI on the truck loan, road permit fee, insurance premium, and the cook's fixed monthly salary**. Even on a day when you sell **zero** items, you still pay these.

- **Variable Costs (VC) — The "Per-Item" Bills:** These costs *grow* with every extra vada pav you make. They include the **cost of potatoes, oil, batter, paper plates, and the helper's overtime wages**. The more you sell, the more you spend on raw materials.

- **Total Cost (TC) = The "Grand Total":** On any given day, if you sold **100 vada pavs**, your **Total Cost** is simply *(what you paid in fixed bills that day) + (the raw materials for those 100 vada pavs)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Fixed Costs create a "floor" of expenditure that must be recovered before the business makes profit, while Variable Costs scale with operational success. Total Cost captures **both** realities into a single number that finance managers analyze daily.

---

## 1.3 Standard Economic Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit (Standard) | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed Cost | FC | ₹ / period (month, year) | Constant, Time-bound |
| Variable Cost | VC | ₹ / period | Output-dependent |
| Total Cost | TC | ₹ / period | Aggregate |
| Output | Q | units (Nos., kg, m³) | Decision Variable |
| Marginal Cost | MC | ₹ / unit | Rate of Change |

> [!TIP]
> Engineering students often confuse FC with *one-time sunk cost* (like a machine purchase). In KTU examinations, **FC is a periodic cost** (e.g., monthly factory rent), not a one-time capitalized expense. This distinction is a common valuation trap.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Costs (Cost Behavior Pattern)

Understanding Total Cost requires decomposing it into its behavioral components. Engineering economists classify costs based on how they **react to changes in production volume (Q)**.

### 2.1.1 Fixed Costs (FC) — The "Inelastic" Component
- Costs that **remain constant in total** over a relevant range of output (called the *relevant range*).
- However, **per-unit FC decreases** as output rises.
- **Examples:** Factory rent, depreciation on buildings, permanent staff salaries, insurance premiums, property taxes.
- **Key Property:** Time-driven, not volume-driven.

### 2.1.2 Variable Costs (VC) — The "Elastic" Component
- Costs that **change in direct proportion** to the volume of output.
- Per-unit VC is generally constant (for linear VC), but total VC rises with Q.
- **Examples:** Raw materials, direct labor wages (piece-rate), packaging, electricity for production machines, fuel.
- **Key Property:** Output-driven, linear or non-linear with Q.

### 2.1.3 Semi-Variable (Mixed) Costs
- Costs that contain **both fixed and variable elements** (e.g., a telephone bill with a fixed rental + per-call charges).
- Often decomposed using the **High-Low Method** in accounting.

### 2.1.4 Step Fixed Costs
- Fixed costs that jump in **discrete steps** at specific capacity thresholds (e.g., hiring a second supervisor after output crosses 1,000 units).

---

## 2.2 The Cost Function Family (Detailed Breakdown)

| Cost Type | Formula | Definition | Curve Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Fixed Cost (TFC)** | $TFC$ | Sum of all periodic fixed costs | Horizontal line |
| **Total Variable Cost (TVC)** | $TVC = v \cdot Q$ | $v$ = variable cost per unit | Upward sloping line |
| **Total Cost (TC)** | $TC = TFC + v \cdot Q$ | Total economic cost | Parallel to TVC (offset by TFC) |
| **Average Fixed Cost (AFC)** | $AFC = \dfrac{TFC}{Q}$ | FC per unit | Hyperbolic (declining) |
| **Average Variable Cost (AVC)** | $AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q}$ | VC per unit | U-shaped (typically) |
| **Average Total Cost (ATC)** | $ATC = \dfrac{TC}{Q}$ | Total cost per unit | U-shaped (typically) |
| **Marginal Cost (MC)** | $MC = \dfrac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q}$ | Cost of producing one additional unit | First derivative of TC |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim. Every KTU 2024 board question on cost analysis will use at least 3 of these formulas.

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Cost | $TC = FC + VC$ | Base cost computation |
| 2 | Total Variable Cost | $TVC = v \cdot Q$ | Linear VC case |
| 3 | Average Cost | $AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q}$ | Per-unit cost analysis |
| 4 | Marginal Cost (Discrete) | $MC_n = TC_n - TC_{n-1}$ | Change-in-cost method |
| 5 | Marginal Cost (Continuous) | $MC = \dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ}$ | Calculus-based derivation |
| 6 | FC Recovery | $FC = TC - VC$ | Cost decomposition |
| 7 | Output at given TC | $Q = \dfrac{TC - FC}{v}$ | Reverse calculation |
| 8 | Total Revenue | $TR = P \cdot Q$ | Used with TC for profit |
| 9 | Profit | $\pi = TR - TC$ | Bottom-line metric |
| 10 | Break-Even Quantity | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{P - v}$ | TC = TR scenario |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** In KTU answer scripts, never confuse **AFC (Average Fixed Cost)** with **AC (Average Cost)**. The full AC includes BOTH fixed and variable components. This is one of the most common 1-mark errors in KTU valuation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Total Cost framework is **not academic** — it underpins:

- **Manufacturing Industries:** A CNC machine shop uses TC analysis to decide batch sizes that minimize ATC.
- **Software/IT Services:** SaaS companies compute TC to determine customer acquisition cost vs. lifetime value.
- **Construction:** Civil engineers use TC = FC + VC to compute unit cost of concrete per m³.
- **Energy Sector:** Power plants separate capacity charges (FC) from fuel costs (VC) for tariff setting.
- **Aerospace:** SpaceX uses TC curves to optimize reusability economics of Falcon 9 boosters.

---

## 2.5 Cost Behavior Visualization Logic

For a **linear VC function** (the most common KTU assumption), TC is also linear:

$$TC(Q) = FC + v \cdot Q$$

The **slope** of the TC line equals the **variable cost per unit ($v$)**, and the **y-intercept** equals **Fixed Cost ($FC$)**. This linear form is heavily used in break-even and CVP problems in Module 2.

For a **non-linear VC function** (curvilinear), TC becomes quadratic or cubic, and MC is derived as the derivative. This appears in advanced optimization questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Total Cost to Average Cost

**Given:** $TC(Q) = FC + VC(Q)$ for any output level $Q$.

**Goal:** Derive the **Average Total Cost (ATC)** function.

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of ATC as total cost divided by output.

$$ATC(Q) = \frac{TC(Q)}{Q}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expanded form of TC.

$$ATC(Q) = \frac{FC + VC(Q)}{Q}$$

**Step 3:** Split the fraction into two terms using algebraic distribution.

$$ATC(Q) = \frac{FC}{Q} + \frac{VC(Q)}{Q}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize each term as a known average concept.

$$ATC(Q) = AFC(Q) + AVC(Q)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Average Total Cost is the sum of Average Fixed Cost and Average Variable Cost. This identity is fundamental to U-shaped cost curve analysis. As $Q \to \infty$, $AFC \to 0$, so $ATC \to AVC$ asymptotically.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Marginal Cost as the Derivative of TC

**Given:** $TC(Q)$ is a differentiable cost function.

**Goal:** Prove that $MC = \dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ}$.

**Step 1:** Recall the discrete definition of marginal cost (cost of the next unit).

$$MC_n = TC_n - TC_{n-1}$$

**Step 2:** Express the difference using a unit change $\Delta Q = 1$.

$$MC = \frac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q}$$

**Step 3:** Take the limit as the change becomes infinitesimally small (the limit definition of a derivative).

$$\lim_{\Delta Q \to 0} \frac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q} = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ}$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions.

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ}$$

**Step 5:** Since $FC$ is constant, $\dfrac{d(FC)}{dQ} = 0$, therefore:

$$MC = \frac{d(VC)}{dQ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Marginal Cost represents the *instantaneous rate* at which total cost rises with each additional unit produced. If $MC < ATC$, average cost is falling; if $MC > ATC$, average cost is rising. The minimum of ATC occurs precisely where $MC = ATC$ — a critical optimization condition.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 (Linear VC Case)

**Problem:** A small-scale LED bulb manufacturing unit has the following data:
- Monthly Fixed Cost (FC) = **₹50,000**
- Variable cost per bulb (v) = **₹120**
- Selling price per bulb (P) = **₹200**

**Find:** (a) Total Cost for producing 1,000 bulbs, (b) Average Cost per bulb, (c) Marginal Cost, (d) Total Revenue, (e) Profit, (f) Break-Even Quantity.

**Solution:**

### Part (a): Total Cost for Q = 1,000

$$TC = FC + v \cdot Q$$

$$TC = 50{,}000 + (120 \times 1{,}000)$$

$$TC = 50{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000$$

$$\boxed{TC = ₹1{,}70{,}000}$$

### Part (b): Average Cost per bulb

$$AC = \frac{TC}{Q} = \frac{1{,}70{,}000}{1{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{AC = ₹170 \text{ per bulb}}$$

### Part (c): Marginal Cost

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = v = ₹120 \text{ per bulb}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a linear cost function, MC is **constant** and equals the variable cost coefficient $v$.

### Part (d): Total Revenue for Q = 1,000

$$TR = P \cdot Q = 200 \times 1{,}000$$

$$\boxed{TR = ₹2{,}00{,}000}$$

### Part (e): Profit

$$\pi = TR - TC = 2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}70{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\pi = ₹30{,}000}$$

### Part (f): Break-Even Quantity

At break-even: $TR = TC$, so $P \cdot Q_{BE} = FC + v \cdot Q_{BE}$.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - v} = \frac{50{,}000}{200 - 120} = \frac{50{,}000}{80}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{BE} = 625 \text{ bulbs}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 (Non-Linear VC Case)

**Problem:** A chemical plant has the following cost function:

$$TC(Q) = 10{,}000 + 50Q + 0.5Q^2$$

Where $Q$ is the output in tonnes and TC is in ₹.

**Find:** (a) FC, VC at Q=100, (b) MC at Q=100, (c) ATC at Q=100, (d) Output at minimum ATC.

**Solution:**

### Part (a): Components of TC

- **Fixed Cost:** $FC = 10{,}000$ (the constant term).
- **Variable Cost at Q = 100:**

$$VC(100) = 50(100) + 0.5(100)^2 = 5{,}000 + 5{,}000 = ₹10{,}000$$

- **Total Cost at Q = 100:**

$$TC(100) = 10{,}000 + 10{,}000 = ₹20{,}000$$

### Part (b): Marginal Cost at Q = 100

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 50 + Q$$

$$MC(100) = 50 + 100 = ₹150 \text{ per tonne}$$

### Part (c): Average Total Cost at Q = 100

$$ATC(100) = \frac{TC(100)}{Q} = \frac{20{,}000}{100} = ₹200 \text{ per tonne}$$

### Part (d): Output at Minimum ATC

$$ATC(Q) = \frac{TC(Q)}{Q} = \frac{10{,}000}{Q} + 50 + 0.5Q$$

Differentiate ATC with respect to $Q$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(ATC)}{dQ} = -\frac{10{,}000}{Q^2} + 0.5 = 0$$

$$0.5 = \frac{10{,}000}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = 20{,}000$$

$$Q = \sqrt{20{,}000} \approx 141.42 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{min\,ATC} \approx 141 \text{ tonnes}}$$

At this output, MC = ATC (verifiable: $MC = 50 + 141.42 \approx 191.42$, and $ATC = 200/141.42 \cdot 1000 \approx 141.4$... recalculation: $ATC(141) = 10000/141 + 50 + 0.5(141) \approx 70.92 + 50 + 70.5 = 191.42$ ✓).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always verify the second derivative is positive ($\dfrac{d^2(ATC)}{dQ^2} > 0$) to confirm a minimum, not a maximum. Here: $\dfrac{d^2(ATC)}{dQ^2} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{Q^3} > 0$ ✓ — confirms a minimum.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | TC Behavior | Decision Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $MC < ATC$ | ATC falling | **Produce more** to reduce unit cost |
| $MC = ATC$ | ATC at minimum | **Optimal output** for cost efficiency |
| $MC > ATC$ | ATC rising | **Reduce output** to avoid cost escalation |
| $TR > TC$ | Positive profit | **Operate / Expand** |
| $TR = TC$ | Zero profit (BEP) | **Threshold volume** achieved |
| $TR < TC$ | Loss | **Re-evaluate pricing or shut down** (if $P < AVC$, shut down) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Total Cost Decomposition Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional decomposition of Total Cost** as it is analyzed in the KTU 2024 syllabus. It maps how engineers conceptually break TC into its constituent cost elements for managerial decision-making.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Cost TC]:::root --> B[Fixed Cost FC]:::fix
    A --> C[Variable Cost VC]:::var
    B --> B1[Factory Rent]:::fix
    B --> B2[Permanent Salaries]:::fix
    B --> B3[Insurance Premium]:::fix
    B --> B4[Depreciation]:::fix
    C --> C1[Raw Materials]:::var
    C --> C2[Direct Labor]:::var
    C --> C3[Power and Fuel]:::var
    C --> C4[Packaging Cost]:::var
    A --> D[Average Cost AC]:::deriv
    A --> E[Marginal Cost MC]:::deriv
    D --> D1[AFC equals FC divided by Q]:::deriv
    D --> D2[AVC equals VC divided by Q]:::deriv
    E --> E1[dTC by dQ]:::deriv
    E --> E2[Change in TC per unit]:::deriv
    A --> F[Engineering Decisions]:::decision
    F --> F1[Make or Buy Analysis]:::decision
    F --> F2[Break Even Point BEP]:::decision
    F --> F3[Profit Maximization]:::decision
    F --> F4[Optimal Output Selection]:::decision

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef fix fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000
    classDef var fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
    classDef deriv fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Flowchart: Cost-Based Decision Logic

This second diagram captures the **decision sequence** an engineering manager follows when using Total Cost analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Engineering Project Initiated]):::start --> P[Identify FC and VC components]:::step
    P --> Q[Determine Output Q]:::step
    Q --> R[Compute TC equals FC plus vQ]:::calc
    R --> T[Compute TR equals P times Q]:::calc
    T --> U{Compare TR and TC}:::judge
    U -->|TR greater than TC| V[Profit Positive: Operate]:::good
    U -->|TR equals TC| W[Break Even: Threshold]:::neutral
    U -->|TR less than TC| X[Loss: Re-evaluate]:::bad
    V --> Y[Analyze MC and ATC]:::step
    Y --> Z{MC versus ATC}:::judge
    Z -->|MC less than ATC| AA[Expand Output]:::good
    Z -->|MC equals ATC| AB[Optimal Cost Efficiency]:::good
    Z -->|MC greater than ATC| AC[Reduce Output]:::bad
    X --> AD{Price versus AVC}:::judge
    AD -->|Price less than AVC| AE[Shut Down Operation]:::bad
    AD -->|Price greater than AVC| AF[Continue but Cut Costs]:::neutral
    AA --> AG([Final Decision: Cost-Optimized Output]):::end
    AB --> AG
    AC --> AG
    AE --> AH([Final Decision: Re-Engineer or Exit]):::end
    AF --> AG

    classDef start fill:#002060,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef end fill:#002060,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
    classDef judge fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#c6efce,stroke:#006100,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#ffeb9c,stroke:#9c5700,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#ffc7ce,stroke:#9c0006,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: TC vs. Output Behavior (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Linear_Case["Linear VC Case"]
        L1[FC constant line]:::fix
        L2[TC starts from FC, rises linearly]:::var
    end
    subgraph Curvilinear_Case["Non-Linear VC Case"]
        N1[TC starts from FC, curves upward]:::deriv
        N2[MC initially falls, then rises]:::deriv
        N3[ATC U-shaped curve]:::deriv
    end
    L2 --> Decision1[Used in BEP and CVP problems]:::decision
    N1 --> Decision2[Used in optimization problems]:::decision
    N2 --> Decision2
    N3 --> Decision2

    classDef fix fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000
    classDef var fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000
    classDef deriv fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Total Cost. Distinguish between Fixed Cost and Variable Cost with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Total Cost (TC):** Total Cost is the aggregate expenditure incurred by a firm to produce a given level of output. It is the sum of Fixed Cost and Variable Cost:

$$TC = FC + VC$$

**Fixed Cost (FC):** Costs that remain constant in total irrespective of the level of output, within a relevant range. They are time-dependent.
- *Example 1:* Annual factory rent of ₹5,00,000.
- *Example 2:* Monthly salary of the production manager (₹80,000/month).

**Variable Cost (VC):** Costs that vary directly and proportionately with the volume of output.
- *Example 1:* Cost of raw materials (₹200 per unit produced).
- *Example 2:* Direct wages paid on a piece-rate basis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** FC is incurred even at **zero output**, while VC is **zero at zero output**. Per-unit FC *decreases* with output, but per-unit VC typically remains constant (in linear cases).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of Marginal Cost. How is it related to Total Cost?**

**Model Answer:**

**Marginal Cost (MC)** is the additional cost incurred in producing **one extra unit** of output. It measures the rate of change of Total Cost with respect to output.

**Discrete (Tabular) Form:**

$$MC_n = TC_n - TC_{n-1}$$

**Continuous (Calculus) Form:**

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ}$$

**Relationship with Total Cost:** Since $TC = FC + VC$, and $FC$ is constant (so $\dfrac{d(FC)}{dQ} = 0$):

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = \frac{d(VC)}{dQ}$$

**Significance:** MC indicates whether producing an additional unit is economical. If $MC < AC$, average cost is falling (economies of scale). If $MC > AC$, average cost is rising (diseconomies of scale). The minimum ATC occurs where $MC = AC$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. You must attempt either **Question A OR Question B** (internal choice).

---

### ✍️ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A firm manufacturing electric vehicles has a fixed cost of ₹8,00,000 per month. The variable cost per vehicle is ₹3,50,000 and the selling price is ₹5,00,000 per vehicle.**

**(a)** Calculate the Total Cost, Average Cost, Total Revenue, and Profit when the firm produces 100 vehicles per month. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the Break-Even Point (BEP) in units and in rupees. Also find the profit/loss if production is 80 vehicles. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Fixed Cost ($FC$) = ₹8,00,000
- Variable cost per unit ($v$) = ₹3,50,000
- Selling price per unit ($P$) = ₹5,00,000
- Output ($Q$) = 100 vehicles

#### **Part (a):** Computations for Q = 100

**Step 1: Total Cost (TC)**

$$TC = FC + v \cdot Q$$

$$TC = 8{,}00{,}000 + (3{,}50{,}000 \times 100)$$

$$TC = 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{TC = ₹3{,}58{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 2: Average Cost (AC)**

$$AC = \frac{TC}{Q} = \frac{3{,}58{,}00{,}000}{100}$$

$$\boxed{AC = ₹3{,}58{,}000 \text{ per vehicle}}$$

**Step 3: Total Revenue (TR)**

$$TR = P \cdot Q = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{TR = ₹5{,}00{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 4: Profit ($\pi$)**

$$\pi = TR - TC = 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}58{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\pi = ₹1{,}42{,}00{,}000}$$

#### **Part (b):** Break-Even Point and Production at Q = 80

**Step 1: Break-Even Quantity (units)**

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - v} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}50{,}000}$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{BE} \approx 5.33 \text{ vehicles} \rightarrow \text{6 vehicles (practical)}}$$

**Step 2: Break-Even Sales (₹)**

$$TR_{BE} = P \cdot Q_{BE} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 5.33$$

$$\boxed{TR_{BE} \approx ₹26{,}66{,}667}$$

**Step 3: TC and TR at Q = 80**

$$TC_{80} = 8{,}00{,}000 + (3{,}50{,}000 \times 80) = 8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}88{,}00{,}000$$

$$TR_{80} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 80 = ₹4{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4: Profit at Q = 80**

$$\pi_{80} = TR_{80} - TC_{80} = 4{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}88{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\pi_{80} = ₹1{,}12{,}00{,}000 \text{ (Profit)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $Q = 80 > Q_{BE} = 5.33$, the firm operates above break-even and earns a profit. The low BEP indicates **high operating leverage** — most of the cost is fixed, so any revenue beyond ₹26.67 lakh is largely profit.

---

### ✍️ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**A manufacturing company's Total Cost function is given by $TC(Q) = 25{,}000 + 200Q + 2Q^2$, where Q is output in units.**

**(a)** Identify the Fixed Cost and Variable Cost components. Calculate TC, AC, and MC at $Q = 50$ units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the output level at which Average Cost is minimum. What is the minimum AC value? Verify using the MC = AC rule. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given Cost Function:**

$$TC(Q) = 25{,}000 + 200Q + 2Q^2$$

#### **Part (a):** Cost Decomposition and Analysis at Q = 50

**Step 1: Identify FC and VC**

Comparing with the general form $TC = FC + aQ + bQ^2$:

$$FC = ₹25{,}000 \quad \text{(constant term)}$$

$$VC(Q) = 200Q + 2Q^2 \quad \text{(Q-dependent term)}$$

**Step 2: Total Cost at Q = 50**

$$TC(50) = 25{,}000 + 200(50) + 2(50)^2$$

$$TC(50) = 25{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 5{,}000$$

$$\boxed{TC(50) = ₹40{,}000}$$

**Step 3: Average Cost at Q = 50**

$$AC(50) = \frac{TC(50)}{Q} = \frac{40{,}000}{50}$$

$$\boxed{AC(50) = ₹800 \text{ per unit}}$$

**Step 4: Marginal Cost at Q = 50**

$$MC = \frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 200 + 4Q$$

$$MC(50) = 200 + 4(50) = 200 + 200$$

$$\boxed{MC(50) = ₹400 \text{ per unit}}$$

#### **Part (b):** Minimum Average Cost Analysis

**Step 1: Express AC as a function of Q**

$$AC(Q) = \frac{TC(Q)}{Q} = \frac{25{,}000}{Q} + 200 + 2Q$$

**Step 2: Differentiate AC with respect to Q and set to zero**

$$\frac{d(AC)}{dQ} = -\frac{25{,}000}{Q^2} + 2 = 0$$

$$2 = \frac{25{,}000}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = 12{,}500$$

$$Q = \sqrt{12{,}500} = 50\sqrt{5} \approx 111.80$$

$$\boxed{Q^* \approx 111.8 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 3: Verify with Second-Order Condition**

$$\frac{d^2(AC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{50{,}000}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{(for all } Q > 0\text{)}$$

This confirms a **minimum** (not maximum). ✓

**Step 4: Minimum AC Value**

$$AC(111.8) = \frac{25{,}000}{111.8} + 200 + 2(111.8)$$

$$AC(111.8) \approx 223.61 + 200 + 223.60$$

$$\boxed{AC_{min} \approx ₹647.21 \text{ per unit}}$$

**Step 5: Verification using MC = AC rule**

At $Q^* \approx 111.8$:

$$MC(111.8) = 200 + 4(111.8) = 200 + 447.2 = ₹647.2$$

$$AC(111.8) \approx ₹647.21$$

$$\boxed{MC = AC \quad \text{✓ Verified}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The verification confirms the **fundamental economic theorem**: Average Cost is at its minimum precisely where Marginal Cost equals Average Cost. This is a KTU favorite for 1-mark conceptual cross-questions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing AC with AFC:** Writing $AC = FC/Q$ (this is **AFC**, not **AC**). Marks deducted: 1 per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract FC from TC** when asked for VC. Always state: $VC = TC - FC$.
> 3. **Rounding Break-Even Q carelessly:** KTU accepts 1–2 decimal places, but **never** show the working with only the final answer. Always show the formula first.
> 4. **Mixing up units:** BEP in *units* ≠ BEP in *rupees*. BEP (₹) = BEP (units) × Price.
> 5. **Ignoring the relevant range:** Linear cost assumptions fail if the question mentions capacity constraints — read carefully.
> 6. **Skipping units in final answers:** Always write **₹/unit** or **₹/period** explicitly to avoid losing 0.5 marks per omission.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- **TC = FC + VC** is the foundational identity. Master it first.
- **FC** is **time-driven**; **VC** is **output-driven**. Use this test to classify any cost.
- **Average Cost (AC)** = $TC/Q$; **Marginal Cost (MC)** = $\Delta TC / \Delta Q$ or $d(TC)/dQ$.
- **AFC falls** continuously as $Q$ rises (hyperbolic decay); AFC → 0 as $Q$ → ∞.
- **MC intersects AC and AVC at their minimum points** — this is a guaranteed KTU question.
- For **linear VC**, MC is constant and equal to $v$ (the variable cost coefficient).
- For **quadratic TC** (e.g., $TC = a + bQ + cQ^2$), MC is **linear**: $MC = b + 2cQ$.
- **Break-Even Point** formula: $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{P - v}$ (assumes linear cost and revenue).
- **Profit maximization rule:** $MR = MC$ in microeconomics; in linear revenue cases ($P$ = constant), profit is maximized at the highest feasible $Q$ within the relevant range.
- **Shut-down rule:** If $P < AVC$ in the short run, the firm should shut down (it cannot cover variable costs).
- **Operating leverage** is high when FC is large relative to VC — small sales changes produce large profit swings.
- Always **show the formula before substitution** in KTU exams — this fetches partial marks even if arithmetic errors occur.
- The **U-shaped ATC curve** is due to the opposing forces of declining AFC and rising AVC.
- For **Module 2** specifically, KTU focuses on linear cost-revenue models and break-even computation — practice these exhaustively.
- **Don't mix** accounting cost with economic cost (economic cost includes *opportunity cost* of capital, not asked in basic KTU 2024 paper).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "The Four A's of Cost":**
> **A**verage Fixed Cost (AFC) + **A**verage Variable Cost (AVC) = **A**verage Total Cost (ATC), and the **A**dditional unit cost = Marginal Cost (MC). All four are derivable from TC in under 30 seconds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breakeven Point and Breakeven Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Breakeven Point and Breakeven Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the level of sales (in units or in monetary value) at which **Total Revenue (TR) equals Total Cost (TC)**. At this point, the firm earns **zero profit and zero loss** — operating expenses are exactly recovered by sales revenue.

Mathematically, the break-even condition is:

$$TR = TC \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Profit} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Break-Even Analysis (BEA) is a **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP)** analytical tool that helps management determine the minimum sales volume required to avoid loss, and to study the effect of changes in cost, volume, and profit on operating income.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a small **lemonade stall** during summer. You have a **fixed** cost of ₹500 (the ice box rental for the day) and each glass of lemonade costs you **₹10** in lemons, sugar, and ice (variable cost). You sell each glass for **₹30**.

- For the **first glass**: you earn ₹30, but your total cost is ₹500 + ₹10 = ₹510 → **loss of ₹20**.
- Each additional glass adds ₹20 of "extra profit" (since ₹30 − ₹10 = ₹20 per glass).
- You will recover the ₹500 fixed cost when you sell: 500 ÷ 20 = **25 glasses**.

That **25th glass is your break-even point**. The 26th glass and beyond? Pure profit. That is the entire essence of break-even analysis.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Cost (FC)** | Costs that remain constant irrespective of output (rent, salary, depreciation) | **Time-based** |
| **Variable Cost (VC)** | Costs that vary in direct proportion to output (raw material, direct labour) | **Volume-based** |
| **Semi-Variable Cost** | Costs having both fixed and variable components (e.g., electricity bill) | **Mixed** |
| **Contribution (C)** | Sales minus Variable Cost — the amount that "contributes" towards covering FC and profit | Per unit |
| **P/V Ratio** | Contribution ÷ Sales × 100 — measures efficiency of variable cost recovery | Percentage |
| **Margin of Safety (MoS)** | Excess of actual/expected sales over break-even sales | Buffer zone |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Valuation:** All monetary values are usually in **Rupees (₹)** and units in **numbers**. P/V ratio is expressed as a **percentage (%)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

Break-even analysis rests on the **classification of costs** into two mutually exclusive categories:

1. **Fixed Costs (FC):** These do **not vary** with the level of production or sales within a relevant range. Examples include rent, insurance, manager's salary, depreciation on plant. Total Fixed Cost is a **horizontal straight line** when plotted against output.

2. **Variable Costs (VC):** These vary **directly and proportionately** with the level of production. Total Variable Cost is a **straight line passing through the origin** when plotted against output.

3. **Total Cost (TC):** $TC = FC + VC$. The TC line is parallel to the VC line, starting at FC on the Y-axis.

4. **Total Revenue (TR):** $TR = \text{Sales Price per unit} \times \text{Quantity sold}$. The TR line passes through the origin with slope equal to the selling price.

5. **Break-Even Point:** The intersection of the **TR line** and the **TC line**. At this intersection, the firm neither earns profit nor incurs loss.

### 2.2 Why Does BEP Matter?

- **Pricing Decisions:** Helps determine minimum selling price to avoid losses.
- **Cost Control:** Identifies how much volume is needed to cover fixed overheads.
- **Profit Planning:** Required sales for a target profit can be computed.
- **Risk Assessment:** Lower BEP ⇒ lower risk; higher BEP ⇒ higher risk.
- **Capacity Expansion:** Assists in evaluating make-or-buy and capacity-addition decisions.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving any KTU numerical on Break-Even Analysis. Memorize these formulas.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Remarks |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Contribution per unit | $c = S - V$ | $S$ = Selling price/unit, $V$ = Variable cost/unit |
| 2 | Total Contribution | $C = (S - V) \times Q$ | Where $Q$ is quantity sold |
| 3 | **BEP in units** | $\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{FC}{S - V}$ | Most frequently asked formula |
| 4 | **BEP in ₹ (Sales Value)** | $\text{BEP}_{₹} = \dfrac{FC}{P/V \text{ ratio}}$ | Uses P/V ratio |
| 5 | P/V Ratio | $\dfrac{C}{S} \times 100 = \dfrac{S - V}{S} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| 6 | Profit at given sales $Q$ | $\pi = (S - V)Q - FC$ | Or: $\pi = C - FC$ |
| 7 | Required sales for target profit $P$ | $Q_P = \dfrac{FC + P}{S - V}$ | Add target profit to numerator |
| 8 | Margin of Safety (in units) | $\text{MoS}_Q = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$ | In quantity |
| 9 | Margin of Safety (in ₹) | $\text{MoS}_{₹} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$ | In monetary value |
| 10 | MoS as % of Sales | $\dfrac{\text{MoS}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ | Indicates risk buffer |
| 11 | Cash Break-Even Point | $\dfrac{FC - \text{Depreciation}}{S - V}$ | Excludes non-cash FC |
| 12 | Shutdown Point | Sales level where revenue equals **variable cost only** | Useful for short-run decisions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Note:** The vertical bar $\vert$ used for **modulus** or **absolute value** in formulas (e.g., $\vert S - V \vert$) is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX to **avoid breaking markdown table syntax**. KTU examiners may write "Contribution = Sales − VC" in words for clarity.

### 2.4 Assumptions of Break-Even Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> KTU exams frequently ask: *"State the assumptions of BEP analysis."* Memorize the following six:

1. **Linear Behaviour:** Both fixed and variable costs behave linearly within the relevant range.
2. **Constant Selling Price:** Sales price per unit remains unchanged irrespective of volume.
3. **Constant Variable Cost per Unit:** Variable cost per unit remains constant.
4. **Single Product (or Constant Mix):** Either one product is sold, or sales mix remains constant in multi-product firms.
5. **No Inventory Changes:** Production equals sales (opening stock = closing stock).
6. **Cost-Volume-Profit Predictability:** Operating efficiency, technology, and market conditions remain stable.

### 2.5 Limitations

- Impractical linearity assumptions in real industries.
- Unsuitable for service organizations with predominantly fixed costs.
- Ignores external factors like competition, taxation changes, inflation.
- Multi-product firms require constant sales-mix assumption.
- Break-even is a static snapshot — not dynamic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Point (in Units)

We start from the fundamental condition for break-even:

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \text{Total Cost}$$

Let:
- $S$ = Selling price per unit
- $V$ = Variable cost per unit
- $FC$ = Total fixed cost
- $Q$ = Number of units produced and sold

Step 1 — Write the equation for Total Revenue:
$$TR = S \times Q$$

Step 2 — Write the equation for Total Cost:
$$TC = FC + (V \times Q)$$

Step 3 — Equate $TR$ and $TC$ (break-even condition):
$$S \times Q = FC + (V \times Q)$$

Step 4 — Bring the variable terms to one side:
$$S \times Q - (V \times Q) = FC$$

Step 5 — Factor out $Q$:
$$Q \times (S - V) = FC$$

Step 6 — Solve for $Q$:
$$Q = \frac{FC}{S - V}$$

This quantity $Q$ is the **Break-Even Point in units**, often denoted $\text{BEP}_Q$.

> [!NOTE]
> The term $(S - V)$ is the **Contribution per unit** ($c$). Hence the BEP can be interpreted as: **"How many units of contribution are needed to recover the fixed cost?"**

### 3.2 Derivation of the Break-Even Point (in Sales Value ₹)

Step 1 — Start from the BEP in units:
$$Q_{\text{BEP}} = \frac{FC}{S - V}$$

Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $S$ to get BEP in monetary value:
$$\text{BEP}_{₹} = S \times Q_{\text{BEP}} = S \times \frac{FC}{S - V}$$

Step 3 — Divide numerator and denominator by $S$:
$$\text{BEP}_{₹} = \frac{FC}{\dfrac{S - V}{S}}$$

Step 4 — Recognize the denominator as the **P/V ratio**:
$$\text{BEP}_{₹} = \frac{FC}{\text{P/V ratio}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** When computing P/V ratio, do **not** multiply by 100 in the denominator — use the **decimal form** (e.g., 0.40, not 40). If you do multiply by 100, then the formula becomes $\text{BEP}_{₹} = \dfrac{FC \times 100}{\text{P/V \%}}$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Required Sales for Target Profit

Step 1 — We require the desired profit $P$ to be earned in addition to recovering FC.
Step 2 — Modify the break-even condition:
$$S \times Q = FC + (V \times Q) + P$$

Step 3 — Rearrange:
$$Q \times (S - V) = FC + P$$

Step 4 — Solve:
$$Q = \frac{FC + P}{S - V}$$

### 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Model Solution Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU Pattern):** A company sells a product at ₹40 per unit. Variable cost is ₹16 per unit and fixed cost per year is ₹96,000. Find: (a) BEP in units and in ₹, (b) Sales required to earn a profit of ₹24,000, (c) Margin of Safety if actual sales are 6,000 units, and (d) Net profit at 6,000 units.

**Given Data:**
- $S = ₹40$
- $V = ₹16$
- $FC = ₹96{,}000$
- Target Profit $P = ₹24{,}000$
- Actual Sales $Q_a = 6{,}000$ units

**Part (a) — BEP in units:**

$$\text{BEP}_Q = \frac{FC}{S - V} = \frac{96{,}000}{40 - 16} = \frac{96{,}000}{24} = 4{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Part (a) — BEP in ₹ (Sales Value):**

First compute P/V ratio:
$$\text{P/V} = \frac{S - V}{S} = \frac{40 - 16}{40} = \frac{24}{40} = 0.60 = 60\%$$

Now compute BEP in sales value:
$$\text{BEP}_{₹} = \frac{FC}{\text{P/V ratio}} = \frac{96{,}000}{0.60} = ₹1{,}60{,}000$$

*Verification:* $4{,}000 \text{ units} \times ₹40 = ₹1{,}60{,}000$ ✓

**Part (b) — Sales for Target Profit of ₹24,000:**

$$Q_P = \frac{FC + P}{S - V} = \frac{96{,}000 + 24{,}000}{24} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{24} = 5{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Part (c) — Margin of Safety (in units and as a percentage):**

$$\text{MoS}_Q = Q_a - \text{BEP}_Q = 6{,}000 - 4{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{MoS}_{\%} = \frac{\text{MoS}_Q}{Q_a} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}000}{6{,}000} \times 100 = 33.33\%$$

**Part (d) — Net Profit at 6,000 units:**

Using the contribution-based approach:
$$\pi = (S - V) \times Q_a - FC = 24 \times 6{,}000 - 96{,}000 = 1{,}44{,}000 - 96{,}000 = ₹48{,}000$$

**Cross-Check using MoS:**
$$\pi = \text{P/V ratio} \times \text{MoS}_{₹} = 0.60 \times (2{,}000 \times 40) = 0.60 \times 80{,}000 = ₹48{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Question Patterns

| Question Type | What is Asked | Core Formula(s) Used |
|---|---|---|
| Basic BEP | BEP in units and ₹ | $\frac{FC}{S-V}$, $\frac{FC}{\text{P/V}}$ |
| Target Profit | Sales units needed for ₹X profit | $\frac{FC+P}{S-V}$ |
| Margin of Safety | Buffer in units / % of sales | $Q_a - \text{BEP}_Q$ |
| Multi-product BEP | Weighted avg. contribution | $\sum (\text{Sales Mix} \times C)$ |
| Cash BEP | Exclude non-cash FC (depreciation) | $\frac{FC_{\text{cash}}}{S-V}$ |
| Change in scenario | New BEP after price/cost change | Recompute with new parameters |
| P/V ratio & Profit | Find missing variable | $\pi = \text{P/V} \times \text{Sales} - FC$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Break-Even Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT PARAMETERS] --> A1[Fixed Cost FC]
    A --> A2[Variable Cost per unit V]
    A --> A3[Selling Price per unit S]
    A --> A4[Actual or Expected Sales Q]

    A1 --> B[CALCULATION LAYER]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B

    B --> B1[Contribution per unit c = S minus V]
    B --> B2[P over V Ratio equals c divided by S times 100]
    B --> B3[BEP in Units equals FC divided by c]
    B --> B4[BEP in Rupees equals FC divided by P over V]
    B --> B5[Margin of Safety equals Q minus BEP Q]

    B1 --> C[OUTPUT LAYER]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C

    C --> C1[Zero profit Loss Zone]
    C --> C2[Profit Zone above BEP]
    C --> C3[Loss Zone below BEP]
    C --> C4[Risk Buffer MoS]

    C1 --> D[DECISION SUPPORT]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> D1[Pricing Strategy]
    D --> D2[Cost Control]
    D --> D3[Capacity Planning]
    D --> D4[Profit Forecasting]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Schematic of the Break-Even Chart

The break-even chart is a **graphical representation** with **Sales Volume (units)** on the X-axis and **Cost/Revenue (₹)** on the Y-axis. Below is the architectural layout of all curves and zones:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph XY[BREAK EVEN CHART COMPONENTS]
        direction TB
        X[Horizontal Axis X: Sales Volume in units]
        Y[Vertical Axis Y: Cost or Revenue in Rupees]

        subgraph LINES[FIVE KEY LINES]
            direction TB
            L1[FC LINE: Horizontal line at FC level]
            L2[VC LINE: Straight line from origin with slope V]
            L3[TC LINE: Parallel to VC starting at FC]
            L4[TR LINE: Straight line from origin with slope S]
        end

        subgraph POINTS[KEY POINTS]
            direction TB
            P1[Origin O]
            P2[Break Even Point BEP intersection of TR and TC]
            P3[Profit Zone above BEP]
            P4[Loss Zone below BEP]
        end

        subgraph ZONES[FOUR ZONES]
            direction TB
            Z1[Fixed Cost Area rectangle between FC line and X axis]
            Z2[Variable Cost Area triangle under VC line]
            Z3[Contribution Area between TR and VC lines]
            Z4[Profit Area triangle between TR and TC above BEP]
        end
    end

    L1 --> P2
    L3 --> P2
    L4 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
```

### 4.3 Visual Description of the Break-Even Chart (For Student Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagination Aid:** Picture a **2-D Cartesian graph**.
> - Draw a **horizontal line** at the FC level → this is the **FC line** (it never changes).
> - Draw a **sloping line from the origin** → this is the **VC line** (slope = $V$).
> - The **TC line** is drawn **parallel to VC**, but starts at height $FC$ on the Y-axis.
> - Draw the **TR line** from the origin with slope $S$ (where $S > V$).
> - The point where **TR crosses TC** is the **BEP**.
> - To the **left** of BEP → **Loss Zone** (TR < TC).
> - To the **right** of BEP → **Profit Zone** (TR > TC).
> - The **rectangle** below TC and above FC is the **Fixed Cost area**.
> - The **triangle** below VC and above the X-axis is the **Variable Cost area**.
> - The **area between TR and TC, to the right of BEP** is the **Profit area**.
> - The **Margin of Safety** is the **horizontal distance** between the BEP and the actual sales point.

### 4.4 Effect of Key Parameters on the Break-Even Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PARAMETER CHANGE] --> A1[Increase in Fixed Cost]
    A --> A2[Increase in Selling Price]
    A --> A3[Increase in Variable Cost]
    A --> A4[Decrease in Selling Price]

    A1 --> R1[FC line shifts UP, TC line shifts UP, BEP moves RIGHT, profit DECREASES at same output]
    A2 --> R2[TR line slope INCREASES, BEP moves LEFT and DOWN, profit INCREASES]
    A3 --> R3[VC and TC line slopes STEEPER, BEP moves RIGHT and UP, profit DECREASES]
    A4 --> R4[TR line slope DECREASES, BEP moves RIGHT and UP, profit DECREASES]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **Break-Even Point**. Explain the concept of **Margin of Safety**.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the level of sales at which **Total Revenue equals Total Cost**, i.e., the firm earns **zero profit and zero loss**. It can be expressed in **units** $\left(\dfrac{FC}{S-V}\right)$ or in **sales value** $\left(\dfrac{FC}{\text{P/V ratio}}\right)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Margin of Safety (MoS)** is the difference between **actual (or expected) sales** and the **break-even sales**. It represents the **buffer zone** of sales that can drop before the firm incurs a loss. It can be expressed in units, in ₹, or as a percentage of actual sales: $\text{MoS}_{\%} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$. A higher MoS indicates lower operating risk.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is **P/V ratio**? Why is it considered a vital tool in Break-Even Analysis?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **P/V Ratio (Profit/Volume Ratio)** is the ratio of **Contribution to Sales**, expressed as a percentage. Mathematically, $\text{P/V} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 = \dfrac{S - V}{S} \times 100$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Importance of P/V ratio:**
> 1. It measures the **efficiency of variable cost recovery** — higher P/V means lower variable cost burden.
> 2. It enables calculation of **BEP in ₹** directly: $\text{BEP}_{₹} = \dfrac{FC}{\text{P/V ratio}}$.
> 3. It helps in **profit forecasting**: $\text{Profit} = \text{P/V ratio} \times \text{Sales} - FC$.
> 4. It aids in **comparative analysis** between product lines and divisions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module examinations follow the **internal choice pattern**. The student answers **either** Option A **or** Option B. Both options are provided below.

---

#### **OPTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**Q. (a)** A manufacturing company has the following cost data:
- Selling Price per unit: **₹200**
- Variable Cost per unit: **₹120**
- Total Fixed Cost: **₹4,00,000**

Compute:
1. Break-Even Point in **units** and in **₹**.
2. Sales required to earn a target profit of **₹6,00,000**.
3. Net profit at an actual sale of **12,000 units**.
4. Margin of Safety (in units and as a %).

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**
- $S = ₹200$, $V = ₹120$, $FC = ₹4{,}00{,}000$, Target profit $P = ₹6{,}00{,}000$, Actual $Q_a = 12{,}000$

**Step 2 — Contribution per unit:**
$$c = S - V = 200 - 120 = ₹80$$

**Step 3 — P/V Ratio:**
$$\text{P/V} = \frac{c}{S} = \frac{80}{200} = 0.40 = 40\%$$

**Step 4 — BEP in units:**
$$\text{BEP}_Q = \frac{FC}{c} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{80} = 5{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — BEP in ₹:**
$$\text{BEP}_{₹} = \frac{FC}{\text{P/V ratio}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = ₹10{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Sales for target profit:**
$$Q_P = \frac{FC + P}{c} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000}{80} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{80} = 12{,}500 \text{ units}$$

**Step 7 — Net Profit at 12,000 units:**
$$\pi = (c \times Q_a) - FC = (80 \times 12{,}000) - 4{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}60{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹5{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 8 — Margin of Safety:**
$$\text{MoS}_Q = Q_a - \text{BEP}_Q = 12{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 7{,}000 \text{ units}$$
$$\text{MoS}_{\%} = \frac{7{,}000}{12{,}000} \times 100 = 58.33\%$$

**Verification of Profit via MoS:**
$$\pi = \text{P/V} \times \text{MoS}_{₹} = 0.40 \times (7{,}000 \times 200) = 0.40 \times 14{,}00{,}000 = ₹5{,}60{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **conceptual diagram of a Break-Even Chart**. List the **assumptions** and **limitations** of Break-Even Analysis.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Conceptual Diagram of Break-Even Chart:**

The Break-Even Chart is a graphical tool where **Sales Volume (units)** is plotted on the **X-axis** and **Cost/Revenue (₹)** on the **Y-axis**. The chart contains the following:

1. **Fixed Cost Line:** A horizontal straight line at the level of total fixed cost.
2. **Variable Cost Line:** A straight line passing through the origin with slope equal to the variable cost per unit.
3. **Total Cost Line:** A line parallel to the VC line, but starting at FC on the Y-axis.
4. **Total Revenue Line:** A straight line from the origin with slope equal to the selling price per unit.
5. **Break-Even Point:** The point of intersection of TR and TC lines.
6. **Loss Zone:** Region to the **left of BEP**, where TR < TC.
7. **Profit Zone:** Region to the **right of BEP**, where TR > TC.
8. **Margin of Safety:** Horizontal distance between BEP and actual sales line.

**Assumptions of BEA:**
1. All costs can be classified into fixed and variable components.
2. Fixed cost remains constant within the relevant range.
3. Variable cost per unit is constant (linearity).
4. Selling price per unit is constant.
5. Either a single product is sold or the sales mix is constant.
6. Production equals sales (no inventory change).

**Limitations of BEA:**
1. Unrealistic assumption of linear cost and revenue behaviour.
2. Cannot handle multi-product firms with varying mixes.
3. Ignores external factors (taxation, inflation, competition).
4. Static analysis — not suitable for long-term strategic decisions.
5. Difficult classification of semi-variable costs.
6. Profit and loss data from a single product line may be misleading.

---

#### **OPTION B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**Q. (a)** A company has fixed costs of **₹3,00,000** and variable cost of **₹50 per unit**. The product is sold at **₹100 per unit**.
1. Calculate the **Break-Even Point** in units and in rupees.
2. What is the **Margin of Safety** if the company sells **8,000 units**?
3. If the selling price is reduced to **₹80 per unit**, what is the **new BEP**?

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**
- $S_1 = ₹100$, $V = ₹50$, $FC = ₹3{,}00{,}000$, $Q_a = 8{,}000$

**Step 2 — Original BEP in units:**
$$\text{BEP}_Q = \frac{FC}{S - V} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{100 - 50} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{50} = 6{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Original BEP in ₹:**
$$\text{BEP}_{₹} = 6{,}000 \times 100 = ₹6{,}00{,}000$$

*Alternative method:* $\text{P/V} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.50$, so $\text{BEP}_{₹} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{0.50} = ₹6{,}00{,}000$ ✓

**Step 4 — Margin of Safety:**
$$\text{MoS}_Q = Q_a - \text{BEP}_Q = 8{,}000 - 6{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$
$$\text{MoS}_{₹} = 2{,}000 \times 100 = ₹2{,}00{,}000$$
$$\text{MoS}_{\%} = \frac{2{,}000}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Step 5 — New BEP after price reduction to ₹80:**

New contribution per unit:
$$c_{\text{new}} = 80 - 50 = ₹30$$

$$\text{BEP}_{Q,\text{new}} = \frac{FC}{c_{\text{new}}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{30} = 10{,}000 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{BEP}_{₹,\text{new}} = 10{,}000 \times 80 = ₹8{,}00{,}000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inference:** A **20% reduction in selling price** has caused BEP to **rise from 6,000 to 10,000 units** — a **66.67% increase** in break-even volume. The firm must now sell **2,000 additional units** beyond original sales just to break even.

---

**Q. (b)** Distinguish between **Cash Break-Even Point** and **Conventional Break-Even Point**. When is the cash BEP preferred?

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Conventional BEP | Cash Break-Even Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Sales level where TR = Total Cost (including non-cash) | Sales level where Cash Inflow = Cash Outflow |
| **Fixed Cost Treatment** | Includes **all** FC, including depreciation | Excludes **non-cash** FC like depreciation |
| **Formula** | $\dfrac{FC}{S - V}$ | $\dfrac{FC - \text{Depreciation}}{S - V}$ |
| **Output Value** | Always **higher** (more units needed) | Always **lower** (fewer units needed) |
| **Use Case** | Long-run profit planning and accounting view | Short-run cash-flow and liquidity planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **When is Cash BEP Preferred?**
> Cash BEP is preferred in the following scenarios:
> 1. When a company is facing **short-term liquidity crisis** and must ensure minimum cash inflows.
> 2. During **startup or turnaround phases**, when actual cash outflows matter more than accounting profits.
> 3. For **bank loan appraisal**, where lenders evaluate the firm's ability to service debt in cash.
> 4. When significant **non-cash expenses** (e.g., heavy depreciation) distort the conventional BEP.
> 5. In **seasonal businesses** where monthly cash flow planning is critical.

**Illustrative Formula:**
$$\text{Cash BEP (units)} = \frac{FC_{\text{cash}}}{S - V} = \frac{FC - \text{Depreciation}}{S - V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
