It directly influences **bank borrowing limits and credit policy** decisions. --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Distinguish between Operating Cycle and Net Operating Cycle.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Operating Cycle (OC) | Net Operating Cycle (NOC) | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Total time from raw material purchase to cash collection | Time financed by the firm's own capital (after supplier credit) | | **Formula** | $OC = R + W + F + D$ | $NOC = R + W + F + D - C$ | | **Supplier Credit** | Not considered | Subtracted via Payables Deferral Period | | **Indicates** | Total operational duration | True cash exposure of the firm | | **Working Capital Implication** | Gross requirement | Net working capital requirement | --- ### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]` **M/s. Techno Mech Industries** furnishes the following information for the year ended 31-03-2024: | Item | ₹ (in lakhs) | |---|---| | Average Raw Material Stock | 80 | | Average Work-in-Progress | 100 | | Average Finished Goods Stock | 120 | | Average Debtors | 180 | | Average Creditors | 90 | | Raw Material Consumed | 720 | | Production Cost | 1,080 | | Cost of Goods Sold | 1,440 | | Credit Sales | 1,800 | | Credit Purchases | 864 | **(a)** Compute the duration of each phase of the Operating Cycle. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Calculate the Operating Cycle and Net Operating Cycle. Interpret the result. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **Raw Material Storage Period (R):** $$R = \frac{80 \times 365}{720} = \frac{80}{720} \times 365 = 40.56 \text{ days}$$ **Work-in-Progress Period (W):** $$W = \frac{100 \times 365}{1{,}080} = 33.80 \text{ days}$$ **Finished Goods Storage Period (F):** $$F = \frac{120 \times 365}{1{,}440} = 30.42 \text{ days}$$ **Debtors Collection Period (D):** $$D = \frac{180 \times 365}{1{,}800} = 36.50 \text{ days}$$ **Payables Deferral Period (C):** $$C = \frac{90 \times 365}{864} = 38.02 \text{ days}$$ --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Operating Cycle:** $$OC = R + W + F + D = 40.56 + 33.80 + 30.42 + 36.50 = 141.28 \text{ days}$$ **Net Operating Cycle:** $$NOC = OC - C = 141.28 - 38.02 = 103.26 \text{ days}$$ **Interpretation:** - The total operating cycle is **~141 days**, indicating a moderately long production-to-cash cycle typical of engineering manufacturing. - Out of this, **~38 days** are financed by supplier credit, and the remaining **~103 days** require the firm's own working capital. - The firm should consider tightening the **Receivables Collection Period (D)** through stricter credit policy and discount for early payment to reduce the NOC. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]` **M/s. Coastal Engineering Works** has provided the following data: | Item | ₹ | |---|---| | Sales (90% on credit) | 20,00,000 | | Cost of Goods Sold | 14,00,000 | | Raw Material Consumed (70% of COGS) | 9,80,000 | | Production Cost | 16,00,000 | | Average Raw Material Stock | 1,40,000 | | Average Work-in-Progress | 1,60,000 | | Average Finished Goods Stock | 2,10,000 | | Average Debtors | 2,80,000 | | Average Creditors | 1,96,000 | | Credit Purchases | 11,76,000 | **(a)** Calculate the Operating Cycle in days. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compute the Net Operating Cycle and discuss how the firm can reduce it. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:** First, derive the credit sales: Credit Sales = 90% × 20,00,000 = **₹18,00,000**. **Raw Material Storage Period (R):** $$R = \frac{1{,}40{,}000 \times 365}{9{,}80{,}000} = 52.14 \text{ days}$$ **Work-in-Progress Period (W):** $$W = \frac{1{,}60{,}000 \times 365}{16{,}00{,}000} = 36.50 \text{ days}$$ **Finished Goods Storage Period (F):** $$F = \frac{2{,}10{,}000 \times 365}{14{,}00{,}000} = 54.75 \text{ days}$$ **Debtors Collection Period (D):** $$D = \frac{2{,}80{,}000 \times 365}{18{,}00{,}000} = 56.78 \text{ days}$$ **Operating Cycle:** $$OC = 52.14 + 36.50 + 54.75 + 56.78 = 200.17 \text{ days}$$ --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Payables Deferral Period (C):** $$C = \frac{1{,}96{,}000 \times 365}{11{,}76{,}000} = 60.82 \text{ days}$$ **Net Operating Cycle:** $$NOC = 200.17 - 60.82 = 139.35 \text{ days}$$ **Strategies to Reduce the Net Operating Cycle:** 1. **Reduce Raw Material Storage (R)** — adopt EOQ model, reduce safety stock, improve procurement planning. 2. **Speed up Production (↓ W)** — implement lean manufacturing, automation, and bottleneck analysis. 3. **Reduce Finished Goods (↓ F)** — use better demand forecasting, Just-In-Time inventory, improved logistics. 4. **Tighten Receivables (↓ D)** — implement early-payment discounts (e.g., 2/10 net 30), penalize late payment, perform credit checks. 5. **Negotiate Longer Credit (↑ C)** — while preserving supplier goodwill, request extended payment terms. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing denominator of D:** Some students use *Total Sales* instead of *Credit Sales* for the Receivables Period. Always use **Credit Sales**. > 2. **Using Closing Stock instead of Average Stock:** When only one stock figure is given, treat it as the **average** unless the problem explicitly says otherwise. > 3. **Forgetting to multiply by 365:** The factor 365 (or 360) is mandatory for converting ratios into **days**. Omitting it costs **2 marks** in Part A and up to **4 marks** in Part B. > 4. **Adding C instead of Subtracting C:** The Payables Deferral Period is **subtracted** in the Net Operating Cycle formula — students frequently add it by mistake, leading to a 360° wrong answer. > 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Stock values and turnover values must be in the **same units** (both in lakhs or both in rupees). Mismatched units invite mark deductions. --- ### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Operating Cycle = R + W + F + D** — sum of four sequential time periods. - ✅ **Net Operating Cycle = OC − C** — true working capital exposure. - ✅ Every period is calculated as: **(Average Stock ÷ Daily Turnover) × 365**. - ✅ Use **Credit Sales** (not Total Sales) for Debtors Period. - ✅ Use **Credit Purchases** (not Total Purchases) for Creditors Period. - ✅ Use **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)** for Finished Goods Period denominator. - ✅ Use **Production Cost** (not COGS) for WIP Period denominator. - ✅ Use **Raw Material Consumed** (not Purchases) for Raw Material Period denominator. - ✅ **Average Stock = (Opening + Closing) / 2**; if only one figure is given, treat it as the average. - ✅ A **shorter NOC** = better liquidity, lower finance cost, stronger creditworthiness. - ✅ **OC never changes** with supplier credit manipulation — only **NOC** changes. - ✅ Engineering applications include manufacturing planning, JIT systems, and bank credit appraisal. - ✅ **Units Rule:** Stock and turnover figures must be in the **same currency unit** before computing ratios. - ✅ The 365-day year is the **default convention** in KTU problems; use 360 only if explicitly stated. ---

topics.title: Working Capital Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Working Capital Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Working Capital (WC)** refers to the capital required by a firm to finance its day-to-day operations and meet its short-term obligations. It is the difference between a firm's **current assets** and **current liabilities**.

Mathematically:

$$\text{Working Capital} = \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}$$

Working Capital Management (WCM) is the strategic and operational process of planning, organizing, directing, and controlling the firm's investment in current assets (cash, receivables, inventory) and the financing of these assets through a mix of short-term and long-term sources.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Household Wallet**
> Imagine your monthly household budget. Your **salary** is your long-term financing. From it, you keep some cash in your **wallet** (cash management), you have items in your **fridge/pantry** (inventory), and you may have given loans to friends that they will return later (receivables). At the same time, you have bills to pay by month-end (creditors/payables). 
> 
> **Working Capital Management** is exactly this — ensuring you always have enough "pocket money" to run the house smoothly, without running out (insolvency) or hoarding too much idle cash (opportunity loss).

### 1.3 Two Perspectives of Working Capital

| Perspective | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Gross Working Capital (GWC)** | Total investment in current assets | GWC = Current Assets |
| **Net Working Capital (NWC)** | Excess of current assets over current liabilities | NWC = CA − CL |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Management perspective favors **Gross Working Capital** (focusing on the *investment decision*), while the accounting/finance perspective favors **Net Working Capital** (focusing on the *financing decision*). Examiners often test the conceptual difference.

### 1.4 Types of Working Capital

**A. Permanent (Fixed) Working Capital**
- The minimum level of current assets required to **permanently** maintain business operations, even during low-activity periods.
- It is a **fixed** portion of capital that stays locked in current assets indefinitely.

**B. Temporary (Variable) Working Capital**
- The **fluctuating** portion of current assets required to meet seasonal or cyclical demand variations.
- Also called **Fluctuating Working Capital**.
- This portion rises and falls with the level of business activity.

> [!TIP]
> **Permanent WC** is usually financed by **long-term sources** (equity, debentures, term loans), while **Temporary WC** is ideally financed by **short-term sources** (bank overdraft, trade credit, commercial paper).

### 1.5 Need / Importance of Working Capital

- Ensures **liquidity** (ability to pay short-term debts).
- Maintains **solvency** and creditworthiness with suppliers and banks.
- Supports uninterrupted **production and sales**.
- Protects against **business shocks** (delayed collections, sudden raw material price hikes).
- Enhances **profitability** through better inventory and receivables turnover.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operating (Working Capital) Cycle

The **Operating Cycle** is the time duration between the acquisition of raw materials and the final cash realization from the sale of finished goods.

**Stages of the Operating Cycle:**

1. **Raw Material Storage Period (R)** — Time raw materials remain in store.
2. **Work-in-Progress Period (W)** — Time taken for manufacturing/conversion.
3. **Finished Goods Storage Period (F)** — Time finished goods wait before sale.
4. **Receivables (Debtors) Collection Period (D)** — Time taken to collect cash from customers.
5. *(LESS)* **Creditors (Payables) Deferral Period (C)** — Time taken to pay suppliers.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operating Cycle (OC) | $OC = R + W + F + D - C$ | Days |
| 2 | Net Operating Cycle (Cash Cycle) | $NOC = OC - C = R + W + F + D - C$ | Days |
| 3 | Raw Material Storage Period | $R = \dfrac{\text{Average Stock of RM}}{\text{RM Consumed per Day}}$ | Days |
| 4 | WIP Holding Period | $W = \dfrac{\text{Average WIP}}{\text{Production Cost per Day}}$ | Days |
| 5 | Finished Goods Holding Period | $F = \dfrac{\text{Average Finished Goods}}{\text{Cost of Goods Sold per Day}}$ | Days |
| 6 | Receivables Collection Period | $D = \dfrac{\text{Trade Receivables}}{\text{Credit Sales per Day}}$ | Days |
| 7 | Payables Deferral Period | $C = \dfrac{\text{Trade Payables}}{\text{Credit Purchases per Day}}$ | Days |
| 8 | Working Capital Turnover Ratio | $WCTR = \dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Net Working Capital}}$ | Times |
| 9 | Current Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Ratio |
| 10 | Quick (Acid-Test) Ratio | $QR = \dfrac{CA - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Expenses}}{CL}$ | Ratio |
| 11 | Absolute Liquid Ratio | $ALR = \dfrac{\text{Absolute Liquid Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Ratio |
| 12 | Inventory Turnover Ratio | $ITR = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Times |
| 13 | Debtors Turnover Ratio | $DTR = \dfrac{\text{Net Credit Sales}}{\text{Average Trade Receivables}}$ | Times |
| 14 | Cash Conversion Cycle | $CCC = DI + DSO - DPO$ | Days |
| 15 | Cost of Trade Credit | $k = \dfrac{d}{1-d} \times \dfrac{365}{C - p}$ | Percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Safeguard:** The above table uses `\dfrac` and `\vert`-style alternates to prevent markdown table breaking. Always use the relative day formulas when given financial statements.

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Working Capital Requirement

**A. Internal / Firm-Specific Factors**

1. **Nature and Size of Business** — Manufacturing firms need more WC than service firms.
2. **Production Cycle Length** — Longer cycle → Higher WC.
3. **Business Cycle Phase** — Boom phase → Higher WC; Recession → Lower.
4. **Credit Policy** — Liberal credit → Higher receivables → Higher WC.
5. **Inventory Policy** — JIT reduces WC; bulk purchasing increases it.
6. **Growth and Expansion Plans** — Expansion → Higher WC.
7. **Profitability and Dividend Policy** — Higher retained earnings reduce external WC need.

**B. External / Environmental Factors**

1. **Interest Rates** — Higher rates make carrying WC costlier.
2. **Inflation** — Higher prices require more funds for the same operations.
3. **Taxation Policy** — Indirect taxes raise working capital needs.
4. **Technology and Banking Infrastructure** — Efficient systems reduce idle balances.
5. **Seasonality of Operations** — Seasonal firms face demand-driven peaks in WC.

### 2.4 Components of Working Capital Management

Working Capital Management has three sub-decisions:

| Sub-Decision | Question Answered | Key Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Cash Management** | How much cash to keep? | Baumol Model, Miller-Orr Model |
| **Receivables Management** | Whom to sell on credit? | Ageing Schedule, Credit Scoring |
| **Inventory Management** | How much to stock? | EOQ, ABC Analysis, JIT |

### 2.5 Sources of Working Capital Finance

**Short-Term Sources:**

- Trade Credit (Creditors)
- Bank Overdraft / Cash Credit
- Short-term Loans
- Commercial Paper (CP)
- Treasury Bills
- Accrued Expenses

**Long-Term Sources:**

- Issue of Shares (Equity)
- Issue of Debentures
- Long-term Bank Loans
- Retained Earnings
- Public Deposits

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management

Engineers running infrastructure projects, manufacturing units, or construction firms use WCM principles to:
- Plan procurement cycles and project cash flows.
- Avoid project delays caused by cash shortages.
- Optimize inventory of spare parts and raw materials.
- Decide between leasing equipment (off-balance sheet WC impact) vs. purchasing (locks up WC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Cycle Formula

**Starting Assumption:** Every rupee invested in operations must complete a full cycle before returning as cash.

**Stage 1 — Raw Material Stage:**
When cash is paid to suppliers, materials enter storage. This consumes capital for the period $R$ days.

**Stage 2 — Work-in-Progress (Manufacturing) Stage:**
Raw materials are converted to WIP, then to finished goods. This consumes capital for the period $W$ days.

**Stage 3 — Finished Goods Storage Stage:**
Finished goods wait for sale. Capital is locked for $F$ days.

**Stage 4 — Receivables Collection Stage:**
After credit sale, cash is recovered after $D$ days. Capital is locked for $D$ days.

**Total Lock-up (Gross Cycle):**
$$OC_{gross} = R + W + F + D$$

**Stage 5 — Payables Deferral:**
The firm obtains trade credit from suppliers for $C$ days. This is essentially *supplier-financed* working capital, reducing net lock-up.

**Net Operating Cycle (Cash Conversion Cycle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NOC &= R + W + F + D - C \\
\text{where:} \quad R &= \text{Raw Material Period} \\
W &= \text{WIP Period} \\
F &= \text{Finished Goods Period} \\
D &= \text{Debtors Period} \\
C &= \text{Creditors Period}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Numerical Derivation: Computing Operating Cycle from Financials

**Problem Setup:**
A manufacturing firm has the following data for a year (assume **365 days**):
- Cost of Raw Materials Consumed: ₹ 18,25,000
- Average Raw Material Stock: ₹ 1,50,000
- Total Production Cost: ₹ 36,50,000
- Average WIP: ₹ 3,00,000
- Cost of Goods Sold (COGS): ₹ 45,00,000
- Average Finished Goods: ₹ 3,75,000
- Credit Sales: ₹ 60,00,000
- Average Trade Receivables: ₹ 6,00,000
- Credit Purchases: ₹ 20,00,000
- Average Trade Payables: ₹ 2,50,000

**Step 1: Raw Material Storage Period (R)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\text{Average RM Stock}}{\text{RM Consumed per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{18{,}25{,}000}{365}\right)} \\
  &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{5{,}000} \\
  &= 30 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: WIP Holding Period (W)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{\text{Average WIP}}{\text{Production Cost per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{36{,}50{,}000}{365}\right)} \\
  &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} \\
  &= 30 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Finished Goods Holding Period (F)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{\text{Average Finished Goods}}{\text{COGS per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{3{,}75{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{45{,}00{,}000}{365}\right)} \\
  &= \frac{3{,}75{,}000}{12{,}328.77} \\
  &= 30.42 \text{ days (approx. 30 days)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Receivables Collection Period (D)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{\text{Trade Receivables}}{\text{Credit Sales per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{60{,}00{,}000}{365}\right)} \\
  &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}64{,}383.56 / 100} \quad \text{(simplification)} \\
  &= 36.5 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Payables Deferral Period (C)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{\text{Trade Payables}}{\text{Credit Purchases per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{365}\right)} \\
  &= \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{5479.45} \\
  &= 45.6 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Net Operating Cycle (NOC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NOC &= R + W + F + D - C \\
    &= 30 + 30 + 30.42 + 36.5 - 45.6 \\
    &= 81.32 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The firm needs working capital to fund approximately **81 days** of operations. The positive cycle (≈81 days) means the firm must arrange external short-term finance for this period each year.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Working Capital Ratios — Benchmarks

| Ratio | Ideal Benchmark | Significance for KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|
| Current Ratio | **2 : 1** | Liquidity cushion |
| Quick Ratio | **1 : 1** | Liquidity excluding inventory |
| Absolute Liquid Ratio | **0.5 : 1** | Pure cash & marketable securities |
| WCTR | Industry-dependent | Efficiency of WC usage |
| ITR | 6–8 times (varies) | Inventory efficiency |
| DTR | Higher is better | Credit collection efficiency |

### 3.4 Cost of Trade Credit Derivation (Conceptual)

If a supplier offers terms **"3/15 net 60"**, it means:
- **3% discount** if paid in **15 days**.
- **Net (full) payment** due in **60 days**.

Forfeiting the discount means paying full price at day 60. The implied annual cost:

$$
k = \frac{d}{1-d} \times \frac{365}{C - p}
$$

where $d$ = discount rate, $C$ = final day, $p$ = discount day.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If the implied cost $k$ exceeds the firm's borrowing rate, **take the discount** and use bank finance to pay early.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Operating Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cash Outflow to Suppliers] --> B[Raw Material Storage]
    B --> C[Work in Progress - Manufacturing]
    C --> D[Finished Goods Storage]
    D --> E[Credit Sales to Customers]
    E --> F[Trade Receivables - Debtors]
    F --> G[Cash Realization]
    G --> A
    H[Trade Payables - Creditors] -.Defers Cash Outflow.-> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Components of Working Capital Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WCM[Working Capital Management]
    WCM --> CM[Cash Management]
    WCM --> RM[Receivables Management]
    WCM --> IM[Inventory Management]
    
    CM --> CM1[Baumol Model]
    CM --> CM2[Miller-Orr Model]
    CM --> CM3[Cash Budgeting]
    
    RM --> RM1[Credit Policy]
    RM --> RM2[Collection Policy]
    RM --> RM3[Ageing Schedule]
    
    IM --> IM1[EOQ Model]
    IM --> IM2[ABC Analysis]
    IM --> IM3[JIT Inventory]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Classification of Working Capital

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WC[Working Capital]
    WC --> GWC[Gross Working Capital]
    WC --> NWC[Net Working Capital]
    
    GWC --> GWC1[Investment in Current Assets]
    NWC --> NWC1[Current Assets minus Current Liabilities]
    
    WC --> TYPES[Types by Permanence]
    TYPES --> PERM[Permanent Working Capital]
    TYPES --> TEMP[Temporary / Variable Working Capital]
    
    PERM --> PERM1[Long-term Finance Source]
    TEMP --> TEMP1[Short-term Finance Source]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Operating Cycle Stage Flow with Time Variables

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Raw Material Storage] -->|R days| S2[Stage 2: WIP Manufacturing]
    S2 -->|W days| S3[Stage 3: Finished Goods Storage]
    S3 -->|F days| S4[Stage 4: Credit Sales]
    S4 -->|D days| S5[Stage 5: Cash Collection]
    S5 -->|Cycle Complete| S1
    
    S0[Stage 0: Trade Payables] -.->|C days deferral| S1
    
    style S0 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S5 fill:#93c47d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Financing Strategy — Matching Approach

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FIN[Working Capital Financing Strategy]
    FIN --> PER[Permanent WC]
    FIN --> VAR[Variable / Temporary WC]
    
    PER --> LTS[Long-Term Sources]
    LTS --> LTS1[Equity Capital]
    LTS --> LTS2[Debentures]
    LTS --> LTS3[Term Loans]
    LTS --> LTS4[Retained Earnings]
    
    VAR --> STS[Short-Term Sources]
    STS --> STS1[Trade Credit]
    STS --> STS2[Bank Overdraft]
    STS --> STS3[Commercial Paper]
    STS --> STS4[Accrued Expenses]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Aid:** The "Matching Principle" diagram above is a frequently asked 7-mark question. The examiner expects the answer to **justify** the matching logic: permanent needs → permanent sources.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> **Define Working Capital. Distinguish between Gross Working Capital and Net Working Capital.**

**Model Answer:**

Working Capital refers to the capital employed in current assets to finance day-to-day operations.

| Basis | Gross Working Capital | Net Working Capital |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Total investment in current assets | Excess of current assets over current liabilities |
| Focus | Investment decision | Financing decision |
| Formula | GWC = Current Assets | NWC = CA − CL |
| Viewpoint | Management / Operational | Accounting / Balance Sheet |
| Implication | Total funds deployed | Net liquidity position |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for distinction table)*

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **Explain the concept of "Operating Cycle" in working capital management.**

**Model Answer:**

The Operating Cycle is the time period between the acquisition of raw materials and the final realization of cash from the sale of finished goods.

It consists of four stages: **(i)** Raw material storage, **(ii)** Work-in-progress conversion, **(iii)** Finished goods storage, and **(iv)** Receivables collection. The total period of the first four stages minus the creditors' deferral period gives the **Net Operating Cycle (NOC)**, also called the **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)**.

$$NOC = R + W + F + D - C$$

A shorter NOC implies better working capital efficiency.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for stages + 1 for formula)*

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

> **Question A.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **(a)** Discuss the various factors affecting the working capital requirements of a firm. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The following data relates to M/s XYZ Enterprises for the year ended 31-03-2024:
>
> | Item | Amount (₹) |
> |---|---|
> | Cost of Raw Materials Consumed | 16,00,000 |
> | Average Stock of Raw Materials | 1,60,000 |
> | WIP (Average) | 2,40,000 |
> | Total Production Cost | 28,00,000 |
> | Average Finished Goods Stock | 2,80,000 |
> | Cost of Goods Sold | 32,00,000 |
> | Credit Sales | 48,00,000 |
> | Average Trade Receivables | 4,80,000 |
> | Credit Purchases | 18,00,000 |
> | Average Trade Payables | 2,70,000 |
>
> **Compute the Operating Cycle and the amount of working capital required. Assume 360 days/year.** **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**A. Internal / Firm-Specific Factors:**

1. **Nature and Size of Business:** A manufacturing firm requires more working capital than a trading or service firm.
2. **Length of Production Cycle:** Longer production cycle → Locked-up capital for longer → Higher WC.
3. **Business Cycle Fluctuations:** During boom, WC requirement rises; during recession, it falls.
4. **Credit Policy:** Liberal credit terms extended to customers → Higher debtors → Higher WC.
5. **Inventory Policy:** Bulk purchasing increases inventory levels and WC; JIT reduces it.
6. **Growth and Expansion:** Rapid growth requires proportionally more WC.
7. **Profitability and Dividend Policy:** Higher retained earnings reduce external WC need.
8. **Operating Efficiency:** Efficient operations reduce idle stock and excess receivables.

**B. External Factors:**

1. **Interest Rates:** Higher rates → Costlier WC financing.
2. **Inflation:** Inflated input prices require more nominal WC.
3. **Taxation Policy:** Indirect taxes (GST, excise) lock funds until remitted.
4. **Seasonality of Operations:** Seasonal industries face cyclical WC peaks.
5. **Banking and Technological Infrastructure:** Modern systems reduce idle balances.

*(7 marks: ~3 internal + ~3 external + 1 conclusion)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Raw Material Period (R)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\text{Avg. RM Stock}}{\text{RM Consumed per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{16{,}00{,}000}{360}\right)} = \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{4444.44} \\
  &= 36 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: WIP Period (W)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{\text{Avg. WIP}}{\text{Production Cost per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{28{,}00{,}000}{360}\right)} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{7777.78} \\
  &= 30.86 \approx 31 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Finished Goods Period (F)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{\text{Avg. FG Stock}}{\text{COGS per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{2{,}80{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{32{,}00{,}000}{360}\right)} = \frac{2{,}80{,}000}{8888.89} \\
  &= 31.5 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Receivables Period (D)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{\text{Trade Receivables}}{\text{Credit Sales per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{48{,}00{,}000}{360}\right)} = \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{13333.33} \\
  &= 36 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Payables Period (C)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{\text{Trade Payables}}{\text{Credit Purchases per Day}} \\
  &= \frac{2{,}70{,}000}{\left(\dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{360}\right)} = \frac{2{,}70{,}000}{5000} \\
  &= 54 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Net Operating Cycle (NOC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NOC &= R + W + F + D - C \\
    &= 36 + 31 + 31.5 + 36 - 54 \\
    &= 80.5 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Working Capital Required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WC_{required} &= \frac{\text{Total Operating Cost}}{\text{Number of Days}} \times NOC \\
\text{(Total Operating Cost approx = COGS + Other expenses)} &\approx 32{,}00{,}000 \\
WC_{required} &= \frac{32{,}00{,}000}{360} \times 80.5 \\
              &= 8888.89 \times 80.5 \\
              &= ₹ \ 7{,}15{,}555.56 \text{ (approx ₹ 7,15,556)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract Creditors Period (C):** A common 2-mark deduction. The Net Operating Cycle is gross cycle *minus* payables period.
> 2. **Wrong denominator in COGS-per-day:** Use **360 or 365 consistently** as given. Mixing days leads to wrong answers.
> 3. **Misinterpreting "Total Operating Cost":** For simple problems, use COGS as proxy. For advanced problems, use the explicitly given cost figure.

---

> **Question B (Internal Choice).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> **(a)** Explain the various sources of working capital finance. Differentiate between long-term and short-term sources. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the cost of trade credit for the following terms using the formula: **(7 marks)**
> - Supplier A: **"2/10 net 45"**
> - Supplier B: **"3/20 net 60"**
> - (Assume 365-day year)
> - Identify which supplier is offering the **cheaper** credit option.

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**A. Short-Term Sources of Working Capital:**

1. **Trade Credit** — Credit extended by suppliers for purchases.
2. **Bank Overdraft / Cash Credit** — Drawing beyond deposit; collateral-based.
3. **Short-term Loans** — Bullet repayment, 6–12 month tenure.
4. **Commercial Paper (CP)** — Unsecured short-term promissory note issued by large corporates.
5. **Treasury Bills** — Government-backed short-term instruments.
6. **Accrued Expenses** — Wages, taxes payable (a free source).

**B. Long-Term Sources:**

1. **Issue of Equity Shares** — Permanent capital, no fixed cost.
2. **Issue of Debentures** — Long-term debt, fixed interest.
3. **Term Loans from Banks** — Medium-to-long tenure.
4. **Retained Earnings** — Internal accrual.
5. **Public Deposits** — From the general public.

**Differentiation Table:**

| Basis | Short-Term | Long-Term |
|---|---|---|
| Tenure | < 1 year | > 1 year |
| Cost | Generally lower per period | Spread over long period |
| Risk | Higher (refinancing) | Lower (stable) |
| Flexibility | High | Low |
| Use | Variable WC | Permanent WC |

*(7 marks: 3 for sources + 2 for differentiation + 2 examples)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Cost of Trade Credit Formula:**

$$
k = \frac{d}{1 - d} \times \frac{365}{C - p}
$$

where:
- $d$ = discount rate
- $C$ = final net payment day
- $p$ = last discount day

**Step 1: Cost of Supplier A's Credit ("2/10 net 45")**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_A &= \frac{0.02}{1 - 0.02} \times \frac{365}{45 - 10} \\
    &= \frac{0.02}{0.98} \times \frac{365}{35} \\
    &= 0.020408 \times 10.4286 \\
    &= 0.2129 \text{ or } 21.29\% \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cost of Supplier B's Credit ("3/20 net 60")**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_B &= \frac{0.03}{1 - 0.03} \times \frac{365}{60 - 20} \\
    &= \frac{0.03}{0.97} \times \frac{365}{40} \\
    &= 0.030928 \times 9.125 \\
    &= 0.2822 \text{ or } 28.22\% \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Decision**

Since $k_A (21.29\%) < k_B (28.22\%)$, **Supplier A's credit is cheaper**. The firm should aim to take the **2% discount** from Supplier A by paying on day 10. For Supplier B, if the firm's own borrowing cost is less than 28.22%, it should still avail the discount; otherwise, it may rationally forgo the discount.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistakes:**
> 1. Using **360 days** instead of 365 — partial mark deduction if 365 is specified.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1-d)$** in the denominator — a full 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing the forgone period**: The "free period" in the denominator is $(C - p)$, NOT just $C$.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **WC = CA − CL**; GWC = CA; NWC = CA − CL.
- [x] **Operating Cycle** has 4 positive stages: R, W, F, D; minus 1 negative stage: C.
- [x] **Permanent WC** is locked capital even in low season — financed long-term.
- [x] **Temporary WC** is cyclical — financed short-term.
- [x] **Matching Principle**: Match maturity of source with maturity of need.
- [x] **Current Ratio = 2:1** (ideal); **Quick Ratio = 1:1** (ideal).
- [x] **WCTR = Sales / NWC** — higher is better.
- [x] **Cost of Trade Credit:** $k = \dfrac{d}{1-d} \times \dfrac{365}{C-p}$.
- [x] **3 WCM Decisions:** Cash, Receivables, Inventory.
- [x] **Sources:** Trade credit, OD, CP, T-bills (short); Equity, Debentures, Term loans (long).
- [x] **Key time formulas** (R, W, F, D, C) all use *average* numerator and *daily* denominator.
- [x] **NOC > 0** means firm needs external WC finance; **NOC < 0** is rare and indicates supplier-financed operations (e.g., supermarkets).
- [x] **Conservative WC policy** → high liquidity, low profitability. **Aggressive** → low liquidity, high profitability.
- [x] **Aging Schedule** of debtors is the most asked receivables-management tool.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Working Capital Management**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Finance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sources of Finance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sources of Finance** refer to the various channels, instruments, and mechanisms through which an organization (firm, company, startup, or government enterprise) raises the monetary funds required to meet its investment, operational, expansion, and working capital needs. In the KTU 2024 framework, sources of finance are classified based on **ownership (Owner's Funds vs. Borrowed Funds)**, **tenure (Short-term, Medium-term, Long-term)**, and **origin (Internal vs. External Sources)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Sources of finance are the specific means by which firms mobilize capital from the financial markets, owners, and operational activities to fund their asset base and growth objectives.*

The fundamental finance equation governing this concept is:

$$ \text{Total Capital Required} = \text{Fixed Assets} + \text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities} $$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **B.Tech engineering student** planning to build a high-performance electric go-kart from scratch. Before the first bolt is tightened, the student needs raw materials, a motor, batteries, tools, and workshop rent. The student has a few options to gather this money:

1. **Use their own savings** (Personal Equity / Retained Earnings).
2. **Ask their parents for a loan, promising to repay later with interest** (Debt / Borrowed Funds).
3. **Convince a friend to co-own the kart in exchange for a share of any future winnings** (Equity Share Capital).
4. **Borrow a toolkit from the college workshop for a week and pay later** (Trade Credit).
5. **Win a college innovation grant** (Venture Capital / Government Subsidy).

Each of these represents a **distinct source of finance**. Just like the student chooses the most suitable combination of these options, a business must intelligently mix and match sources to minimize its **cost of capital** and maintain a healthy **capital structure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The "best" source of finance is never universal — it depends on the firm's risk profile, life-cycle stage, tax position, and the macroeconomic interest rate environment.

### 1.3 Critical Terminology for KTU 2024 Board Exams

| Term | Meaning | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Owner's Equity** | Funds contributed by promoters/shareholders | Forms the base of capital structure |
| **Borrowed Funds** | Capital raised through loans/debentures | Creates financial leverage |
| **Cost of Capital** | The effective rate of return required by providers of capital | Used to evaluate investment decisions |
| **Financial Leverage** | Use of debt to amplify returns to equity holders | High leverage = High risk |
| **Working Capital** | Short-term funds for daily operations | Funded by short-term sources |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU 2024:** Examiners frequently test the *classification* of sources. Memorize the distinction between **internal vs. external** and **short-term vs. long-term** as a 2x2 matrix.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Sources of Finance

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to demonstrate a deep understanding of the layered hierarchy of finance sources. The classification is built on **three orthogonal dimensions**:

**Dimension 1: By Ownership / Source Origin**
- **Internal Sources** — Generated within the firm through operations.
- **External Sources** — Mobilized from outside the firm via financial markets.

**Dimension 2: By Tenure / Duration**
- **Short-Term Sources** (Up to **1 year**)
- **Medium-Term Sources** (**1 to 5 years**)
- **Long-Term Sources** (**More than 5 years**)

**Dimension 3: By Nature of Claim**
- **Owner's Funds** (Equity) — No fixed repayment obligation.
- **Creditor's Funds** (Debt) — Fixed interest and principal repayment.

### 2.2 Detailed Inventory of Sources

#### A. Long-Term Sources of Finance

| # | Source | Type | Key Characteristics |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equity Share Capital** | External, Owned | Permanent capital; voting rights; dividend not mandatory |
| 2 | **Preference Share Capital** | Hybrid | Fixed dividend; preference in dividend & liquidation |
| 3 | **Retained Earnings** | Internal, Owned | Ploughed-back profits; no flotation cost |
| 4 | **Debentures / Bonds** | External, Borrowed | Fixed coupon rate; tax-deductible interest |
| 5 | **Term Loans from Banks / FIs** | External, Borrowed | Secured by collateral; amortized over years |
| 6 | **Venture Capital (VC)** | External, Owned | For high-growth startups; equity in exchange for funding |
| 7 | **Private Equity (PE)** | External, Owned | Investment in mature, established private firms |
| 8 | **IPO / FPO (Public Issue)** | External, Owned | Listed on stock exchange; wide investor base |
| 9 | **Rights Issue** | External, Owned | Offered to existing shareholders at discounted price |
| 10 | **Convertible Debentures** | Hybrid | Convertible into equity after a specified period |

#### B. Short-Term Sources of Finance

| # | Source | Type | Key Characteristics |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trade Credit** | External, Borrowed | Credit from suppliers; usually 30–90 days |
| 2 | **Bank Overdraft** | External, Borrowed | Withdraw beyond balance; high interest rate |
| 3 | **Cash Credit / Working Capital Loan** | External, Borrowed | Continuous borrowing against stock & debtors |
| 4 | **Commercial Paper (CP)** | External, Borrowed | Unsecured promissory note; 15–365 days |
| 5 | **Factoring & Bill Discounting** | External, Borrowed | Sale of receivables to a factor at a discount |
| 6 | **Short-term Loans** | External, Borrowed | Bullet repayment within 1 year |
| 7 | **Accrued Expenses & Provisions** | Internal, Borrowed | Free float — wages, taxes due but unpaid |
| 8 | **Customer Advances** | External, Borrowed | Advance payments received against orders |

#### C. Medium-Term Sources of Finance

| # | Source | Tenure Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Leasing & Hire-Purchase** | 1–5 years |
| 2 | **Medium-term Bank Loans** | 1–5 years |
| 3 | **Redeemable Preference Shares** | Typically 5–7 years |
| 4 | **Public Deposits** | 6 months – 5 years |
| 5 | **Deferred Credit (Installment purchase)** | 1–5 years |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following formulas are absolutely essential for solving numerical problems on Sources of Finance and Capital Structure in the KTU 2024 scheme.

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are required for the calculation of cost of various sources — a frequently tested KTU 2024 topic.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cost of Equity (Dividend Model)** | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | Gordon's Growth Model for equity shares |
| 2 | **Cost of Preference Share** | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_0}$ | Annual fixed dividend / Issue price |
| 3 | **Cost of Debt (Before Tax)** | $K_d = \dfrac{I}{NP}$ | Annual interest / Net proceeds |
| 4 | **Cost of Debt (After Tax)** | $K_d^{AT} = K_d \times (1 - t)$ | Tax shield on interest expense |
| 5 | **Cost of Retained Earnings** | $K_r = K_e \times (1 - t)$ | Adjusted for personal tax on dividend |
| 6 | **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** | $WACC = \sum W_i \times K_i$ | Weighted average over all sources |
| 7 | **Indifference Point (EBIT)** | $\dfrac{(EBIT - I_1)(1-t)}{E_1} = \dfrac{(EBIT - I_2)(1-t)}{E_2}$ | Comparing two financial plans |
| 8 | **Degree of Financial Leverage** | $DFL = \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$ | Risk of debt in capital structure |
| 9 | **Times Interest Earned (TIE)** | $TIE = \dfrac{EBIT}{I}$ | Solvency / debt-servicing capacity |
| 10 | **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $D/E = \dfrac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Equity}}$ | Capital structure proportion |

Where:
- $D_1$ = Expected dividend per share next year
- $P_0$ = Current market price of share
- $g$ = Constant growth rate of dividends
- $I$ = Annual interest payment
- $NP$ = Net proceeds from issue
- $t$ = Corporate tax rate
- $W_i$ = Weight of capital source *i*
- $K_i$ = Cost of capital source *i*
- $EBIT$ = Earnings Before Interest and Tax
- $E$ = Number of equity shares

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU 2024:** Whenever a numerical problem asks for "the cheapest source of finance," students **must** use the **after-tax cost** for all sources and compare. A common pitfall is comparing pre-tax costs of debt with post-tax costs of equity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, the choice of finance sources directly impacts:
- **Technology Startups (B.Tech Ventures):** Bootstrap → Angel Funding → VC → IPO.
- **Infrastructure Projects (Civil Engineering):** Long-term debentures + Government grants.
- **Manufacturing Firms (Mechanical):** Term loans + Working capital limits.
- **Working Capital Intensive Firms:** Heavy reliance on trade credit and bank overdraft.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

**Problem Statement:** A company is financed by the following capital structure. Calculate the WACC.

| Source | Book Value (₹ Lakhs) | Specific Cost (%) |
|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 600 | 18% |
| Preference Share Capital | 200 | 14% |
| Debentures (10% coupon) | 400 | 10% (after tax) |
| **Total** | **1200** | — |

**Applicable Tax Rate (t)** = 30%

---

**Step 1: Compute the total capital and weights of each source.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Capital (V)} &= E + P + D \\
&= 600 + 200 + 400 \\
&= 1200 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The total capital is the sum of all long-term sources. This becomes the denominator for computing weights.

---

**Step 2: Calculate the weight of each source of finance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_E &= \dfrac{E}{V} = \dfrac{600}{1200} = 0.50 \\
W_P &= \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{200}{1200} = 0.1667 \\
W_D &= \dfrac{D}{V} = \dfrac{400}{1200} = 0.3333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Weights represent the proportion of each source in the total capital structure. They must always sum to 1.0 (or 100%).

**Verification:**
$$ 0.50 + 0.1667 + 0.3333 = 1.0000 \quad \checkmark $$

---

**Step 3: Apply the WACC formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WACC &= W_E \times K_E + W_P \times K_P + W_D \times K_D^{AT} \\
WACC &= (0.50 \times 18) + (0.1667 \times 14) + (0.3333 \times 10) \\
WACC &= 9.00 + 2.3338 + 3.333 \\
WACC &= 14.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The WACC is the weighted sum of individual costs. This is the **hurdle rate** the company should earn on its investments. Any project with IRR greater than 14.67% is value-accretive.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Indifference Point (EBIT Level)

**Problem Statement:** A firm is evaluating two financing plans:
- **Plan A (Debt-Heavy):** Equity = ₹400 Lakhs (40,000 shares), Debt = ₹600 Lakhs @ 12% interest.
- **Plan B (Equity-Heavy):** Equity = ₹700 Lakhs (70,000 shares), Debt = ₹300 Lakhs @ 12% interest.

**Tax Rate (t)** = 30%. Find the **indifference EBIT level** at which the EPS is identical under both plans.

---

**Step 1: Define the EPS equation for each plan.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPS_A &= \dfrac{(EBIT - I_A)(1 - t)}{E_A} \\
EPS_B &= \dfrac{(EBIT - I_B)(1 - t)}{E_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** EPS is profit after tax and after interest, divided by the number of equity shares.

---

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

- $I_A = 12\% \times 600 = 72$ Lakhs
- $I_B = 12\% \times 300 = 36$ Lakhs
- $E_A = 40,000$ shares; $E_B = 70,000$ shares

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPS_A &= \dfrac{(EBIT - 72)(1 - 0.30)}{40,000} \\
EPS_B &= \dfrac{(EBIT - 36)(1 - 0.30)}{70,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3: Equate the two EPS expressions to find the indifference point.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{(EBIT - 72)(0.70)}{40,000} &= \dfrac{(EBIT - 36)(0.70)}{70,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Cancel the common factor (1 - t) = 0.70 on both sides.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{(EBIT - 72)}{40,000} &= \dfrac{(EBIT - 36)}{70,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 5: Cross-multiply and solve for EBIT.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
70,000 \times (EBIT - 72) &= 40,000 \times (EBIT - 36) \\
7 \times (EBIT - 72) &= 4 \times (EBIT - 36) \\
7 EBIT - 504 &= 4 EBIT - 144 \\
7 EBIT - 4 EBIT &= 504 - 144 \\
3 EBIT &= 360 \\
EBIT &= 120 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
EPS_A &= \dfrac{(120 - 72)(0.70)}{40,000} = \dfrac{48 \times 0.70}{40,000} = \dfrac{33.6}{40,000} = 0.00084 \text{ Lakhs/share} \\
EPS_B &= \dfrac{(120 - 36)(0.70)}{70,000} = \dfrac{84 \times 0.70}{70,000} = \dfrac{58.8}{70,000} = 0.00084 \text{ Lakhs/share} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** At an EBIT level of **₹120 Lakhs**, both plans yield identical EPS. **If expected EBIT > ₹120 Lakhs**, choose **Plan A (Debt-Heavy)** to benefit from financial leverage. **If expected EBIT < ₹120 Lakhs**, choose **Plan B (Equity-Heavy)** to minimize risk.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Merits vs. Demerits)

> [!NOTE]
> This comparative matrix is a **high-yield KTU 2024 exam tool**. Examiners often set Part A questions asking to compare two specific sources.

| Comparison Axis | Equity Shares | Preference Shares | Debentures | Retained Earnings |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost of Capital** | Highest (15-20%) | Medium (12-15%) | Lowest (8-12% post-tax) | Moderate (slightly below equity) |
| **Obligation to Pay** | No mandatory dividend | Mandatory fixed dividend | Mandatory fixed interest | Discretionary |
| **Risk to Issuer** | Low (no fixed payout) | Medium | High (bankruptcy risk) | Very Low |
| **Voting Rights** | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Tax Deductibility** | Dividends not tax-deductible | Dividends not tax-deductible | Interest is tax-deductible | Not applicable |
| **Dilution of Control** | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Flotation Cost** | Highest | Medium | Lowest | None |
| **Impact on EPS** | Dilutes EPS | Moderate dilution | Increases EPS via leverage | Slightly dilutes EPS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
