$$FW_P = 4{,}61{,}580 + 5{,}38{,}623 + 50{,}000$$

$$\boxed{FW_P = \text{₹}10{,}50{,}203}$$

**(a) Future Worth of Scheme Q (cont. 7 Marks):**

The principal of ₹3,00,000 is invested and grows for 5 years. The ₹6,00,000 is received at $t=5$, so its future worth at $t=5$ is itself (it has nowhere further to grow).

$$FW_Q = 3{,}00{,}000 \times (1.09)^5 + 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$FW_Q = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 1.5386 + 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$FW_Q = 4{,}61{,}580 + 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{FW_Q = \text{₹}10{,}61{,}580}$$

---

**(b) Decision:**

Since $FW_Q = \text{₹}10{,}61{,}580 > FW_P = \text{₹}10{,}50{,}203$:

> **Scheme Q is financially more attractive.** Although Scheme P provides regular annual cash flows (which may be useful for liquidity), in pure time-value terms Scheme Q yields a future worth that is **₹11,377 higher** at the 9% benchmark rate, making it the superior investment choice.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for KTU 2024 TVM Module):**

- ✅ **Core Principle:** A rupee today > a rupee tomorrow (due to earning potential, inflation, and risk).
- ✅ **Reference Point:** $t = 0$ is the present; the choice of reference does not change the decision, only the numbers.
- ✅ **Six Master Factors to Memorize:** $(F/P), (P/F), (F/A), (A/F), (P/A), (A/P)$ — all depend on $(1+i)^n$.
- ✅ **Reciprocal Rule:** $(F/P) \times (P/F) = 1$ and $(F/A) \times (A/F) = 1$ and $(P/A) \times (A/P) = 1$ — use it to **cross-check** your answer.
- ✅ **Default Annuity Type:** KTU problems default to **Ordinary Annuity** (end-of-period). Annuity Due requires multiplication by $(1+i)$.
- ✅ **Salvage Value Rule:** Always at $t = n$. Discount it back to $t = 0$ using $(P/F, i, n)$ for PW analysis, or to annual worth using $(A/F, i, n)$.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Outflows = **negative**, Inflows = **positive**. Never mix.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** Accept a project if $PW > 0$ OR $AW > 0$ OR $FW > 0$ (all three give consistent verdicts).
- ✅ **CFD First:** Always draw a **Cash Flow Diagram** before substituting into formulas — it prevents sign errors and clarifies timing.
- ✅ **Units:** Keep $i$ as a **decimal**, $n$ in the same time unit as $i$ (e.g., if $i$ is per year, $n$ must be in years).
- ✅ **Master Identity:** $F = P(1+i)^n$ is the *mother formula* — every other factor is derived from it.
- ✅ **Geometric Growth:** Compound interest is exponential — small changes in $i$ or $n$ produce large changes in $F$ or $P$.
- ✅ **Equivalent Annual Worth (AW) is the most powerful tool** for comparing alternatives with **unequal lives**, as it removes the time variable entirely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cash Flow Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cash Flow Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cash Flow Diagram (CFD)** is a graphical representation of a financial transaction or engineering economic problem plotted on a two-dimensional plane, where the **horizontal axis represents the timeline (n)** and the **vertical axis represents the magnitude of cash flows**, with **upward arrows indicating cash inflows (receipts/benefits/revenues)** and **downward arrows indicating cash outflows (disbursements/costs/expenses)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:**
> - **Cash Inflow (+ve)**: Money received, revenue earned, salvage value, savings — represented by an **upward arrow (↑)**.
> - **Cash Outflow (-ve)**: Money paid, investment, cost, maintenance — represented by a **downward arrow (↓)**.
> - **Time Axis (n)**: Discrete time periods (usually years), numbered 0, 1, 2, ... n.
> - **Present (n=0)**: The reference time at which the analysis begins.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **financial balance scale stretched across time**. Think of your engineering project (say, setting up a small solar plant) as a journey through time.

- **At time 0 (today)**: You invest ₹5,00,000 to buy the equipment. Money leaves your pocket → **Downward arrow**.
- **Every year for 5 years**: You earn ₹1,20,000 from selling electricity. Money enters your pocket → **Upward arrows**.
- **At year 5**: You get ₹50,000 by selling the worn-out equipment (salvage value). Money enters → **Upward arrow**.

If we draw all these arrows on a single horizontal timeline, we get a **Cash Flow Diagram**. It is the "storyboard" of money — telling us visually **when money flows in, when it flows out, and how much flows each time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU convention, **a cash flow diagram is NOT optional decoration** — it is the **mandatory first step** before any time-value-of-money calculation. The diagram guides you to identify the correct formula (P, F, A, or G).

## 1.3 Standard Cash Flow Variables (KTU Notation)

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| **P** | Present Worth (Present Value) | ₹ (Rupees) |
| **F** | Future Worth (Future Value) | ₹ (Rupees) |
| **A** | Uniform Annual Series (Annuity / End-of-period payment) | ₹/year |
| **G** | Arithmetic Gradient (linear change in cash flow per period) | ₹/year² |
| **i** | Interest Rate per Period | % per year |
| **n** | Number of Interest Periods (years, months) | Integer |
| **SV** | Salvage Value | ₹ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Constructing a Cash Flow Diagram

To draw a correct KTU-style cash flow diagram, follow this 5-step process:

1. **Establish the Time Axis**: Draw a horizontal line and mark discrete periods `0, 1, 2, 3, ..., n`. Each unit is one interest period (typically one year).
2. **Plot Cash Outflows**: Place **downward arrows (↓)** at the periods where money is spent. Scale arrow length to magnitude.
3. **Plot Cash Inflows**: Place **upward arrows (↑)** at the periods where money is received. Scale arrow length to magnitude.
4. **Identify the Reference Point**: Period **n = 0** is the *present*. Period **n** at the right edge is the *horizon*.
5. **Annotate Variables**: Label each arrow with its cash flow symbol (P, A, F, G, or numerical value).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip**: The KTU evaluator instantly grants partial credit if a student draws a clean CFD even before solving numerical problems. Always start with the diagram.

## 2.2 The Four Fundamental Cash Flow Patterns

### Pattern 1 — Single Payment (Lump Sum)
Only **one cash flow** occurs — either at the present or at the future.

```
        F
        |
        |
   ─────●─────●─────●─────●─────► n
   0     1     2     3    ...  n
        P (at t=0)
```

### Pattern 2 — Uniform Series (Annuity, A)
The **same cash flow A** repeats every period for `n` periods. Most common in engineering problems (e.g., annual maintenance cost, annual revenue).

```
   A   A   A   A   A
   ↑   ↑   ↑   ↑   ↑
   ●───●───●───●───●───► n
   0   1   2   3   4   5
```

### Pattern 3 — Gradient Series (Arithmetic, G)
Cash flows **change by a constant amount G** each period. Typically starts at 0 and increases (e.g., escalating operating costs).

```
        0   G  2G  3G
        |   ↑   ↑   ↑
   ─────●───●───●───●───► n
   0     1   2   3   4
```

### Pattern 4 — Mixed / Irregular Cash Flows
Combinations of P, A, F, and G — typical of real projects. The CFD simply shows all of them together on a single time axis.

## 2.3 The Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sign Convention (CRITICAL for Marks):**
> - **Cash Inflow = +ve** → Upward arrow (↑) at the time of receipt.
> - **Cash Outflow = -ve** → Downward arrow (↓) at the time of payment.
> - **Viewer's Perspective**: Always draw from the investor's / decision-maker's point of view.
> - The arrow length is **proportional to the magnitude** (a ₹5 Lakh arrow should be visibly longer than a ₹50,000 arrow).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

The following formulas are derived **from the cash flow diagrams** and are tested in KTU exams. All these are "Time Value of Money" formulas with `i` = interest rate per period, `n` = number of periods.

| # | Find | Given | Single-Payment Formula | Uniform-Series Formula | Gradient Formula |
|---|------|-------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **F** | P | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | — | — |
| 2 | **P** | F | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | — | — |
| 3 | **F** | A | — | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | — |
| 4 | **P** | A | — | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | — |
| 5 | **A** | P | — | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | — |
| 6 | **A** | F | — | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | — |
| 7 | **P** | G | — | — | $P = G \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$ |
| 8 | **A** | G | — | — | $A = G \cdot \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ |

### Standard Notation for Factors

- $F/P, i\%, n = (1+i)^{n}$ → **Single Payment Compound Amount Factor (SPCAF)**
- $P/F, i\%, n = (1+i)^{-n}$ → **Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)**
- $F/A, i\%, n = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ → **Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor (USCAF)**
- $P/A, i\%, n = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ → **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)**
- $A/P, i\%, n = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ → **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**
- $A/F, i\%, n = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ → **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**
- $P/G, i\%, n = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$ → **Gradient Present Worth Factor (GPWF)**
- $A/G, i\%, n = \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ → **Gradient Uniform Series Factor (GUSF)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder**: If `i` is given as **% per year**, divide by **100** before using. Period `n` must be in the **same unit** as `i` (e.g., i = 6% per year, n = 5 years — match units).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cash flow diagrams and the associated time-value formulas are the **backbone of engineering economy**. They are used in:

- **Capital Budgeting** — selecting between alternative equipment purchases.
- **Public Infrastructure** — evaluating toll roads, bridges, and water supply projects.
- **Energy Sector** — comparing solar vs. diesel vs. grid power over 20–25 years.
- **Manufacturing** — analysing whether to replace or overhaul an aging machine.
- **Loan Amortization** — calculating EMI, principal vs. interest components.
- **Insurance & Annuities** — retirement planning, pension fund design.

In every case, the **first deliverable is a cash flow diagram**, and every subsequent calculation (NPV, IRR, BCR) is built upon it.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Payment Compound Amount Factor ($F = P(1+i)^{n}$)

**Starting Point (from Cash Flow Diagram):**

```
        F
        |
        |
   ─────●─────●─────●─────●─────► n
   0     1     2     3    ...  n
        P
```

**Step 1: Define the relationship.**
At the end of period 1, the principal P grows by interest $i \cdot P$, so the amount becomes:

$$
F_{1} = P + i \cdot P = P(1+i)
$$

**Step 2: Apply the same logic at the end of period 2.**
The new principal is $P(1+i)$, and interest is added on it:

$$
F_{2} = F_{1} + i \cdot F_{1} = F_{1}(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Recognize the pattern.**
After n periods, by induction:

$$
F_{n} = P(1+i)^{n}
$$

**Step 4: Re-write in standard KTU notation.**

$$
\boxed{F = P(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Step 5: Invert to find the Present Worth factor.**

$$
P = F(1+i)^{-n}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
A machine costs **P = ₹1,00,000** today. What is its value after **n = 5 years** at **i = 10% per year**?

$$
F = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.10)^{5} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 1.61051 = \text{₹1,61,051}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor ($F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$)

**Starting Point (from Cash Flow Diagram):**

```
   A    A    A    A    A
   ↑    ↑    ↑    ↑    ↑
   ●────●────●────●────●────► n
   0    1    2    3    4    5
                              F
                              ↓ (or ↑ at n)
```

**Step 1: Each A is a single payment that must be compounded to period n.**
The last A (at period n) compounds for 0 periods → value at n = A.
The second-last A (at period n−1) compounds for 1 period → value at n = $A(1+i)^{1}$.
The k-th-from-last A (at period n−k) compounds for k periods → value at n = $A(1+i)^{k}$.
The first A (at period 1) compounds for (n−1) periods → value at n = $A(1+i)^{n-1}$.

**Step 2: Sum the geometric series.**

$$
F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \dots + A(1+i)^{0}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply both sides by (1+i) to get a telescoping form.**

$$
F(1+i) = A(1+i)^{n} + A(1+i)^{n-1} + \dots + A(1+i)^{1}
$$

**Step 4: Subtract the original F from F(1+i).**

$$
F(1+i) - F = A(1+i)^{n} - A
$$

**Step 5: Factor and solve.**

$$
F \cdot i = A \left[(1+i)^{n} - 1\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
You deposit **A = ₹20,000** at the end of every year for **n = 6 years** at **i = 8% per year**. Find F at year 6.

$$
F = 20{,}000 \times \dfrac{(1.08)^{6} - 1}{0.08}
$$

$$
(1.08)^{6} = 1.586874
$$

$$
F = 20{,}000 \times \dfrac{1.586874 - 1}{0.08} = 20{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.586874}{0.08} = 20{,}000 \times 7.33593
$$

$$
\boxed{F = \text{₹1,46,718.6}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capital Recovery Factor ($A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$)

**Step 1: Start from the Present Worth of a Uniform Series.**

$$
P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Step 2: Invert the equation to isolate A.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{A} = \dfrac{1}{P} \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Step 3: Take the reciprocal.**

$$
A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = P \cdot (A/P, i\%, n)}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
A machine costing **P = ₹5,00,000** is financed at **i = 12% per year** over **n = 5 years**. Find the equivalent annual instalment A.

$$
A = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.12 \times (1.12)^{5}}{(1.12)^{5} - 1}
$$

$$
(1.12)^{5} = 1.762342
$$

$$
A = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.12 \times 1.762342}{1.762342 - 1} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.211481}{0.762342}
$$

$$
A = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.277410 = \text{₹1,38,705}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gradient Series Formulas

**Starting Point (from Cash Flow Diagram):**

```
        0    G   2G   3G   4G
        |    ↑    ↑    ↑    ↑
   ─────●────●────●────●────●────► n
   0     1   2    3    4    5
```

A gradient G means cash flows grow arithmetically: 0, G, 2G, 3G, ... (n−1)G.

**Step 1: Express gradient cash flow as kG at period k, where k = 0, 1, 2, ..., (n−1).**

**Step 2: Present worth of the k-th term (kG) discounted back to period 0.**

$$
P_{k} = \dfrac{kG}{(1+i)^{k}}
$$

**Step 3: Sum from k=1 to k=n−1 (the k=0 term is zero, so it can be ignored).**

$$
P = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{kG}{(1+i)^{k}}
$$

**Step 4: Use the closed-form geometric-arithmetic sum identity.**
This sum is a standard result that simplifies to:

$$
P = G \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = G \cdot (P/G, i\%, n)}
$$

**Step 5: To find the equivalent annual A from this P, use the capital recovery factor.**

$$
A = P \cdot (A/P, i\%, n) = G \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{A = G \cdot (A/G, i\%, n)}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
Maintenance costs form a gradient series with **G = ₹2,000/year²** over **n = 5 years**, with **i = 10%**. Find the equivalent A.

$$
A/G \text{ factor} = \dfrac{1}{0.10} - \dfrac{5}{(1.10)^{5} - 1}
$$

$$
(1.10)^{5} = 1.61051
$$

$$
A/G = 10 - \dfrac{5}{0.61051} = 10 - 8.1889 = 1.8111
$$

$$
A = 2{,}000 \times 1.8111 = \text{₹3,622.2 per year}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Which Formula When?

| Cash Flow Diagram Pattern | Identify By | Standard Formula Pair | Typical KTU Question |
|---------------------------|-------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| One arrow at t=0, one at t=n | Only 2 arrows | $(F/P)$ or $(P/F)$ | "Find F given P" or vice versa |
| Repeated equal arrows, n periods | Same magnitude each period | $(F/A)$ or $(P/A)$ | "Equivalent annual cost" |
| Repeated equal arrows + lump sum at end | A-series + F at n | Use A → F then add F | Salvage value problem |
| Growing arrows: 0, G, 2G, ... | Linear arithmetic growth | $(P/G)$ or $(A/G)$ | "Escalating maintenance cost" |
| Random unequal arrows | No pattern | Sum of $(P/F)$ for each arrow | "Mixed cash flow → PW" |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Cash Flow Diagram — Standard Convention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis0["n = 0 (Present)"]:::timepoint
    axis1["n = 1"]:::timepoint
    axis2["n = 2"]:::timepoint
    axis3["n = 3"]:::timepoint
    axisN["n = N (Horizon)"]:::timepoint

    inflow1["Inflow +A"]:::inflow
    inflow2["Inflow +A"]:::inflow
    inflow3["Inflow +A"]:::inflow
    inflowN["Inflow +F"]:::inflow

    present["Outflow P at n=0"]:::outflow
    horizon["Outflow at n=N"]:::outflow

    axis0 --- axis1 --- axis2 --- axis3 --- axisN

    present -.-> axis0
    inflow1 -.-> axis1
    inflow2 -.-> axis2
    inflow3 -.-> axis3
    inflowN -.-> axisN
    horizon -.-> axisN

    classDef timepoint fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545
    classDef inflow fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef outflow fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F0000
```

> **Reading the Diagram**: The blue nodes are time periods on the horizontal axis. Green nodes above are **cash inflows** (money received). Red nodes below are **cash outflows** (money paid). This is the universal KTU convention.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — From CFD to Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify the Engineering Problem]:::step
    B[Step 2: List all Cash Inflows and Outflows with Time Stamps]:::step
    C[Step 3: Draw the Cash Flow Diagram CFD]:::step
    D[Step 4: Classify Pattern Single, Uniform, Gradient, Mixed]:::step
    E[Step 5: Select the Correct KTU Formula Pair]:::step
    F[Step 6: Compute P F A or G Equivalent Value]:::step
    G[Step 7: Apply Decision Rule NPV greater than 0 Accept]:::step

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G

    classDef step fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#3E2723
```

> **Interpretation**: This block diagram captures the analytical pipeline that a student must follow in the KTU exam. Each block corresponds to marks awarded. Skipping Block C (the CFD) typically costs **2 marks** even if the final answer is correct.

## 4.3 Cash Flow Diagram — Gradient Series (G) Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t = 0"]:::tp
    t1["t = 1: cash flow = 0"]:::zero
    t2["t = 2: cash flow = G"]:::smallg
    t3["t = 3: cash flow = 2G"]:::smallg
    t4["t = 4: cash flow = 3G"]:::smallg
    t5["t = 5: cash flow = 4G"]:::smallg

    t0 --- t1 --- t2 --- t3 --- t4 --- t5

    classDef tp fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2545
    classDef zero fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#9E9E9E,stroke-width:1px,color:#212121
    classDef smallg fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C
```

> **Reading the Diagram**: The first cash flow is **0** (period 1), then it grows by **G** each period. This is the **Arithmetic Gradient Series** — the third major pattern tested by KTU.

## 4.4 Cash Flow Diagram — Uniform Series with Salvage Value

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0["t = 0: Outflow P (Investment)"]:::out
    p1["t = 1: Inflow A"]:::in
    p2["t = 2: Inflow A"]:::in
    p3["t = 3: Inflow A"]:::in
    p4["t = 4: Inflow A"]:::in
    p5["t = 5: Inflow A + Salvage SV"]:::insv

    p0 --- p1 --- p2 --- p3 --- p4 --- p5

    classDef out fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F0000
    classDef in fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef insv fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:3px,color:#004D40
```

> **Interpretation**: A typical equipment purchase problem. The single down-arrow at t=0 is the capital cost P. Equal up-arrows at t=1 to t=5 are the annual benefits A. The last arrow at t=5 carries **both A and Salvage Value (SV)**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Cash Flow Diagram. Explain the convention used to represent cash inflows and cash outflows.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Cash Flow Diagram (CFD)** is a graphical tool used in engineering economics to represent the timing and magnitude of cash flows over the life of a project. The **horizontal axis represents time (n)** in discrete periods, and the **vertical axis represents the magnitude of cash flows**.

- **Upward arrows (↑)** represent **cash inflows** — money received, revenue earned, salvage value realized.
- **Downward arrows (↓)** represent **cash outflows** — money invested, costs incurred, payments made.
- The arrow length is **proportional to the magnitude** of the cash flow.
- **Period 0** is the *present*, and the right edge of the diagram is the *horizon* of the project.

A cash flow diagram is the **mandatory first step** before applying any time-value-of-money formula.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the four basic cash flow patterns used in engineering economy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four basic cash flow patterns are:

1. **Single Payment (Lump Sum)**: A single cash flow occurring at a specific point in time. Represented by only one arrow on the diagram. Example: A one-time investment of ₹1,00,000 at t=0.

2. **Uniform Series (Annuity)**: A series of **equal cash flows** repeating every period for `n` periods. Example: Annual maintenance cost of ₹10,000 for 10 years.

3. **Gradient Series**: Cash flows that **change by a constant amount G** each period, starting from zero. Example: Maintenance cost of ₹0, ₹2,000, ₹4,000, ₹6,000 in years 1, 2, 3, 4.

4. **Mixed (Irregular) Cash Flows**: A combination of P, A, F, and G in a single diagram. Example: An initial investment P plus equal annual revenues A plus a final salvage F.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A manufacturing company is evaluating two machines, A and B, for a 6-year project with an interest rate of 10% per year.**

| Parameter | Machine A | Machine B |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Initial Cost (P) | ₹6,00,000 | ₹8,50,000 |
| Annual Operating Cost (A) | ₹1,50,000 | ₹1,00,000 |
| Salvage Value (SV) | ₹80,000 | ₹1,20,000 |

**Determine which machine should be selected using the Present Worth method. Draw the cash flow diagram for each.**

#### (a) Draw the Cash Flow Diagram for Machine A and Machine B. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Machine A — Cash Flow Diagram:**

```
   P = 6,00,000
      ↓
   ●────●────●────●────●────●────●───► n
   0    1    2    3    4    5    6
        ↑    ↑    ↑    ↑    ↑   ↑
        A    A    A    A    A   A+SV
        1.5L 1.5L 1.5L 1.5L 1.5L 1.5L+0.8L
```

**Machine B — Cash Flow Diagram:**

```
   P = 8,50,000
      ↓
   ●────●────●────●────●────●────●───► n
   0    1    2    3    4    5    6
        ↑    ↑    ↑    ↑    ↑   ↑
        A    A    A    A    A   A+SV
        1.0L 1.0L 1.0L 1.0L 1.0L 1.0L+1.2L
```

> **Marking Note**: The student must clearly show P as a downward arrow at n=0, A as upward arrows for n=1 to n=6, and the Salvage Value as an additional upward arrow at n=6. **(7 marks: 2 for arrows, 2 for time axis, 1.5 for labels, 1.5 for salvage annotation)**

#### (b) Compute the Present Worth of costs for each machine and recommend the best alternative. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**General formula for Present Worth (cost side):**

$$
PW = P + A \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 6) - SV \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 6)
$$

**Step 1: Look up / compute the factors at i = 10%, n = 6.**

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 6) = \dfrac{(1.10)^{6} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{6}} = \dfrac{1.771561 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.771561} = \dfrac{0.771561}{0.177156} = 4.3553
$$

$$
(P/F, 10\%, 6) = (1.10)^{-6} = \dfrac{1}{1.771561} = 0.5645
$$

**Step 2: Compute PW for Machine A.**

$$
PW_{A} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 4.3553 - 80{,}000 \times 0.5645
$$

$$
PW_{A} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}53{,}295 - 45{,}160 = \text{₹12,08,135}
$$

**Step 3: Compute PW for Machine B.**

$$
PW_{B} = 8{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 4.3553 - 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.5645
$$

$$
PW_{B} = 8{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}35{,}530 - 67{,}740 = \text{₹12,17,790}
$$

**Step 4: Decision.**

Since $PW_{A} = \text{₹12,08,135} < PW_{B} = \text{₹12,17,790}$, **Machine A is preferred** (lower equivalent present cost).

> **Examiner Note**: Both A and B are cost-only problems (no revenue), so the **lower PW** is selected. Students often confuse "lower" vs. "higher" — clarify in exam.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A construction company is offered two payment plans for a contract worth ₹10,00,000 at an interest rate of 12% per year.**

- **Plan 1**: Pay ₹10,00,000 at the end of year 5 (lump sum).
- **Plan 2**: Pay ₹2,50,000 at the end of each year for 5 years (uniform series).

**Find the Present Worth of both plans and recommend the cheaper option. Draw the cash flow diagrams.**

#### (a) Draw the Cash Flow Diagram for both plans and compute the Present Worth of each plan. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Plan 1 — Cash Flow Diagram (Single Payment at n=5):**

```
                    F = 10,00,000
                      ↓
   ─────●─────●─────●─────●─────●───► n
   0     1     2     3     4     5
```

**Plan 2 — Cash Flow Diagram (Uniform Series A = 2,50,000):**

```
                A    A    A    A    A
                ↓    ↓    ↓    ↓    ↓
   ─────●─────●─────●─────●─────●─────► n
   0     1     2     3     4     5
```

**Step 1: Look up / compute factors at i = 12%, n = 5.**

$$
(P/F, 12\%, 5) = (1.12)^{-5} = \dfrac{1}{1.762342} = 0.5674
$$

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{(1.12)^{5} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{5}} = \dfrac{0.762342}{0.211481} = 3.6048
$$

**Step 2: Compute PW of Plan 1.**

$$
PW_{1} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674 = \text{₹5,67,400}
$$

**Step 3: Compute PW of Plan 2.**

$$
PW_{2} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048 = \text{₹9,01,200}
$$

#### (b) Compare and recommend. Also compute the equivalent uniform annual cost of Plan 1 to cross-verify. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Comparison.**

$$
PW_{1} = \text{₹5,67,400} < PW_{2} = \text{₹9,01,200}
$$

**Recommendation: Plan 1 (lump sum payment) is cheaper by ₹3,33,800 in present-value terms.** Choose Plan 1.

**Step 2: Cross-verify using equivalent annual cost (A) of Plan 1.**

$$
A_{1} = P \cdot (A/P, 12\%, 5) = 5{,}67{,}400 \times \dfrac{0.12 \times 1.762342}{0.762342}
$$

$$
A_{1} = 5{,}67{,}400 \times 0.27741 = \text{₹1,57,386 per year}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with Plan 2's annual cost.**

Plan 2's annual cost is the actual A = ₹2,50,000.

Since $A_{1} = \text{₹1,57,386} < A_{2} = \text{₹2,50,000}$, **Plan 1 is again confirmed as the cheaper alternative.** ✓

**Step 4: Savings in annual terms.**

$$
\text{Savings} = 2{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}57{,}386 = \text{₹92,614 per year}
$$

> **Examiner Note**: Cross-verification using a different time-value method is a **bonus point** in valuation. It shows the student understands the equivalence of P, F, and A at a given interest rate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Cash Flow Diagram Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the Cash Flow Diagram (CFD)** — Costs **2 marks** directly. KTU 2024 scheme mandates the diagram.
> 2. **Inverting arrow direction** — Showing a *receipt* as a down-arrow (or vice-versa) signals the student has misunderstood the sign convention. Costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up `i` and `n` units** — If `i` is given as 6% per year, do **NOT** use n in months (or vice versa). Always normalize units. Costs up to **3 marks** if the entire numerical answer is wrong.
> 4. **Confusing P, F, A, and G** — Students often write "Find A" but apply the F/P formula. Carefully re-read what is being asked.
> 5. **Wrong factor naming** — Using $F/A$ when $P/A$ is required. The factor name **must match** the unknowns.
> 6. **Not showing the salvage value separately** at the end of the diagram — the last arrow should show **both A and SV** combined, or have SV as a separate arrow. Annotate clearly.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention the perspective** — Always clarify "from the investor's point of view" or "from the company's point of view" when setting up the CFD.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Cash Flow Diagram (CFD)** is a **time-vs-money graph** with arrows. Horizontal axis = time, vertical = magnitude.
- ✅ **Upward arrows (↑) = Cash Inflows** (revenue, salvage, savings). **Downward arrows (↓) = Cash Outflows** (investment, cost, payment).
- ✅ **Period 0** is the present. The right edge is the horizon.
- ✅ The **four standard patterns** are: (1) Single Payment, (2) Uniform Series, (3) Gradient Series, (4) Mixed.
- ✅ **Standard KTU symbols**: P, F, A, G, i, n, SV — know each one cold.
- ✅ **Six core formulas** to memorize:
  - $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ — Single Payment Compound Amount
  - $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ — Single Payment Present Worth
  - $F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ — Uniform Series Future Worth
  - $P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ — Uniform Series Present Worth
  - $A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ — Capital Recovery
  - $A = G \cdot \left[\frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}\right]$ — Gradient to Annuity
- ✅ The **interest rate `i`** must be in **decimal form** (10% = 0.10) before substituting.
- ✅ The number of periods `n` must be in the **same time unit** as `i` (years ↔ years, months ↔ months).
- ✅ **Draw the CFD FIRST**, identify the pattern, **THEN** select the formula.
- ✅ **Cross-verify** answers by computing equivalent A or F — KTU rewards consistency.
- ✅ For cost-only problems, **lower PW is preferred**. For revenue-only problems, **higher PW is preferred**.
- ✅ The CFD is worth **at least 2 marks** even in a 7-mark sub-question. Never skip it.
- ✅ Know the **factor name** you are using: SPCAF, SPPWF, USCAF, USPWF, CRF, SFF, GPWF, GUSF.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interest Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interest Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Interest

**Interest** is the compensation paid by a borrower to a lender for the use of borrowed money, expressed as a percentage of the principal over a specified period. In engineering economics, interest is the economic rent on capital and is the fundamental driver of the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Value of Money (TVM):** A fundamental financial principle stating that **a unit of money received today is worth more than the same unit of money received in the future**, because today's money can be invested to earn a positive return.

The **Interest Rate ($i$)** is defined mathematically as:

$$i = \frac{\text{Interest earned or charged per time period}}{\text{Principal (amount of money borrowed or lent)}}$$

The principal notations used universally in KTU engineering economics problems are:
- **$P$** = Present Worth / Principal (money at time $t = 0$)
- **$F$** = Future Worth / Future Value (money at time $t = n$)
- **$A$** = Uniform Annual Series / Annuity (equal end-of-period payments)
- **$n$** = Number of compounding periods (years, months, etc.)
- **$i$** = Interest rate per period (as a decimal)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Money as a Seed" Idea

Think of money as a **seed**:
- If you bury ₹1,000 in your backyard today, it remains ₹1,000 after a year.
- But if you "plant" ₹1,000 in a savings account at 8% interest, after one year it grows to ₹1,080 — you now have more "trees" (rupees) than you started with.
- **Interest is the harvest** that money generates when given time to grow in a financial "field" (the bank/investment).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme for *Finance for Engineering* expects mastery of four pillars under Interest Calculations:
> 1. Simple Interest
> 2. Compound Interest
> 3. Effective vs. Nominal Interest Rates
> 4. Uniform Series (Annuity) Calculations

### 1.3 Why Engineering Students Must Learn This

Engineers are routinely required to perform **economic feasibility analysis** of projects — comparing alternatives with different cash flow patterns, evaluating loans for equipment purchase, or determining the minimum attractive rate of return (MARR). Without the ability to convert money across time using interest formulas, **no meaningful economic comparison is possible**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Cardinal Interest Systems

#### 2.1.1 Simple Interest (SI)
In simple interest, the interest is computed **only on the original principal** for every period. The principal never changes.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Identify Principal $P$, Rate $i$, Time $n$.
2. Compute interest per period: $I = P \cdot i$.
3. Total interest over $n$ periods: $I_n = P \cdot i \cdot n$.
4. Future worth is the sum of principal plus accumulated interest.

#### 2.1.2 Compound Interest (CI)
In compound interest, the interest earned in each period is **added back to the principal**, so subsequent periods earn "interest on interest" — a process called **capitalization**.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Period 1: $F_1 = P(1 + i)$.
2. Period 2: $F_2 = F_1(1 + i) = P(1+i)^2$.
3. Period $n$: $F_n = P(1+i)^n$.
4. Total interest earned = $F_n - P = P[(1+i)^n - 1]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple vs. Compound — Key Intuition:** With simple interest, money grows *linearly* with time. With compound interest, money grows *exponentially*. For any $i > 0$ and $n \geq 2$, CI always exceeds SI — the difference is the **"power of compounding."**

### 2.2 Nominal vs. Effective Interest Rates

- **Nominal Interest Rate ($i_{nom}$ or $i^{(m)}$):** The *stated* annual rate compounded $m$ times per year. Each sub-period rate is $i/m$.
- **Effective Annual Interest Rate ($i_{eff}$ or $i_a$):** The *actual* annual rate earned when compounding occurs more than once per year.

The relationship is:
$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

**Special case — Continuous Compounding ($m \to \infty$):**
$$i_{eff} = e^{i_{nom}} - 1$$

### 2.3 Uniform Series (Annuity) Fundamentals

An **annuity ($A$)** is a series of equal payments made at the end of each compounding period over $n$ consecutive periods.

| Annuity Type | Payment Timing | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Ordinary Annuity | End of period | $F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ |
| Annuity Due | Beginning of period | $F_{due} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} \cdot (1+i)$ |
| Deferred Annuity | Starts after $k$ periods of no payment | Use $(1+i)^k$ discount factor |

### 2.4 The KTU Master Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Single-Payment, Uniform-Series, and Gradient factors below form the entire KTU Module 4 problem-solving arsenal.** Memorize these — they are the **only** formulas required for numerical problems.

| Factor | Symbol | Formula | Solves For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single Payment Compound Amount | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^n$ | $F$ given $P$ |
| Single Payment Present Worth | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | $P$ given $F$ |
| Uniform Series Sinking Fund | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $A$ given $F$ |
| Uniform Series Compound Amount | $(F/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | $F$ given $A$ |
| Capital Recovery | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $A$ given $P$ |
| Uniform Series Present Worth | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | $P$ given $A$ |
| Effective Rate | $i_a$ | $\left(1 + \dfrac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m - 1$ | Equivalent annual rate |
| Continuous Compounding | $F$ | $P \cdot e^{i \cdot n}$ | $F$ with continuous compounding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention used in KTU textbooks:** $(X/Y, i, n)$ is read as: "Find $X$, given $Y$, at rate $i$ over $n$ periods." Example: $(A/P, 10\%, 5)$ = "Find the equivalent annual amount $A$, given a present sum $P$, at 10% for 5 years."

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Equipment Purchase Financing:** An engineer purchasing a CNC machine on a 5-year loan uses **Capital Recovery** to compute monthly instalments.
- **Project Feasibility:** A B.Tech project evaluating a solar installation uses **Present Worth** to discount lifetime savings back to year 0 for comparison.
- **Savings Goals:** Determining the yearly deposit needed to retire a bond issue uses the **Sinking Fund** factor.
- **Inflation-Adjusted Decisions:** Converting vendor "12% compounded quarterly" quotes into a comparable effective rate using the effective interest rate formula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Compound Interest Formula

We start with a principal $P$ earning interest rate $i$ per period. Let $F_n$ denote the future worth at the end of period $n$.

**Step 1 — Period 1 balance:**
The interest earned in period 1 is $P \cdot i$. Adding it to the principal:
$$F_1 = P + P \cdot i = P(1 + i)$$

**Step 2 — Period 2 balance (interest on interest begins):**
At the start of period 2, the new principal is $F_1$. Interest is earned on this:
$$F_2 = F_1 + F_1 \cdot i = F_1(1 + i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to period $n$ by mathematical induction:**
Assume $F_{n-1} = P(1+i)^{n-1}$. Then:
$$F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-1} \cdot i = F_{n-1}(1+i) = P(1+i)^{n-1}(1+i) = P(1+i)^n$$

$$\boxed{\therefore F = P(1+i)^n}$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the present worth $P$:**
Divide both sides by $(1+i)^n$:
$$P = F(1+i)^{-n} = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^n}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Effective Interest Rate

Suppose a nominal rate of $i_{nom}$ is compounded $m$ times per year. The sub-period rate is $i_{nom}/m$. Starting with $P$, after one year ($m$ sub-periods):

$$F = P\left(1 + \frac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m$$

By definition, the effective annual rate $i_{eff}$ would give the same $F$ in one period:
$$F = P(1 + i_{eff})$$

Equating the two expressions:
$$P(1 + i_{eff}) = P\left(1 + \frac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m$$

Dividing by $P$ and subtracting 1:
$$\boxed{i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m - 1}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Future Worth of an Ordinary Annuity

Consider an annuity $A$ deposited at the **end** of each of $n$ periods, earning rate $i$ per period. We want the future worth $F$ at the end of period $n$.

**Step 1 — Cash flow timeline (each $A$ earns interest until period $n$):**
- The first payment (period 1) compounds for $(n-1)$ periods: $A(1+i)^{n-1}$
- The second payment (period 2) compounds for $(n-2)$ periods: $A(1+i)^{n-2}$
- ... continuing ...
- The $(n-1)$th payment compounds for 1 period: $A(1+i)^{1}$
- The $n$th payment (period $n$) does not earn interest: $A(1+i)^{0} = A$

**Step 2 — Sum the geometric series:**
$$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i) + A$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $A$ and multiply by $(1+i)/(1+i)$ to apply the geometric sum formula:**

Let $x = (1+i)$. The series is:
$$F = A\left[x^{n-1} + x^{n-2} + \cdots + x + 1\right]$$

Multiply both sides by $x$:
$$xF = A\left[x^n + x^{n-1} + \cdots + x^2 + x\right]$$

Subtract the original from this:
$$xF - F = A\left[x^n - 1\right]$$

Factor and isolate $F$:
$$F(x - 1) = A(x^n - 1)$$

Substitute back $x = 1 + i$:
$$\boxed{F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor

We want the annual equivalent $A$ that recovers a present sum $P$ over $n$ periods at rate $i$.

**Step 1 — Use the equivalence principle.** A present sum $P$ is equivalent to a future sum $F$ via:
$$F = P(1+i)^n$$

**Step 2 — The same $F$ can be generated by an annuity $A$ via:**
$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

**Step 3 — Set the two expressions for $F$ equal:**
$$P(1+i)^n = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $A$:**
$$\boxed{A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}}$$

### 3.5 The Uniform-Series Present Worth Factor (P/A) Derivation

By the equivalence principle, the present worth of an annuity $A$ over $n$ periods is found by taking the future worth and discounting:

$$P = \frac{F}{(1+i)^n} = \frac{1}{(1+i)^n} \cdot A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

Simplifying:
$$\boxed{P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}}$$

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A B.Tech graduate deposits ₹20,000 at the end of every year into an account earning 9% compounded annually. Find the amount at the end of 6 years.

**Given:** $A = 20{,}000$, $i = 0.09$, $n = 6$.

**Step 1 — Identify the factor:** $F$ is required given $A$, so we use $(F/A, 9\%, 6)$.

**Step 2 — Compute $(1+i)^n$:**
$$(1.09)^6 = 1.677100$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator $(1+i)^n - 1$:**
$$1.677100 - 1 = 0.677100$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator $i$:**
$$i = 0.09$$

**Step 5 — Apply the formula:**
$$F = 20000 \cdot \frac{0.677100}{0.09} = 20000 \cdot 7.523335 = 150{,}466.69$$

$$\boxed{F \approx ₹1{,}50{,}467}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Interest Calculation Knowledge Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Interest Calculations] --> B[Simple Interest]
    A --> C[Compound Interest]
    A --> D[Effective vs Nominal Rate]
    A --> E[Uniform Series Annuity]
    B --> B1[Linear Growth: F = P 1 + i n]
    C --> C1[Exponential Growth: F = P 1+i power n]
    C1 --> C2[Single Payment Compounding]
    C1 --> C3[Equal Payment Annuity]
    D --> D1[Nominal i nom Compounded m times]
    D --> D2[Effective i eff = 1 + i nom over m power m minus 1]
    D --> D3[Continuous Compounding F = P e power i n]
    E --> E1[Ordinary Annuity End of Period]
    E --> E2[Annuity Due Beginning of Period]
    E --> E3[Deferred Annuity Starts Later]
    E1 --> E4[Future Worth F of A]
    E1 --> E5[Present Worth P of A]
    E1 --> E6[Capital Recovery A from P]
    E1 --> E7[Sinking Fund A to F]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Cash Flow Time Line Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[Time t equals 0: P Present Worth] --> T1[Time t equals 1: Payment 1]
    T1 --> T2[Time t equals 2: Payment 2]
    T2 --> T3[Time t equals 3 dots dots dots]
    T3 --> TN[Time t equals n: Final Payment]
    T0 --> A[Interest Rate i per period]
    A --> T1
    A --> T2
    A --> T3
    A --> TN
    TN --> F[Future Worth F at time n]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Decision Flow for Choosing the Correct Factor

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Identify Known Variables] --> Q1{What is the cash flow pattern}
    Q1 -->|Single sum P or F| SP[Use Single Payment Factors]
    Q1 -->|Equal payments A| UA[Use Uniform Series Factors]
    Q1 -->|Compounding frequency conversion| ER[Use Effective Rate Formula]
    SP --> SP1{Find P or F}
    SP1 -->|Find F| SPF1[F equals P times 1+i power n]
    SP1 -->|Find P| SPF2[P equals F times 1+i power minus n]
    UA --> UA1{What is the goal}
    UA1 -->|Find A from P| UAF1[A equals P times A over P factor]
    UA1 -->|Find P from A| UAF2[P equals A times P over A factor]
    UA1 -->|Find F from A| UAF3[F equals A times F over A factor]
    UA1 -->|Find A to reach F| UAF4[A equals F times A over F factor]
    ER --> ER1[ia equals 1 plus i nom over m power m minus 1]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Simple Interest and Compound Interest. State one practical scenario where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Simple Interest | Compound Interest |
|---|---|---|
| Base for interest | Always original principal $P$ | Principal plus accumulated interest |
| Growth pattern | Linear with time | Exponential with time |
| Total interest for $n$ periods | $I = P \cdot i \cdot n$ | $I = P[(1+i)^n - 1]$ |

**Scenario:** Simple interest is preferred for **short-term personal loans and bridge financing** where the lending period is less than one year (e.g., a 90-day vendor credit). Compound interest is preferred for **long-term savings, mortgages, and equipment financing** where the time value effect materially changes the repayment amount (e.g., a 20-year home loan).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is meant by the "Effective Annual Interest Rate"? Why is it always greater than or equal to the nominal rate when $m > 1$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Effective Annual Interest Rate ($i_{eff}$ or $i_a$)** is the actual interest earned or charged in one year when compounding occurs $m$ times per year at a nominal rate $i_{nom}$. Its formula is:
$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

**Reason it is $\geq i_{nom}$:** By the **Binomial Theorem**, $\left(1 + \frac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m$ expands to $1 + i_{nom} + (\text{positive cross-terms})$, so it always exceeds $1 + i_{nom}$, making $i_{eff} \geq i_{nom}$. Equality holds **only** when $m = 1$ (annual compounding) or $i_{nom} = 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, candidates answer ONE full question of 14 marks from Module 4. Two alternative choices are provided below.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** A piece of engineering equipment is purchased for ₹5,00,000 by paying ₹1,50,000 as a down payment and the balance in 8 equal annual instalments at 10% interest compounded annually. Calculate the amount of each annual instalment using the capital recovery concept. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A student invests ₹1,00,000 in a scheme that offers 12% interest compounded quarterly. Find (i) the effective annual rate, and (ii) the amount after 3 years. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the unpaid balance that must be recovered by instalments:**
$$P = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Identify the factor:** We need $A$ given $P$, $i = 0.10$, $n = 8$. This is the **Capital Recovery Factor** $(A/P, 10\%, 8)$.

**Step 3 — Compute $(1+i)^n$:**
$$(1.10)^8 = 2.143589$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator $i(1+i)^n$:**
$$0.10 \times 2.143589 = 0.214359$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator $(1+i)^n - 1$:**
$$2.143589 - 1 = 1.143589$$

**Step 6 — Compute the capital recovery factor:**
$$\frac{A}{P} = \frac{0.214359}{1.143589} = 0.187444$$

**Step 7 — Compute the annual instalment:**
$$A = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.187444 = 65{,}605.40$$

$$\boxed{A \approx ₹65{,}605 \text{ per year}}$$

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $P = 1{,}00{,}000$, $i_{nom} = 0.12$, $m = 4$ (quarterly), $n = 3$ years.

**(i) Effective Annual Rate:**

Apply the formula with $i_{nom}/m = 0.12/4 = 0.03$:
$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + 0.03\right)^4 - 1 = 1.12550881 - 1 = 0.12550881$$

$$\boxed{i_{eff} \approx 12.55\%}$$

**(ii) Future Worth after 3 years:**

Method 1 — Use the effective annual rate with annual compounding:
$$F = P(1 + i_{eff})^n = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.12550881)^3$$
$$(1.12550881)^3 = 1.42576$$
$$F = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 1.42576 = 1{,}42{,}576$$

$$\boxed{F \approx ₹1{,}42{,}576}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** A company wants to create a sinking fund to replace a machine that costs ₹8,00,000 after 5 years. If the fund earns 8% interest compounded annually, determine (i) the equal annual deposit required, and (ii) the total interest earned by the fund. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the time value of money. An engineer is offered ₹5,00,000 today or ₹9,00,000 after 6 years. At what minimum rate of return should the engineer be indifferent between the two options? **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Given:** $F = 8{,}00{,}000$, $i = 0.08$, $n = 5$.

**(i) Annual deposit $A$ — Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, 8\%, 5)$:**

**Step 1 — Compute $(1+i)^n$:**
$$(1.08)^5 = 1.469328$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator $i$:**
$$i = 0.08$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator $(1+i)^n - 1$:**
$$1.469328 - 1 = 0.469328$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sinking fund factor:**
$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{0.08}{0.469328} = 0.170456$$

**Step 5 — Compute the annual deposit:**
$$A = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.170456 = 1{,}36{,}365$$

$$\boxed{A \approx ₹1{,}36{,}365 \text{ per year}}$$

**(ii) Total interest earned:**

**Step 1 — Total amount deposited over 5 years:**
$$\text{Total deposits} = 5 \times 1{,}36{,}365 = 6{,}81{,}825$$

**Step 2 — Total interest earned:**
$$I = F - \text{Total deposits} = 8{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}81{,}825 = 1{,}18{,}175$$

$$\boxed{I \approx ₹1{,}18{,}175}$$

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Time Value of Money (TVM):** TVM is the financial principle that **a rupee available today is worth more than the same rupee available in the future** because the present rupee can be invested to earn interest or returns. Three reasons justify TVM: (1) inflation erodes purchasing power, (2) money has investment/earning potential, and (3) there is inherent risk in postponing consumption.

**Indifference Rate Calculation:**

The engineer is indifferent when the future amount's present worth equals the immediate offer:
$$P_{immediate} = P_{future}$$
$$5{,}00{,}000 = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{(1+i)^6}$$

Rearranging:
$$(1+i)^6 = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.8$$

Taking the 6th root:
$$1 + i = (1.8)^{1/6} = 1.10266$$

$$\boxed{i \approx 10.27\%}$$

**Interpretation:** If the engineer can earn more than 10.27% elsewhere, the future ₹9,00,000 option is preferable; if not, the ₹5,00,000 today is better.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "1 +" in effective rate formula:** A frequent error is writing $i_{eff} = (i_{nom}/m)^m - 1$ — this gives a wrong (and negative) answer.
> 2. **Mixing up $n$:** In continuous compounding, $n$ is in years; in quarterly compounding, $n$ must be the **total number of quarters** (years × 4), not years.
> 3. **Annuity Due vs. Ordinary Annuity confusion:** KTU defaults to **ordinary annuity** (end-of-period) unless the problem explicitly states "beginning of each year/month." No marks are awarded for an annuity-due multiplier when the question asks for an ordinary annuity.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in the sinking fund factor:** Always verify the rate $i$ and the period $n$ are in the **same** units (both annual, both monthly, etc.). A 12% nominal rate used with annual $n = 5$ without converting to effective annual rate is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Interest Calculations**

- **Simple Interest Formula:** $F = P(1 + in)$ — linear growth; interest computed only on the original principal.
- **Compound Interest Formula:** $F = P(1+i)^n$ — exponential growth; interest compounds on previously earned interest.
- **Six Master Factors to Memorize:** $(F/P, i, n)$, $(P/F, i, n)$, $(F/A, i, n)$, $(P/A, i, n)$, $(A/P, i, n)$, $(A/F, i, n)$.
- **Effective Rate Formula:** $i_{eff} = \left(1 + \dfrac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m - 1$ — always $\geq i_{nom}$; equality only when $m = 1$.
- **Continuous Compounding:** $F = P \cdot e^{i \cdot n}$ and $i_{eff} = e^{i_{nom}} - 1$.
- **Sinking Fund:** Used to accumulate a future sum $F$ by equal annual deposits $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **Capital Recovery:** Used to recover a present sum $P$ by equal annual payments $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **Cash Flow Convention:** Receipts are positive (upward arrows), disbursements are negative (downward arrows). Always draw the cash flow diagram before substituting into a formula.
- **Indifference Rate:** Set $P_{immediate} = P_{future}$ and solve for $i$ — used to compare lump-sum offers at different times.
- **Mutually Exclusive Factor Inverse Property:** $(F/A) = 1/(A/F)$ and $(P/A) = 1/(A/P)$. This reciprocal relationship is a powerful verification tool during KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compound Interest Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Compound Interest Factors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Compound Interest Factors** are pre-derived mathematical multipliers (also called **Time Value of Money Factors** or **Discount Factors**) used in engineering economics to translate a single cash flow or a uniform series of cash flows occurring at different points in time into an equivalent value at a common reference point (Present, Future, or per period). They are standardized functions of the **interest rate ($i$)** and the **number of compounding periods ($n$)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, these six primary factors form the analytical spine of all capital budgeting, benefit-cost analysis, and equivalence calculations in the Finance for Engineering course. ## 1.2 The Six Primary Compound Interest Factors | Symbol | Factor Name | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $(F/P, i, n)$ | Single Payment Compound Amount | Find $F$ given $P$ | | $(P/F, i, n)$ | Single Payment Present Worth | Find $P$ given $F$ | | $(F/A, i, n)$ | Uniform Series Compound Amount | Find $F$ given $A$ | | $(A/F, i, n)$ | Sinking Fund Factor | Find $A$ given $F$ | | $(P/A, i, n)$ | Uniform Series Present Worth | Find $P$ given $A$ | | $(A/P, i, n)$ | Capital Recovery Factor | Find $A$ given $P$ | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Money Time Machine" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine money as a "time-traveling substance." A rupee today is **not** the same as a rupee ten years from now because the rupee today can be *invested* to grow. Compound Interest Factors are the **gear-boxes of this time machine**: they tell you precisely how many "future-rupees" one "present-rupee" is worth, or vice-versa, after shifting across $n$ years at interest rate $i$. Think of $i$ as the **speed of the time machine** (higher $i$ = faster growth), and $n$ as the **distance traveled** (number of years). The factors are the conversion ratios on the dashboard. ## 1.4 Why Engineers Study This Every engineering project (bridge, software, factory, power plant) involves cash flows spread over years. To decide *whether* a project is worth doing, all those future cash flows **must** be brought to a single, comparable time point. This is impossible without compound interest factors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A minimum of **one 14-mark question** in the End Semester Exam is guaranteed to involve direct application of these factors for evaluating engineering project alternatives. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why Compounding Works Step-by-Step 1. **You deposit a sum $P$ at time $t = 0$** with a bank paying interest rate $i$ per period. 2. **At end of period 1**, the bank pays interest $= P \cdot i$. The new balance becomes $P + P \cdot i = P(1+i)$. 3. **In period 2**, interest is now calculated on $P(1+i)$ (not just on $P$). This is the "compound" effect. 4. **At end of period 2**, balance $= P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$. 5. **Generalizing to $n$ periods**, the future value $F = P(1+i)^n$. > [!NOTE] > The **"Why"** behind compounding: Interest earns interest — the basis of all long-term wealth (and debt) accumulation. Einstein allegedly called it *"the most powerful force in the universe."* ## 2.2 Derivation of Each Factor (Conceptual Hooks) ### Factor 1: $(F/P, i, n)$ — Single Payment Compound Amount - **How it works:** Start with $P$, multiply by $(1+i)$ once per period for $n$ periods. - **Logic:** Each period, the existing balance grows by factor $(1+i)$. ### Factor 2: $(P/F, i, n)$ — Single Payment Present Worth - **How it works:** This is the algebraic inverse of Factor 1. - **Logic:** To bring a future sum "back to the present," we must *discount* it. ### Factor 3: $(F/A, i, n)$ — Uniform Series Compound Amount - **How it works:** Imagine depositing $A$ at the *end* of every period for $n$ periods into an account paying $i$. The first deposit earns interest for $(n-1)$ periods, the second for $(n-2)$, ..., the last deposit earns no interest. - **Logic:** Sum of a finite geometric series. ### Factor 4: $(A/F, i, n)$ — Sinking Fund Factor - **How it works:** Inverse of Factor 3. - **Real-world utility:** How much must you save *per year* to accumulate a target future sum $F$ (e.g., replacing a machine after 10 years). ### Factor 5: $(P/A, i, n)$ — Uniform Series Present Worth - **How it works:** Multiply $A$ by the appropriate conversion. Used extensively in **engineering project evaluation** (lump-sum equivalent of an annuity). - **Real-world utility:** Computing the *Present Worth* of equal annual savings or revenues from a project. ### Factor 6: $(A/P, i, n)$ — Capital Recovery Factor - **How it works:** Inverse of Factor 5. - **Real-world utility:** Computing the equivalent *annual cost* of a capital investment (loan repayment + interest). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (The High-Yield Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mastery Mandate:** For KTU exams, you must memorize all six formulas below. No formula sheet is provided in the ESE. | # | Factor | Standard Notation | Formula | Functional Inversion | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Single Payment Compound Amount | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{n}$ | $(P/F, i, n)$ | | 2 | Single Payment Present Worth | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | $(F/P, i, n)$ | | 3 | Uniform Series Compound Amount | $(F/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | $(A/F, i, n)$ | | 4 | Sinking Fund Factor | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $(F/A, i, n)$ | | 5 | Uniform Series Present Worth | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | $(A/P, i, n)$ | | 6 | Capital Recovery Factor | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $(P/A, i, n)$ | ### The Three Universal Equivalence Identities (KTU Favorites) $$\boxed{(F/P, i, n) \cdot (P/F, i, n) = 1}$$ $$\boxed{(F/A, i, n) \cdot (A/F, i, n) = 1}$$ $$\boxed{(P/A, i, n) \cdot (A/P, i, n) = 1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These identities are extremely useful in the *Unequal-Life Alternative* problems and *Multiple-Rate of Return* problems in Module 4. Master them — they save time in the 14-mark questions. ## 2.4 The Arithmetic Gradient Factors (Bonus — Frequently Tested) For cash flows increasing by a constant amount $G$ each period, KTU expects familiarity with: | Factor | Formula | | :--- | :--- | | $(P/G, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$ | | $(A/G, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | | $(F/G, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}}$ | The cash flow pattern for a gradient series: $0, G, 2G, 3G, \ldots, nG$ at the end of periods $1, 2, \ldots, n$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of $(F/P, i, n)$ **Setup:** A principal $P$ is invested at time $t=0$. Interest $i$ is compounded once per period. **Step 1:** Compute balance at end of period 1. $$F_1 = P + P \cdot i = P(1 + i)$$ **Step 2:** Compute balance at end of period 2. Interest now applies to $F_1$ (compound effect). $$F_2 = F_1 + F_1 \cdot i = F_1(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$$ **Step 3:** Compute balance at end of period 3. $$F_3 = F_2(1+i) = P(1+i)^2 (1+i) = P(1+i)^3$$ **Step 4:** By mathematical induction, at end of period $n$: $$F_n = P(1+i)^n$$ This gives us the formal definition: $$\boxed{(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^n}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic:** The exponent $n$ represents *the number of times* the multiplicative factor $(1+i)$ is applied. Period-by-period multiplication accumulates the growth. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of $(P/F, i, n)$ **Setup:** Given a future sum $F$ at time $n$, find the present equivalent $P$ at time $0$. **Step 1:** Start from the result of Section 3.1. $$F = P(1+i)^n$$ **Step 2:** Divide both sides by $(1+i)^n$ to isolate $P$. $$P = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^n}$$ **Step 3:** Rewrite as the inverse of $(F/P, i, n)$. $$\boxed{(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n} = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic:** Discounting is *future-to-present division*. We "undo" the compounding by repeatedly dividing. ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of $(F/A, i, n)$ — The Geometric Series Method **Setup:** A uniform series of end-of-period payments $A$ is deposited for $n$ periods at interest rate $i$. Find the future value $F$ at time $n$. **Step 1:** Construct the timeline (last deposit earns no interest, first deposit earns interest for $n-1$ periods). $$\begin{aligned} \text{Deposit at } t=1 &: \text{Future value at } t=n \text{ is } A(1+i)^{n-1} \\ \text{Deposit at } t=2 &: \text{Future value at } t=n \text{ is } A(1+i)^{n-2} \\ \text{Deposit at } t=3 &: \text{Future value at } t=n \text{ is } A(1+i)^{n-3} \\ \vdots \\ \text{Deposit at } t=n-1 &: \text{Future value at } t=n \text{ is } A(1+i)^{1} \\ \text{Deposit at } t=n &: \text{Future value at } t=n \text{ is } A(1+i)^{0} = A \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Sum all these future values to obtain the total $F$. $$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \ldots + A(1+i)^1 + A(1+i)^0$$ **Step 3:** Factor out $A$ and recognize the geometric series (ratio $r = 1+i$). $$F = A \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+i)^{k}$$ **Step 4:** Apply the closed-form geometric series formula. For ratio $r \neq 1$: $$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^{k} = \dfrac{r^{n} - 1}{r - 1}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute $r = (1+i)$. $$F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$ **Step 6:** Isolate the factor. $$\boxed{(F/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic:** Each payment is "compounded forward" to the endpoint; the resulting geometric series collapses to a closed-form ratio. The numerator is the *total growth*, the denominator $i$ is the *growth rate per period*. ## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation of $(A/F, i, n)$ — Sinking Fund Factor **Setup:** Given a desired future amount $F$, find the equivalent uniform annual deposit $A$ over $n$ periods. **Step 1:** Start with the formula from Section 3.3. $$F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$ **Step 2:** Invert the equation to solve for $A$. $$A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$ **Step 3:** Identify the factor. $$\boxed{(A/F, i, n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}}$$ ## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation of $(P/A, i, n)$ — The Multiplication Bridge **Setup:** Given uniform end-of-period payments $A$ for $n$ periods, find the present equivalent $P$ at $t=0$. **Step 1:** Use the equivalence chain: $A \to F$ using $(F/A, i, n)$, then $F \to P$ using $(P/F, i, n)$. $$P = A \cdot (F/A, i, n) \cdot (P/F, i, n)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the known formulas. $$P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the algebraic expression. $$P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$$ **Step 4:** Identify the factor. $$\boxed{(P/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}}$$ ## 3.6 Exhaustive Derivation of $(A/P, i, n)$ — Capital Recovery Factor **Setup:** Given a present investment $P$, find the equivalent annual payment $A$ over $n$ periods. **Step 1:** Start with the result of Section 3.5. $$P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$$ **Step 2:** Invert the relation to solve for $A$. $$A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$ **Step 3:** Identify the factor. $$\boxed{(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}}$$ ## 3.7 Numerical Demonstration (3-Period, 10% Example) Let $P = 1000$, $i = 10\% = 0.10$, $n = 3$. $$\begin{aligned} (F/P, 10\%, 3) &= (1+0.10)^3 = 1.331 \\ (P/F, 10\%, 3) &= (1.331)^{-1} = 0.7513 \\ (F/A, 10\%, 3) &= \dfrac{1.331 - 1}{0.10} = 3.310 \\ (A/F, 10\%, 3) &= \dfrac{0.10}{0.331} = 0.3021 \\ (P/A, 10\%, 3) &= \dfrac{0.331}{0.10 \times 1.331} = 2.4868 \\ (A/P, 10\%, 3) &= \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.331}{0.331} = 0.4021 \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification Check (Inversion Test):** $(F/P, 10\%, 3) \times (P/F, 10\%, 3) = 1.331 \times 0.7513 \approx 1.000$ ✓ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Cash Flow Time Line (Visualizing the Six Factors) The diagram below shows how each compound interest factor links two cash flow events on a typical engineering project timeline. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef nodeStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef arStyle fill:#065f46,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef axStyle fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff P0["P at t=0"]:::nodeStyle P1["P at t=0"]:::nodeStyle P2["P at t=0"]:::nodeStyle P3["P at t=0"]:::nodeStyle P4["P at t=0"]:::nodeStyle P5["P at t=0"]:::nodeStyle F0["F at t=n"]:::arStyle A0["A per period for n periods"]:::axStyle P1 -->|"(F/P, i, n)"| F0 F0 -->|"(P/F, i, n)"| P1 P2 -->|"(A/P, i, n)"| A0 A0 -->|"(P/A, i, n)"| P2 A0 -->|"(F/A, i, n)"| F0 F0 -->|"(A/F, i, n)"| A0 ``` ## 4.2 The Factor Equivalence Network (Block Architecture) This block diagram illustrates the analytical bridge between Present ($P$), Future ($F$), and Annual ($A$) cash flow representations. The blocks are the six standard factors; the arrows represent the direction of conversion. ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef inputNode fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef outputNode fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef midNode fill:#047857,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef factorNode fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px Pnode["P - PRESENT VALUE
at time t equals 0"]:::inputNode Anode["A - UNIFORM SERIES
equal cash flows per period"]:::midNode Fnode["F - FUTURE VALUE
at time t equals n"]:::outputNode F1["(F/P, i, n) equals (1+i) to power n"]:::factorNode F2["(P/F, i, n) equals 1 divided by (1+i) to power n"]:::factorNode F3["(F/A, i, n) equals (1+i)^n minus 1 divided by i"]:::factorNode F4["(A/F, i, n) equals i divided by (1+i)^n minus 1"]:::factorNode F5["(P/A, i, n) equals (1+i)^n minus 1 divided by i times (1+i)^n"]:::factorNode F6["(A/P, i, n) equals i times (1+i)^n divided by (1+i)^n minus 1"]:::factorNode Pnode -- F1 --> Fnode Fnode -- F2 --> Pnode Anode -- F3 --> Fnode Fnode -- F4 --> Anode Pnode -- F6 --> Anode Anode -- F5 --> Pnode ``` ## 4.3 Cash Flow Pattern Reference Diagram This is the canonical visualization of how each factor maps onto a real cash flow stream — a critical reference for KTU numerical problems. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef cfStyle fill:#312e81,stroke:#a5b4fc,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px t0["t=0"]:::cfStyle t1["t=1"]:::cfStyle t2["t=2"]:::cfStyle tn["t=n-1"]:::cfStyle tnm["t=n"]:::cfStyle P0["P (lump sum)"]:::cfStyle F0["F (lump sum)"]:::cfStyle A1["A"]:::cfStyle A2["A"]:::cfStyle A3["A"]:::cfStyle A4["A"]:::cfStyle t0 --- P0 t1 --- A1 t2 --- A2 tn --- A3 tnm --- A4 tnm --- F0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The arrows in the **equivalence network (4.2)** are the actual conversion operations. A KTU numerical problem will always tell you two knowns (e.g., $A$ and $i, n$) and ask for the third (e.g., $P$). You then trace the corresponding arrow. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i, n)$. Derive its mathematical expression.** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** The Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i, n)$ is the factor used to determine the equivalent future value $F$ of a present sum $P$ after $n$ periods at compound interest rate $i$ per period. **Derivation:** A principal $P$ invested at $t=0$ grows to $P(1+i)$ at $t=1$. Applying the same growth in the second period yields $P(1+i)^2$. Continuing this logic for $n$ periods, the balance becomes: $$F = P(1+i)^n$$ Therefore, the factor is $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^n$. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is a Sinking Fund Factor? Where is it applied in engineering project evaluation?** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** The Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, i, n)$ converts a known future sum $F$ into an equivalent uniform annual deposit $A$ over $n$ periods at interest rate $i$. It is mathematically given by $(A/F, i, n) = i / [(1+i)^n - 1]$. **Engineering Application:** In engineering economics, the sinking fund concept is used to: 1. Determine the equivalent annual depreciation that, when invested at the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)**, accumulates to the original asset cost at the end of its useful life (replacement fund). 2. Compute the annual contribution required to retire a future liability (e.g., decommissioning cost of a nuclear plant, mine site reclamation). 3. Establish a "**depreciation reserve fund**" so that capital assets can be replaced without a fresh large capital outflow. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Forgetting to convert the interest rate and compounding period consistently** (e.g., annual rate 12% but $n$ given in months). Always verify: $i$ and $n$ must be in the *same time unit*. > - **Sign errors** in cash flow diagrams. Outflows are negative, inflows are positive — KTU examiners explicitly check this. > - **Rounding too early.** Maintain at least 5 decimal places in intermediate factors. KTU values precision to 2 decimal places in the final answer. > - **Selecting the wrong factor** because the student memorized symbols but did not draw the cash flow diagram. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] A small-scale engineering firm is evaluating the purchase of a CNC machining center. The following cash flow information is given: - **Initial Investment (at $t=0$):** $\text{Rs. } 5{,}00{,}000$ - **Net Annual Revenue (end of year 1 through year 8):** $\text{Rs. } 1{,}20{,}000$ per year - **Salvage Value (at end of year 8):** $\text{Rs. } 80{,}000$ - **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return):** $12\%$ compounded annually **Tasks:** **(a)** Compute the Present Worth ($PW$) of the project at $i = 12\%$. Use standard compound interest factors. (7 marks) **(b)** Determine whether the project is economically viable. If not, compute the actual rate of return realized by the firm. (7 marks) --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Step 1: Identify the cash flows and the factor to use.** The annual revenue of $\text{Rs. } 1{,}20{,}000$ for 8 years is a uniform series — use $(P/A, 12\%, 8)$. The salvage value is a single future payment — use $(P/F, 12\%, 8)$. The initial investment is already a present value at $t=0$. **Step 2: Compute the relevant factors at $i = 12\%, n = 8$.** $$\begin{aligned} (P/A, 12\%, 8) &= \dfrac{(1+0.12)^8 - 1}{0.12 \times (1+0.12)^8} = \dfrac{2.4760 - 1}{0.12 \times 2.4760} = \dfrac{1.4760}{0.29712} = 4.9676 \\[6pt] (P/F, 12\%, 8) &= (1.12)^{-8} = \dfrac{1}{2.4760} = 0.4039 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Compute Present Worth of revenues and salvage.** $$PW_{\text{revenue}} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4.9676 = \text{Rs. } 5{,}96{,}112$$ $$PW_{\text{salvage}} = 80{,}000 \times 0.4039 = \text{Rs. } 32{,}312$$ **Step 4: Compute Net Present Worth.** $$PW_{\text{net}} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}96{,}112 + 32{,}312 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}28{,}424$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** $PW = +\text{Rs. } 1{,}28{,}424$ (positive). #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Step 1: Economic viability test.** Since $PW > 0$ at $i = 12\%$, the project **earns more than the MARR**, hence it is **economically viable**. ✓ **Step 2: Compute the actual rate of return ($i^*$) using trial-and-error / interpolation.** The equation to solve is: $$-5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times (P/A, i^*, 8) + 80{,}000 \times (P/F, i^*, 8) = 0$$ **Try $i = 18\%$:** $$\begin{aligned} (P/A, 18\%, 8) &= \dfrac{(1.18)^8 - 1}{0.18 \times (1.18)^8} = \dfrac{3.7589 - 1}{0.18 \times 3.7589} = \dfrac{2.7589}{0.6766} = 4.0776 \\[6pt] (P/F, 18\%, 8) &= (1.18)^{-8} = 0.2660 \end{aligned}$$ $$PW_{18\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4.0776 + 80{,}000 \times 0.2660$$ $$= -5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}89{,}312 + 21{,}280 = +\text{Rs. } 10{,}592$$ Still positive — try a higher rate. **Try $i = 19\%$:** $$\begin{aligned} (P/A, 19\%, 8) &= \dfrac{(1.19)^8 - 1}{0.19 \times (1.19)^8} = \dfrac{4.0234 - 1}{0.19 \times 4.0234} = \dfrac{3.0234}{0.7644} = 3.9544 \\[6pt] (P/F, 19\%, 8) &= (1.19)^{-8} = 0.2486 \end{aligned}$$ $$PW_{19\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times 3.9544 + 80{,}000 \times 0.2486$$ $$= -5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}74{,}528 + 19{,}888 = -\text{Rs. } 5{,}584$$ Now negative. **Step 3: Linear interpolation between $i = 18\%$ and $i = 19\%$.** $$i^* = 18\% + \dfrac{10{,}592}{10{,}592 + 5{,}584} \times (19\% - 18\%)$$ $$i^* = 18\% + \dfrac{10{,}592}{16{,}176} \times 1\% = 18\% + 0.65\% = 18.65\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** The project yields a real return of approximately **$18.65\%$**, which is well above the MARR of 12%. The project is **highly recommended**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice A consulting engineer must choose between two mutually exclusive designs for a small hydroelectric power unit. The two alternatives are: | Item | Design X (Pelton Wheel) | Design Y (Francis Turbine) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Initial Cost | $\text{Rs. } 8{,}00{,}000$ | $\text{Rs. } 12{,}00{,}000$ | | Annual Operating Cost | $\text{Rs. } 1{,}50{,}000$ | $\text{Rs. } 90{,}000$ | | Salvage Value | $\text{Rs. } 1{,}00{,}000$ | $\text{Rs. } 2{,}00{,}000$ | | Useful Life | 10 years | 10 years | | Annual Power Revenue | $\text{Rs. } 3{,}50{,}000$ | $\text{Rs. } 4{,}50{,}000$ | | MARR | $10\%$ | $10\%$ | **Tasks:** **(a)** Compute the Annual Worth ($AW$) of each design and recommend the better one. Use compound interest factors. (7 marks) **(b)** Verify your recommendation by computing the Future Worth ($FW$) of each design at the end of year 10. (7 marks) --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Step 1: Recall the capital recovery formulas.** The Annual Worth of an investment is the annual cost (or net cash flow) that is equivalent to all the project's cash flows, expressed on a per-year basis over the entire life. We use: $$AW = -P \cdot (A/P, i, n) - \text{Annual Operating Cost} + \text{Annual Revenue} + S \cdot (A/F, i, n)$$ where $S$ is the salvage value. This uses the **Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$** and the **Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, i, n)$**. **Step 2: Compute the factors at $i = 10\%, n = 10$.** $$\begin{aligned} (A/P, 10\%, 10) &= \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{1.5937} = 0.16275 \\[6pt] (A/F, 10\%, 10) &= \dfrac{0.10}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10}{1.5937} = 0.06275 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Compute $AW$ for Design X (Pelton).** $$\begin{aligned} AW_X &= -8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.16275 - 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.06275 \\ &= -1{,}30{,}200 - 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 + 6{,}275 \\ &= +\text{Rs. } 76{,}075 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Compute $AW$ for Design Y (Francis).** $$\begin{aligned} AW_Y &= -12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.16275 - 90{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.06275 \\ &= -1{,}95{,}300 - 90{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 12{,}550 \\ &= +\text{Rs. } 1{,}77{,}250 \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision (by Annual Worth):** $AW_Y = \text{Rs. } 1{,}77{,}250 > AW_X = \text{Rs. } 76{,}075$. **Choose Design Y (Francis Turbine).** #### Part (b) — Model Solution (Verification via Future Worth) **Step 1: The $FW$ formula for any project is:** $$FW = -P \cdot (F/P, i, n) - \text{Annual Operating Cost} \cdot (F/A, i, n) + \text{Annual Revenue} \cdot (F/A, i, n) + S$$ Alternatively: $FW = AW \cdot (F/A, i, n)$, which is the more efficient verification route. **Step 2: Compute $(F/A, 10\%, 10)$.** $$(F/A, 10\%, 10) = \dfrac{(1.10)^{10} - 1}{0.10} = \dfrac{1.5937}{0.10} = 15.937$$ **Step 3: Compute $FW$ for Design X.** $$FW_X = 76{,}075 \times 15.937 = \text{Rs. } 12{,}12{,}569$$ **Step 4: Compute $FW$ for Design Y.** $$FW_Y = 1{,}77{,}250 \times 15.937 = \text{Rs. } 28{,}24{,}829$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification Result:** $FW_Y = \text{Rs. } 28.24$ lakh $\gg FW_X = \text{Rs. } 12.12$ lakh. **The recommendation of Design Y stands confirmed.** The equivalence principle ($AW$ and $FW$ methods yielding consistent decisions) is validated. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Compound Interest Factors** - **Six core factors** must be memorized: $(F/P)$, $(P/F)$, $(F/A)$, $(A/F)$, $(P/A)$, $(A/P)$. - **Every factor is a function of two variables only: $i$ and $n$.** $P$, $F$, and $A$ are the cash flow variables — never inside the factor! - **Inversion pairs:** $(F/P) \cdot (P/F) = 1$; $(F/A) \cdot (A/F) = 1$; $(P/A) \cdot (A/P) = 1$. Use these to derive any factor from another. - **Compounding chain (the bridge):** $A \xrightarrow{(F/A)} F \xrightarrow{(P/F)} P$. This is the most-used derivation trick. - **Arithmetic gradient factors** $(P/G)$, $(A/G)$, $(F/G)$ are tested for cash flows that grow by a constant amount $G$ each period. - **Always check time-unit consistency** for $i$ and $n$. Quarterly compounding for 3 years = 12 periods, rate = annual $/ 4$. - **For multiple alternatives**, both Annual Worth and Future Worth methods will yield the **same decision** (equivalence principle). - **Sign convention:** Cash outflows (initial cost, annual expenses) are negative; cash inflows (revenues, salvage) are positive. - **Common KTU 14-mark pattern:** Compute $PW$ or $AW$ of a multi-period project, then perform a **rate-of-return analysis** by interpolation. - **Higher $i$** → lower present worth of future sums (discounting intensifies). **Higher $n$** → amplified compounding effect. - **Gradient series pattern:** $0, G, 2G, 3G, \ldots, nG$ — use $(A/G, i, n)$ to convert to an equivalent uniform annual amount. > [!NOTE] > **Final Mantra for KTU 2024:** *Draw the cash flow diagram first. Then identify the two knowns. Then pick the factor that converts them. Then compute. Never trust a problem solution that does not start with a diagram.*

topics.title: Interest Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interest Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interest Tables** (also called **Time Value of Money Factor Tables** or **Discrete Compounding Tables**) are pre-tabulated numerical values of the standard mathematical factors that convert between **Present Worth (P)**, **Future Worth (F)**, and **Uniform Annual Series (A)** cash flows at a given **interest rate (i%)** over a given **number of compounding periods (n)**.

These tables are an engineering economist's ready-reckoner.
