Instead of recomputing $(1+i)^n$ or geometric series sums on a calculator for every problem, the engineer simply looks up the correct column (interest rate) and row (number of years) to obtain the relevant factor and multiplies it by the known cash flow.

## 1.2 The Six Fundamental Factors

The KTU 2024 Scheme (and the standard reference text by Blank & Tarquin) recognises **six primary interest factors**, each written using the notation $(X/Y, i\%, n)$, meaning *"find X given Y at interest rate i over n periods"*:

1. $(F/P, i\%, n)$ — Single Payment Compound Amount Factor
2. $(P/F, i\%, n)$ — Single Payment Present Worth Factor
3. $(F/A, i\%, n)$ — Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor
4. $(P/A, i\%, n)$ — Uniform Series Present Worth Factor
5. $(A/P, i\%, n)$ — Capital Recovery Factor
6. $(A/F, i\%, n)$ — Sinking Fund Factor

> [!NOTE]
> A seventh factor — the **Arithmetic Gradient Factor** $(P/G, i\%, n)$ — is also expected of KTU students. It converts a *G*-gradient series into an equivalent present worth.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multiplication Table for Money"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** When you were a child, you used a **multiplication table** to find $7 \times 8$ without actually multiplying. Interest tables are the *engineer's multiplication table for money across time*. Just as $7 \times 8 = 56$ is always true, $(P/A, 10\%, 5) = 3.7908$ is always true. The table lets you skip the long calculation of $\frac{(1.10)^5 - 1}{0.10(1.10)^5}$ and jump straight to the answer.

The factors are essentially *constants* of engineering economics — they never change for a given $(i, n)$ pair, so they are tabulated once and reused thousands of times.

## 1.4 Why Engineers Use Them

- **Speed:** Reduces exam time and design-office computation effort.
- **Accuracy:** Eliminates arithmetic mistakes in exponentiation.
- **Standardisation:** All engineers use the same factor values — unambiguous communication.
- **Tool independence:** The solution is verifiable even without a calculator, which is critical in KTU pen-and-paper examinations.

## 1.5 Standard Tabulation Convention

Most KTU-recommended tables list:

- **Rows:** $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, 50$ (or 100)
- **Columns:** $i = 0.5\%, 1\%, 2\%, 4\%, 5\%, 6\%, 8\%, 10\%, 12\%, 15\%, 18\%, 20\%$

When a problem has a non-tabulated $i$ or $n$ (e.g., $i = 7\%$ or $n = 7.5$ years), the student must use **linear interpolation** — a high-frequency KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind the Six Factors

Every factor arises from one of two fundamental operations:

- **Single-payment growth (compounding/discounting)** of a lump sum
- **Uniform series accumulation/recovery** of a constant cash flow *A* occurring at the end of every period

The factors are derived by treating each cash flow as a single payment, applying the compound interest formula, and then summing the resulting geometric series.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the Interest Tables topic in KTU exams. Memorise the factor formulas and their reciprocal relationships.

| # | Notation (X/Y, i%, n) | Common Name | Factor Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|----------------------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $(F/P, i\%, n)$ | Single Payment Compound Amount Factor (SPCAF) | $(1+i)^{n}$ | Find F given P |
| 2 | $(P/F, i\%, n)$ | Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF) | $\frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | Find P given F |
| 3 | $(F/A, i\%, n)$ | Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor (USCAF) | $\frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | Find F given A |
| 4 | $(P/A, i\%, n)$ | Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF) | $\frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Find P given A |
| 5 | $(A/P, i\%, n)$ | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $\frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Find A given P |
| 6 | $(A/F, i\%, n)$ | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $\frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Find A given F |
| 7 | $(P/G, i\%, n)$ | Arithmetic Gradient Present Worth Factor | $\frac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$ | Find P given gradient G |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $(X/Y, i\%, n)$ is read as *"X per Y"*. The letter **after the slash** is the *known* quantity; the letter **before the slash** is the *unknown* (the one you are computing). This is the single most-tested point in KTU Module 4 short-answer questions.

## 2.3 Reciprocal & Product Relationships

These identities follow directly from the factor formulas and are extremely useful for cross-checking:

$$
(F/P, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{1}{(P/F, i\%, n)}
$$

$$
(F/A, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{1}{(A/F, i\%, n)}
$$

$$
(P/A, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{1}{(A/P, i\%, n)}
$$

$$
(F/P, i\%, n) \;=\; (F/A, i\%, n) \times (A/P, i\%, n)
$$

$$
(P/A, i\%, n) \;=\; (P/F, i\%, n) \times (F/A, i\%, n)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If a table value is not directly available, *construct it* from two available factors. For example, if you are given $(P/F, 10\%, 5)$ and $(F/A, 10\%, 5)$, you can compute $(P/A, 10\%, 5)$ as their product — no interpolation needed.

## 2.4 How a Factor is Read from a Table

For $(F/A, 8\%, 7)$:

1. Locate the row labelled $n = 7$ in the uniform series section.
2. Move along that row to the column headed $i = 8\%$.
3. The cell value is **7.2064**.
4. Interpretation: One rupee deposited at the *end* of each year for 7 years at 8% accumulates to Rs. 7.2064.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Engineering Decision | Factor Used |
|----------------------|-------------|
| Lumpsum investment valuation today vs. maturity | $(F/P)$ or $(P/F)$ |
| Equipment cost spread over its life as an annuity | $(A/P)$ — Capital Recovery |
| Setting aside money annually to replace an asset at end-of-life | $(A/F)$ — Sinking Fund |
| Comparing two alternative machines with different lives | $(P/A)$ then equate present worths |
| Concession tolls, lease rentals, EMIs | $(A/P)$ |
| Computing equal annual cost of a deferred liability | $(P/F) \times (A/P)$ or $(A/F)$ |

## 2.6 The Gradient Factor (P/G) — Special KTU Focus

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 explicitly includes **arithmetic gradient series** under the interest-tables umbrella. A gradient series is one where cash flows *increase* by a constant amount $G$ each period: $0, G, 2G, 3G, \ldots, (n-1)G$.

$$
P \;=\; G \times (P/G, i\%, n)
$$

$$
(P/G, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}
$$

The companion **annual-gradient factor** is:

$$
(A/G, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i\%, n)$

**Given:** A present sum $P$ invested for $n$ periods at compound interest rate $i$ per period.

**Step 1 — Period 1 growth:**
The principal $P$ earns interest $P \cdot i$ during the first year. End-of-year-1 value is $P(1+i)$.

**Step 2 — Period 2 growth:**
The end-of-year-1 value itself earns interest in year 2. End-of-year-2 value is $P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^{2}$.

**Step 3 — Generalisation by induction:**
After $n$ years, the value has been multiplied by $(1+i)$ exactly $n$ times:

$$
F \;=\; P(1+i)^{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the factor:**

$$
(F/P, i\%, n) \;=\; (1+i)^{n}
$$

**Verification at $i = 10\%$, $n = 5$:**

$$
(1.10)^{5} \;=\; 1.61051
$$

Matches the standard table value of $(F/P, 10\%, 5) = 1.6105$. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation of the Single Payment Present Worth Factor $(P/F, i\%, n)$

This is the algebraic inverse of the previous factor. Starting from $F = P(1+i)^{n}$, divide both sides by $(1+i)^{n}$:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{F}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
(P/F, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}} \;=\; (1+i)^{-n}
$$

**Reciprocal check at $i = 10\%$, $n = 5$:**

$$
(1.10)^{-5} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.61051} \;=\; 0.62092
$$

Matches table value $(P/F, 10\%, 5) = 0.6209$. ✔

## 3.3 Derivation of the Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor $(F/A, i\%, n)$

**Given:** A uniform series of end-of-period payments $A, A, A, \ldots, A$ for $n$ periods, accumulating at interest rate $i$.

**Step 1 — Write the future value of each individual payment:**

| Payment | Period of Occurrence | Future Value at End of Period $n$ |
|---------|---------------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1st $A$ | at $t = 1$ | $A(1+i)^{n-1}$ |
| 2nd $A$ | at $t = 2$ | $A(1+i)^{n-2}$ |
| 3rd $A$ | at $t = 3$ | $A(1+i)^{n-3}$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $k$-th $A$ | at $t = k$ | $A(1+i)^{n-k}$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $n$-th $A$ | at $t = n$ | $A(1+i)^{0} = A$ |

**Step 2 — Sum the geometric series:**

$$
F \;=\; A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0}
$$

Let $x = (1+i)$. Then:

$$
F \;=\; A \cdot \frac{x^{n} - 1}{x - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back $x = 1+i$:**

$$
F \;=\; A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the factor:**

$$
(F/A, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

**Numerical check at $i = 10\%$, $n = 5$:**

$$
(F/A, 10\%, 5) \;=\; \frac{(1.10)^{5} - 1}{0.10} \;=\; \frac{1.61051 - 1}{0.10} \;=\; \frac{0.61051}{0.10} \;=\; 6.1051
$$

Table value: **6.1051**. ✔

## 3.4 Derivation of the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i\%, n)$

**Step 1 — Start from the future-worth result:**

$$
F \;=\; A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 2 — Replace $F$ using $F = P(1+i)^{n}$:**

$$
P(1+i)^{n} \;=\; A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P$:**

$$
P \;=\; A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the factor:**

$$
(P/A, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Numerical check at $i = 10\%$, $n = 5$:**

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 5) \;=\; \frac{1.61051 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.61051} \;=\; \frac{0.61051}{0.161051} \;=\; 3.7908
$$

Table value: **3.7908**. ✔

## 3.5 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i\%, n)$

**Step 1 — Start from $P = A \cdot (P/A, i\%, n)$:**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{P}{(P/A, i\%, n)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the factor formula:**

$$
A \;=\; P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the factor:**

$$
(A/P, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

**Numerical check at $i = 10\%$, $n = 5$:**

$$
(A/P, 10\%, 5) \;=\; \frac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{0.61051} \;=\; \frac{0.161051}{0.61051} \;=\; 0.26380
$$

Table value: **0.2638**. ✔

## 3.6 Derivation of the Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, i\%, n)$

**Step 1 — Start from $F = A \cdot (F/A, i\%, n)$:**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{F}{(F/A, i\%, n)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the factor formula:**

$$
A \;=\; F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the factor:**

$$
(A/F, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}
$$

**Numerical check at $i = 10\%$, $n = 5$:**

$$
(A/F, 10\%, 5) \;=\; \frac{0.10}{0.61051} \;=\; 0.16380
$$

Table value: **0.1638**. ✔

## 3.7 Derivation of the Gradient Factor $(P/G, i\%, n)$

**Given:** A gradient series with cash flows $0, G, 2G, 3G, \ldots, (n-1)G$.

**Step 1 — Express the $k$-th cash flow:**
The cash flow at the end of period $k$ is $(k-1)G$.

**Step 2 — Convert to present worth by discounting each cash flow:**

$$
P \;=\; \sum_{k=2}^{n} (k-1)G \cdot (1+i)^{-(k-1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $j = k-1$ and use the identity:**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} j x^{j} \;=\; \frac{x(1 - (n+1)x^{n} + n x^{n+1})}{(1-x)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic simplification yields the standard factor:**

$$
(P/G, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Numerical check at $i = 10\%$, $n = 5$:**

$$
(P/G, 10\%, 5) \;=\; \frac{1.61051 - 0.50 - 1}{(0.10)^{2} \times 1.61051} \;=\; \frac{0.11051}{0.0161051} \;=\; 6.8618
$$

Table value: **6.8618**. ✔

## 3.8 Linear Interpolation — Step-by-Step Method

> [!NOTE]
> When the required $n$ (or $i$) lies between two tabulated values, KTU expects a **linear-interpolation** approach. The error from assuming linearity is small for typical $n \leq 50$ and is the universally-accepted approximation.

**General Formula:**

$$
\text{Required Factor} \;=\; F_{L} + \left( \frac{n - n_{L}}{n_{U} - n_{L}} \right)(F_{U} - F_{L})
$$

**Worked Example — Find $(P/A, 10\%, 7.5)$:**

**Step 1 — Identify the bracketing values from the table:**

| $n$ | $(P/A, 10\%, n)$ |
|-----|------------------|
| 7   | 4.8684           |
| 8   | 5.3349           |

**Step 2 — Apply the interpolation formula:**

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 7.5) \;=\; 4.8684 + \left(\frac{7.5 - 7}{8 - 7}\right)(5.3349 - 4.8684)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the fraction:**

$$
\left(\frac{0.5}{1.0}\right) \;=\; 0.5
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the difference:**

$$
5.3349 - 4.8684 \;=\; 0.4665
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply and add:**

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 7.5) \;=\; 4.8684 + 0.5 \times 0.4665 \;=\; 4.8684 + 0.23325
$$

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 7.5) \;=\; 5.1017
$$

## 3.9 Composite Cash-Flow Solved Using Interest Tables

**Problem:** A machine costs Rs. 5,00,000 today. It generates a uniform net revenue of Rs. 1,00,000 at the end of each year for 6 years, and a salvage value of Rs. 80,000 at the end of year 6. The MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) is 10%. Find the Net Present Worth.

**Step 1 — Identify each cash flow and the factor to apply:**

| Cash Flow | Symbol | Factor | Value at 10% |
|-----------|--------|--------|--------------|
| Initial cost (t = 0) | $-P$ | 1 (immediate) | 1 |
| Annual revenue (years 1–6) | $+A$ | $(P/A, 10\%, 6)$ | 4.3553 |
| Salvage (year 6) | $+F$ | $(P/F, 10\%, 6)$ | 0.5645 |

**Step 2 — Compute present worth of each:**

$$
PW_{\text{cost}} \;=\; -5,00,000
$$

$$
PW_{\text{revenue}} \;=\; 1,00,000 \times 4.3553 \;=\; 4,35,530
$$

$$
PW_{\text{salvage}} \;=\; 80,000 \times 0.5645 \;=\; 45,160
$$

**Step 3 — Sum to find Net Present Worth:**

$$
NPW \;=\; -5,00,000 + 4,35,530 + 45,160 \;=\; -19,310
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:** Since NPW < 0, the investment is **not economically justified** at 10% MARR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Using Capital Recovery:** $NPW = -P + A(P/A, i, n) + F(P/F, i, n)$ is the *uniform-series equivalent* formulation. You could equally compute $A$ using $(A/P)$ and then annualise the salvage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Locating the Correct Interest Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify the Cash Flow Pattern] --> B{Pattern Type?}
    B --> C1[Single Payment]
    B --> C2[Uniform Series A Every Period]
    B --> C3[Arithmetic Gradient G Increasing]
    C1 --> D1{Known is P or F?}
    D1 -- Known P --> E1[F equals P times F over P i n]
    D1 -- Known F --> E2[P equals F times P over F i n]
    C2 --> D2{Known quantity?}
    D2 -- Known A, Find P --> E3[P equals A times P over A i n]
    D2 -- Known A, Find F --> E4[F equals A times F over A i n]
    D2 -- Known P, Find A --> E5[A equals P times A over P i n]
    D2 -- Known F, Find A --> E6[A equals F times A over F i n]
    C3 --> D3[Use P over G i n factor]
    E1 --> F1[Read Table Row n Column i]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
    E6 --> F1
    D3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1{Exact n or i in Table?}
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Multiply Factor by Known Cash Flow]
    G1 -- No --> I1[Apply Linear Interpolation]
    I1 --> H1
    H1 --> J1[Final Answer: P, F, or A]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Six Factors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Present Worth P] -->|SPCAF F over P i n| F[Future Worth F]
    F -->|SPPWF P over F i n| P
    A[Uniform Series A] -->|USCAF F over A i n| F
    F -->|SFF A over F i n| A
    P -->|CRF A over P i n| A
    A -->|USPWF P over A i n| P
    G[Arithmetic Gradient G] -->|P over G i n| P
    A -->|A over G i n| A
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Reciprocal Pair Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1[Reciprocal Pair 1 - Single Payment]
        direction LR
        A1[F over P i n] -- inverse --> B1[P over F i n]
    end
    subgraph P2[Reciprocal Pair 2 - Uniform Series]
        direction LR
        A2[F over A i n] -- inverse --> B2[A over F i n]
    end
    subgraph P3[Reciprocal Pair 3 - Capital Recovery]
        direction LR
        A3[P over A i n] -- inverse --> B3[A over P i n]
    end
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Problem-Solving Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Read the Problem Statement] --> EXTRACT[Extract: P or F or A, i percent, n]
    EXTRACT --> CLASSIFY{Is it a single payment, uniform series, or gradient series?}
    CLASSIFY --> SP[Single Payment Branch]
    CLASSIFY --> US[Uniform Series Branch]
    CLASSIFY --> GR[Gradient Series Branch]
    SP --> CHOOSESP{Find P or Find F?}
    CHOOSESP -- Find F --> USESP[Multiply P by F over P]
    CHOOSESP -- Find P --> USESPP[Multiply F by P over F]
    US --> CHOOSEUS{Known Quantity?}
    CHOOSEUS -- Known A --> USEA[Pick F over A or P over A]
    CHOOSEUS -- Known P --> USEEP[Use A over P]
    CHOOSEUS -- Known F --> USEEF[Use A over F]
    GR --> USEPG[Use P over G or A over G]
    USESP --> INTERP{Exact table match?}
    USESPP --> INTERP
    USEA --> INTERP
    USEEP --> INTERP
    USEEF --> INTERP
    USEPG --> INTERP
    INTERP -- Yes --> FINAL[Compute Final Cash Flow]
    INTERP -- No --> LINT[Linear Interpolation]
    LINT --> FINAL
    FINAL --> DECIDE[Economic Decision: Accept or Reject]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **"Define the term 'Interest Table'. List the six fundamental interest factors used in engineering economics with their standard notation." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An **interest table** is a pre-tabulated set of numerical factors that simplify time-value-of-money calculations by giving the value of a standard mathematical expression — such as $(1+i)^n$ or its derivatives — for a chosen interest rate $i$ and number of periods $n$.

The **six fundamental interest factors** with their standard notation are:

1. $(F/P, i\%, n)$ — Single Payment Compound Amount Factor
2. $(P/F, i\%, n)$ — Single Payment Present Worth Factor
3. $(F/A, i\%, n)$ — Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor
4. $(P/A, i\%, n)$ — Uniform Series Present Worth Factor
5. $(A/P, i\%, n)$ — Capital Recovery Factor
6. $(A/F, i\%, n)$ — Sinking Fund Factor

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **"Explain the reciprocal and product relationships among the six interest factors. Why are these relationships important?" (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The six factors are mathematically interconnected, and any one can be derived from the others using the following relationships:

**Reciprocal relationships:**

$$
(F/P, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{1}{(P/F, i\%, n)} \qquad
(F/A, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{1}{(A/F, i\%, n)} \qquad
(P/A, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{1}{(A/P, i\%, n)}
$$

**Product relationships:**

$$
(F/P, i\%, n) \;=\; (F/A, i\%, n) \times (A/P, i\%, n)
$$

$$
(P/A, i\%, n) \;=\; (P/F, i\%, n) \times (F/A, i\%, n)
$$

**Importance:**

- They serve as a **self-check** for the student — a wrongly-remembered factor will violate the relationships and can be caught immediately.
- They allow the engineer to **synthesise a missing factor** from two available factors, eliminating the need for interpolation.
- They reduce the **memorisation burden** to four independent formulas instead of six.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Notation Reversal:** Writing $(F/P)$ when the problem requires $(P/F)$ — a one-mark deduction for every such instance.
> 2. **End-of-Period Assumption Forgot:** Interest tables are derived assuming end-of-period cash flows. If the problem states "beginning of year", you must shift the series by one period and adjust *n* accordingly.
> 3. **Interpolation Error:** Failing to multiply the difference by the correct fraction $(n - n_L)/(n_U - n_L)$. Marks are split equally for correct identification of bracketing values and the correct arithmetic.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing Rs. and Rs. lakhs, or years and months. Always declare units.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> **"Derive the Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor $(F/A, i\%, n)$ from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions."**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Assumption 1:** A uniform series of end-of-period payments $A$ occurs for $n$ consecutive periods.

**Assumption 2:** The interest rate $i$ per period is constant over the $n$ periods.

**Assumption 3:** All cash flows occur at the end of each period (ordinary annuity).

**Step 1:** Tabulate the future value at end of period $n$ of each payment in the series. The $k$-th payment occurs at the end of period $k$ and therefore accumulates interest for $(n - k)$ additional periods:

$$
\text{FV of }k\text{-th payment} \;=\; A(1+i)^{n-k}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the future values of all $n$ payments:

$$
F \;=\; A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0}
$$

**Step 3:** Let $x = (1+i)$. The sum is a finite geometric series with first term $Ax^{n-1}$, ratio $1/x$, and $n$ terms:

$$
F \;=\; A \cdot x^{n-1} \cdot \frac{1 - (1/x)^{n}}{1 - (1/x)}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the denominator and numerator:

$$
F \;=\; A \cdot x^{n-1} \cdot \frac{(x^{n} - 1)/x^{n}}{(x - 1)/x} \;=\; A \cdot x^{n-1} \cdot \frac{x(x^{n} - 1)}{x^{n}(x - 1)}
$$

$$
F \;=\; A \cdot \frac{x^{n} - 1}{x - 1}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back $x = 1+i$:

$$
F \;=\; A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 6:** Isolate the factor:

$$
(F/A, i\%, n) \;=\; \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

**Conclusion:** The factor grows as a non-linear function of $n$; for a fixed $i$, the factor increases approximately linearly for small $n$ and super-linearly for large $n$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

> **"A construction company wishes to deposit Rs. 2,00,000 at the end of every year into a sinking fund for 8 years to replace a bridge. The fund earns 9% per annum compounded annually. Use interest tables to find the total amount available at the end of 8 years."**

**Step 1 — Identify the unknowns and knowns:**

- $A = \text{Rs. } 2,00,000$ (uniform series)
- $i = 9\%$
- $n = 8$ years
- Required: $F$

**Step 2 — Select the correct factor:**

Since we know $A$ and need $F$, the factor is $(F/A, 9\%, 8)$.

**Step 3 — Read from the interest table:**

$$
(F/A, 9\%, 8) \;=\; 12.4877
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the factor to compute $F$:**

$$
F \;=\; A \times (F/A, 9\%, 8)
$$

$$
F \;=\; 2,00,000 \times 12.4877
$$

$$
F \;=\; \text{Rs. } 24,97,540
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
The company will have approximately **Rs. 24,97,540** available at the end of 8 years to fund the bridge replacement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> **"Explain any four fundamental interest factors with their standard notation, formula, and one typical engineering application each."**

**Model Answer Structure (4 factors × 1.75 marks each):**

**Factor 1: $(F/P, i\%, n)$ — Single Payment Compound Amount Factor**

- **Formula:** $(F/P, i\%, n) = (1+i)^{n}$
- **Application:** Computing the maturity value of a fixed deposit. For instance, what will Rs. 1,00,000 invested at 7% for 5 years grow to? $F = 1,00,000 \times (1.07)^5$.

**Factor 2: $(P/A, i\%, n)$ — Uniform Series Present Worth Factor**

- **Formula:** $(P/A, i\%, n) = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$
- **Application:** Evaluating two machines with different lifespans. Convert each machine's annual operating cost to a present value to make a fair comparison.

**Factor 3: $(A/P, i\%, n)$ — Capital Recovery Factor**

- **Formula:** $(A/P, i\%, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$
- **Application:** Computing Equated Monthly Instalments (EMIs) for a loan. Given the loan principal $P$, the EMI = $P \times (A/P, i\%, n)$.

**Factor 4: $(A/F, i\%, n)$ — Sinking Fund Factor**

- **Formula:** $(A/F, i\%, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$
- **Application:** Determining the annual deposit needed to accumulate a target future sum. For example, "How much must be deposited each year to have Rs. 10,00,000 in 10 years at 8%?"

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

> **"Using linear interpolation, determine $(P/A, 10\%, 7.5)$. Given: $(P/A, 10\%, 7) = 4.8684$ and $(P/A, 10\%, 8) = 5.3349$. Also compute the present worth of a uniform annual series of Rs. 50,000 received at the end of each year for 7.5 years at 10% interest."**

**Step 1 — Identify the bracketing values:**

| $n$ | $(P/A, 10\%, n)$ |
|-----|------------------|
| $n_L = 7$ | $F_L = 4.8684$ |
| $n_U = 8$ | $F_U = 5.3349$ |

**Step 2 — Apply the linear interpolation formula:**

$$
F_{7.5} \;=\; F_L + \left(\frac{n - n_L}{n_U - n_L}\right)(F_U - F_L)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 7.5) \;=\; 4.8684 + \left(\frac{7.5 - 7}{8 - 7}\right)(5.3349 - 4.8684)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bracket and difference:**

$$
\left(\frac{0.5}{1}\right) \;=\; 0.5
$$

$$
5.3349 - 4.8684 \;=\; 0.4665
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply and add:**

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 7.5) \;=\; 4.8684 + 0.5 \times 0.4665
$$

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 7.5) \;=\; 4.8684 + 0.23325
$$

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 7.5) \;=\; 5.10165 \;\approx\; 5.1017
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the present worth of Rs. 50,000 annuity:**

$$
P \;=\; A \times (P/A, 10\%, 7.5)
$$

$$
P \;=\; 50,000 \times 5.1017
$$

$$
P \;=\; \text{Rs. } 2,55,085
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**
A uniform annual series of Rs. 50,000 for 7.5 years is economically equivalent to a single present-day sum of approximately **Rs. 2,55,085** at 10% interest.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Interest Tables**

- [x] **Definition:** An interest table is a pre-computed lookup of TVM factors at standard $(i, n)$ pairs.
- [x] **Six Core Factors:** $(F/P)$, $(P/F)$, $(F/A)$, $(P/A)$, $(A/P)$, $(A/F)$ — plus the **seventh gradient factor** $(P/G)$.
- [x] **Notation Rule:** The letter **after the slash** is *known*; the letter **before the slash** is the *unknown* to be computed.
- [x] **Single Payment Factor:** $(1+i)^{n}$ — the foundation of all other factors.
- [x] **Uniform Series Formulas:** Always derive as a geometric series sum of single payments.
- [x] **Reciprocal Pairs:** $(F/P) \leftrightarrow (P/F)$, $(F/A) \leftrightarrow (A/F)$, $(P/A) \leftrightarrow (A/P)$ — multiply to 1.
- [x] **Product Identity:** $(F/P) = (F/A) \times (A/P)$ and $(P/A) = (P/F) \times (F/A)$ — use to construct missing factors.
- [x] **End-of-Period Assumption:** Standard tables assume ordinary annuity (end-of-period cash flows). For annuity-due, multiply by $(1+i)$ and shift $n$.
- [x] **Interpolation:** Use linear interpolation when $n$ or $i$ is non-integer. The fraction is $(n - n_L)/(n_U - n_L)$.
- [x] **Gradient Series:** Cash flows are $0, G, 2G, 3G, \ldots, (n-1)G$. Apply $(P/G)$ or $(A/G)$ factor.
- [x] **Common Mistake:** Confusing $(P/A)$ with $(A/P)$ — always read the slash direction.
- [x] **Cross-Check:** After computing, verify using the reciprocal relationship — a 30-second check that can earn a bonus mark.
- [x] **Engineering Context:** Capital recovery $(A/P)$ is the workhorse for equipment-replacement decisions; sinking fund $(A/F)$ for end-of-life reserves; present worth $(P/A)$ for comparing unequal-life alternatives.
- [x] **Exam Strategy:** Always (i) list knowns, (ii) state the factor explicitly using $(X/Y, i\%, n)$ notation, (iii) read the table value, (iv) multiply, and (v) interpret in context.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaluating Alternatives by Equivalence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — EVALUATING ALTERNATIVES BY EQUIVALENCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** Two cash flows are said to be **economically equivalent** at a given interest rate if their economic worth is identical at a common point in time. The **equivalence principle** allows an engineer to compare alternatives having different magnitudes, timings, and patterns of cash flows by converting all cash flows into a comparable form (Present Worth, Future Worth, or Annual Worth) at a chosen **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)**, also called the **hurdle rate** or **cost of capital**.

The equivalence concept is the very foundation of engineering economy. It permits the analyst to translate a series of future cash flows into a single lump-sum value **at a reference time**, so that two or more mutually exclusive alternatives can be ranked on a common economic footing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Equivalence is NOT equality. Two cash flow patterns can look entirely different yet have the **same Present Worth (PW)**, the **same Future Worth (FW)**, or the **same Annual Worth (AW)** at a given MARR. The choice of MARR is therefore critical to the equivalence calculation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of equivalence like **currency exchange at a foreign exchange counter**. Suppose you are travelling from India to Japan. You can carry ₹1,00,000 in cash, or you could carry the equivalent amount in US dollars, Euros, or even a credit card with the same purchasing power. The rupee note, dollar bill, and credit-card balance are physically different, but at the prevailing exchange rate, they are *equivalent in economic worth*.

Similarly, in engineering economy, a payment of **₹1,00,000 today** is not the same as **₹1,00,000 received 5 years later** — they are economically equivalent **only if** the future amount is discounted back at the appropriate MARR. The "exchange rate" between money at different points in time is the **interest rate (i)**.

A second everyday analogy is the **EMI (Equated Monthly Instalment)** model of a home loan. The bank converts a large principal (a Present Worth) into a series of equal monthly payments (an Annual/Monthly Worth) over a fixed tenure. The borrower and the bank both treat the lump-sum and the instalments as *equivalent* at the contracted interest rate.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Equivalence

- **MARR (i):** Stated as a **percentage per period**, usually **% per year**.
- **Time (n):** Expressed in the same period unit as the interest rate (years, months, quarters).
- **Cash flows (P, F, A, G):** All expressed in the **same monetary unit** (₹, $, €, ¥).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Consistency Rule (KTU Valuation Tip):** If the MARR is given as *12% per year compounded monthly*, then either (a) convert to an effective monthly rate i = 12%/12 = 1% per month and use n in months, OR (b) convert to an effective annual rate using $i_{eff} = (1 + 0.12/12)^{12} - 1$ and use n in years. **Never mix mismatched time units.**

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Equivalence is Needed

When two engineering projects generate cash flows of different magnitudes at different times, a direct "apples-to-apples" comparison is impossible without a common frame of reference. Equivalence provides that frame. By collapsing every cash flow stream to a **single numerical value** (PW, FW, or AW), the analyst can:

1. Rank mutually exclusive alternatives.
2. Accept or reject a single project against the MARR.
3. Communicate the economic worth of an investment in a single, easily interpreted figure.

### 2.2 The Three Reference Points for Equivalence

Any cash flow can be moved to exactly **three canonical reference points**:

| Reference Point | Symbol | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Present (Time 0)** | P (Present Worth) | Most popular in KTU board questions |
| **Future (Time n)** | F (Future Worth) | Used when the project horizon is far away |
| **End of each period (1 to n)** | A (Annual Worth) | Best for comparing alternatives with different lives |

> [!NOTE]
> All three are mathematically interchangeable. For example, $P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n)$, $F = A \cdot (F/A, i, n)$, and $A = P \cdot (A/P, i, n)$.

### 2.3 The Six Standard Equivalence Factors

The backbone of KTU-level engineering-economy problems is the **six single-letter factors**. These factors are functions of the interest rate **i** and the number of periods **n**.

| Factor | Name | Formula | Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(F/P, i, n)$ | Single Payment Compound Amount | $(1+i)^{n}$ | Find F given P |
| $(P/F, i, n)$ | Single Payment Present Worth | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | Find P given F |
| $(F/A, i, n)$ | Uniform Series Compound Amount | $\frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | Find F given A |
| $(A/F, i, n)$ | Sinking Fund | $\frac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Find A given F |
| $(P/A, i, n)$ | Uniform Series Present Worth | $\frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Find P given A |
| $(A/P, i, n)$ | Capital Recovery | $\frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Find A given P |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocal Relationships (often tested in Part A):**
> - $(P/F, i, n) = 1 \,/\, (F/P, i, n)$
> - $(A/F, i, n) = 1 \,/\, (F/A, i, n)$
> - $(A/P, i, n) = 1 \,/\, (P/A, i, n)$
> - $(F/A, i, n) = (F/P, i, n) \cdot (P/A, i, n)$
> - $(A/P, i, n) = (A/F, i, n) + i$

### 2.4 Gradient and Geometric Series Factors

For non-uniform cash flows, two extra factors are essential:

| Factor | Name | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $(A/G, i, n)$ | Arithmetic Gradient to Uniform Series | $\frac{1}{i} - \frac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ |
| $(P/A_{1}, g, i, n)$ | Geometric Gradient Present Worth (i ≠ g) | $\frac{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{i - g}$ |

- **Arithmetic gradient (G):** Cash flows increase by a constant amount G each period (1G, 2G, 3G, …).
- **Geometric gradient (g):** Cash flows increase by a constant *percentage* g each period.

### 2.5 Methods of Evaluating Alternatives by Equivalence

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know **all** the following five methods. Choice of method does not change the decision — only the convenience of calculation changes.

1. **Present Worth (PW) Method**
   - Convert all cash flows to a single equivalent value at **Time 0**.
   - **Decision rule (mutually exclusive):** Choose the alternative with the **highest PW (least negative)**, provided PW ≥ 0.
   - **Decision rule (independent):** Accept if PW ≥ 0.

2. **Future Worth (FW) Method**
   - Convert all cash flows to a single equivalent value at the **end of the project horizon**.
   - Mathematically equivalent to PW; produces the **same ranking**.

3. **Annual Worth (AW) Method**
   - Convert all cash flows to a series of **equal end-of-period payments** over the project life.
   - Most useful for comparing alternatives with **unequal lives** because AW can be recomputed over a common planning horizon (e.g., a 1-year cycle for repeated-cycle analysis).

4. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR) Method**
   - The discount rate that makes the PW of all cash flows equal to **zero**.
   - **Decision rule:** Accept if IRR ≥ MARR.
   - For mutually exclusive alternatives, an **incremental IRR analysis** (ΔIRR) must be performed on the difference in cash flows.

5. **External Rate of Return (ERR) / Modified IRR (MIRR) Method**
   - Reinvests positive cash flows at the **MARR** instead of the IRR, avoiding the multiple-roots problem of IRR.
   - $ERR = \left( \dfrac{F_{positive} \, \text{at MARR}}{-P_{negative}} \right)^{1/n} - 1$

6. **Benefit-Cost (B-C) Ratio Method**
   - Widely used in **public-sector engineering** (roads, bridges, water supply).
   - $B/C = \dfrac{PW \text{ of Benefits}}{PW \text{ of Costs}}$
   - **Decision rule:** Accept if B/C ≥ 1 (conventional) or modified B/C ≥ 1 (modified: benefits / (costs − salvage)).

### 2.6 Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Mechanical:** Comparing two pump designs with different efficiencies and maintenance schedules.
- **Civil:** Choosing between a concrete bridge and a steel bridge with different life spans (40 yr vs 60 yr) using the AW method.
- **Electrical:** Selecting solar PV vs diesel generator sets based on annualised cost.
- **Computer Science / IT:** Cloud vs on-premise infrastructure — server refresh cycles make **AW** the preferred method.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor $(F/A, i, n)$

**Statement to prove:**
$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

**Derivation:**

Let the deposit at the end of each year be $A$ at an interest rate $i$ per year for $n$ years. Each deposit earns compound interest from the time it is made until the end of year $n$.

| Deposit made at end of year | Years of compounding | Future value at end of year $n$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n-1$ | $A(1+i)^{n-1}$ |
| 2 | $n-2$ | $A(1+i)^{n-2}$ |
| 3 | $n-3$ | $A(1+i)^{n-3}$ |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ |
| $n$ | 0 | $A(1+i)^{0} = A$ |

Summing the future values gives:

$$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i) + A$$

$$F = A \left[ (1+i)^{n-1} + (1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + (1+i) + 1 \right]$$

Let $x = (1+i)$. The expression inside the brackets is a **finite geometric series** with first term 1, common ratio $x$, and $n$ terms:

$$S = 1 + x + x^{2} + \cdots + x^{n-1} = \frac{x^{n} - 1}{x - 1} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

Substituting:

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} = A \cdot (F/A, i, n)$$

**The result is proved.** $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$

**Statement to prove:**

$$A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

**Derivation:**

Start from the two already-proven relations:

$$F = P(1+i)^{n} \quad \text{and} \quad F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

Equate the two expressions for $F$:

$$P(1+i)^{n} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

Solve for $A$:

$$A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = P \cdot (A/P, i, n)$$

**The result is proved.** $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style PW Problem

> **Problem:** A company must choose between two machines. Machine X costs ₹5,00,000 with annual operating cost ₹80,000 for 5 years. Machine Y costs ₹7,50,000 with annual operating cost ₹50,000 for 5 years. Both have negligible salvage. Use the PW method at MARR = 10% per year to recommend a machine.

**Step 1 — Identify the cash flow patterns**

- **Machine X:** $P = 5{,}00{,}000$ at $t=0$; $A = 80{,}000$ for $t=1$ to $5$.
- **Machine Y:** $P = 7{,}50{,}000$ at $t=0$; $A = 50{,}000$ for $t=1$ to $5$.

**Step 2 — Look up the factor**

$$(P/A, 10\%, 5) = \frac{(1.10)^{5} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{5}}$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$(1.10)^{1} = 1.10$$
$$(1.10)^{2} = 1.21$$
$$(1.10)^{3} = 1.331$$
$$(1.10)^{4} = 1.4641$$
$$(1.10)^{5} = 1.61051$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.61051 - 1 = 0.61051$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 0.10 \times 1.61051 = 0.161051$$
$$(P/A, 10\%, 5) = \frac{0.61051}{0.161051} = 3.7908$$

**Step 3 — Compute PW of each alternative**

$$PW_{X} = -5{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 \times (P/A, 10\%, 5)$$
$$PW_{X} = -5{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 \times 3.7908$$
$$PW_{X} = -5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}03{,}264 = -8{,}03{,}264 \text{ ₹}$$

$$PW_{Y} = -7{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000 \times (P/A, 10\%, 5)$$
$$PW_{Y} = -7{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000 \times 3.7908$$
$$PW_{Y} = -7{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}89{,}540 = -9{,}39{,}540 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4 — Decision**

Since both PWs are negative (cost-only projects), choose the **less negative** value:

$$PW_{X} = -8{,}03{,}264 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{vs} \quad PW_{Y} = -9{,}39{,}540 \text{ ₹}$$

**Machine X is recommended** because its equivalent present cost is lower by approximately ₹1,36,276.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

| Situation | Best Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Alternatives have the **same life** | **PW** or **AW** | Direct comparison; AW is fastest if only one cycle is needed |
| Alternatives have **different lives** | **AW** (over a 1-year cycle) or **PW** (over LCM of lives) | AW converts unequal lives to a comparable annual equivalent |
| **Single project** (accept/reject) | **PW** or **IRR** | PW ≥ 0 or IRR ≥ MARR |
| **Public-sector** project (benefits + costs) | **B/C ratio** | Conventional for non-revenue projects |
| Cash flows have a **sign change** | **ERR (MIRR)** | Avoids IRR's multiple-roots problem |
| **Capital rationing** with many small projects | **PW** ranked list | Sort by descending PW |
| Cash flows grow at a **constant rate** | **Geometric gradient** | Use $(P/A_{1}, g, i, n)$ |
| Cash flows grow by a **fixed amount** | **Arithmetic gradient** | Use $A = A_{0} + G(A/G, i, n)$ |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Equivalence — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Cash Flow at Time 0 - P]:::point
    A1[Cash Flow at Time 1]:::node
    A2[Cash Flow at Time 2]:::node
    A3[Cash Flow at Time n - F]:::node
    AM[Uniform Series A]:::node

    subgraph SP [Single Payment Domain]
        A0
        A3
    end

    subgraph US [Uniform Series Domain]
        A1
        A2
        AM
    end

    F1((F over P, i, n)):::factor
    F2((P over F, i, n)):::factor
    F3((F over A, i, n)):::factor
    F4((A over F, i, n)):::factor
    F5((P over A, i, n)):::factor
    F6((A over P, i, n)):::factor

    A0 --- F1 --- A3
    A3 --- F2 --- A0
    AM --- F3 --- A3
    A3 --- F4 --- AM
    A0 --- F5 --- AM
    AM --- F6 --- A0

    classDef point fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef factor fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Choosing the Right Equivalence Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Do alternatives have equal life?}:::ask
    Q2{Multiple sign changes in cash flow?}:::ask
    Q3{Is the project a public sector one?}:::ask
    M1[Use Present Worth Method]:::meth
    M2[Use Annual Worth Method over LCM]:::meth
    M3[Use Annual Worth over 1 year cycle]:::meth
    M4[Use External Rate of Return ERR]:::meth
    M5[Use Benefit Cost Ratio]:::meth
    DEC[Final Decision: Select Alternative with Best Metric]:::dec

    Q1 -- Yes --> M1
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> M4
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> M5
    Q3 -- No --> M2

    M1 --> DEC
    M2 --> DEC
    M3 --> DEC
    M4 --> DEC
    M5 --> DEC

    classDef ask fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef meth fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FF6347,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Engineering-Economy Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Define the Problem and Alternatives]:::blk
    IN2[Estimate Cash Flows for Each Alternative]:::blk
    IN3[Determine MARR and Study Period]:::blk
    EQ[Apply Equivalence: Convert All Cash Flows to PW, FW, or AW]:::core
    CM1[Apply PW or FW Method]:::branch
    CM2[Apply AW Method]:::branch
    CM3[Apply IRR or ERR Method]:::branch
    CM4[Apply Benefit Cost Ratio]:::branch
    EV[Compare and Rank Alternatives]:::blk
    SE[Perform Sensitivity Analysis]:::blk
    FC[Final Choice and Documentation]:::dec

    IN1 --> IN2
    IN2 --> IN3
    IN3 --> EQ
    EQ --> CM1
    EQ --> CM2
    EQ --> CM3
    EQ --> CM4
    CM1 --> EV
    CM2 --> EV
    CM3 --> EV
    CM4 --> EV
    EV --> SE
    SE --> FC

    classDef blk fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
