### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A0`, `Q1`, `IN1`, `M1`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
- All node labels are clean uppercase text without bold/italic markers or HTML tags.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the **equivalence principle** in engineering economy. State any two assumptions on which the equivalence calculations are based.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Equivalence Principle:** Two cash flows occurring at different times are said to be *equivalent* at a given interest rate if their **economic worth is identical at a common point in time**.

**Two Assumptions (1.5 marks each):**
1. **The interest rate (MARR) is known, constant, and positive** over the analysis period.
2. **Cash flows occur at discrete, regular intervals** (end of each period) and are positive or negative single values.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for the definition, 0.5 mark for each of two clear assumptions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List the **six standard single-letter equivalence factors** used in engineering-economy analysis. State the relationship between $(P/A, i, n)$ and $(A/P, i, n)$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six factors are:
1. $(F/P, i, n)$ — single payment compound amount
2. $(P/F, i, n)$ — single payment present worth
3. $(F/A, i, n)$ — uniform series compound amount
4. $(A/F, i, n)$ — sinking fund
5. $(P/A, i, n)$ — uniform series present worth
6. $(A/P, i, n)$ — capital recovery

**Relationship:**

$$(P/A, i, n) = \frac{1}{(A/P, i, n)} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$$

> **Mark Distribution:** 0.5 mark per factor (3 marks = 6 factors × 0.5), minus half a mark for missing the relationship. Since 6 × 0.5 = 3, the relationship question is extra credit and not counted in the 3 marks. Adjust: 2 marks for listing all six correctly and 1 mark for the relationship.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **Q A(a) [7 marks]:** A civil engineering firm must select one of two earth-moving machines. Machine A has a first cost of **₹8,00,000**, an annual operating cost of **₹1,20,000**, and a salvage value of **₹1,50,000** at the end of its **5-year** life. Machine B has a first cost of **₹11,00,000**, an annual operating cost of **₹90,000**, and a salvage value of **₹2,00,000** at the end of its **5-year** life. Using the **Present Worth method** at MARR = 12% per year, recommend the best machine.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the factors needed:**

$$(P/A, 12\%, 5) = \frac{(1.12)^{5} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{5}}$$

Compute $(1.12)^{5}$ step-by-step:

$$(1.12)^{1} = 1.12$$
$$(1.12)^{2} = 1.2544$$
$$(1.12)^{3} = 1.404928$$
$$(1.12)^{4} = 1.57351936$$
$$(1.12)^{5} = 1.7623416832$$

$$(P/A, 12\%, 5) = \frac{1.7623416832 - 1}{0.12 \times 1.7623416832} = \frac{0.7623416832}{0.2114810020} = 3.6048$$

$$(P/F, 12\%, 5) = \frac{1}{1.7623416832} = 0.5674$$

**Step 2 — Compute PW of Machine A:**

$$PW_{A} = -8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 \times (P/A, 12\%, 5) + 1{,}50{,}000 \times (P/F, 12\%, 5)$$

$$PW_{A} = -8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.5674$$

$$PW_{A} = -8{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}32{,}576 + 85{,}110 = -11{,}47{,}466 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3 — Compute PW of Machine B:**

$$PW_{B} = -11{,}00{,}000 - 90{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674$$

$$PW_{B} = -11{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}24{,}432 + 1{,}13{,}480 = -13{,}10{,}952 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$PW_{A} = -11{,}47{,}466 \text{ ₹} \quad > \quad PW_{B} = -13{,}10{,}952 \text{ ₹}$$

Both are costs (negative), so the **less negative PW** is preferred. **Machine A is recommended** (saves approximately ₹1,63,486 in equivalent present cost).

> **Mark Distribution:** Factor calculation 1 mark, PW_A 2.5 marks, PW_B 2.5 marks, Decision 1 mark.

---

> **Q A(b) [7 marks]:** Solve the same problem using the **Annual Worth method** and verify that the ranking is the same as in part (a).

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Capital recovery factor:**

$$(A/P, 12\%, 5) = \frac{1}{3.6048} = 0.2774$$

**Step 2 — Sinking fund factor:**

$$(A/F, 12\%, 5) = (A/P, 12\%, 5) - i = 0.2774 - 0.12 = 0.1574$$

**Step 3 — AW of Machine A:**

$$AW_{A} = -8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2774 - 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.1574$$

$$AW_{A} = -2{,}21{,}920 - 1{,}20{,}000 + 23{,}610 = -3{,}18{,}310 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

**Step 4 — AW of Machine B:**

$$AW_{B} = -11{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2774 - 90{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1574$$

$$AW_{B} = -3{,}05{,}140 - 90{,}000 + 31{,}480 = -3{,}63{,}660 \text{ ₹/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

Since $AW_{A} = -3{,}18{,}310 > AW_{B} = -3{,}63{,}660$, **Machine A is recommended**, confirming the PW result.

**Verification:** $AW_{A} \times (P/A, 12\%, 5) = -3{,}18{,}310 \times 3.6048 = -11{,}47{,}471$ ≈ $PW_{A}$ ✓

> **Mark Distribution:** Factor calculations 1.5 marks, AW_A 2 marks, AW_B 2 marks, Decision and verification 1.5 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q B(a) [7 marks]:** A construction company is evaluating a project that requires an initial investment of **₹6,00,000** and is expected to generate net cash inflows of **₹2,00,000 per year for 4 years**. At the end of year 4, an additional salvage recovery of **₹50,000** is expected. Determine the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** of the project using the trial-and-error method. Should the project be accepted if the MARR is **15% per year**?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the PW equation and solve for i:**

$$PW(i) = -6{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times (P/A, i, 4) + 50{,}000 \times (P/F, i, 4) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Try i = 15%:**

$$(P/A, 15\%, 4) = \frac{(1.15)^{4} - 1}{0.15 \times (1.15)^{4}}$$

Compute $(1.15)^{4} = 1.74900625$:

$$(P/A, 15\%, 4) = \frac{1.74900625 - 1}{0.15 \times 1.74900625} = \frac{0.74900625}{0.2623509} = 2.8550$$

$$(P/F, 15\%, 4) = \frac{1}{1.74900625} = 0.5718$$

$$PW(15\%) = -6{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2.8550 + 50{,}000 \times 0.5718$$

$$PW(15\%) = -6{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}71{,}000 + 28{,}590 = -340 \text{ ₹} \approx 0$$

The IRR is approximately **15%**.

**Step 3 — Refine using linear interpolation:**

Try i = 14%:

$$(1.14)^{4} = 1.68896016$$
$$(P/A, 14\%, 4) = \frac{0.68896016}{0.14 \times 1.68896016} = \frac{0.68896016}{0.2364544} = 2.9137$$
$$(P/F, 14\%, 4) = \frac{1}{1.68896016} = 0.5921$$

$$PW(14\%) = -6{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2.9137 + 50{,}000 \times 0.5921$$
$$PW(14\%) = -6{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}82{,}740 + 29{,}605 = +12{,}345 \text{ ₹}$$

Try i = 16%:

$$(1.16)^{4} = 1.81063936$$
$$(P/A, 16\%, 4) = \frac{0.81063936}{0.16 \times 1.81063936} = \frac{0.81063936}{0.28970230} = 2.7982$$
$$(P/F, 16\%, 4) = \frac{1}{1.81063936} = 0.5523$$

$$PW(16\%) = -6{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2.7982 + 50{,}000 \times 0.5523$$
$$PW(16\%) = -6{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}59{,}640 + 27{,}615 = -12{,}745 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4 — Linear interpolation:**

$$IRR = 14\% + \frac{12{,}345}{12{,}345 + 12{,}745} \times (16\% - 14\%)$$

$$IRR = 14\% + \frac{12{,}345}{25{,}090} \times 2\% = 14\% + 0.984\% \approx 14.98\% \approx 15\%$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

Since **IRR ≈ 15% ≈ MARR**, the project is **marginally acceptable**. To be safe, the firm should reject the project because IRR is not strictly greater than MARR.

> **Mark Distribution:** PW equation 1 mark, trial at 15% 1.5 marks, refinement 2 marks, interpolation 1.5 marks, decision 1 mark.

---

> **Q B(b) [7 marks]:** Two mutually exclusive water-pumping schemes are being considered. Scheme I costs **₹3,00,000** initially and **₹40,000/year** for **5 years**. Scheme II costs **₹4,50,000** initially and **₹25,000/year** for **5 years**. Both have negligible salvage. Use the **incremental IRR method** to select the better scheme if the MARR is **10% per year**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Order the alternatives by initial cost (ascending):**

- Scheme I: $P = 3{,}00{,}000$, $A = 40{,}000$, $n = 5$
- Scheme II: $P = 4{,}50{,}000$, $A = 25{,}000$, $n = 5$

Order: Scheme I (cheaper) → Scheme II (costlier).

**Step 2 — Compute the incremental cash flow (II − I):**

- $\Delta P = 4{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}50{,}000$ (extra investment)
- $\Delta A = 25{,}000 - 40{,}000 = -15{,}000$ (annual savings, i.e., a cost reduction)

So the incremental cash flow is: invest $1{,}50{,}000$ extra, save $15{,}000$ per year for 5 years.

**Step 3 — Set up the incremental PW equation to find $\Delta IRR$:**

$$-1{,}50{,}000 + 15{,}000 \times (P/A, \Delta IRR, 5) = 0$$

$$(P/A, \Delta IRR, 5) = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{15{,}000} = 10.0000$$

**Step 4 — Check factors at various rates:**

- At i = 0%: $(P/A, 0\%, 5) = 5$ — too small.
- At i = 1%: $(P/A, 1\%, 5) = 4.8534$ — too small.
- At i = 2%: $(P/A, 2\%, 5) = 4.7135$ — too small.
- The factor needed is 10.0000, but the **maximum possible value** of $(P/A, i, 5)$ is 5 (as i → 0). Therefore, $(P/A, \Delta IRR, 5)$ can **never** equal 10.

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Since the required factor (10) is unattainable, this means the annual savings of $15{,}000$ are **not enough** to recover the extra investment of $1{,}50{,}000$ **at any positive interest rate**. Equivalently, the **incremental IRR is negative**.

**Step 6 — Decision using the incremental rule:**

Since $\Delta IRR < 0 < MARR$, the extra investment in Scheme II is **not justified**.

**Final Decision:** Choose **Scheme I** (the lower-cost alternative), as the extra cost of Scheme II is not recovered by its annual savings.

> **Mark Distribution:** Ordering 1 mark, incremental cash flow 1.5 marks, PW equation 1.5 marks, factor check 1.5 marks, final decision 1.5 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Equivalence Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (lose up to 2 marks):** Mixing months and years or nominal and effective rates without conversion. Always state the **time unit** explicitly.
> 2. **Sign Convention (lose up to 1 mark):** Costs are negative, revenues are positive. Use the correct sign in PW/FW/AW equations.
> 3. **Factor Notation (lose up to 1 mark):** The correct notation is $(P/A, i, n)$ with **comma separators** and a **percentage or decimal** for i. Writing "P/A" or "P over A" loses marks.
> 4. **Decision Rule for Mutually Exclusive Alternatives (lose up to 2 marks):** For IRR, you MUST perform **incremental IRR analysis** — comparing IRRs to MARR is **not** enough for mutually exclusive projects.
> 5. **End-of-Period Assumption (lose up to 1 mark):** All standard factors assume end-of-period cash flows. If the problem says "beginning of year", apply the **annuity-due** correction.
> 6. **MARR is the Hurdle, not the Investment Rate (lose up to 1 mark):** Do not confuse the MARR with the IRR. MARR is what the firm requires; IRR is what the project earns.
> 7. **Arithmetic vs Geometric Gradient (lose up to 2 marks):** A common KTU trap is to use the arithmetic-gradient factor on a geometric-gradient cash flow (or vice versa). Check the wording: "increases by ₹1,000 each year" → arithmetic; "increases by 8% each year" → geometric.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Evaluating Alternatives by Equivalence**
>
> - **Equivalence** is the idea that different cash flows can have the same economic worth at a common time for a given interest rate.
> - The **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** is the discount rate used in all equivalence calculations. It is the firm's opportunity cost of capital.
> - The **three reference points** for equivalence are: **Present (P)**, **Future (F)**, and **Annual (A)**. All three give the **same ranking** of alternatives.
> - The **six standard factors** $(F/P, P/F, F/A, A/F, P/A, A/P)$ are reciprocals or products of each other; remember their algebraic relationships.
> - The **arithmetic gradient factor $(A/G, i, n)$** converts a linearly increasing cash flow into an equivalent uniform series.
> - The **geometric gradient factor $(P/A_{1}, g, i, n)$** handles cash flows growing at a constant percentage rate.
> - **Decision rules (independent project):** Accept if $PW \geq 0$, or $FW \geq 0$, or $AW \geq 0$, or $IRR \geq MARR$, or $B/C \geq 1$.
> - **Decision rule (mutually exclusive):** Pick the alternative with the **highest PW** (or AW), or highest IRR **only after** the incremental analysis.
> - **Incremental IRR analysis** is mandatory for mutually exclusive projects; compare the IRR of the **difference in cash flows** to the MARR.
> - **Unequal lives:** use the **AW over a 1-year cycle** (repeated-cycle assumption) or the **PW over the LCM** of the lives.
> - **ERR (External Rate of Return / MIRR)** is preferred over IRR when there are **multiple sign changes** in the cash flow stream.
> - **B/C ratio** is used in **public-sector / government** projects where benefits are often non-monetary or difficult to monetise directly.
> - **Cash-flow sign convention:** inflows (revenues, salvage) are **positive**; outflows (costs, investments) are **negative**.
> - Always **state your factors, units, and decision** clearly — KTU examiners award marks for clarity as well as numerical accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Worth Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Present Worth Comparison # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) **Present Worth (PW) Comparison** is a fundamental technique in **Engineering Economic Analysis** used to evaluate and rank mutually exclusive investment alternatives by converting all future cash inflows and outflows across the entire life cycle of each alternative into a **single equivalent value at time zero (t = 0)**, using a specified **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** or discount rate, denoted as **i%**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** > "Present Worth (PW) is the equivalent worth of all cash flows (revenues and costs) of a project, evaluated at the beginning of its life (t = 0) by discounting them at the appropriate rate of interest (MARR). The alternative with the **maximum Present Worth (for revenue projects)** or **minimum Present Worth of Costs (for cost-only/service projects)** is selected." Mathematically, the Present Worth of a cash flow series $A_t$ occurring at the end of year $t$ over a project life of $N$ years is expressed as: $$ PW = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{A_t}{(1+i)^t} $$ where: - $A_t$ = Net cash flow at the end of year $t$ - $i$ = MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return), expressed as a decimal - $N$ = Useful life of the alternative in years - $t$ = Time index (year) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are deciding between **two job offers**: - **Offer A** gives you ₹3,00,000 today as a lump sum. - **Offer B** gives you ₹1,50,000 today, ₹1,00,000 next year, and ₹1,00,000 the year after. On the surface, both look similar (₹3,00,000 vs ₹3,50,000), but money in the future is **worth less** than money today because of inflation and the opportunity cost of not having it now (you could have invested today's money in a bank FD at 8% interest!). **Present Worth Comparison** is essentially a mathematical time-machine that: 1. Takes all those future amounts and **rolls them backward in time** to "today's value." 2. Tells you what each offer is genuinely worth in today's rupees. 3. The offer with the **higher Present Worth** is the smarter financial choice. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Use Case:** > Consider a Civil Engineering project — should a contractor buy a **₹50 lakh excavator** that saves ₹12 lakh/year in labor for 6 years, OR **rent one** at ₹10 lakh/year for 6 years? PW Comparison answers this with mathematical precision, factoring in the **time value of money**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU 2024 - **MARR (i):** Minimum Attractive Rate of Return — the lowest rate of return an investor will accept. - **P (Present Worth):** Value at t = 0. - **F (Future Worth):** Value at time $N$. - **A (Annual Worth):** Uniform series of cash flows. - **NPV:** Net Present Value = Present Worth of Benefits − Present Worth of Costs. - **Capitalized Cost (CC):** PW when $N \to \infty$ (perpetual projects like bridges, dams, public infrastructure). - **PW of Costs (PWC):** Used for **service alternatives** (cost-only, same revenue) — choose lowest PWC. - **PW of Benefits / Net PW:** Used for **revenue alternatives** — choose highest. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** > The present worth method is preferred over the Annual Worth method when **unequal lives** are involved, because PW can directly compare alternatives on a common ground (PW over LCM of lives). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why "Discounting" and Not "Compounding"? In **Future Worth** analysis, we compound forward. In **Present Worth** analysis, we **discount backward**. The two are mathematically inverse operations. The discount factor for a single future cash flow $F$ occurring at year $t$ is: $$ \text{Single Payment Present Worth Factor} = \frac{1}{(1+i)^t} = (P/F, i\%, t) $$ This factor is read as **"P given F"** at interest rate $i$% for $t$ periods. ## 2.2 The Six Step-by-Step Logic Behind Present Worth Comparison 1. **Identify all cash flows** for each alternative (initial investment P, annual operating/maintenance costs, salvage value S, annual revenue R). 2. **Determine the MARR (i)** — usually given in the problem, otherwise assume 8%–12% for KTU problems. 3. **Determine the life (N)** of each alternative. If unequal, use the **Least Common Multiple (LCM) method** or **infinite horizon (capitalized cost)** approach. 4. **Apply the appropriate discount factor** to each cash flow and sum them to get PW. 5. **Apply the decision rule**: - **Revenue/Mixed alternatives (different revenues):** Select the alternative with the **largest positive PW (or NPV)**. - **Cost-only / Service alternatives (same revenue/output):** Select the alternative with the **smallest Present Worth of Cost (PWC)**. 6. **Perform incremental analysis** if both alternatives have positive PW to identify the best option. ## 2.3 Decision Rules Summary Table | Scenario | Decision Rule | Selected Alternative | |---|---|---| | **Independent Projects** | If PW ≥ 0, Accept | Each project judged individually | | **Mutually Exclusive — Revenue (B-C)** | Maximize PW | Highest positive PW | | **Mutually Exclusive — Cost Only (same output)** | Minimize PWC | Lowest PW of Costs | | **Mutually Exclusive — Equal Lives** | Direct PW Comparison | Higher PW wins | | **Mutually Exclusive — Unequal Lives** | Use LCM or Capitalized Cost | Re-evaluate over common horizon | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these 4 discount factors — they cover 90% of KTU PW problems.** | # | Factor Name | Algebraic Form | Engineering Economics Notation | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Single Payment Present Worth | $\dfrac{F}{(1+i)^N}$ | $(P/F, i\%, N)$ | Find P from a single future F | | 2 | Uniform Series Present Worth | $A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N}$ | $(P/A, i\%, N)$ | Find P from uniform annual A series | | 3 | Capital Recovery | $P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | $(A/P, i\%, N)$ | Find A from a single present P | | 4 | Sinking Fund | $F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | $(A/F, i\%, N)$ | Find A to accumulate to F | ### Additional PW Formulas for KTU Board Exams | Cash Flow Type | PW Formula | Notation | |---|---|---| | **Initial Cost only** | $PW = -P$ | One-time outflow at t = 0 | | **Annual Cost A for N years** | $PW = -A \cdot (P/A, i\%, N)$ | Recurring expenses | | **Salvage Value S at year N** | $PW = +S \cdot (P/F, i\%, N)$ | End-of-life recovery | | **Perpetual Annual Cost (Capitalized Cost)** | $PW = \dfrac{A}{i}$ | When $N \to \infty$ | | **Gradient Series (G)** | $PW = G \cdot \left[\dfrac{(1+i)^N - iN - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^N}\right]$ | Arithmetically increasing costs | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Application of PW Comparison | |---|---| | **Civil Engineering** | Bridge construction, road pavement — choose between concrete vs. steel over 50–100 years using **Capitalized Cost**. | | **Mechanical Engineering** | Buy vs. lease decisions for CNC machines, boilers, compressors. | | **Electrical Engineering** | Power plant selection — thermal vs. hydro vs. solar, comparing lifetime costs. | | **Industrial Engineering** | Process selection — manual vs. automated assembly lines. | | **Public Sector Projects** | Evaluating government infrastructure — dams, metros, airports using capitalized equivalent. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why PW is preferred in KTU 2024 Scheme:** > The PW method is the most **fundamentally correct** of all engineering economic methods because it preserves the **time value of money** exactly and handles **unequal lives** naturally. KTU frequently tests this topic with the **LCM method** and **Capitalized Cost** for long-lived public projects. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Single Payment Present Worth Factor **Given:** A future amount $F$ at the end of year $N$ at compound interest rate $i$. **To Derive:** The equivalent Present Worth $P$ at year 0. **Step 1:** Apply the compound interest formula forward: $$ F = P(1+i)^N $$ **Step 2:** Solve for $P$ (divide both sides by $(1+i)^N$): $$ P = \frac{F}{(1+i)^N} $$ **Step 3:** Identify the discount factor notation: $$ P = F \cdot (P/F, i\%, N) $$ where $(P/F, i\%, N) = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^N}$ **Engineering Logic:** Each rupee earned $N$ years in the future is equivalent to $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^N}$ rupees today. As $N$ increases, the factor shrinks (future value loses weight). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Uniform Series Present Worth Factor **Given:** An end-of-year uniform cash flow $A$ for $N$ years at rate $i$. **To Derive:** $PW$ of the entire series. **Step 1:** Write the present worth of each annual cash flow separately: $$ PW = \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^N} $$ **Step 2:** Factor out $A$: $$ PW = A \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^N} \right] $$ **Step 3:** Recognize this as a **Geometric Progression** with first term $a = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$ and common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$. **Step 4:** Apply the sum formula for geometric series $S_N = \dfrac{a(1-r^N)}{1-r}$: $$ PW = A \cdot \frac{ \dfrac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{(1+i)}\right)^N \right] }{ 1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)} } $$ **Step 5:** Simplify the denominator: $$ 1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)} = \frac{(1+i) - 1}{(1+i)} = \frac{i}{(1+i)} $$ **Step 6:** Substitute and simplify: $$ PW = A \cdot \frac{ \dfrac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \left[ 1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^N} \right] }{ \dfrac{i}{(1+i)} } $$ **Step 7:** Cancel $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$ in numerator and denominator: $$ PW = A \cdot \frac{ 1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^N} }{ i } $$ **Step 8:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^N$: $$ PW = A \cdot \frac{ (1+i)^N - 1 }{ i(1+i)^N } $$ **Step 9:** Final canonical form: $$ \boxed{PW = A \cdot (P/A, i\%, N)} $$ This is the **most frequently used formula** in KTU PW problems. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capitalized Cost (Present Worth of Perpetual Service) **Given:** A project (e.g., a public road or bridge) that must provide service **forever** ($N \to \infty$). **To Derive:** Capitalized Cost (CC) = Equivalent present investment needed to perpetually fund an annual cost $A$ at interest rate $i$. **Step 1:** Start with the Uniform Series PW formula: $$ PW = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N} $$ **Step 2:** Take the limit as $N \to \infty$: $$ CC = \lim_{N \to \infty} A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N} $$ **Step 3:** Split the fraction: $$ CC = A \cdot \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{(1+i)^N}{i(1+i)^N} - A \cdot \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{i(1+i)^N} $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate each limit. As $N \to \infty$: $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{(1+i)^N}{i(1+i)^N} = \frac{1}{i} $$ $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{i(1+i)^N} = 0 $$ **Step 5:** Substitute: $$ CC = A \cdot \frac{1}{i} - 0 = \frac{A}{i} $$ **Step 6:** Final result: $$ \boxed{CC = \frac{A}{i}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** If a project needs ₹1,00,000/year forever for maintenance, and the MARR is 10%, you must invest **₹10,00,000 today** (since ₹10,00,000 × 10% = ₹1,00,000/year interest). This is the **capitalized equivalent** — one of the most elegant results in engineering economics. --- ## 3.4 Solved Analytical Problem (Worked Example) **Problem (KTU Style):** Two machines, X and Y, are being considered. MARR = 10%. Cash flow data: | Parameter | Machine X | Machine Y | |---|---|---| | Initial Cost (P) | ₹5,00,000 | ₹8,00,000 | | Annual Operating Cost (A) | ₹1,00,000 | ₹60,000 | | Salvage Value (S) | ₹50,000 | ₹80,000 | | Useful Life (N) | 6 years | 6 years | **Step 1:** Identify the problem type — **Cost-Only (Service) Alternative with Equal Lives.** **Step 2:** Apply the decision rule — **Minimize Present Worth of Cost (PWC).** **Step 3:** Recall the PWC formula: $$ PWC = P + A \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 6) - S \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 6) $$ **Step 4:** Look up the factors (or compute): - $(P/A, 10\%, 6) = 4.3553$ - $(P/F, 10\%, 6) = 0.5645$ **Step 5:** Calculate PWC for Machine X: $$ PWC_X = 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 4.3553 - 50{,}000 \times 0.5645 $$ $$ PWC_X = 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}35{,}530 - 28{,}225 = 9{,}07{,}305 $$ **Step 6:** Calculate PWC for Machine Y: $$ PWC_Y = 8{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000 \times 4.3553 - 80{,}000 \times 0.5645 $$ $$ PWC_Y = 8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}61{,}318 - 45{,}160 = 10{,}16{,}158 $$ **Step 7:** Compare and decide: $$ PWC_X = 9{,}07{,}305 < PWC_Y = 10{,}16{,}158 $$ **Decision:** ✅ **Select Machine X** because it has the **lower Present Worth of Cost.** # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — PW Comparison Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Two or More Alternatives]) --> q1{Alternatives have
equal or unequal lives?} q1 -- Equal Lives --> q2{Do they generate
same revenue?} q1 -- Unequal Lives --> u1[Use LCM Method
or Capitalized Cost] u1 --> q2 q2 -- Yes Cost Only --> c1[Compute PWC for each
P plus A times P over A,i,N
minus S times P over F,i,N] q2 -- No Revenue Mixed --> r1[Compute PW of Net Cash Flows
Benefits minus Costs] c1 --> c2{Compare PWC values} r1 --> r2{Compare PW values} c2 -- Lowest PWC --> sel1([Select Alternative with
MINIMUM Present Worth of Cost]) c2 -- Tie --> tie1[Apply Incremental B minus C
Analysis to Break Tie] r2 -- Highest PW --> sel2([Select Alternative with
MAXIMUM Net Present Worth]) r2 -- All Negative --> rej([Reject all if PW less than 0
at MARR]) tie1 --> sel1 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cash Flow to PW Conversion Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Stage] cashflow[Cash Flow Series
P, A, S, G over N years] marr[MARR i percent
Minimum Attractive Rate] end subgraph PROC[Processing Stage] discount[Apply Discount Factors
P over F equals 1 over 1 plus i to N] paf[Use Uniform Series Factor
P over A equals one over i times
1 minus 1 over 1 plus i to N] cap[Apply Capitalized Cost
CC equals A divided by i
when N equals infinity] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage] pwc[Present Worth of Cost PWC] npv[Net Present Value NPV] cc[Capitalized Cost CC] end cashflow --> discount marr --> discount discount --> paf discount --> cap paf --> pwc paf --> npv cap --> cc ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Timeline — Cash Flow Position on Time Axis ```mermaid gantt title Engineering Project Cash Flow Timeline (N = 6 years) dateFormat YYYY axisFormat Year %y section Initial Investment P equals 5 lakh at t0 :milestone, 2024, 0d section Annual Costs A equals 1 lakh per year :active, 2024, 6y section Salvage Recovery S equals 50k at t6 :milestone, 2030, 0d ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — PW Method Family ```mermaid flowchart TB root[Present Worth Analysis Family] --> a1[Single Payment PW
P equals F times P over F] root --> a2[Uniform Series PW
P equals A times P over A] root --> a3[Gradient Series PW
P equals G times P over G] root --> a4[Capitalized Cost
CC equals A over i] a1 --> a2 a2 --> a3 a2 --> a4 a4 --> a5[Public Infrastructure
Bridges, Dams, Roads] a3 --> a6[Escalating O and M Costs
typical industrial project] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define Present Worth. State the decision rule for selecting a mutually exclusive cost-only alternative using the Present Worth method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Present Worth is the equivalent worth of all cash flows (revenues and costs) of a project, evaluated at time **t = 0** (the beginning of the project), by discounting future cash flows at the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR, $i$). $$ PW = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \frac{A_t}{(1+i)^t} $$ **Decision Rule (1 mark):** For mutually exclusive **cost-only / service alternatives** (where revenues are equal), select the alternative with the **smallest Present Worth of Cost (PWC)**, i.e., **Minimize PWC.** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is Capitalized Cost? State its formula and one engineering application.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Capitalized Cost (CC) is the present worth of a project that provides service **perpetually** (for an infinite life, $N \to \infty$). **Formula (1 mark):** $$ CC = \frac{A}{i} $$ where $A$ = uniform annual cost and $i$ = MARR. **Engineering Application (1 mark):** Used for evaluating long-lived public infrastructure projects such as **bridges, dams, highways, and railway tracks**, which are expected to serve indefinitely. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **Two machines A and B are being considered for a manufacturing process. MARR = 12%.** | Parameter | Machine A | Machine B | |---|---|---| | Initial Cost | ₹3,00,000 | ₹5,00,000 | | Annual Operating Cost | ₹80,000 | ₹50,000 | | Salvage Value | ₹40,000 | ₹70,000 | | Useful Life | **5 years** | **8 years** | **(a) Compare the machines using the Present Worth method over the LCM of their lives. Identify the economically superior alternative.** **(7 marks)** **(b) If the machines are to provide perpetual service, determine the Capitalized Cost of each machine. Which machine is preferred?** **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution:** **Given data:** MARR $i = 12\% = 0.12$. Lives: $N_A = 5$ years, $N_B = 8$ years. **Part (a) — LCM Method (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Find the LCM of 5 and 8: $$ LCM(5, 8) = 40 \text{ years} $$ So Machine A must be replaced **8 times** (at years 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35) and Machine B must be replaced **5 times** (at years 0, 8, 16, 24, 32). Salvage value at end of LCM life is included in the last replacement. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Look up required factors at 12%: - $(P/A, 12\%, 5) = 3.6048$ - $(P/F, 12\%, 5) = 0.5674$ - $(P/A, 12\%, 8) = 4.9676$ - $(P/F, 12\%, 8) = 0.4039$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute PW of one life-cycle of Machine A: $$ PW_A^{cycle} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 \times 3.6048 - 40{,}000 \times 0.5674 $$ $$ PW_A^{cycle} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}88{,}384 - 22{,}696 = 5{,}65{,}688 $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Convert to PW over 40 years (treating 5-year cycle as a uniform series of 8 cycles): $$ PW_A^{40} = PW_A^{cycle} \times (P/A, 12\%, 5) \times \text{replacements factor} $$ **Simpler LCM approach (1 mark):** Use the standard LCM formula: $$ PW_A = P_A + A_A \cdot (P/A, 12\%, 40) - S_A \cdot (P/F, 12\%, 40) $$ - $(P/A, 12\%, 40) = 8.2438$ - $(P/F, 12\%, 40) = 0.0107$ $$ PW_A = 3{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 \times 8.2438 - 40{,}000 \times 0.0107 $$ $$ PW_A = 3{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}59{,}504 - 428 = 9{,}59{,}076 $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute PW for Machine B similarly: - $(P/A, 12\%, 8) = 4.9676$, but for 40 years the cycles differ. Using direct LCM formula: - $(P/A, 12\%, 40) = 8.2438$, $(P/F, 12\%, 40) = 0.0107$ $$ PW_B = 5{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 \times 8.2438 - 70{,}000 \times 0.0107 $$ $$ PW_B = 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}12{,}190 - 749 = 9{,}11{,}441 $$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** **Decision:** Since both are cost-only alternatives, **select minimum PWC.** $$ PW_B = 9{,}11{,}441 < PW_A = 9{,}59{,}076 $$ ✅ **Select Machine B.** --- **Part (b) — Capitalized Cost Method (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Recall formula: $CC = P + A/i$ (treating annual cost perpetually) — or more precisely, the capitalized cost of one life-cycle. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Convert each machine's annual cost to a perpetual annual equivalent. The standard method uses the capitalized equivalent of one cycle: **For Machine A** (life = 5 years): $$ A_{A,equiv} = P_A \cdot (A/P, 12\%, 5) + A_A - S_A \cdot (A/F, 12\%, 5) $$ - $(A/P, 12\%, 5) = 0.2774$ - $(A/F, 12\%, 5) = 0.1574$ $$ A_{A,equiv} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2774 + 80{,}000 - 40{,}000 \times 0.1574 $$ $$ A_{A,equiv} = 83{,}220 + 80{,}000 - 6{,}296 = 1{,}56{,}924 $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Capitalized Cost of Machine A: $$ CC_A = \frac{A_{A,equiv}}{i} = \frac{1{,}56{,}924}{0.12} = 13{,}07{,}700 $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** For Machine B (life = 8 years): - $(A/P, 12\%, 8) = 0.2013$ - $(A/F, 12\%, 8) = 0.0813$ $$ A_{B,equiv} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2013 + 50{,}000 - 70{,}000 \times 0.0813 $$ $$ A_{B,equiv} = 1{,}00{,}650 + 50{,}000 - 5{,}691 = 1{,}44{,}959 $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Capitalized Cost of Machine B: $$ CC_B = \frac{1{,}44{,}959}{0.12} = 12{,}07{,}992 $$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** **Decision:** $$ CC_B = 12{,}07{,}992 < CC_A = 13{,}07{,}700 $$ ✅ **Select Machine B** (consistent with Part (a) — lower capitalized cost). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]` **A construction company must choose between two excavators E1 and E2. MARR = 10%.** | Parameter | Excavator E1 | Excavator E2 | |---|---|---| | Initial Cost | ₹8,00,000 | ₹12,00,000 | | Annual Revenue (savings in labor) | ₹3,50,000 | ₹4,50,000 | | Annual Operating Cost | ₹80,000 | ₹60,000 | | Salvage Value | ₹1,00,000 | ₹1,50,000 | | Useful Life | 6 years | 6 years | **(a) Compute the Net Present Worth of each alternative and identify the better option.** **(7 marks)** **(b) Perform an incremental Rate of Return / incremental B−C analysis to confirm the choice. Is the extra investment in E2 justified?** **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution:** **Given:** $i = 10\% = 0.10$, $N = 6$ years (equal lives — direct PW comparison possible). **Required factors at 10%, N = 6:** - $(P/A, 10\%, 6) = 4.3553$ - $(P/F, 10\%, 6) = 0.5645$ **Part (a) — Net PW Computation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify net annual cash flow: - $A_{E1} = 3{,}50{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 2{,}70{,}000$ - $A_{E2} = 4{,}50{,}000 - 60{,}000 = 3{,}90{,}000$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute PW for E1: $$ PW_{E1} = -8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}70{,}000 \times 4.3553 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5645 $$ $$ PW_{E1} = -8{,}00{,}000 + 11{,}75{,}931 + 56{,}450 = 4{,}32{,}381 $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute PW for E2: $$ PW_{E2} = -12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}90{,}000 \times 4.3553 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.5645 $$ $$ PW_{E2} = -12{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}98{,}567 + 84{,}675 = 5{,}83{,}242 $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** **Decision:** Both are revenue alternatives, so **maximize PW.** $$ PW_{E2} = 5{,}83{,}242 > PW_{E1} = 4{,}32{,}381 $$ ✅ **Select Excavator E2.** **Step 5 (1 mark):** Note that since both are positive, incremental analysis is required to confirm. --- **Part (b) — Incremental Analysis E2 − E1 (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute incremental cash flows (E2 − E1): - $\Delta P = 12{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000$ (extra investment) - $\Delta A = 3{,}90{,}000 - 2{,}70{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000$ (extra annual net benefit) - $\Delta S = 1{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000$ (extra salvage) **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute incremental PW at 10%: $$ \Delta PW = -4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4.3553 + 50{,}000 \times 0.5645 $$ $$ \Delta PW = -4{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}22{,}636 + 28{,}225 = 1{,}50{,}861 $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute incremental IRR approximately. Try $i = 25\%$: - $(P/A, 25\%, 6) = 2.9514$, $(P/F, 25\%, 6) = 0.2621$ $$ \Delta PW_{25\%} = -4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times 2.9514 + 50{,}000 \times 0.2621 $$ $$ \Delta PW_{25\%} = -4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}54{,}168 + 13{,}105 = -32{,}727 $$ Try $i = 22\%$: - $(P/A, 22\%, 6) \approx 3.0413$, $(P/F, 22\%, 6) \approx 0.2800$ $$ \Delta PW_{22\%} = -4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times 3.0413 + 50{,}000 \times 0.2800 $$ $$ \Delta PW_{22\%} = -4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}64{,}956 + 14{,}000 = -21{,}044 $$ Try $i = 20\%$: - $(P/A, 20\%, 6) = 3.3255$, $(P/F, 20\%, 6) = 0.3349$ $$ \Delta PW_{20\%} = -4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times 3.3255 + 50{,}000 \times 0.3349 $$ $$ \Delta PW_{20\%} = -4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}99{,}060 + 16{,}745 = +15{,}805 $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Interpolate to find incremental IRR: $$ \Delta IRR \approx 20\% + 15{,}805 \times \frac{(22\% - 20\%)}{15{,}805 - (-21{,}044)} $$ $$ \Delta IRR \approx 20\% + \frac{15{,}805 \times 2\%}{36{,}849} \approx 20.86\% $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** **Decision:** Since $\Delta IRR = 20.86\% > MARR = 10\%$, the **extra investment in E2 is justified.** ✅ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Using wrong sign convention:** Initial Cost should be **negative** (outflow); Revenue/Savings should be **positive** (inflow). Forgetting the sign leads to a **completely reversed decision.** > 2. **Mixing Annual and Future cash flows:** Forgetting to multiply annual costs by $(P/A, i\%, N)$ and directly subtracting them from P. > 3. **Forgetting Salvage Value:** Many students ignore $S \cdot (P/F, i\%, N)$ — this is a **3-mark** loss in a 14-mark question. > 4. **Using $N$ for both machines in unequal-life problems:** You MUST use **LCM** or **Capitalized Cost** — directly comparing $PW_5$ vs $PW_8$ is a **fatal conceptual error** in KTU 2024. > 5. **Misreading "Cost-Only" vs "Revenue" problem:** Cost-only → minimize PWC; Revenue → maximize PW. Mixing this up = **wrong answer.** > 6. **Rounding errors in factor lookup:** Use at least **4 decimal places** for KTU board calculations; the difference of 0.0001 compounds over 30-year projects. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - ✅ **PW Definition:** Equivalent worth of all cash flows at $t = 0$ using MARR $i$. - ✅ **Core Formula:** $PW = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{A_t}{(1+i)^t}$ - ✅ **Two key factors to memorize:** $(P/F, i\%, N) = (1+i)^{-N}$ and $(P/A, i\%, N) = \dfrac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N}$ - ✅ **Capitalized Cost:** $CC = A/i$ — used for **perpetual** public projects. - ✅ **Decision Rule (Cost-Only):** Minimize PWC. Formula: $PWC = P + A(P/A, i\%, N) - S(P/F, i\%, N)$. - ✅ **Decision Rule (Revenue):** Maximize Net PW. Formula: $NPW = -P + R(P/A) + S(P/F)$. - ✅ **Unequal Lives:** Always use **LCM method** (discrete replacement) or **Capitalized Cost** (perpetual). - ✅ **Incremental Analysis:** When both alternatives have positive PW, compute (B − C) incremental and check if $\Delta IRR \geq MARR$. - ✅ **Always show factor lookups** explicitly in KTU exam; don't just write numerical answers. - ✅ **Salvage value is a POSITIVE inflow** at $t = N$, not a cost. - ✅ **Inflation vs. MARR:** KTU problems usually use **real MARR**; if inflation is given separately, use $(1 + i_{nominal}) = (1 + i_{real})(1 + f)$ where $f$ = inflation rate. - ✅ **Watch out for "beginning-of-year"** vs **"end-of-year"** cash flows in KTU questions — beginning-of-year shifts by one period. - ✅ **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Structure your answer as Given Data → Formula → Factor Lookup → Computation → Comparison → Decision (boxed).

topics.title: Future Worth Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Future Worth Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Future Worth (FW) Comparison** is an engineering economic analysis method in which all cash flows (initial investment, recurring operating costs/revenues, and salvage values) associated with one or more mutually exclusive alternatives are converted to an equivalent lump-sum value at the **end of the analysis period** (or at a common future point in time) using the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** as the discount/compounding rate. The alternative with the most favorable Future Worth is then selected.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, FW comparison is treated as one of the three principal equivalence methods for evaluating capital investment alternatives, alongside **Present Worth (PW)** and **Annual Worth (AW)** methods. All three are mathematically equivalent because they differ only in *where* in time the equivalent value is computed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time-Value Anchor:** Future Worth is the single value, at time *t = n*, that is economically equivalent to the entire stream of cash flows between *t = 0* and *t = n*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two friends, **Rahul** and **Anita**, who each start a small business on the same day:

- **Rahul** puts **₹1,00,000** into a high-yield fixed deposit at **10% per annum** for 4 years and does nothing else.
- **Anita** puts **₹1,00,000** into a shop, earns irregular yearly profits, pays rent, and at the end of year 4 closes the shop and recovers some salvage value.

After 4 years, who is wealthier in absolute rupee terms? You cannot just add Rahul's deposit balance to Anita's net shop earnings because the money arrived at *different times*. Future Worth comparison solves this by **compounding every rupee forward** to a common end-point — like bringing all the scattered coins from a piggy bank and counting them at a single moment.

> **The Future Worth acts as a "lump-sum final balance" — a single number that captures the entire compounded history of cash inflows and outflows.**

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Symbols

The following symbols are used uniformly across the KTU Finance for Engineering syllabus:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:------:|:--------|
| **P** | Present Worth (a single payment at *t = 0*) |
| **F** | Future Worth (a single payment at *t = n*) |
| **A** | Uniform annual series (End-of-Period payment) |
| **G** | Arithmetic gradient (₹ change per period) |
| **i** | Effective interest rate per period (**MARR**) |
| **n** | Number of compounding periods / service life |
| **S** | Salvage value (realized at end of useful life) |
| **t** | Time (in years or periods) |

---

## 1.4 Decision Criterion

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule for FW Comparison**
> - For a **cost-only** project (cash flows are all expenses): select the alternative with the **least negative FW** (i.e., the smallest equivalent future cost).
> - For a **revenue / benefit** project: select the alternative with the **largest positive FW**.
> - For a **mixed** project (revenue and cost): select the alternative with the **highest net FW** (FW of benefits minus FW of costs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind FW Comparison

The economic justification for FW comparison rests on a fundamental principle of finance: **a rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received tomorrow.** Because cash flows of an engineering project occur at *different times*, they must be moved to a common temporal reference frame before they can be added or compared. The **Future Worth** is that common frame placed at the end of the project's economic life.

> The key logic is the *equivalence principle*: two cash-flow streams are equivalent if their Future Worths (at the same interest rate *i* and same time *n*) are equal.

---

## 2.2 Three Core Engineering Cash-Flow Patterns

Almost every engineering investment can be decomposed into three elementary patterns. KTU board questions almost always test the combination of these three.

### 2.2.1 Single Payment → Future Worth

A one-time cash flow **P** at time 0, compounded at rate *i* for *n* periods, becomes:

> **F = P (1 + i)ⁿ**

This factor **(1 + i)ⁿ** is the **Single Payment Compound Amount Factor**, symbolized as **(F/P, i, n)**.

### 2.2.2 Uniform Annual Series → Future Worth

A constant end-of-period cash flow **A** for *n* periods, accumulated at *i*, becomes:

> **F = A × [ ( (1 + i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i ]**

This factor **[ ( (1 + i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i ]** is the **Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor**, symbolized as **(F/A, i, n)**.

*Intuition:* each year's A is independently compounded forward, and the resulting geometric series is summed.

### 2.2.3 Arithmetic Gradient → Future Worth

When cash flows change by a constant amount **G** each year (e.g., maintenance costs rising by ₹500 every year), the equivalent Future Worth is:

> **F = G × [ ( (1 + i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i²  −  n / i ]**

This factor is the **Gradient Future Worth Factor**, **(F/G, i, n)**.

---

## 2.3 Conversion Relationships Among PW, AW, and FW

All three equivalence methods are linked by simple multiplicative factors. This means a Future Worth comparison can be performed **without ever drawing a cash-flow diagram with money at the end of the project**, simply by computing PW or AW first and converting.

The relevant conversions are:

> **F = P (1 + i)ⁿ**
>
> **F = A × [ ( (1 + i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i ]**
>
> **P = A × [ ( (1 + i)ⁿ − 1 ) / ( i (1 + i)ⁿ ) ]**

---

## 2.4 Procedure for FW Comparison of Mutually Exclusive Alternatives

The KTU 2024 scheme lays down a structured step-by-step procedure:

1. **Identify** all cash flows for each alternative: initial cost (at t = 0), annual operating & maintenance cost, annual revenue (if any), and salvage value (at end of life).
2. **Decide** the analysis period:
   - If all alternatives have the **same service life**, use that life as the analysis period.
   - If lives are **unequal**, use the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of the lives (repeatability assumption) OR a pre-defined **study period**.
3. **Convert** every cash flow to its Future Worth at the end of the analysis period using the appropriate factor.
4. **Sum** the FW components algebraically for each alternative.
5. **Apply the decision criterion** — choose the alternative with the most favorable FW.
6. For **incremental analysis** (when initial costs differ significantly), perform an **incremental FW analysis** on (Higher-Cost − Lower-Cost) to ensure the extra investment earns at least the MARR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repeatability Assumption (KTU favourite):** When alternatives have different service lives, KTU expects you to assume that each alternative will be replaced by an identical one at the end of its life, and that the cash-flow pattern repeats indefinitely. The analysis is then carried out to the **LCM of the lives** so that all alternatives are on a comparable footing.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** for the Future Worth comparison method. Memorize the factor symbols and the boundary conditions.

| # | Cash-Flow Pattern | Future Worth Formula | Factor Symbol | Boundary Conditions |
|:-:|:------------------|:---------------------|:-------------:|:--------------------|
| 1 | Single Payment P at t = 0 | F = P (1 + i)ⁿ | (F/P, i, n) | i > 0, n ≥ 1 |
| 2 | Uniform Series A, n periods | F = A [ ( (1+i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i ] | (F/A, i, n) | i > 0, n ≥ 1 |
| 3 | Arithmetic Gradient G | F = G [ ( (1+i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i² − n / i ] | (F/G, i, n) | i > 0, n ≥ 2 |
| 4 | Salvage Value S at t = n | S = P (1+i)ⁿ ⇒ P = S / (1+i)ⁿ | (P/F, i, n) | i > 0 |
| 5 | PW to FW Conversion | F = P (F/P, i, n) | — | — |
| 6 | AW to FW Conversion | F = A (F/A, i, n) | — | — |
| 7 | LCM Repeat Factor | F_LCM = F_cycle × [ ((1+i)^N − 1) / ((1+i)^m − 1) ] | — | N = LCM, m = asset life |

> **Memory Tip for KTU Students:**
> - **F/P** = "Find, given Present" — multiply Present by this to get Future.
> - **F/A** = "Find, given Annual" — multiply Annual by this to get Future lump sum.
> - **P/A** = "Present, given Annual" — divide Annual by this to get Present.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Future Worth comparison is not just an academic exercise — it has direct, practical applications in:

- **Equipment Replacement Decisions:** Comparing two CNC machines to determine which has the lower equivalent future operating cost.
- **Power Plant Sizing:** Comparing thermal vs. solar plants over a 25-year horizon.
- **Civil Infrastructure:** Bridges, tunnels, and dams with multi-decade cash-flow streams.
- **Telecommunications & IT:** Server hardware with 3- to 5-year refresh cycles and ongoing licensing costs.
- **Automotive Engineering:** Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) studies of EVs vs. ICE vehicles over 10 years.

The advantage of FW is that the final number literally represents the **size of the bank balance** an investor would need at the end of the horizon to fund all the cash flows — an intuitive and powerful interpretation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: FW of a Single Payment (F = P(1 + i)ⁿ)

**Logical Premise:** A sum *P* invested today at a periodic interest rate *i* grows by the interest amount *iP* at the end of period 1. The new principal is *(P + iP) = P(1 + i)*. The process repeats each period.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

- At end of period 0: balance = **P**
- At end of period 1: balance = P + iP = **P (1 + i)**
- At end of period 2: balance = P(1+i) + i·P(1+i) = **P (1 + i)²**
- At end of period 3: balance = **P (1 + i)³**
- Continuing the pattern for *n* periods:

$$
F = P (1 + i)^{n}
$$

This is the foundational single-payment compound amount formula. KTU students must be able to derive it on paper in under 30 seconds.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: FW of a Uniform Annual Series (F = A[ ( (1+i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i ])

**Logical Premise:** Each end-of-period payment *A* independently compounds forward to time *n*. The last payment (at *t = n*) has zero time to compound, while the first payment (at *t = 1*) compounds for *n − 1* periods. The total Future Worth is the sum of all these individual Future Worths.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

- FW of the last payment (at t = n) = A (1 + i)⁰ = A
- FW of the (n−1)ᵗʰ payment (at t = n−1) = A (1 + i)¹
- FW of the (n−2)ⁿᵈ payment (at t = n−2) = A (1 + i)²
- ...
- FW of the 1ˢᵗ payment (at t = 1) = A (1 + i)ⁿ⁻¹

Summing all contributions:

$$
F = A \left[ 1 + (1+i) + (1+i)^{2} + \cdots + (1+i)^{n-1} \right]
$$

The bracketed term is a **finite geometric series** with first term 1, common ratio (1 + i), and n terms. The sum of such a series is:

$$
\text{Sum} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

This is the **Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor (F/A, i, n)** — the single most-tested factor in KTU's finance module.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion Between PW ↔ FW ↔ AW

**Step 1: PW to FW**

Starting from the identity F = P(1+i)ⁿ, multiply both sides by (1+i)ⁿ:

$$
F = P \, (1+i)^{n}
$$

**Step 2: AW to FW**

A stream of A's over n periods is equivalent to a single sum F at t = n. From the derivation above:

$$
F = A \, \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 3: AW to PW (Cap ital Recovery)**

Divide the AW → FW equation by (1+i)ⁿ:

$$
\frac{F}{(1+i)^{n}} = P = A \, \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \, (1+i)^{n}}
$$

This is the **Present Worth factor for an annuity (P/A, i, n)**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example (Equal Lives)

**Problem Statement (KTU-pattern):**

> A manufacturing company must choose between **Machine X** and **Machine Y**, both having a useful life of **5 years**. The MARR is **10% per annum**. The cash flows are:

| Cash Flow | Machine X | Machine Y |
|:----------|----------:|----------:|
| Initial Cost (t = 0) | ₹ 2,00,000 | ₹ 3,50,000 |
| Annual O&M Cost (t = 1 to 5) | ₹ 30,000 | ₹ 15,000 |
| Salvage Value (t = 5) | ₹ 20,000 | ₹ 50,000 |

**Step 1 — Tabulate the interest factors (i = 10%, n = 5):**

- (F/P, 10%, 5) = (1.10)⁵ = **1.6105**
- (F/A, 10%, 5) = [ (1.10)⁵ − 1 ] / 0.10 = [ 1.6105 − 1 ] / 0.10 = **6.1051**

**Step 2 — Compute Future Worth of Machine X:**

The cash flow of Machine X has three components, all shifted to t = 5:
- Initial cost (negative cash flow): −2,00,000 × (F/P, 10%, 5) = −2,00,000 × 1.6105 = **−3,21,100**
- O&M series (5 payments of 30,000, negative): −30,000 × (F/A, 10%, 5) = −30,000 × 6.1051 = **−1,83,153**
- Salvage value (positive cash inflow at t = 5): **+20,000**

$$
F_{X} = -2{,}00{,}000 (1.6105) - 30{,}000 (6.1051) + 20{,}000
$$

$$
F_{X} = -3{,}21{,}100 - 1{,}83{,}153 + 20{,}000
$$

$$
F_{X} = -4{,}84{,}253 \text{ (rupees)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Future Worth of Machine Y:**

- Initial cost: −3,50,000 × 1.6105 = **−5,63,675**
- O&M series: −15,000 × 6.1051 = **−91,576.5**
- Salvage value: **+50,000**

$$
F_{Y} = -3{,}50{,}000 (1.6105) - 15{,}000 (6.1051) + 50{,}000
$$

$$
F_{Y} = -5{,}63{,}675 - 91{,}576.5 + 50{,}000
$$

$$
F_{Y} = -6{,}05{,}251.5 \text{ (rupees)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Decision Criterion:**

Both FW values are **negative** because we are evaluating costs. The "better" alternative is the one whose negative FW is **closer to zero** (i.e., the larger algebraic value).

Comparing:
- F_X = −4,84,253
- F_Y = −6,05,251.5

Since **F_X > F_Y** (less negative), **Machine X is the economically preferred alternative** because it costs ₹ 1,20,998.5 less in future-value terms over 5 years.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Check with PW Method:** PW_X = −4,84,253 / 1.6105 = −3,00,684 (≈ same number reported in PW-method solutions). The three methods always agree.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example (Unequal Lives — LCM)

**Problem Statement:**

> A factory must choose between two pumping systems, **A** and **B**, with unequal lives. MARR = **8% per annum**.

| Cash Flow | System A | System B |
|:----------|----------:|----------:|
| Initial Cost (t = 0) | ₹ 1,50,000 | ₹ 2,40,000 |
| Annual O&M Cost (t = 1 to end of life) | ₹ 25,000 | ₹ 12,000 |
| Salvage Value (end of life) | ₹ 30,000 | ₹ 60,000 |
| Useful Life | 3 years | 4 years |

**Step 1 — Determine LCM of Lives:**

$$
\text{LCM}(3, 4) = 12 \text{ years}
$$

System A must be replaced 4 times (at t = 0, 3, 6, 9); System B must be replaced 3 times (at t = 0, 4, 8). The analysis extends to t = 12.

**Step 2 — Required Interest Factors (i = 8%):**

- (1.08)³ = 1.2597 ⇒ **(F/P, 8%, 3) = 1.2597**
- (1.08)⁴ = 1.3605 ⇒ **(F/P, 8%, 4) = 1.3605**
- (1.08)⁶ = 1.5869
- (1.08)⁹ = 1.9990
- (1.08)¹² = 2.5182
- (F/A, 8%, 3) = (1.2597 − 1)/0.08 = **3.2464**
- (F/A, 8%, 4) = (1.3605 − 1)/0.08 = **4.5061**

**Step 3 — Future Worth of One Cycle of System A at the End of Its Life (t = 3):**

$$
F_{A,3} = -1{,}50{,}000 (1.2597) - 25{,}000 (3.2464) + 30{,}000
$$

$$
F_{A,3} = -1{,}88{,}955 - 81{,}160 + 30{,}000 = -2{,}40{,}115
$$

**Step 4 — Repeatability Factor for 4 Cycles of System A:**

Using the geometric-series identity for repeated equivalent cycles:

$$
\text{Repeat Factor} = \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{(1+i)^{m} - 1} = \frac{(1.08)^{12} - 1}{(1.08)^{3} - 1} = \frac{2.5182 - 1}{1.2597 - 1} = \frac{1.5182}{0.2597} = 5.8457
$$

**Step 5 — Future Worth of System A at t = 12:**

$$
F_{A,12} = F_{A,3} \times 5.8457 = -2{,}40{,}115 \times 5.8457 = -14{,}03{,}540 \text{ (rupees)}
$$

**Step 6 — Future Worth of One Cycle of System B at the End of Its Life (t = 4):**

$$
F_{B,4} = -2{,}40{,}000 (1.3605) - 12{,}000 (4.5061) + 60{,}000
$$

$$
F_{B,4} = -3{,}26{,}520 - 54{,}073.2 + 60{,}000 = -3{,}20{,}593.2
$$

**Step 7 — Repeatability Factor for 3 Cycles of System B:**

$$
\text{Repeat Factor} = \frac{(1.08)^{12} - 1}{(1.08)^{4} - 1} = \frac{1.5182}{0.3605} = 4.2114
$$

**Step 8 — Future Worth of System B at t = 12:**

$$
F_{B,12} = F_{B,4} \times 4.2114 = -3{,}20{,}593.2 \times 4.2114 = -13{,}50{,}107 \text{ (rupees)}
$$

**Step 9 — Decision:**

Since both FW values are negative (cost project), the **larger** (less negative) value wins:

- F_A,12 = −14,03,540
- F_B,12 = −13,50,107

**F_B,12 > F_A,12**, so **System B is preferred** — its future equivalent cost is ₹ 53,433 lower.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Future Worth Comparison Procedure

The following block diagram captures the **complete decision logic** for solving a FW comparison problem, including the branch point for equal vs. unequal service lives and the repeatability assumption used for unequal lives.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Identify All Alternatives And Cash Flows"] --> B["Tabulate P At T0, A Series, S At Tn For Each Alternative"]
    B --> C{"Do All Alternatives Have The Same Service Life?"}
    C -- "YES, EQUAL LIVES" --> D["Set Analysis Period n Equal To The Common Life"]
    C -- "NO, UNEQUAL LIVES" --> E["Compute LCM Of All Service Lives To Define Analysis Horizon"]
    D --> F["Choose Interest Rate MARR Equal To i Percent"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Select Proper Future Worth Factor For Each Cash Flow Type"]
    G --> G1["Single Payment P: Use Factor F OVER P I N"]
    G --> G2["Uniform Series A: Use Factor F OVER A I N"]
    G --> G3["Gradient G: Use Factor F OVER G I N"]
    G1 --> H["Convert Each Cash Flow Of Alternative 1 To F At Horizon End"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I{"Is The Project A Cost Or Revenue Project?"}
    I -- "COST PROJECT" --> J["Compute Total Future Worth F Of Costs For Alt 1"]
    I -- "REVENUE PROJECT" --> K["Compute Total Future Worth F Of Revenues For Alt 1"]
    J --> L["Repeat Computation For Alternative 2 And Any Other Alternatives"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["Tabulate All F Values At The Common Horizon"]
    M --> N{"Is This A Cost Or Revenue Project?"}
    N -- "COST PROJECT" --> O["SELECT ALTERNATIVE WITH THE LEAST NEGATIVE FW HIGHEST F"]
    N -- "REVENUE PROJECT" --> P["SELECT ALTERNATIVE WITH THE HIGHEST POSITIVE FW"]
    O --> Q["END: Document Selected Alternative And FW Value"]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Cash-Flow Schematic: Multi-Cycle Repeatability

The following diagram illustrates how the **repeatability assumption** works for two alternatives with unequal lives. Each triangle represents one full service-life cycle, and the arrow shows how the future worth of each cycle is compounded forward to the LCM horizon.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph System_A["SYSTEM A: Life = 3 Years, 4 Repeats in 12-Year Horizon"]
        A0["Cycle 1: Years 0 To 3"] -- "Compound 9 Years" --> A_T12["F at Year 12"]
        A3["Cycle 2: Years 3 To 6"] -- "Compound 6 Years" --> A_T12
        A6["Cycle 3: Years 6 To 9"] -- "Compound 3 Years" --> A_T12
        A9["Cycle 4: Years 9 To 12"] -- "No Compound" --> A_T12
    end
    subgraph System_B["SYSTEM B: Life = 4 Years, 3 Repeats in 12-Year Horizon"]
        B0["Cycle 1: Years 0 To 4"] -- "Compound 8 Years" --> B_T12["F at Year 12"]
        B4["Cycle 2: Years 4 To 8"] -- "Compound 4 Years" --> B_T12
        B8["Cycle 3: Years 8 To 12"] -- "No Compound" --> B_T12
    end
    A_T12 --> COMPARE["Compare FW of A Versus FW of B at t = 12"]
    B_T12 --> COMPARE
    COMPARE --> DECIDE["Select Alternative with Most Favorable FW"]
```

## 4.3 Factor-Application Block Diagram

The following block-level diagram clarifies *which* factor is applied to *which* cash-flow component when constructing a Future Worth equation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs["Cash Flow Inputs at Their Respective Times"]
        P0["P: Initial Investment at t = 0"]
        A1["A: Uniform Annual Series from t = 1 to n"]
        Gn["G: Arithmetic Gradient Series"]
        Sn["S: Salvage Value at t = n"]
    end
    subgraph Factors["Compounding Factors from i and n"]
        F1["F OVER P I N equals 1 plus i raised to n"]
        F2["F OVER A I N equals bracket 1 plus i raised to n minus 1 over i bracket"]
        F3["F OVER G I N equals gradient FW factor"]
        F4["Salvage S is already at t = n, no compounding needed"]
    end
    subgraph Output["Single Future Worth F at t = n"]
        FW["FW equals negative P times F OVER P plus negative A times F OVER A plus F OVER G times G plus S"]
    end
    P0 --> F1 --> FW
    A1 --> F2 --> FW
    Gn --> F3 --> FW
    Sn --> F4 --> FW
```
