# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]
**Define Future Worth comparison. State the decision criterion for selecting an alternative using this method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
> Future Worth comparison is a method of engineering economic analysis in which all cash flows (initial cost, annual operating costs, salvage value, etc.) associated with one or more alternatives are converted to a single equivalent lump-sum value at the end of the analysis period, using a specified interest rate (MARR). The alternative with the most favorable future worth is selected.

**Decision Criterion (1 mark):**
>
> - For a **cost project**: choose the alternative with the **least negative (smallest) Future Worth**.
> - For a **revenue project**: choose the alternative with the **largest positive Future Worth**.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]
**State the relationship between Present Worth, Future Worth, and Annual Worth. Why are all three methods mathematically equivalent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Relationships (2 marks):**
>
> - F = P × (1 + i)ⁿ
> - F = A × [ ( (1 + i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i ]
> - P = A × [ ( (1 + i)ⁿ − 1 ) / ( i (1 + i)ⁿ ) ]

**Mathematical Equivalence (1 mark):**
> All three methods are mathematically equivalent because they evaluate the *same* stream of cash flows at the *same* interest rate *i* and differ only in the **time reference point** at which the equivalent value is computed (t = 0 for PW, end of each year for AW, and t = n for FW). Since equivalence at one point in time implies equivalence at every other point in time (under the principle of economic equivalence), the choice of method does not affect the final ranking of alternatives.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** with internal choice. The model below provides **Question A** and **Question B**, each with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Future Worth comparison in engineering economic analysis. Discuss the procedure to be followed when comparing alternatives with **(i)** equal service lives and **(ii)** unequal service lives. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
> Future Worth comparison is a capital investment evaluation technique in which every cash flow (initial cost, recurring costs, salvage) of each alternative is compounded forward at the MARR to a single equivalent value at the end of the analysis horizon. The alternative with the most favorable future worth is selected.

**Equal Lives (2 marks):**
> When all alternatives have the same useful life *n*, that life is taken as the analysis horizon. Each cash flow is compounded to *t = n* using the appropriate factor:
> - Initial cost P → F = P(1+i)ⁿ
> - Annual series A → F = A[(1+i)ⁿ − 1]/i
> - Salvage S → already at t = n, no compounding
> The future worths are summed and the most favorable value is chosen.

**Unequal Lives (3 marks):**
> When alternatives have different lives, the **repeatability assumption** is invoked: each alternative is assumed to be replaced by an identical one at the end of its life, and the analysis is carried out to the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of the lives. For each alternative:
> 1. Compute the future worth of one cycle at the end of its own life.
> 2. Apply the **repeat factor** = [(1+i)^N − 1] / [(1+i)^m − 1] where N = LCM and m = the asset's own life.
> 3. The product is the FW at the LCM horizon. Compare across alternatives.

---

**(b)** Two machines are being considered for a manufacturing process. **Machine P** has an initial cost of **₹ 2,00,000**, annual operating cost of **₹ 30,000**, salvage value of **₹ 20,000**, and a useful life of **5 years**. **Machine Q** has an initial cost of **₹ 3,50,000**, annual operating cost of **₹ 15,000**, salvage value of **₹ 50,000**, and the same useful life of **5 years**. The MARR is **10% per annum**. Using the Future Worth method, determine which machine should be selected. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Required Factors (i = 10%, n = 5):**
>
> - (F/P, 10%, 5) = (1.10)⁵ = **1.6105**
> - (F/A, 10%, 5) = (1.6105 − 1) / 0.10 = **6.1051**

**Step 2 — FW of Machine P at t = 5:**

$$
F_{P} = -2{,}00{,}000 (1.6105) - 30{,}000 (6.1051) + 20{,}000
$$

$$
F_{P} = -3{,}21{,}000 - 1{,}83{,}153 + 20{,}000
$$

$$
F_{P} = -4{,}84{,}153
$$

*(Correction:* −2,00,000 × 1.6105 = −3,21,100; re-summing gives F_P = **−4,84,253**)

**Step 3 — FW of Machine Q at t = 5:**

$$
F_{Q} = -3{,}50{,}000 (1.6105) - 15{,}000 (6.1051) + 50{,}000
$$

$$
F_{Q} = -5{,}63{,}675 - 91{,}577 + 50{,}000 = -6{,}05{,}252
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

Both FW values are negative (cost project). The preferred alternative is the one with the **higher (less negative) FW**.

| Alternative | Future Worth (₹) |
|:------------|-----------------:|
| Machine P   | −4,84,253        |
| Machine Q   | −6,05,252        |

Since F_P > F_Q, **Machine P should be selected**. The equivalent future cost of Machine P is ₹ 1,20,999 lower than that of Machine Q over the 5-year horizon.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a suitable derivation, obtain the formula for the **Future Worth of a Uniform Series**. Explain its significance with reference to the KTU syllabus. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a uniform end-of-period cash flow A for n periods, to be accumulated at interest rate i to time t = n.

- The nᵗʰ (last) payment is at t = n → compounded for 0 periods → FW = A
- The (n − 1)ᵗʰ payment is at t = n − 1 → compounded for 1 period → FW = A(1 + i)
- The (n − 2)ⁿᵈ payment is at t = n − 2 → compounded for 2 periods → FW = A(1 + i)²
- ⋯
- The 1ˢᵗ payment is at t = 1 → compounded for (n − 1) periods → FW = A(1 + i)ⁿ⁻¹

Summing all contributions:

$$
F = A \left[ 1 + (1+i) + (1+i)^{2} + \cdots + (1+i)^{n-1} \right]
$$

The bracket is a finite geometric series with first term 1, common ratio (1 + i), and n terms. Its sum is:

$$
\text{Sum} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F = A \, \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
> This is the **Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor (F/A, i, n)**, which appears in nearly every engineering economic problem that involves recurring annual costs or revenues (e.g., annual maintenance of a machine, annual fuel cost of a vehicle, annual lease payment). It allows the entire series to be converted to a single future lump sum, enabling direct comparison across alternatives.

---

**(b)** A construction company must choose between two dewatering pumps, **Pump M** and **Pump N**, with unequal lives. The data is given below. MARR is **8% per annum**. Use the **Future Worth method with repeatability assumption** to recommend the better pump. (7 marks)

| Cash Flow           | Pump M   | Pump N   |
|:--------------------|---------:|---------:|
| Initial Cost (t = 0)| ₹ 1,50,000 | ₹ 2,40,000 |
| Annual O&M (t = 1 …)| ₹ 25,000   | ₹ 12,000   |
| Salvage Value       | ₹ 30,000   | ₹ 60,000   |
| Useful Life         | 3 years    | 4 years    |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — LCM of Lives:**

$$
N = \text{LCM}(3, 4) = 12 \text{ years}
$$

Pump M repeats 4 times; Pump N repeats 3 times. Analysis horizon = 12 years.

**Step 2 — Required Factors at i = 8%:**

> - (1.08)³ = 1.2597
> - (1.08)⁴ = 1.3605
> - (1.08)¹² = 2.5182
> - (F/A, 8%, 3) = 0.2597 / 0.08 = **3.2464**
> - (F/A, 8%, 4) = 0.3605 / 0.08 = **4.5061**

**Step 3 — FW of One Cycle of Pump M at t = 3:**

$$
F_{M,3} = -1{,}50{,}000 (1.2597) - 25{,}000 (3.2464) + 30{,}000
$$

$$
F_{M,3} = -1{,}88{,}955 - 81{,}160 + 30{,}000 = -2{,}40{,}115
$$

**Step 4 — Repeat Factor for 4 Cycles of Pump M:**

$$
k_{M} = \frac{(1.08)^{12} - 1}{(1.08)^{3} - 1} = \frac{1.5182}{0.2597} = 5.8457
$$

**Step 5 — FW of Pump M at t = 12:**

$$
F_{M,12} = -2{,}40{,}115 \times 5.8457 = -14{,}03{,}540
$$

**Step 6 — FW of One Cycle of Pump N at t = 4:**

$$
F_{N,4} = -2{,}40{,}000 (1.3605) - 12{,}000 (4.5061) + 60{,}000
$$

$$
F_{N,4} = -3{,}26{,}520 - 54{,}073 + 60{,}000 = -3{,}20{,}593
$$

**Step 7 — Repeat Factor for 3 Cycles of Pump N:**

$$
k_{N} = \frac{(1.08)^{12} - 1}{(1.08)^{4} - 1} = \frac{1.5182}{0.3605} = 4.2114
$$

**Step 8 — FW of Pump N at t = 12:**

$$
F_{N,12} = -3{,}20{,}593 \times 4.2114 = -13{,}50{,}107
$$

**Step 9 — Decision:**

| Alternative | FW at t = 12 (₹) |
|:------------|-----------------:|
| Pump M      | −14,03,540        |
| Pump N      | −13,50,107        |

Since F_N,12 > F_M,12 (less negative), **Pump N is preferred** — it is ₹ 53,433 cheaper in future-value terms over the 12-year horizon.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in FW Comparison — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Sign Confusion:** Students often add the salvage value to the initial cost (instead of treating it as a positive cash inflow at t = n). Always remember: **Salvage is money received, not money paid.**
> 2. **Wrong Factor Selection:** A common error is to apply (P/F, i, n) when (F/P, i, n) is required, or vice versa. The rule is simple: the factor must move the cash flow *forward* to t = n — so always use a factor with **F in the numerator**.
> 3. **Forgetting LCM for Unequal Lives:** Students compute FWs up to each machine's own life and compare directly. This is **wrong** — different time horizons make the comparison invalid. You **must** extend the analysis to the LCM.
> 4. **Forgetting to Multiply the Salvage:** Some students add the salvage value without compounding it. If the horizon is the LCM (and the asset is replaced), only the **last** salvage is at t = N; earlier salvages must themselves be compounded forward.
> 5. **Mixing Discount and Compound Factors:** When converting PW or AW into FW, students occasionally divide by (1+i)ⁿ instead of multiplying. Remember: PW → FW means **multiplying by (F/P, i, n)**.
> 6. **Decision Rule Reversal:** For a cost project, students sometimes select the alternative with the *most* negative FW. The correct rule is the **least negative (largest algebraic) FW**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Future Worth Comparison**

- ✅ **FW = P (1 + i)ⁿ** — single-payment compound amount
- ✅ **FW = A [ ( (1 + i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i ]** — uniform series compound amount
- ✅ **FW = G [ ( (1 + i)ⁿ − 1 ) / i² − n / i ]** — gradient future worth
- ✅ For **equal lives**, set the horizon *n* equal to the common life.
- ✅ For **unequal lives**, use the **LCM** of the lives with the **repeatability assumption**.
- ✅ The **repeat factor** is **[ (1+i)^N − 1 ] / [ (1+i)^m − 1 ]**, where N = LCM and m = the asset's own life.
- ✅ **Decision Rule for cost projects:** choose the alternative with the **least negative** (largest algebraic) FW.
- ✅ **Decision Rule for revenue projects:** choose the alternative with the **largest positive** FW.
- ✅ **Salvage value is a positive cash inflow** at the end of life — never subtract it.
- ✅ All three equivalence methods (PW, AW, FW) give the **same ranking** of alternatives.
- ✅ Use **standard interest factor tables** (or a calculator) and write each factor with its symbol, e.g., **(F/A, 10%, 5) = 6.1051** — KTU examiners reward explicit factor notation.
- ✅ When in doubt, **draw a cash-flow diagram** on rough work — it eliminates sign errors.
- ✅ In numerical answers, always **state the units** (₹ in this case) and **round to 2-3 significant figures** consistent with the input data.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Annual Cost and Worth Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET725 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Annual Cost and Worth Comparison ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** is an engineering economic measure that converts all cash flows associated with an investment alternative — initial cost, salvage value, operating & maintenance (O&M) costs, and any periodic expenditures — into a single, uniform annual amount occurring over the entire life cycle of the project, evaluated at the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)**, denoted as **i** (in % per year). The **Annual Worth Comparison Method** ranks mutually exclusive alternatives by computing either their **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC / EUAC)** for cost-driven problems or their **Equivalent Annual Worth (EAW)** for revenue-driven problems. For **cost-only alternatives**, the decision rule is: > **Select the alternative with the LOWEST EUAC.** > **Capitalized Cost (P∞ or CC)** is the present worth of a perpetual (infinite-life) investment. It is defined as the lump-sum amount which, if invested at MARR **i**, would generate an annual return sufficient to cover all perpetual expenses in perpetuity. Mathematically: $$P_{\infty} = \frac{A}{i}$$ where **A** is the perpetual uniform annual cost and **i** is the MARR expressed as a decimal. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car EMI" Intuition Imagine you are buying a car priced at **₹8,00,000**. The showroom offers a loan, and the bank quotes a monthly **Equated Monthly Instalment (EMI)**. That EMI is *exactly* what EUAC does to a project! - The car's price (₹8,00,000) is the **Present Worth (P)**. - The EMI is a single uniform amount paid every month that **mathematically equals** the price + interest + maintenance spread evenly over the loan tenure. - Similarly, EUAC spreads the *entire lifecycle cost* of a project (purchase + operations - salvage) into **one neat annual figure**, making dissimilar projects (one lasting 5 years, another 12 years) directly comparable on the **same "per-year" ruler**. > [!NOTE] > **Capitalized Cost Analogy** — Think of the **Endowment Fund** of a university. A philanthropist donates ₹100 Crore such that the *interest earned annually* (at 8% → ₹8 Crore/year) forever pays the salaries, electricity, and maintenance. The **₹100 Crore principal** is the **Capitalized Cost**, while the recurring **₹8 Crore annual interest** is the perpetual "operating cost". The principal never gets spent; it just keeps working. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **MARR (i)** — usually **8%, 10%, 12%, or 15%** in KTU problems unless otherwise stated. - **Useful Life (n)** — must be in years, integer or fractional. - **Salvage Value (S)** — terminal resale/scrap value at year *n*. - **Annual Operating Cost (AOC)** — uniform yearly O&M, insurance, labour, energy. - **Arithmetic signs**: Costs = **positive (+)**; Revenues/Savings = **negative (−)** for cost-method problems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Do We Need Annual Cost Comparison? When alternatives have **unequal lives**, direct comparison of Present Worth (PW) becomes misleading because one alternative may repeat many times during another's life. The EUAC method inherently assumes the alternative is *repeated identically* over an infinite planning horizon (the **repeatability assumption**), bypassing the need for the Least Common Multiple (LCM) of lives. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight**: Per KTU Module 4, students must be able to (a) compute EUAC for a single alternative, (b) compare 2–3 mutually exclusive alternatives using EUAC, and (c) apply the Capitalized Cost method for permanent installations like bridges, dams, and rail infrastructure. ### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Identify all cash flows**: - Initial Investment (*P*) at year 0 - Salvage Value (*S*) at year *n* - Annual Operating Cost (*AOC*) every year 1 to *n* - Any recurring periodic costs (e.g., overhaul every 5 years) **Step 2 — Convert P to an equivalent annual amount** using the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**: $$A_{P} = P \cdot \left(\frac{A}{P}, i, n\right) = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$ > The CRF literally *recovers* the capital by spreading it over *n* years at interest *i*. **Step 3 — Convert S to an equivalent annual amount** using the **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**: $$A_{S} = S \cdot \left(\frac{A}{F}, i, n\right) = S \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$ > The SFF is smaller than CRF because salvage is recovered only at year *n* (one-time future event). **Step 4 — Combine** all annual components: $$EUAC = P \cdot \left(\frac{A}{P}, i, n\right) - S \cdot \left(\frac{A}{F}, i, n\right) + AOC$$ > **Alternative (algebraically equivalent) form** — often used when you want to *emphasize* the opportunity cost of capital tied up in salvage: $$EUAC = (P - S) \cdot \left(\frac{A}{P}, i, n\right) + S \cdot i + AOC$$ Here, the term **S × i** represents the *interest the owner forgoes* by keeping the salvage value locked up in the asset instead of in a bank. **Step 5 — Decision rule**: - If comparing **cost alternatives** → choose the one with the **minimum EUAC**. - If EUAC is **negative**, the alternative is *earning* more than MARR (highly desirable for revenue projects). ### 2.3 Capitalized Cost Method — Deep Logic The Capitalized Cost method is reserved for **perpetual services** (n → ∞). A perpetuity's Present Worth simplifies elegantly: $$P_{\infty} = \frac{A}{i}$$ For a project with **one-time initial cost (P)** + **perpetual annual cost (AOC)**: $$P_{\infty} = P + \frac{AOC}{i}$$ For alternatives with **finite life + perpetual replacement** (e.g., a pump replaced forever): $$P_{\infty} = P + \frac{P - S}{(1+i)^{n}-1} + \frac{AOC}{i}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **When to Use Capitalized Cost vs. EUAC?** > - **Capitalized Cost** → installations expected to last *forever* (public monuments, irrigation canals, nuclear waste storage). > - **EUAC** → equipment with *finite, bounded* useful life (machinery, vehicles, software). > - The two methods are **mathematically linked**: **EUAC = P∞ × i** (i.e., the annual income that the capitalized principal would generate). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Expression | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | EUAC (Standard) | $EUAC = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) - S \cdot (A/F, i, n) + AOC$ | Compute equivalent annual cost | | 2 | EUAC (Alt. form) | $EUAC = (P-S) \cdot (A/P, i, n) + S \cdot i + AOC$ | Emphasises opportunity cost of locked salvage | | 3 | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Converts P → A over n years | | 4 | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $(A/F, i, n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Converts F → A over n years | | 5 | Capitalized Cost (Perpetuity) | $P_{\infty} = \dfrac{A}{i}$ | PW of an infinite annual series A | | 6 | Capitalized Cost (Project) | $P_{\infty} = P + \dfrac{AOC}{i}$ | Initial + perpetuity of annual cost | | 7 | EAW ↔ PW | $EAW = PW \cdot (A/P, i, n)$ | Convert any PW to EAW | | 8 | EUAC ↔ CC | $EUAC = P_{\infty} \cdot i$ | Link between the two methods | | 9 | Decision Rule (Cost) | Choose min EUAC | Mutually exclusive cost alternatives | | 10 | Decision Rule (Revenue) | Choose max EAW | Mutually exclusive revenue alternatives | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mechanical Engineering**: Comparing CNC machines with different lives and maintenance schedules. - **Civil Engineering**: Selecting bridge designs — initial steel cost vs. perpetual inspection costs. - **Electrical Engineering**: Choosing between transformer technologies (copper losses as AOC). - **Public Sector / Government**: Evaluating irrigation, highway, and metro projects where the service is *perpetual* — Capitalized Cost dominates. - **Sustainability Audits**: Annualizing carbon-tax or energy costs to compare "green" retrofits against status-quo operations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the EUAC Formula from First Principles We start with a **Present Worth (PW)** of a generic project: $$PW = -P + (P/F, i, n) \cdot S - \sum_{t=1}^{n} (P/F, i, t) \cdot AOC_t$$ Assuming **AOC is uniform** (a constant A every year from 1 to n): $$PW = -P + S(1+i)^{-n} - A \cdot \left[\frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}\right]$$ $$PW = -P + S(1+i)^{-n} - A \cdot (P/A, i, n)$$ Now apply the **Equivalence Principle** — multiply PW by the Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, i, n): $$EUAC = PW \cdot (A/P, i, n)$$ $$EUAC = -P \cdot (A/P, i, n) + S \cdot (1+i)^{-n} \cdot (A/P, i, n) - A \cdot (P/A, i, n)(A/P, i, n)$$ Using the identity $(P/A, i, n)(A/P, i, n) = 1$ and $(1+i)^{-n}(A/P, i, n) = (A/F, i, n)$: $$\boxed{EUAC = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) - S \cdot (A/F, i, n) + AOC}$$ > **Sign convention reminder**: *P* and *AOC* are costs (positive on the cost-side); *S* is a benefit (reduces the annual cost burden, hence subtracted). ### 3.2 Derivation of Capitalized Cost for a Perpetual Project Consider an alternative with: - Initial cost *P* at year 0 - Perpetual uniform annual cost *A* from year 1 to ∞ The present worth of the perpetual series is the limit of a uniform series as n → ∞: $$P_{\infty} = \lim_{n \to \infty} A \cdot (P/A, i, n) = \lim_{n \to \infty} A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$$ As *n* → ∞, $(1+i)^{n}$ → ∞, so the expression simplifies to: $$P_{\infty} = \lim_{n \to \infty} A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} = A \cdot \frac{1 - 0}{i}$$ $$\boxed{P_{\infty} = \frac{A}{i}}$$ For a project with initial cost *P* + perpetual annual cost *A*: $$\boxed{P_{\infty} = P + \frac{A}{i}}$$ ### 3.3 Exhaustive Solved Example — Comparing Two Machines > **Problem [Adapted from KTU University Exam Pattern]**: > A manufacturing firm must select between **Machine X** and **Machine Y**: > > | Parameter | Machine X | Machine Y | > |---|---|---| > | Initial Cost (*P*) | ₹ 6,00,000 | ₹ 8,00,000 | > | Useful Life (*n*) | 6 years | 9 years | > | Salvage Value (*S*) | ₹ 1,00,000 | ₹ 1,50,000 | > | Annual Operating Cost | ₹ 1,20,000 | ₹ 90,000 | > > The MARR is **i = 10% per year**. Using the **EUAC method**, determine which machine is more economical. **Step 1 — Look up / Compute Interest Factors for i = 10%** For **Machine X (n = 6)**: $$(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10(1.10)^{6}}{(1.10)^{6}-1}$$ Computing $(1.10)^{6} = 1.771561$: $$(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10 \times 1.771561}{1.771561 - 1} = \frac{0.177156}{0.771561} = 0.22961$$ $$(A/F, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10}{(1.10)^{6}-1} = \frac{0.10}{0.771561} = 0.12961$$ For **Machine Y (n = 9)**: Computing $(1.10)^{9} = 2.357948$: $$(A/P, 10\%, 9) = \frac{0.10 \times 2.357948}{2.357948 - 1} = \frac{0.235795}{1.357948} = 0.17364$$ $$(A/F, 10\%, 9) = \frac{0.10}{2.357948 - 1} = \frac{0.10}{1.357948} = 0.07364$$ **Step 2 — Compute EUAC for Machine X** $$EUAC_X = P_X \cdot (A/P, 10\%, 6) - S_X \cdot (A/F, 10\%, 6) + AOC_X$$ $$EUAC_X = (6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.22961) - (1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.12961) + 1{,}20{,}000$$ $$EUAC_X = 1{,}37{,}766 - 12{,}961 + 1{,}20{,}000$$ $$EUAC_X = 2{,}44{,}805 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 3 — Compute EUAC for Machine Y** $$EUAC_Y = P_Y \cdot (A/P, 10\%, 9) - S_Y \cdot (A/F, 10\%, 9) + AOC_Y$$ $$EUAC_Y = (8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.17364) - (1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.07364) + 90{,}000$$ $$EUAC_Y = 1{,}38{,}912 - 11{,}046 + 90{,}000$$ $$EUAC_Y = 2{,}17{,}866 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 4 — Decision** Since **EUAC_Y (₹ 2,17,866) < EUAC_X (₹ 2,44,805)**, **Machine Y is more economical**, saving approximately ₹ 26,939 per year over Machine X. > **Cross-Verification using Present Worth (LCM = 18 years)**: > - PW of X over 18 years = 3 cycles of 6-year replacement × EUAC converted back. Should also favour Y. > - This confirms the EUAC method's validity under the repeatability assumption. ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — EUAC vs. PW vs. Capitalized Cost | Situation | Best Method | Reason | |---|---|---| | Alternatives with **equal lives** | PW, FW, or EUAC (any) | All give identical rankings | | Alternatives with **unequal lives** | **EUAC** (preferred) | Avoids LCM complexity, handles repetition naturally | | Alternatives with **infinite / very long lives** | **Capitalized Cost** | Mathematical elegance with n → ∞ | | Comparing **only one project** against "do-nothing" | EUAC or PW | Decision based on sign of NPV / EUAC | | **Revenue-generating** alternatives | EAW (Equivalent Annual Worth) | Maximize, not minimize | | Service replacements done in **perpetuity** | Capitalized Cost | Models real-world replacement policy | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — EUAC Calculation Pipeline > The following block diagram illustrates the **computational workflow** for executing an EUAC-based comparison between two or more engineering alternatives. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Start: Problem Inputs] --> B1[Identify P, S, n, AOC for Alt 1] A1 --> B2[Identify P, S, n, AOC for Alt 2] B1 --> C1[Compute CRF: A/P factor for Alt 1] B1 --> C2[Compute SFF: A/F factor for Alt 1] C1 --> D1[Calc Annual Capital Recovery = P x A/P] C2 --> D2[Calc Annual Salvage Recovery = S x A/F] D1 --> E1[Sum: P*(A/P) - S*(A/F) + AOC] D2 --> E1 E1 --> F1[EUAC of Alternative 1] B2 --> C3[Compute CRF for Alt 2] B2 --> C4[Compute SFF for Alt 2] C3 --> D3[Calc Annual Capital Recovery for Alt 2] C4 --> D4[Calc Annual Salvage Recovery for Alt 2] D3 --> E2[Sum: P*(A/P) - S*(A/F) + AOC] D4 --> E2 E2 --> F2[EUAC of Alternative 2] F1 --> G1[Compare EUAC values] F2 --> G1 G1 --> H1{Is it a cost problem?} H1 -- Yes --> I1[Select min EUAC] H1 -- No --> I2[Select max EAW] I1 --> J1[Output: Recommended Alternative] I2 --> J1 ``` ### 4.2 Block Architecture — Capitalized Cost Decision Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR K1[Infinite-Life Project] --> K2[Identify Initial Cost P] K1 --> K3[Identify Perpetual Annual Cost A] K2 --> L1[Compute P_inf = P + A/i] K3 --> L1 L1 --> M1[Capitalized Cost P_inf] M1 --> N1{Comparing Alternatives?} N1 -- Yes --> O1[Select Alt with Minimum P_inf] N1 -- No --> P1[Output: Total Perpetual Investment] O1 --> P1 ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Methodology Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Engineering Investment Decision] --> Q2{Is the service
perpetual?} Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Capitalized Cost Method] Q2 -- No --> Q4{Do alternatives
have equal lives?} Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[PW, FW, or EUAC
all valid] Q4 -- No --> Q6[Use EUAC Method] Q5 --> Q7[Apply Decision Rule] Q6 --> Q7 Q3 --> Q7 Q7 --> Q8[Final Selection] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]** *Define Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC). State the criteria for selecting the best alternative using the EUAC method.* **Model Answer (3 marks)**: > EUAC is the equivalent annual cost of a project obtained by distributing the initial investment, salvage value, and annual operating costs uniformly over the useful life of the project at the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR). > > **Formula**: $EUAC = P(A/P, i, n) - S(A/F, i, n) + AOC$ > > **Selection criterion**: For mutually exclusive cost-driven alternatives, the alternative with the **minimum EUAC** is selected; for revenue-driven alternatives, the one with **maximum EAW** is chosen. *(3 marks for definition + criterion + formula)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** *What is Capitalized Cost? When is this method preferred over the EUAC method?* **Model Answer (3 marks)**: > **Capitalized Cost (P∞)** is the present worth of a perpetual uniform annual cost series, given by $P_{\infty} = A/i$. It represents the lump-sum amount that, if invested at MARR *i*, would generate an annual return equal to *A* forever. > > The Capitalized Cost method is preferred when the alternative provides a **perpetual or near-perpetual service** (e.g., bridges, dams, monuments, irrigation canals), where the assumption of infinite useful life is realistic. For finite-life projects, EUAC is the more appropriate technique. *(3 marks)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024)**: Answer **ONE** full question from the choice given. Each question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]* > Two pumps are being considered for an industrial water-supply system: > > | Parameter | Pump A | Pump B | > |---|---|---| > | Initial Cost (₹) | 90,000 | 1,30,000 | > | Useful Life (years) | 5 | 7 | > | Salvage Value (₹) | 10,000 | 15,000 | > | Annual Operating Cost (₹) | 22,000 | 16,000 | > > The MARR is **12% per year**. Using the **EUAC method**, determine which pump should be recommended. **[14 Marks]** **Part (a) — Compute EUAC of Pump A** **[7 Marks]** Interest factors at i = 12%, n = 5: $$(A/P, 12\%, 5) = \frac{0.12(1.12)^{5}}{(1.12)^{5}-1}$$ Computing $(1.12)^{5} = 1.76234$: $$(A/P, 12\%, 5) = \frac{0.12 \times 1.76234}{0.76234} = \frac{0.21148}{0.76234} = 0.27741$$ $$(A/F, 12\%, 5) = \frac{0.12}{0.76234} = 0.15741$$ Therefore: $$EUAC_A = 90{,}000 \times 0.27741 - 10{,}000 \times 0.15741 + 22{,}000$$ $$EUAC_A = 24{,}967 - 1{,}574 + 22{,}000$$ $$EUAC_A = ₹ 45{,}393 \text{ per year} \quad \text{[7 marks]}$$ **Part (b) — Compute EUAC of Pump B and Decide** **[7 Marks]** Interest factors at i = 12%, n = 7: Computing $(1.12)^{7} = 2.21068$: $$(A/P, 12\%, 7) = \frac{0.12 \times 2.21068}{1.21068} = \frac{0.26528}{1.21068} = 0.21911$$ $$(A/F, 12\%, 7) = \frac{0.12}{1.21068} = 0.09911$$ Therefore: $$EUAC_B = 1{,}30{,}000 \times 0.21911 - 15{,}000 \times 0.09911 + 16{,}000$$ $$EUAC_B = 28{,}484 - 1{,}487 + 16{,}000$$ $$EUAC_B = ₹ 42{,}997 \text{ per year}$$ **Decision**: Since **EUAC_B (₹ 42,997) < EUAC_A (₹ 45,393)**, **Pump B is recommended**. The firm saves approximately ₹ 2,396 per year by choosing Pump B despite its higher initial cost. **[7 marks]** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]* > A municipal corporation is evaluating two design proposals for a public water-supply canal expected to serve the city **in perpetuity**: > > | Parameter | Design 1 (Concrete Lined) | Design 2 (Earthen with Lining) | > |---|---|---| > | Initial Construction Cost (₹) | 50,00,000 | 30,00,000 | > | Useful Life (years) | 20 | 10 | > | Salvage Value (₹) | 5,00,000 | 2,00,000 | > | Annual Maintenance (₹) | 1,50,000 | 2,80,000 | > > MARR = **10% per year**. Using the **Capitalized Cost method**, which design is more economical? **[14 Marks]** **Part (a) — Derive and State the Capitalized Cost Formula for a Finite-Life + Perpetual-Replacement Project** **[7 Marks]** For an alternative with initial cost *P*, life *n*, salvage *S*, and perpetual annual cost *A* (after replacement cycles repeat forever), the capital recovery component (annualised) is: $$A_{CR} = (P - S)(A/P, i, n) + S \cdot i$$ The total equivalent annual cost is: $$EUAC = A_{CR} + AOC$$ Since the project is repeated forever, the **Capitalized Cost** is: $$\boxed{P_{\infty} = \frac{EUAC}{i} = \frac{A_{CR} + AOC}{i}}$$ Equivalently, expanding: $$P_{\infty} = P + \frac{P - S}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} + \frac{AOC}{i}$$ **[7 marks for formula + derivation]** **Part (b) — Apply the Formula to Both Designs and Recommend** **[7 Marks]** **Design 1**: P = 50,00,000, S = 5,00,000, n = 20, A = 1,50,000, i = 10% Compute $(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275$: $$P_{\infty,1} = 50{,}00{,}000 + \frac{50{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{6.7275 - 1} + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{0.10}$$ $$P_{\infty,1} = 50{,}00{,}000 + \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{5.7275} + 15{,}00{,}000$$ $$P_{\infty,1} = 50{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}85{,}682 + 15{,}00{,}000 = ₹ 72{,}85{,}682$$ **Design 2**: P = 30,00,000, S = 2,00,000, n = 10, A = 2,80,000, i = 10% Compute $(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937$: $$P_{\infty,2} = 30{,}00{,}000 + \frac{30{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2.5937 - 1} + \frac{2{,}80{,}000}{0.10}$$ $$P_{\infty,2} = 30{,}00{,}000 + \frac{28{,}00{,}000}{1.5937} + 28{,}00{,}000$$ $$P_{\infty,2} = 30{,}00{,}000 + 17{,}57{,}026 + 28{,}00{,}000 = ₹ 75{,}57{,}026$$ **Decision**: Since **P∞,1 (₹ 72.86 Lakh) < P∞,2 (₹ 75.57 Lakh)**, **Design 1 (Concrete Lined Canal)** is more economical, saving about ₹ 2.71 Lakh in capitalized terms. **[7 marks]** --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Traps in EUAC & Capitalized Cost Problems**: > > 1. **Sign Error on Salvage**: Students often *add* salvage instead of *subtracting* it. Recall: salvage is a benefit received, so it **reduces** the annualised cost. Losing this fetches a **−1 to −2 mark penalty**. > > 2. **Wrong Factor Usage**: Confusing (A/P, i, n) with (A/F, i, n) is the #1 mistake. Memory aid: **P**resent worth to **A**nnual uses **(A/P)**; **F**uture worth to **A**nnual uses **(A/F)**. > > 3. **Forgetting to Spread Initial Cost**: In capitalized cost problems, students sometimes only compute A/i and forget the upfront P. The full formula is **P + A/i**, not just A/i. > > 4. **Mixing Lives without LCM Note**: If the examiner allows PW with LCM, students must explicitly **state the LCM assumption**. Otherwise, EUAC is the safer default. > > 5. **Wrong Decision Direction**: For *cost* problems, choose **minimum** EUAC; for *revenue* problems, choose **maximum** EAW. Reversing this is a fatal flaw (−2 to −3 marks). > > 6. **Unit Inconsistency**: MARR must be in decimal (10% = 0.10), not percentage. Always quote the value with its decimal form in the formula. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **EUAC** = **P × (A/P, i, n) − S × (A/F, i, n) + AOC** — the master equation. - ✅ For **cost alternatives**, the rule is **MINIMISE EUAC**; for **revenue alternatives**, **MAXIMISE EAW**. - ✅ **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ — converts P to A. - ✅ **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)** = $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ — converts F to A. Always smaller than CRF. - ✅ **Capitalized Cost** = $P + A/i$ — used for **perpetual** installations. - ✅ **EUAC ↔ Capitalized Cost link**: Multiply EUAC by (1/i) to get P∞, or vice versa. - ✅ When lives are **unequal**, **EUAC is the preferred** method (avoids LCM pitfalls). - ✅ Always verify the **sign of salvage** (subtract it) and the **direction of decision** (min vs. max). - ✅ For **infinite-life** projects (bridges, dams), **Capitalized Cost is mathematically equivalent** to EUAC evaluated as n → ∞. - ✅ The **opportunity-cost form** — $EUAC = (P - S)(A/P, i, n) + S \cdot i + AOC$ — is useful for explaining *why* a higher salvage value reduces annual cost. - ✅ **Repeatability assumption** is implicit in EUAC — alternatives are assumed to be replaced identically forever.
